claim c7 [quantitative]: FaE and EaE both answer around a quarter of answerable questions in zero-shot setting (without fine-tuning) on FreebaseQA Wikidata Answerable, with FaE showing slight advantage (26.9% vs 24.8%)
claim c8 [qualitative]: The model is pretrained on Wikipedia (30.58 million 128-token pieces, 85.58 million training examples) and Wikidata KB (1.54 million KB triples), with 500,000 training steps at batch size 2048

An Enhanced Knowledge Injection Model for Commonsense Generation (2020)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: retrieve-and-edit, knowledge-injection, prototype-weighting, position-encoding, auxiliary-classification
EKI-BART augments BART (pretrained encoder-decoder transformer) with two trainable modules that operate on retrieved prototype sentences. The system concatenates input concepts and a prototype retrieved from external corpora, then adds group embeddings to distinguish concept tokens from prototype tokens. A scaling module learns per-token importance weights applied during encoder-decoder attention, downweighting noise and highlighting scenario-relevant tokens. A position indicator function computes the minimum distance from each prototype token to the nearest concept token, encoding this distance as an additional embedding added to the decoder's attention. An auxiliary classification loss during training encourages the scaling module to predict which prototype tokens will appear in the target output.
claim c1 [qualitative]: EKI-BART achieves 24.36 BLEU-2 and 45.42 BLEU-4 on CommonGen test set with in-domain prototype corpus, compared to BART baseline of 22.87 BLEU-2 and 41.44 BLEU-4
claim c2 [qualitative]: EKI-BART achieves 25.43 BLEU-2 and 46.53 BLEU-4 on CommonGen test set with out-of-domain prototype corpus (Wikipedia), compared to BART baseline of 23.15 BLEU-2 and 44.71 BLEU-4
claim c3 [qualitative]: All evaluation metrics show improvement: ROUGE-2/L, BLEU-3/4, METEOR, CIDEr, and SPICE all increase with EKI-BART across in-domain and out-of-domain settings
claim c4 [qualitative]: The scaling module outperforms hard masking strategies (HM1, HM2) because hard masking removes additional effective concepts that contribute to the target scene, whereas soft weighting retains this information while downweighting noise
claim c5 [quantitative]: Adding prototype position indicator to BART+GE+SM yields 0.7 BLEU-3 improvement on development set and 0.8 BLEU-3 improvement on test set (from 44.7 to 45.4 dev; 45.2 to 46.0 test)
claim c6 [qualitative]: Group embedding (GE) that distinguishes concept tokens from prototype tokens improves generation quality, and the combination of GE with scaling module (SM) further enhances performance
claim c7 [qualitative]: In-domain corpora (VaTeX, SNLI, Activity, CommonGen training) retrieve more relevant prototypes than out-of-domain Wikipedia, though out-of-domain retrieval still provides measurable benefit
claim c8 [negative]: Retrieve-only baseline (using only retrieved prototypes without editing via BART) performs substantially worse than pretrained models, indicating that retrieval alone is insufficient for commonsense generation

Verb Knowledge Injection for Multilingual Event Processing (2020)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: adapter-injection, lexical-knowledge, verb-semantics, event-extraction, cross-lingual-transfer, knowledge-specialization
The system injects explicit verb knowledge from curated lexical resources (VerbNet, FrameNet) into pretrained Transformer encoders via dedicated adapter modules. Verb adapters are trained on a binary word-pair classification task (predicting if two verbs belong to the same semantic class) using lexical constraints extracted from the resources. During downstream fine-tuning for event extraction, the verb adapters complement the pretrained LM knowledge either through full fine-tuning (both Transformer and adapter parameters updated) or task-adapter fine-tuning (Transformer and verb adapters frozen, new task adapter trained on top). For cross-lingual transfer, the approach either applies English-trained adapters zero-shot via multilingual BERT, or automatically translates English verb pairs to target languages, filters them via a relation prediction model, and trains target-language adapters.
claim c1 [quantitative]: VerbNet adapter injection yields consistent statistically significant improvements on English TempEval event trigger identification and classification in task-adapter setup
claim c2 [quantitative]: FrameNet adapter provides significant performance boosts on English ACE trigger identification task
claim c3 [quantitative]: VerbNet adapter improves English ACE argument identification task performance in task-adapter setup
claim c4 [quantitative]: FrameNet adapter improves zero-shot transfer on Spanish TempEval via multilingual BERT in task-adapter setup
claim c5 [quantitative]: Noisily translated VerbNet verb pairs via vtrans yield consistent gains on Chinese TempEval in monolingual task-adapter setup
claim c6 [quantitative]: FrameNet adapter provides significant improvements on Arabic ACE trigger identification via zero-shot mBERT transfer
claim c7 [negative]: Random adapter baseline (same architecture as verb adapter but randomly initialized) shows no consistent improvements, indicating gains stem from the verb knowledge signal rather than parameter capacity alone
claim c8 [qualitative]: Noisily translated verb pairs carry sufficient useful signal through to target languages despite translation errors from imperfect cross-lingual embedding spaces and polysemy

Web Question Answering with Neurosymbolic Program Synthesis (2021)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: program-synthesis, neurosymbolic-dsl, tree-navigation, optimal-search, transductive-learning, ensemble-selection
WebQA is a neurosymbolic program synthesis system that extracts information from structurally heterogeneous webpages. It combines pre-trained neural NLP models (keyword matching, question answering, entity extraction) with symbolic constructs for tree navigation and string manipulation in a domain-specific language (DSL). Given a natural language query, keywords, and a few labeled webpages, the system synthesizes DSL programs via optimal enumerative search that maximizes F1 score, then uses transductive learning over unlabeled webpages to select a single program with good generalization.
claim c1 [qualitative]: WebQA significantly outperforms existing tools such as state-of-the-art question answering models and wrapper induction systems
claim c2 [qualitative]: The synthesis algorithm generates all DSL programs that achieve optimal F1 score on the training examples
claim c3 [qualitative]: The synthesis technique is compositional, prunes the search space by exploiting a monotonicity property of the DSL with respect to recall, and uses transductive learning to select programs with good generalization power
claim c4 [qualitative]: The approach combines pre-trained neural NLP models for keyword matching, question answering, and entity extraction with standard programming language constructs for string processing and tree traversal
claim c5 [qualitative]: Transductive learning generates soft labels for unlabeled webpages based on an ensemble of optimal candidate programs and selects the program whose output most closely matches the soft labels
claim c6 [negative]: Standard heuristics (e.g., based on program size) are not effective at distinguishing between the many (hundreds of) optimal programs on labeled data

The Power of Scale for Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning (2021)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: soft-prompts, parameter-efficient-tuning, frozen-model-adaptation, learned-input-conditioning
Prompt tuning learns soft prompt embeddings prepended to frozen T5 encoder inputs. A fixed sequence of special tokens has learnable embedding parameters (soft prompts) that are updated via backpropagation during task training, while all model weights remain frozen. The soft prompt is concatenated with embedded task input and flows through the standard T5 encoder-decoder as normal text-to-text generation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Prompt tuning matches the strong performance of model tuning (where all model weights are tuned) as model size exceeds billions of parameters, closing the gap on SuperGLUE.
claim c2 [quantitative]: At XXL size (11 billion parameters), prompt tuning matches multi-task model tuning baseline despite having over 20,000× fewer task-specific parameters.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Prompt-tuned T5-Small matches GPT-3 XL (over 400× larger), and prompt-tuned T5-Large beats GPT-3 175B (over 100× larger) on SuperGLUE few-shot.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Prompt tuning outperforms model tuning on zero-shot domain transfer to TextbookQA with a +12.5 F1 gap, and shows larger gains in cases of larger domain shifts (e.g., to Biomedical and Textbooks).
claim c5 [quantitative]: Five-prompt ensemble trained on single frozen T5-XXL achieves 91.3 SuperGLUE score, exceeding both the single-prompt average (90.5) and the best individual prompt (91.0).
claim c6 [qualitative]: XXL model is robust to hyperparameter choices across prompt length, initialization strategy, and pre-training objective; largest model is most forgiving of non-ideal configurations.
claim c7 [negative]: T5 models pre-trained on span corruption objective show instability across model sizes (only 1 out of 5 models work well), with mid-sized models never learning to output legal class labels, copying sub-spans from input, or predicting empty strings; LM adaptation resolves this unreliability across all sizes.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Learned soft prompts show little interpretability as sequences, but nearest-neighbor analysis reveals tight semantic clustering (e.g., 'Technology' cluster, 'entirely/completely' cluster), suggesting prompts learn word-like representations; class-label initialization often persists after training.

Enriching Transformers with Structured Tensor-Product Representations for Abstractive Summarization (2021)
seam: own-activations; tags: tensor-product-representation, role-filler-binding, discrete-role-vectors, structured-representations, compositional-semantics, interpretability-probing
TP-Transformer enriches the standard Transformer encoder-decoder with Tensor Product Representations (TPR) by factorizing each token's representation into two components at every layer: a role vector (encoding structural/syntactic information) computed via attention over a learned dictionary of role embeddings, and a filler vector (the multi-head attention output encoding semantic content). These are bound via Hadamard product and combined with residual connections before passing through feed-forward layers. This introduces a structural inductive bias that encourages discrete role spaces and disentangled syntax/semantics.
claim c1 [qualitative]: On XSum with beam search (4), TP-Transformer (TPT-d) achieves ROUGE-1/ROUGE-2/ROUGE-L/METEOR of 35.84/14.06/28.40/25.79, outperforming standard Transformer at 34.46/12.97/27.21/24.42
claim c2 [qualitative]: On Wikihow with beam search (4), TP-Transformer achieves ROUGE-1/ROUGE-2/ROUGE-L/METEOR of 36.70/13.75/27.36/22.53, outperforming Transformer at 35.91/13.49/27.01/21.57
claim c3 [qualitative]: On Arxiv with beam search (4), TP-Transformer achieves ROUGE-1/ROUGE-2/ROUGE-L/METEOR of 38.36/11.43/34.29/23.61, outperforming Transformer at 38.01/11.33/34.02/23.19
claim c4 [qualitative]: On CNN/Daily Mail with beam search (4), TP-Transformer achieves 41.01/18.38/37.91/34.34, achieving competitive performance with Transformer at 41.22/18.70/38.09/34.50
claim c5 [qualitative]: On XSum human evaluation (120 samples), TP-Transformer wins 52 out of 120 on overall preference versus Transformer's 48, with TP-Transformer winning 47 samples on grammar versus Transformer's 39
claim c6 [qualitative]: More than 90% of learned role attention distributions have a maximum score larger than 0.98, indicating that role vectors reside in a highly discrete space and induce syntactic structures
claim c7 [qualitative]: Probing decoder role vectors on POS tagging shows they encode grammatical information, while filler vectors represent semantics; TPR representations outperform both roles and fillers alone at every layer
claim c8 [negative]: On CNN/Daily Mail, the least abstractive dataset, TP-Transformer achieves only competitive performance with standard Transformer, with the largest advantages observed on more abstractive datasets (XSum, Wikihow)

Bridge Networks: Relating Inputs through Vector-Symbolic Manipulations (2021)
seam: own-activations; tags: vector-symbolic-architecture, information-bottleneck, symbolic-reasoning, local-losses
Bridge networks combine information bottleneck theory with vector-symbolic architectures to create an information processing system that relates inputs through vector-symbolic manipulations. The architecture aims to address global loss dependencies and catastrophic forgetting by processing information through structured symbolic representations rather than end-to-end gradient flows.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Bridge networks provide unique advantages which can address the problem of global losses and catastrophic forgetting
claim c2 [qualitative]: The architecture provides a basis for increasing energy efficiency of execution
claim c3 [qualitative]: The architecture provides a basis for the ability to reason symbolically

Techniques for Symbol Grounding with SATNet (2021)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: symbol-grounding, neurosymbolic, self-supervised-pretraining, permutation-learning, knowledge-distillation, differentiable-sat-solver
The system combines unsupervised clustering (InfoGAN), knowledge distillation into a digit classifier (LeNet), a differentiable MAXSAT solver (SATNet), and a learned permutation-alignment mechanism. Information flows from raw images → clustering → distilled classifier → SATNet constraint layer with a novel Symbol Grounding Loss that learns the permutation between pre-trained and label encodings without access to input labels. An optional proofreader layer (linear transformation) is applied post-training. The architecture solves ungrounded Visual MAXSAT problems where only output variable labels are available.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Our method achieves full accuracy even with a harder problem setup that prevents any label leakage, improving the state of the art on Ungrounded Visual Sudoku from 0% to 64.8% total board accuracy
claim c2 [quantitative]: Our method achieves 98.4 ± 0.2% per-cell accuracy on Ungrounded Visual Sudoku
claim c3 [quantitative]: Our method achieves 98.9 ± 0.1% visual accuracy (input cell classification) on Ungrounded Visual Sudoku
claim c4 [quantitative]: With proofreading, our method achieves 64.8 ± 3.0% total board accuracy on Ungrounded Visual Sudoku, compared to 62.8 ± 3.2% without proofreading
claim c5 [quantitative]: InfoGAN clustering achieves 89.6 ± 7.7% clustering accuracy on the digit clustering task
claim c6 [quantitative]: Distilled LeNet achieves 86.2 ± 13.5% digit clustering accuracy, distilled from InfoGAN (89.6 ± 7.7%), with 1,049,080 parameters vs InfoGAN's 7,307,997 parameters
claim c7 [negative]: Our system requires roughly at least 88% clustering accuracy in order for the rest of the pipeline to progress; performance shows a sharp drop below this threshold
claim c8 [negative]: 4 out of 10 runs of the pre-training pipeline converge to a clustering below the 88% threshold necessary to ground symbols, indicating sensitivity to random seed initialization

Multimodal Few-Shot Learning with Frozen Language Models (2021)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vision-language-grounding, prefix-tuning, frozen-lm, multimodal-prompting, image-text-interleaving, few-shot-learning
Frozen trains a vision encoder (NF-ResNet-50) to map images into continuous embeddings matching a pre-trained frozen language model's token embedding space. The vision encoder outputs are linearly projected and reshaped into a sequence of 2 visual prefix embeddings. During training, image-caption pairs from Conceptual Captions are used; the language model weights remain frozen while backpropagation flows through its self-attention layers to optimize the encoder. At inference, images and text can be interleaved arbitrarily in the prompt, enabling the frozen language model to perform multimodal tasks via in-context learning.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On VQAv2, zero-shot transfer from captioning training achieves 29.5% accuracy; with 1-shot in-context learning 35.7%; with 4-shot 38.2%.
claim c2 [quantitative]: On OKVQA (requiring outside knowledge), the 7B parameter model achieves 12.5% zero-shot accuracy and 19.6% with VQAv2 training data mixed in.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Open-Ended miniImageNet 2-way with task induction and 5 inner-shots, Frozen achieves 58.9% accuracy; performance scales with number of distinct exemplars (inner-shots) more than repetitions of single exemplars.
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Open-Ended miniImageNet 5-way, Frozen achieves only 22.3% accuracy with 3 inner-shots and task induction, not significantly above 20% chance baseline.
claim c5 [quantitative]: On Fast-VQA (binding new words to visual categories), Frozen improves from 1.6% with 0 shots to 7.9% with 5 shots; blind baseline remains near 0.4%.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Keeping the language model frozen generalizes substantially better to visual question-answering than fine-tuning the language model weights.
claim c7 [negative]: A model trained from scratch end-to-end shows no transfer at all from captioning to VQA, suggesting that the generalization abilities of large language models rely upon large-scale text-only training in which predicting the next token mimics the test setting.
claim c8 [negative]: Frozen achieves far from state-of-the-art performance on specific tasks it learns in a few shots compared to systems using the full training set for those tasks.

Program Synthesis with Large Language Models (2021)
seam: text; tags: few-shot-prompting, fine-tuning, code-generation, program-synthesis, in-context-learning
Dense left-to-right decoder-only Transformer language models (244M to 137B parameters) pre-trained on web documents, dialog data, Wikipedia, and code-containing websites. Models are evaluated in two regimes: few-shot prompting via in-context examples, and fine-tuning on small task-specific datasets. The system generates Python code token-by-token conditioned on natural language problem descriptions and optional input-output examples. Functional correctness is verified by executing generated code against test cases.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On MBPP, the largest models without finetuning can synthesize solutions to 59.6% of the problems using few-shot learning with a well-designed prompt
claim c2 [quantitative]: Fine-tuning on MBPP improves performance by about 10 percentage points across most model sizes
claim c3 [quantitative]: On MathQA-Python, the largest fine-tuned model achieves 83.8% accuracy
claim c4 [qualitative]: Synthesis performance scales log-linearly with model size across evaluated models from 244M to 137B parameters
claim c5 [quantitative]: Natural language feedback from a human halves the error rate compared to the model's initial prediction, improving few-shot performance from 30% without human feedback to 65% with four turns of dialog
claim c6 [negative]: Even the largest models are generally unable to predict the output of a program given a specific input, whether few-shot or with fine-tuning
claim c7 [quantitative]: Only 32 of 974 problems (3.3%) in MBPP had more than half of their lines matched in the pre-training data; 91.5% had only one or two lines of code overlapping with the training set
claim c8 [qualitative]: Few-shot performance is quite sensitive to the particular examples given in the prompt; one set of examples solves 60% of tasks while many other examples solve far fewer, and ensembling prompts boosts performance from 59.6% to 66.4%

PICARD: Parsing Incrementally for Constrained Auto-Regressive Decoding from Language Models (2021)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, incremental-parsing, beam-search, sql-validation, inference-time
PICARD is an inference-time constrained decoding method that wraps any auto-regressive language model decoder. At each generation step, it receives the model's log-softmax scores over vocabulary and the partial hypothesis token sequence. PICARD restricts scoring to the top-k highest-probability tokens, then applies incremental SQL parsing (via monadic parser combinators) to reject inadmissible tokens by setting their scores to negative infinity. The method operates on the detokenized surface form of SQL and has access to schema metadata (table/column names). Four parsing modes—lexing, parsing-without-guards, parsing-with-guards, and off—control the stringency of constraint checking. Accepted tokens retain their model score; rejected tokens are masked from beam search. PICARD is entirely inference-time; the base model is frozen and unmodified.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On Spider development set, T5-3B+Picard achieves exact-set-match accuracy of 75.5% and execution accuracy of 79.3%
claim c2 [quantitative]: On Spider test set, T5-3B+Picard achieves exact-set-match accuracy of 71.9% and execution accuracy of 75.1%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On CoSQL dialog state tracking development set, T5-3B+Picard achieves question match accuracy of 56.9% and interaction match accuracy of 24.2%
claim c4 [quantitative]: With Picard parsing with guards at top-2, T5-3B reduces invalid SQL predictions from 12% to 2% on Spider development set
claim c5 [quantitative]: Decoding speed on T5-3B with beam size 4 on NVIDIA A100 GPU: 2.5 seconds per sample without Picard, 3.1 seconds per sample with Picard
claim c6 [qualitative]: PICARD is compatible with any existing auto-regressive language model decoder and vocabulary, does not require custom control tokens or model architecture, and can be easily enabled or disabled at inference time without modifying the pre-trained model
claim c7 [qualitative]: PICARD operates through four parsing modes of increasing stringency: lexing (lexical checking), parsing-without-guards (grammatical checking), parsing-with-guards (semantic binding enforcement), and off (no checking); each higher mode subsumes all constraints of lower modes
claim c8 [negative]: Without Picard, these T5 models do not benefit from beam search; performance is largely independent of beam size in lexing mode

LiT: Zero-Shot Transfer with Locked-image text Tuning (2021)
seam: text; tags: contrastive-learning, vision-language-alignment, zero-shot-transfer, frozen-backbone, image-text-retrieval
LiT (Locked-image Tuning) is a contrastive learning method that aligns pre-trained image models with text models for zero-shot transfer. During training, a frozen pre-trained image encoder produces fixed embeddings, while a trainable text encoder learns to map natural language descriptions into the image embedding space via contrastive loss (maximizing similarity of paired image-text embeddings, minimizing non-paired). At test time, class descriptions are embedded with the text model and matched against image embeddings for zero-shot classification or retrieval. The method decouples image representation learning (via supervised or self-supervised pre-training on clean labeled data) from vision-language alignment (via noisier image-text web data).
claim c1 [quantitative]: With the transformer-based pre-trained ViT-g/14 model, the LiT model achieves 85.2% zero-shot transfer accuracy on the ImageNet test set
claim c2 [quantitative]: LiT achieves 82.5% accuracy on the challenging ObjectNet test set, outperforming the previous state-of-the-art method (CLIP) by 10.2%
claim c3 [quantitative]: With LiT the model achieves 81.7% top-1 accuracy on zero-shot ImageNet transfer with only 300M image-text pairs seen, compared to CLIP which required 12.8B image-text pairs to reach 76.2% accuracy
claim c4 [quantitative]: Using only public datasets (YFCC100m-CLIP and CC12M), LiT achieves 75.7% zero-shot transfer on ImageNet, an absolute improvement of 30.9% over the previously reported state-of-the-art result
claim c5 [quantitative]: On the 4 billion image-text pair dataset, the Lu (locked image) setting achieves 70.1% ImageNet accuracy compared to Uu (unlocked pre-trained image) at 57.2% and uu (random image) at 50.6%
claim c6 [qualitative]: Locking the image tower leads to substantially worse contrastive loss on the dataset used for LiT, but substantially better loss on out-of-distribution datasets such as COCO captions, indicating that the locked text model learns to align with a general image representation rather than specializing to the tuning dataset
claim c7 [qualitative]: LiT works reliably with multiple pre-training methods (supervised and unsupervised) and across diverse architectures (ResNet, Vision Transformers and MLP-Mixer) using three different image-text datasets
claim c8 [qualitative]: Training on the full unfiltered YFCC100m dataset with multilingual text improves performance on non-English languages much more than on English, and using a multilingual pre-trained text model (mT5) significantly helps languages that do not use the Latin script

Improving language models by retrieving from trillions of tokens (2021)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: retrieval-augmented-generation, chunked-cross-attention, semi-parametric, frozen-retriever, kv-database, nearest-neighbour-search
RETRO is a retrieval-enhanced autoregressive language model that splits input sequences into chunks and retrieves similar document chunks from a 2-trillion-token database using frozen BERT embeddings. Retrieved neighbours are encoded by a bi-directional transformer encoder, then integrated into the main causal decoder via a chunked cross-attention (CCA) mechanism that attends across both neighbour and time dimensions. The frozen retriever and differentiable encoder-decoder allow the model to condition token prediction on retrieved context while maintaining autoregressivity and enabling training at scale.
claim c1 [qualitative]: RETRO obtains comparable performance to GPT-3 and Jurassic-1 on the Pile, despite using 25× fewer parameters
claim c2 [quantitative]: The performance gain of retrieval models remains constant with model scale and is comparable to multiplying the parametric model size by 25×
claim c3 [qualitative]: Performance gain increases with retrieval database size and plateaus beyond 40 neighbours on C4 validation set
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Wikitext103, RETRO retrofitted from baseline transformer achieves perplexity of 18.97 (test) when retrieving from Wikipedia (4B tokens), compared to kNN-LM perplexity of 16.12
claim c5 [qualitative]: RETRO outperforms baseline transformer on majority of Pile test subsets; on dm_mathematics and ubuntu_irc, retrieved neighbours are not helpful and model underperforms baseline
claim c6 [qualitative]: RETRO can be retrofitted into pre-trained transformers by freezing baseline weights and training only chunked cross-attention and retrieval encoder parameters (less than 10% of weights for 7B model) on 3% of pre-training tokens
claim c7 [qualitative]: Retrieval encoder is conditioned on decoder activations through cross-attention, allowing representations to be modulated by the retrieving chunk in a differentiable way
claim c8 [negative]: RETRO models may benefit more easily from evaluation dataset leakage due to direct access to training dataset during evaluation; performance improvements on Wikitext103 are partly due to partial overlap with training data not caught by deduplication

Synchromesh: Reliable code generation from pre-trained language models (2022)
seam: text; tags: few-shot-retrieval, constrained-decoding, completion-engine, brzozowski-derivatives, syntactic-constraint, semantic-constraint
Synchromesh is a two-stage framework for improving code generation from frozen pre-trained LLMs (GPT-3, Codex). First, Target Similarity Tuning (TST) fine-tunes a Sentence-BERT model to retrieve few-shot examples by matching program structure (AST similarity) rather than surface natural language similarity. Second, Constrained Semantic Decoding (CSD) guides token-by-token generation by querying domain-specific Completion Engines (CEs) that encode syntactic and semantic constraints, using Brzozowski derivatives to align LLM tokenization with language-valid token sequences. Neither component requires retraining the base LM.
claim c1 [quantitative]: CSD improves execution accuracy on SQL from 56% to 61% for Codex 175B
claim c2 [quantitative]: CSD improves validity (executable programs) in SQL from 73% to 85% for Codex 175B
claim c3 [quantitative]: CSD eliminates execution errors in 29% of SQL queries generated by GPT-3 13B (validity improves from 43% to 72%)
claim c4 [quantitative]: CSD + TST improves exact-match accuracy in SMCalFlow from 45% to 63% for Codex 175B
claim c5 [quantitative]: In Vega-Lite, GPT-3 13B produces valid charts for 55% of queries unconstrained; CSD eliminates all errors (100% valid)
claim c6 [quantitative]: CSD reduces average normalized tree edit distance from 0.41 to 0.18 for GPT-3 175B in SMCalFlow
claim c7 [qualitative]: CSD adds an average of 8% overhead to the sampling procedure
claim c8 [negative]: TST cannot guarantee elimination of conceptual errors; when conceptual errors occur, CSD is usually insufficient to correct the prediction

Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT (2022)
seam: own-activations; tags: causal-tracing, knowledge-editing, mlp-intervention, rank-one-update, factual-association
The system combines two causal analysis methods on autoregressive transformers (GPT-2, GPT-J). First, Causal Tracing uses paired interventions (clean run, corrupted run, corrupted-with-restoration run) to measure indirect effects of hidden states on factual predictions, identifying mid-layer MLP modules at the subject's final token as decisive. Second, Rank-One Model Editing (ROME) treats MLP layers as linear associative memories, computing a rank-one weight update to insert new key-value pairs (subject→object) directly into feed-forward weights, targeting the identified causal sites.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Factual associations in GPT correspond to localized, directly-editable computations in mid-layer feed-forward MLP modules at the final token of the subject entity
claim c2 [quantitative]: Average Indirect Effect of MLP contributions at the early site (last subject token) peaks at 6.6%
claim c3 [quantitative]: ROME achieves 99.8% efficacy on zero-shot relation extraction (zsRE) task
claim c4 [quantitative]: On CounterFact dataset, ROME achieves harmonic mean Score of 89.2 on GPT-2 XL and 91.5 on GPT-J, combining efficacy, generalization, and specificity
claim c5 [qualitative]: ROME demonstrates both generalization and specificity simultaneously on counterfactual assertions, whereas fine-tuning (FT) and hypernetwork methods (KE, MEND) sacrifice one or the other
claim c6 [quantitative]: ROME's Neighborhood Score (specificity) on CounterFact is 75.4% for GPT-2 XL and 78.9% for GPT-J, compared to FT+L at 70.3% and 78.6%
claim c7 [qualitative]: ROME edits generalize best at middle layers (peak at layer 18 for GPT-2 XL) and specifically at the final subject token position, confirming causal tracing findings
claim c8 [negative]: ROME introduces some loss in fluency; evaluators rated ROME as 1.3 times less likely to be more fluent than FT+L, though 1.8 times more likely to be consistent with inserted facts

Memorizing Transformers (2022)
seam: own-activations; tags: knn-retrieval, external-memory, long-context, gated-attention, approximate-search
A decoder-only Transformer extended with approximate kNN-augmented attention in one layer near the top of the stack. During inference, a non-differentiable external memory stores (key, value) pairs from recent tokens across documents. For each query token, the model performs both local dense self-attention (over a sliding 512-token window) and approximate kNN lookup into the external memory (retrieving top-k keys/values). The two attention outputs are combined via a learned per-head gate. Position bias is applied only to local attention; retrieved memories receive no position encoding. Memory is cleared at document boundaries. Training uses Transformer-XL style caching of keys/values from the previous step.
claim c1 [quantitative]: adding memory of size 8192 improves perplexity on C4(4K+) from 17.20 to 14.42 for vanilla Transformer with context size 512
claim c2 [qualitative]: performance steadily improves when increasing memory size up to 262K tokens
claim c3 [qualitative]: a Memorizing Transformer with 8k tokens in memory matches the perplexity of a vanilla Transformer with 5X more trainable parameters
claim c4 [qualitative]: step time increased from 0.2s to 0.25s with 8K memory and to 0.6s with 65K memory for a 200M parameter model
claim c5 [qualitative]: the model is capable of making use of newly defined functions and theorems during test time
claim c6 [qualitative]: the model retrieved function and variable names most frequently when accessing external memory
claim c7 [qualitative]: in 8 out of 10 manually checked examples on Isabelle, the model found the body of the lemma it needed to predict
claim c8 [qualitative]: a pretrained 1B vanilla Transformer can be finetuned to use external memory; within 20K finetuning steps it closes half the gap to a 1B Memorizing Transformer, and closes the gap entirely after 100K steps

Achieving Conversational Goals with Unsupervised Post-hoc Knowledge Injection (2022)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: knowledge-retrieval, dpp-selection, gradient-decoding, constrained-generation, entailment-constraint, unsupervised-ranking
POKI is a post-hoc knowledge injection framework for dialog systems that operates at decoding time without retraining. Given a dialog history and an initial response from a base dialog model, it retrieves diverse knowledge snippets from parametric (language models) and non-parametric (corpus) sources. A Determinantal Point Process selects relevant but diverse snippets using PMI-based relevance and redundancy scores. For each selected snippet, gradient-based constrained decoding iteratively modifies the base model's hidden representations via backward passes (maximizing knowledge fidelity and entailment objectives) and forward passes (maintaining fluency), producing candidate responses. An unsupervised ranker selects the final response via Z-normalized aggregation of distinct bigrams and GPT-2 likelihood.
claim c1 [quantitative]: POKI achieves 71.1% accuracy on MultiWOZ test set
claim c2 [quantitative]: POKI achieves 3.78 distinct bigrams (D-2) on MultiWOZ
claim c3 [quantitative]: POKI achieves 3.65 distinct bigrams (D-2) on Wizard-of-Wikipedia
claim c4 [qualitative]: In human evaluation on MultiWOZ, POKI is judged as more engaging (94.3% win vs SimpleTOD) and more interesting (92.7% win vs SimpleTOD) compared to baselines
claim c5 [quantitative]: In real-time user study on goal-oriented dialog (MultiWOZ), POKI achieves 86% goal success rate compared to 69% for Rewriter baseline
claim c6 [quantitative]: In real-time user study on goal-oriented dialog, POKI achieves goal completion in average 4 turns compared to 8 turns for Rewriter
claim c7 [quantitative]: In real-time user study on knowledge-grounded dialog (WoW), 89% of POKI's successful goal cases were directly influenced by post-hoc knowledge injection compared to 70% for BART baseline
claim c8 [qualitative]: The knowledge fidelity constraint is critical for diversity and accuracy; ablation without knowledge fidelity drops accuracy to 70.0% and D-2 to 0.95 on MultiWOZ, while the entailment constraint primarily influences coherence

Atlas: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models (2022)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmented-generation, dense-retrieval, joint-training, distillation, few-shot-learning
Atlas is a retrieval-augmented sequence-to-sequence model consisting of two jointly-trained components: a dense retriever (based on Contriever, using dual-encoder architecture with average pooling) that retrieves top-k documents from a large corpus, and a language model (T5 with Fusion-in-Decoder) that processes retrieved documents independently in the encoder, concatenates their representations, and performs cross-attention decoding. Information flows from query → retriever embeddings → top-k document retrieval → language model encoder (processing each document with query prepended) → concatenated encoder outputs → decoder with cross-attention → text output.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Atlas reaches over 42% accuracy on Natural Questions using only 64 examples, outperforming a 540B parameters model by 3% despite having 50x fewer parameters
claim c2 [quantitative]: Atlas achieves 64.0% accuracy in full-dataset setting with a Wikipedia index on NaturalQuestions, establishing a new state of the art by 8 points
claim c3 [qualitative]: On 64-shot KILT tasks, Atlas with Perplexity Distillation achieves 45.0% exact match on NaturalQuestions, 15.0% F1 on Wizard of Wikipedia, and 77.0% accuracy on FEVER
claim c4 [qualitative]: Jointly pre-training the retriever and language model is crucial for few-shot performance, with models using retrieval during pre-training strongly outperforming models without joint pre-training
claim c5 [qualitative]: Among retriever training objectives (Attention Distillation, EMDR2, Perplexity Distillation, Leave-one-out Perplexity Distillation), no significant systematic differences are observed between methods, with Perplexity Distillation adopted for subsequent experiments due to stability and computational efficiency
claim c6 [qualitative]: Query-side fine-tuning (fixing document encoder parameters) avoids index refresh overhead with minimal performance impact in few-shot settings
claim c7 [qualitative]: Index refresh overhead during pre-training is around 30% compared to training with a fixed retriever, when refreshing every 1,000 steps out of 10,000 total pre-training iterations
claim c8 [qualitative]: Masked language modeling and prefix language modeling pretext tasks both achieve comparable few-shot performance, with masked language modeling slightly preferred (42.4% vs 40.1% average on 64-shot)

Code as Policies: Language Model Programs for Embodied Control (2022)
seam: text; tags: few-shot-prompting, code-generation, hierarchical-codegen, policy-synthesis, robot-control, perception-to-action
Code as Policies (CaP) uses few-shot prompted large language models (LLMs) trained on code completion to generate Python policy code from natural language instructions. The system reads perception outputs (from object detectors, vision-language models) and generates executable Python code that parameterizes control primitive APIs. Code generation is hierarchical: the LLM can recursively define undefined functions, and generated code is executed via Python's exec() with a curated globals dictionary of available perception and control APIs. The mechanism chains logic structures (if/else, loops), third-party libraries (NumPy, Shapely), and first-party robot APIs to map language commands to reactive or waypoint-based policies.
claim c1 [quantitative]: hierarchical code-generation improves state-of-the-art to solve 39.8% of problems on the HumanEval benchmark
claim c2 [quantitative]: hierarchical code-generation achieves 95% pass rate on RoboCodeGen with the Codex davinci model
claim c3 [quantitative]: on unseen task attributes and instructions in simulated table-top manipulation, CaP achieves 80% success rate on long-horizon tasks
claim c4 [quantitative]: on unseen task attributes and instructions in simulated table-top manipulation, CaP achieves 62% success rate on spatial-geometric tasks
claim c5 [qualitative]: hierarchical code-gen helps Productivity the most, which is when the new instruction requires longer code, or code with more logic layers than those in Examples
claim c6 [qualitative]: LMPs can express reactive policies (e.g., impedance controllers) and waypoint-based policies (vision-based pick and place, trajectory-based control) across multiple real robot platforms
claim c7 [negative]: CaP today are restricted by the scope of what the perception APIs can describe (e.g., no visual-language models to date can describe whether a trajectory is bumpy or more C-shaped) and which control primitives are available
claim c8 [negative]: CaP also struggle to interpret commands that are significantly longer or more complex, or operate at a different abstraction level than the given Examples

The Whole Truth and Nothing But the Truth: Faithful and Controllable Dialogue Response Generation with Dataflow Transduction and Constrained Decoding (2022)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, external-engine, grammar-intersection, dataflow-transduction, qcfg, truthfulness-control
A hybrid dialogue response generation system combining rule-based content selection with neural language modeling. Dataflow transduction rules map executable dataflow graphs (representing dialogue agent actions and results) into quasi-synchronous context-free grammars (QCFGs) that specify the space of truthful, contextually acceptable responses. A constrained decoding procedure then performs beam search to select fluent utterances that satisfy both the QCFG constraints and maximize probability under a pretrained neural language model, effectively intersecting grammar-based content control with LM-based fluency.
claim c1 [quantitative]: QCFG-constrained decoding achieves 100% truthfulness on 297 randomly sampled SMCalFlow validation examples in human evaluation, whereas unconstrained LM decoding achieves 82%
claim c2 [qualitative]: QCFG-constrained decoding with 3% training data (268 examples) achieves BLEU scores on par with unconstrained decoding using 100% of training data
claim c3 [qualitative]: On SMCalFlow validation data, QCFG random sampling achieves 100% truthfulness but only 56% relevance; unconstrained decoding achieves 97% relevance but 82% truthfulness; QCFG-constrained decoding achieves 97% relevance and 100% truthfulness
claim c4 [quantitative]: On MultiWOZ 2.1 (100 test examples manually evaluated), QCFG-constrained decoding produces truthful responses on all 100 examples; MTTOD baseline achieves 89 truthful responses
claim c5 [qualitative]: 187 dataflow transduction rules, written by authors in a matter of hours, were sufficient to cover all gold system responses in the SMCalFlow calendar event query subset (8938 training examples)
claim c6 [qualitative]: The system outperforms both rule-based (QCFG random sampling) and end-to-end learned approaches (unconstrained LM decoding) in human evaluations of fluency, relevance, and truthfulness
claim c7 [negative]: Even QCFG-constrained decoding produces pragmatically unhelpful truthful omissions in a few cases, e.g., 'Looks like it' instead of more specific response
claim c8 [negative]: Authoring dataflow transduction rules is labor-intensive for complex domains, and the approach has only been tested on dataflow graphs and English

Relative representations enable zero-shot latent space communication (2022)
seam: own-activations; tags: anchor-based-similarity, latent-isometry-invariance, zero-shot-stitching, relative-representations
The paper proposes using relative representations (latent similarity between each sample and a fixed set of anchors) as an invariant data representation across neural networks. A sample's encoding in one latent space is mapped to similarities with anchor points, creating a representation that is invariant to latent isometries and rescalings. This enables zero-shot communication between latent spaces from different models or training runs without additional training. Information flows from a model's latent space through similarity computation with frozen anchors to produce the relative representation, which can then be used for downstream tasks or cross-model comparison.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Under the same data and modeling choices, the angles between the encodings within distinct latent spaces do not change
claim c2 [qualitative]: Relative representations enforce invariance to latent isometries and rescalings without any additional training
claim c3 [qualitative]: The approach enables zero-shot model stitching and latent space comparison between diverse settings
claim c4 [qualitative]: The generalization capability is validated across different datasets, spanning various modalities (images, text, graphs), tasks (e.g., classification, reconstruction) and architectures (e.g., CNNs, GCNs, transformers)

PAL: Program-aided Language Models (2022)
seam: external-engine; tags: program-generation, code-as-reasoning, interpreter-offload, neuro-symbolic, few-shot-prompting, verifier-loop
PAL uses a large language model (Codex or similar) to read natural language problem descriptions and generate intermediate reasoning steps as Python code with natural language comments. The LLM decomposes the problem into programmatic steps; the generated Python code is then executed by a standard Python interpreter to produce the final answer. The seam between the neural and symbolic components is text: the LLM outputs code-as-text, which crosses the boundary and is executed externally.
claim c1 [quantitative]: PaL using Codex achieves state-of-the-art few-shot accuracy on the gsm8k benchmark of math word problems, surpassing PaLM-540B which uses chain-of-thought by absolute 15% top-1
claim c2 [quantitative]: On gsm-hard (a harder version of gsm8k with larger numbers), PaL remains stable at 61.2%, dropping by only 14.3% from gsm8k, while CoT drops from 65.6% to 23.1% (a relative drop of almost 70%)
claim c3 [quantitative]: PaL using Codex achieves 72.0% solve rate on gsm8k, outperforming CoT with PaLM-540B (58.8%), CoT with LaMDA-137B (58.8%), CoT with UL2 (56.9%), and CoT with Codex (65.6%)
claim c4 [quantitative]: Using majority voting with 40 samples and nucleus sampling (p=0.95, temperature=0.7), PaL achieves 80.4% on gsm8k, obtaining 1.9% higher accuracy than Minerva-540B using the same number of samples
claim c5 [quantitative]: On Colored Objects, PaL improves over CoT by 8.8% and by 19.4% over direct prompting, achieving 95.1%
claim c6 [quantitative]: On Object Counting, PaL achieves 96.7%, improving over CoT by absolute 23.7%
claim c7 [qualitative]: PaL is not limited to code-LMs; it works with natural language LMs such as text-davinci-002 and text-davinci-003 once the LM's code modeling ability is sufficiently high, though weaker models like text-davinci-001 show CoT outperforming PaL
claim c8 [negative]: When requiring the neural LM to execute Python code without using an interpreter (following Nye et al. 2021), the solve rate is 23.2% on gsm8k, much lower than PaL (72.0%) and only 4.5 points higher than Direct prompting, showing the main benefit of PaL comes from synergy with the interpreter, not only from having a better prompt

When Not to Trust Language Models: Investigating Effectiveness of Parametric and Non-Parametric Memories (2022)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmentation, adaptive-gating, parametric-vs-nonparametric, popularity-threshold, open-domain-qa
The paper evaluates and proposes methods for combining parametric knowledge (memorized in LM parameters) and non-parametric knowledge (retrieved text) for open-domain QA. The core system is a retrieval-augmented LM pipeline where a retrieval module (BM25, Contriever, or GenRead) fetches relevant passages from Wikipedia, which are concatenated with the input question before being fed to a decoder-only LM (GPT-3, GPT-Neo, OPT). The 'Adaptive Retrieval' method adds a popularity-based gate that decides whether to invoke retrieval at all, based on the subject entity's Wikipedia page-view count and relationship type, allowing the LM to rely on parametric memory when reliable and retrieval when necessary.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LMs struggle with less popular factual knowledge; scaling fails to appreciably improve memorization of factual knowledge in the long tail. For the 4,000 least popular questions in PopQA, GPT-j 6B has 16% accuracy and GPT-3 davinci-003 has 19% accuracy.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Subject entity popularity has a strong positive correlation with model accuracy across relationship types. GPT-3 davinci-003 shows the highest positive correlation (roughly 0.4) while GPT-Neo-1.3B shows relatively weak positive correlations (approximately 0.1).
claim c3 [quantitative]: Retrieval-augmented LMs significantly outperform unassisted LMs. A much smaller LM (GPT-Neo 2.7B) augmented by Contriever retrieval outperforms vanilla GPT-3 davinci-003. Contriever gives 7% accuracy gains on top of GPT-3 davinci-003.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Retrieval-augmentation can hurt performance on questions about popular entities. For 10% of questions, retrieval causes the LM to incorrectly answer a question it could otherwise answer correctly, with significantly lower recall@1 (0.14 vs 0.42 overall).
claim c5 [quantitative]: Adaptive Retrieval improves performance over non-adaptive methods. The best performance on PopQA using GPT-3 davinci-003 adaptively with GenRead and Contriever yields 46.5% accuracy, 5.3% higher than any non-adaptive method.
claim c6 [quantitative]: Adaptive Retrieval reduces inference latency. For GPT-J 6B, inference time latency almost doubles with concatenated retrieved context; Adaptive Retrieval enables reducing inference time up to 9% from standard retrieval. Adaptive Retrieval also reduces GPT-3 API costs by half.
claim c7 [quantitative]: On EntityQuestions, Adaptive Retrieval reduces API costs by 15% while maintaining equivalent performance to retrieval-only.
claim c8 [negative]: The paper emphasizes that its findings are limited to entity-centric factual knowledge evaluated on synthetic datasets (PopQA and EntityQuestions), and the extent to which results apply to naturally occurring factual knowledge has not been firmly established. It is an open question if the same findings are applicable to other types of world knowledge such as commonsense.

Causal Abstraction: A Theoretical Foundation for Mechanistic Interpretability (2023)
seam: none; tags: causal-abstraction, mechanistic-interpretability, interchange-interventions, alignment-theory, faithfulness-grading, constructive-abstraction
This is a theoretical paper formalizing causal abstraction as a mathematical framework for mechanistic interpretability of neural networks. It does not present a novel neural architecture or training method. Instead, it provides formal definitions and proofs connecting high-level causal models (e.g., symbolic algorithms) to low-level causal models (e.g., neural networks) through alignments between variables, causal consistency conditions, and interchange interventions. Information flows conceptually: a researcher specifies an alignment between clusters of low-level variables and high-level variables; causal abstraction then validates whether interventions on the low-level model produce results consistent with the high-level model's causal laws.
claim c1 [theoretical]: Causal abstraction provides a mathematical framework for mechanistic interpretability by defining when a human-intelligible, high-level causal explanation is a faithful interpretation of opaque low-level details.
claim c2 [theoretical]: We generalize causal abstraction to cyclic causal structures and typed high-level variables, extending beyond prior work limited to acyclic models.
claim c3 [theoretical]: We prove that constructive abstraction holds exactly when the high-level model can be constructed from the low-level model by marginalizing away variables, merging sets of variables, and merging values of variables.
claim c4 [theoretical]: A notion of approximate causal abstraction allows assessment of the degree to which a high-level causal model is a causal abstraction of a lower-level one, defining graded faithfulness.
claim c5 [theoretical]: Multi-source interchange interventions can be used to conduct causal abstraction analyses for high-level causal models with multiple intermediate variables, generalizing prior work restricted to single intermediate variables.
claim c6 [theoretical]: The methods LIME, causal effect estimation, causal mediation analysis, iterated nullspace projection, and circuit-based explanations are all formalizable as special cases of causal abstraction analysis.
claim c7 [theoretical]: Integrated gradients can be used to compute interchange interventions and conduct causal abstraction analysis.

N-best T5: Robust ASR Error Correction using Multiple Input Hypotheses and Constrained Decoding Space (2023)
seam: text; tags: transfer-learning, constrained-decoding, lattice-constrained, n-best-ranking, score-interpolation
N-best T5 is a fine-tuned T5 sequence-to-sequence model for ASR error correction. The encoder processes concatenated N-best ASR hypotheses (prefixed with 'text correction:' and separated by special tokens) instead of a single 1-best hypothesis. The decoder generates corrected transcriptions. The system integrates ASR N-best lists or lattices into the generation space via constrained decoding algorithms that restrict outputs to paths with high ASR probability, combining ASR scores with language model scores via interpolation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Using 10-best T5 with unconstrained decoding achieves 7.2% WER reduction compared to the baseline ASR model on test sets
claim c2 [quantitative]: Using 10-best T5 with lattice-constrained decoding achieves up to 12% WER reduction on the test sets
claim c3 [quantitative]: Oracle WER in 10-best list improves by 42.8% compared to ASR baseline on test sets
claim c4 [quantitative]: Using 5-best T5 achieves 4.7% WER reduction compared to baseline ASR model
claim c5 [qualitative]: Lattice-constrained decoding yields slightly better performance than N-best constrained decoding because a lattice contains more possible paths compared to an N-best list and yields lower oracle WER
claim c6 [qualitative]: 1-best T5 fails to yield good results only using the 1-best decoding hypothesis as model input
claim c7 [qualitative]: The model can be applied to black-box ASR models integrated in cloud services since N-best lists can be easily obtained from ASR systems without the need of in-depth access
claim c8 [qualitative]: Constrained decoding with interpolation weight λ around 0.75 outperforms plain beam search

Eliciting Latent Predictions from Transformers with the Tuned Lens (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: early-exiting, probing, activation-decoding, iterative-inference, causal-analysis
The tuned lens is an interpretability method that decodes intermediate hidden states of frozen pretrained transformers into vocabulary distributions. For each transformer layer, a learned affine transformation (translator) maps the hidden state from that layer's representation space into the final layer's representation space, where it is composed with the pretrained unembedding matrix to produce a distribution over tokens. This enables layer-by-layer analysis of how model predictions evolve during inference without modifying the base model.
claim c1 [qualitative]: tuned lens predictions have substantially lower perplexity than logit lens predictions across language models with up to 20B parameters
claim c2 [quantitative]: logit lens bias measured by KL divergence between marginal distributions is around 4 to 5 bits for most layers of GPT-Neo-2.7B, whereas tuned lens shows significantly lower bias
claim c3 [qualitative]: tuned lens translators can zero-shot transfer to nearby layers with only modest increase in perplexity; transfer penalties are low especially for nearby layers
claim c4 [qualitative]: strong correlation (Spearman ρ) between causal influence of features on tuned lens and their influence on the model itself
claim c5 [quantitative]: tuned lens achieves perfect or near-perfect AUROC (>0.99) on five out of nine prompt injection detection tasks
claim c6 [qualitative]: tuned lens prediction depth correlates with iteration learned (difficulty metric) on all 11 tasks investigated; outperforms logit lens on 8 out of 11 tasks
claim c7 [qualitative]: tuned lens training is fast: code trains a full set of probes on a single 8A40 GPU node in under an hour
claim c8 [negative]: logit lens fails to elicit useful information from GPT-Neo, BLOOM, and OPT 125M; logit lens outputs are often the input token rather than plausible continuation tokens in more than half the layers of BLOOM and OPT 125M

Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning (2023)
seam: text; tags: in-context-learning, episodic-memory, verbal-feedback-loop, self-reflection, iterative-refinement
Reflexion is a framework for reinforcing language agents through iterative verbal feedback rather than weight updates. The system comprises three LLM-based components: an Actor that generates actions/text conditioned on state and episodic memory, an Evaluator that scores outputs (via exact match, heuristics, or LLM classification), and a Self-Reflection model that converts sparse reward signals (binary or scalar) into natural language summaries stored in episodic memory. The Actor conditions future decisions on both short-term memory (current trajectory) and long-term memory (accumulated self-reflections, bounded to 1-3 experiences). The loop iterates until task completion or max trials.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Reflexion achieves a 91% pass@1 accuracy on the HumanEval coding benchmark, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art GPT-4 that achieves 80%
claim c2 [quantitative]: Reflexion agents improve on decision-making AlfWorld tasks over strong baseline approaches by an absolute 22% in 12 iterative learning steps
claim c3 [quantitative]: Reflexion improves on reasoning questions in HotPotQA by 20%
claim c4 [quantitative]: ReAct + Reflexion completes 130 out of 134 AlfWorld tasks using the simple heuristic to detect hallucinations and inefficient planning, while ReAct-only converges at a 22% hallucination rate
claim c5 [quantitative]: On HumanEval (Rust), Reflexion achieves 68.0% pass@1 accuracy compared to GPT-4's 60.0%
claim c6 [quantitative]: On Chain-of-Thought with ground truth context (CoT GT), Reflexion improves accuracy by 14% absolute on HotPotQA, correcting mistakes on 14% of the 39% of initially unsolved questions
claim c7 [qualitative]: Reflexion is lightweight and does not require fine-tuning the LLM, allows for more nuanced forms of feedback compared to scalar rewards, maintains explicit and interpretable episodic memory over prior experiences, and provides more explicit hints for actions in future episodes
claim c8 [negative]: Reflexion may still succumb to non-optimal local minima solutions; code generation is bound to the model's ability to write diverse, comprehensive tests, risking false positive and false negative evaluations; practical limitations exist for test-driven development in specifying accurate input-output mappings for non-deterministic, impure, or concurrent functions

Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback (2023)
seam: text; tags: iterative-refinement, self-feedback, few-shot-prompting, test-time-only, no-training
Self-Refine is a test-time iterative refinement loop that uses a single LLM as generator, feedback provider, and refiner. The system generates an initial output via few-shot prompting, then feeds that output back to the same LLM (with a task-specific feedback prompt) to generate natural-language critiques. The LLM then uses its own feedback plus the history of previous iterations to produce a refined output. This feedback-refine cycle repeats for up to 4 iterations or until a task-specific stopping condition is met. No training, RL, or external models are required—only prompts and the base LLM's in-context learning capacity.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Across all evaluated tasks, outputs generated with Self-Refine are preferred by humans and automatic metrics over those generated with the same LLM using conventional one-step generation, improving by ~20% absolute on average in task performance.
claim c2 [quantitative]: GPT-4 + Self-Refine improves over base GPT-4 by 8.7% (absolute) in Code Optimization, increasing optimization percentage from 27.3% to 36.0%.
claim c3 [quantitative]: In Dialogue Response Generation, GPT-4 preference score improves by 49.2% – from 25.4% to 74.6%.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Self-Refine improves the initial generation by up to absolute 13% when applied to strong code models such as Codex (code-davinci-002) in code-generation tasks.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Self-Refine does not require any supervised training data, additional training, or reinforcement learning, and instead uses a single LLM as the generator, refiner and the feedback provider.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Feedback quality plays a crucial role in Self-Refine. Specific, actionable feedback yields superior results compared to generic feedback or no feedback. In sentiment transfer, changing from actionable feedback to generic feedback leads to a significant performance drop (43.2 to 31.2), and the task fails without feedback.
claim c7 [negative]: The modest performance gains in Math Reasoning can be traced back to the inability to accurately identify whether there is any error. In math, errors can be nuanced and sometimes limited to a single line or incorrect operation. A consistent-looking reasoning chain can deceive LLMs to think that everything looks good (e.g., ChatGPT feedback for 94% instances is 'everything looks good').
claim c8 [negative]: Self-Refine does not work consistently with weaker models. When instantiated with Vicuna-13B, it struggled significantly with the refinement process—the model was not able to consistently generate feedback in the required format and often failed to adhere to refinement prompts, either repeating the same output or generating hallucinated content.

Chameleon: Plug-and-Play Compositional Reasoning with Large Language Models (2023)
seam: external-engine; tags: tool-composition, program-synthesis, in-context-planning, multi-modal-reasoning, modular-decomposition
Chameleon is a plug-and-play compositional reasoning framework that augments LLMs with an inventory of external tools (vision models, web search, knowledge retrieval, Python execution, heuristic modules). An LLM-based planner generates natural-language-like sequential programs that compose these tools without training. Each module executes in sequence, reading the query and cached results from prior steps, updating both for downstream modules, and finally producing a task-specific answer.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Chameleon powered by GPT-4 achieves an 86.54% overall accuracy on ScienceQA, improving the best published few-shot result by 11.37%
claim c2 [quantitative]: On TabMWP, GPT-4-powered Chameleon improves the accuracy by 17.0%, lifting the state of the art to 98.78%
claim c3 [quantitative]: Chameleon (ChatGPT) achieves 79.93% accuracy on ScienceQA, a 1.62% improvement over Chain-of-Thought prompted ChatGPT
claim c4 [quantitative]: Chameleon (ChatGPT) outperforms ChatGPT CoT and ChatGPT PoT on TabMWP by 11.25% and 3.79%, reaching 93.28% accuracy
claim c5 [qualitative]: GPT-4 planner exhibits more consistent and rational tool selection via inferring potential constraints from instructions, compared to ChatGPT planner; GPT-4 calls Knowledge Retrieval more frequently (81% vs. 72%) and Bing Search more (11% vs. 3%) on ScienceQA
claim c6 [qualitative]: Knowledge Retrieval plays a vital role in both ScienceQA and TabMWP tasks; domain-specific tools such as search engines and vision models for ScienceQA and program tools for TabMWP also prove important
claim c7 [negative]: Inaccurate responses may arise from the limitations of the current modules or from suboptimal programs generated by the planner. The module inventory may lack tools capable of addressing specific abilities.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Chameleon generates natural-language-like programs without requiring any training or carefully curated rules, prompted only by tool descriptions and usage examples in few-shot setup

Multi-hop Commonsense Knowledge Injection Framework for Zero-Shot Commonsense Question Answering (2023)
seam: text; tags: knowledge-injection, synthetic-qa-generation, contrastive-learning, multi-hop-reasoning, mlm-scoring
A two-stage framework for zero-shot commonsense question answering. Stage 1 synthesizes multi-hop QA pairs from knowledge graphs (ConceptNet) using two generation methods (compositive and conjunctive) that extract 2-hop paths and generate hard negative samples via linguistic templates. Stage 2 pre-trains RoBERTa-Large on the synthetic dataset using masked language modeling (MLM) scoring combined with InfoNCE contrastive loss, where QA pairs are concatenated and token-by-token MLM losses summed to rank candidates. At inference, lowest MLM score selects the answer.
claim c1 [quantitative]: achieves state-of-art performance on CommonsenseQA with accuracy of 71.0%
claim c2 [quantitative]: improves accuracy by approximately 5.3% on MC-TACO benchmark
claim c3 [quantitative]: achieves 72.5% accuracy on Abductive NLI benchmark
claim c4 [quantitative]: achieves 73.1% accuracy on PhysicalIQA benchmark
claim c5 [quantitative]: achieves 61.0% accuracy on Wino-Grande benchmark
claim c6 [quantitative]: removing hard negative samples degrades performance by 1.3% on CommonsenseQA
claim c7 [quantitative]: removing all multi-hop knowledge degrades performance by 2.6% on CommonsenseQA
claim c8 [negative]: framework only utilizes ConceptNet for multi-hop QA generation; other KGs such as ATOMIC have no explicit multi-hop relationships and were not explored

Large Language Model Guided Tree-of-Thought (2023)
seam: text; tags: tree-search-with-backtracking, multi-round-prompting, external-verifier, policy-gradient-control, memory-augmentation
Tree-of-Thought (ToT) augments an auto-regressive LLM (GPT-3.5-turbo) with four external modules that enable multi-round conversational problem-solving with backtracking: a prompter agent generates structured prompts from memory; a checker module (rule-based or neural) validates intermediate solutions; a memory module records conversation history and search tree state; and a ToT controller (rule-based or learned via policy gradient) decides when to backtrack or continue exploration. The LLM generates short-range reasoning steps (next intermediate solution) guided by prompts; the external system orchestrates tree search, error recovery, and state management.
claim c1 [quantitative]: For 3x3 Sudoku puzzles, the ToT-based solver was able to solve all puzzles (success rate = 1.0), improving by 11% compared to the second best (few-shot CoT solver)
claim c2 [quantitative]: For 4x4 Sudoku puzzles, the ToT-based solver achieved 90% success rate (9 out of 10 solved), which is 80% higher than one-shot and few-shot solvers at approximately 50%
claim c3 [quantitative]: For 5x5 Sudoku puzzles, the ToT-based solver achieved 80% success rate (8 out of 10 solved), which is 60% higher than one-shot and few-shot solvers at approximately 50%
claim c4 [qualitative]: The ToT framework augments an LLM with a prompter agent, checker module, memory module, and ToT controller to enable tree-like exploration with backtracking for multi-round problem-solving
claim c5 [qualitative]: The checker module can be implemented either as rule-based (for NP problems with explicit polynomial-time verification algorithms like Sudoku) or as a trained neural network classifier
claim c6 [qualitative]: The ToT controller can be implemented as simple rule-based heuristics (backtrack on invalid node or after exploring >5 children) or as a learned policy network trained with REINFORCE
claim c7 [negative]: The rule-based ToT controller lacks awareness of whether a partially-filled board can be completed without violating Sudoku rules, decreasing solution search efficiency and causing failures on 4x4 (1 failure) and 5x5 (2 failures) puzzles before reaching maximum 100 conversation rounds
claim c8 [negative]: The rule-based checker requires custom domain-specific logic (e.g. Sudoku rule validation), limiting adaptability to other problem types; generalization to mathematical reasoning or logical problems where rule-based checking is difficult remains as future work

Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: tree-search, self-evaluation, prompting, deliberate-reasoning, bfs-dfs, backtracking
Tree of Thoughts (ToT) is a prompting framework that augments language model inference with deliberate tree search over coherent text units ('thoughts'). The LM generates candidate thoughts at each tree node, evaluates them via self-reasoning (value scoring or voting), and a search algorithm (BFS or DFS) explores the tree with lookahead and backtracking. Information flows from problem input → thought generation (prompted sampling or sequential proposal) → state evaluation (LM-based scoring) → search algorithm selection → final output. No model training occurs; all components use the frozen LM via prompting.
claim c1 [quantitative]: in Game of 24, while GPT-4 with chain-of-thought prompting only solved 4% of tasks, our method achieved a success rate of 74%
claim c2 [quantitative]: ToT with breadth b=1 achieves a success rate of 45%, while b=5 achieves 74%
claim c3 [quantitative]: ToT generates more coherent passages (7.56) than IO (6.19) and CoT (6.93) on average in Creative Writing, as measured by GPT-4 zero-shot evaluation score
claim c4 [quantitative]: in human evaluation of Creative Writing, humans prefer ToT over CoT in 41 out of 100 passage pairs, while preferring CoT over ToT in only 21 pairs
claim c5 [quantitative]: in Mini Crosswords, ToT achieves a word-level success rate of 60% and solves 4 out of 20 games, compared to IO and CoT which achieve less than 16%
claim c6 [negative]: around 60% of CoT samples already failed the task after generating the first step in Game of 24, highlighting issues with direct left-to-right decoding
claim c7 [negative]: iterative-refine method requires groundtruth feedback signals about equation correctness to work, whereas ToT operates without such external supervision
claim c8 [qualitative]: ToT lacks mechanisms to handle tasks for which GPT-4 already excels, and explores relatively simple tasks that challenge GPT-4; more complex real-world tasks like coding, data analysis, and robotics present opportunities for future work

ToolkenGPT: Augmenting Frozen Language Models with Massive Tools via Tool Embeddings (2023)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vocabulary-expansion, embedding-learning, tool-token, in-context-bridge, parameter-efficient
ToolkenGPT augments a frozen LLM by adding learnable tool embeddings to the model's vocabulary. Each tool is represented as a discrete token (toolken) with an embedding vector concatenated to the standard token embedding matrix. During inference, the LLM operates in two modes: reasoning mode (generating text normally, with toolkens as candidate next tokens) and tool mode (when a toolken is predicted, the LLM uses in-context demonstrations to generate tool arguments, executes the external tool, and injects the result back into the sequence). The LLM parameters remain frozen; only the toolken embedding matrix is trained on paired sequences of tool calls and their outputs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: ToolkenGPT enables LLMs to master massive tools without the need for any LLM fine-tuning, while still allowing for quick adaptation to new tools.
claim c2 [qualitative]: ToolkenGPT can effectively utilize arithmetic tools during the reasoning process, outperforming Chain-of-Thought and ReAct in numerical reasoning problems.
claim c3 [qualitative]: ToolkenGPT significantly outperforms baselines by training only on synthetic data when evaluating tool-handling capability with an expanded set of 13 arithmetic functions.
claim c4 [qualitative]: ToolkenGPT accommodates a substantial number of relation APIs (over 200) from a knowledge base for knowledge-based question answering.
claim c5 [qualitative]: ToolkenGPT learns toolken embeddings for 58 grounded actions and objects in embodied agent task planning, offering better grounding than previous in-context learning and specialized decoding methods.
claim c6 [qualitative]: ToolkenGPT training maintains nearly the same GPU memory as LLM inference, unlike prompt tuning or prefix tuning methods.
claim c7 [qualitative]: ToolkenGPT allows extensive demonstration data for learning toolken embeddings, contrasting with in-context learning which is limited by context length.
claim c8 [negative]: Fine-tuning LLMs to learn tools is computationally expensive and lacks adaptability to new tools.

Logic-LM: Empowering Large Language Models with Symbolic Solvers for Faithful Logical Reasoning (2023)
seam: external-engine; tags: neuro-symbolic, external-solver, problem-formulation, self-refinement, auto-formalization, multi-solver-integration
Logic-LM is a neuro-symbolic framework that decomposes logical reasoning into three stages: (1) Problem Formulation, where an LLM translates a natural language problem into symbolic representations (logic programming, first-order logic, constraint satisfaction, or SAT formulas) via few-shot in-context learning; (2) Symbolic Reasoning, where a deterministic external solver (Pyke, Prover9, python-constraint, or Z3) performs inference on the formulated problem; (3) Result Interpretation, where outputs are mapped back to natural language answers. A self-refinement module iteratively revises symbolic formalizations using error messages from the solver as feedback.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Logic-LM achieves an average performance boost of 39.2% over using LLM alone with standard prompting
claim c2 [quantitative]: Logic-LM achieves an average improvement of 18.4% over LLM with chain-of-thought prompting
claim c3 [qualitative]: With GPT-3.5, Logic-LM outperforms standard LLM on all datasets with an average improvement of 39.2%
claim c4 [qualitative]: GPT-4 achieves executable rate (Exe_Rate) of 79.9% on FOLIO and 32.6% on AR-LSAT
claim c5 [quantitative]: On ProofWriter depth-5 problems, Logic-LM outperforms CoT by 39.4%
claim c6 [quantitative]: Self-refinement increases executable rate by 5.01 percentage points on average
claim c7 [negative]: GPT-4 demonstrates near 100% executable rates on synthetic datasets (PrOntoQA, ProofWriter, LogicalDeduction) but notably lower performance on real-world expert-written datasets (FOLIO and AR-LSAT), with Exe_Rate of 79.9% and 32.6% respectively
claim c8 [negative]: Self-refinement increases executable formulations but accuracy tends to stagnate in subsequent rounds because error messages from the symbolic solver fix grammar validity but not semantic correctness of problem formulation

PaD: Program-aided Distillation Can Teach Small Models Reasoning Better than Chain-of-thought Fine-tuning (2023)
seam: external-engine; tags: distillation, program-synthesis, external-verifier, self-refinement, step-wise-verification, constrained-decoding
PaD is a distillation pipeline that transfers reasoning ability from LLMs to small models (CodeT5, 60M–770M parameters) by synthesizing executable Python reasoning programs from teacher LLMs via in-context learning, filtering them with an external Python interpreter for correctness, fine-tuning the small model on the filtered program data, and enhancing generation via self-refinement (multi-task learning with injected error cases) and step-wise beam search (scoring candidate intermediate steps using a pre-trained semantic alignment function before auto-regressive continuation).
claim c1 [quantitative]: smaller models using PaD can not only outperform certain LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-1 13B) but also achieve strong improvement over baselines with a significantly smaller scale of parameters and data
claim c2 [quantitative]: Comparing with stand fine-tune, PaD achieves average 40% increase
claim c3 [quantitative]: PaD achieves a 10% improvement while utilizing just 35% of the baseline model's data size
claim c4 [qualitative]: PaD accomplishes comparable performance utilizing merely 4.5% of the baseline model's data size
claim c5 [quantitative]: on symbolic reasoning tasks (Coin Flip and Last Letter Concatenation), PaD achieves 100% accuracy across all CodeT5 model sizes
claim c6 [quantitative]: PaD reaches nearly 49% of GPT-4's, 61% of GPT-3.5's, and 79% of LLaMA-2 70B's performance in mathematical reasoning
claim c7 [negative]: While improving the reasoning capability of smaller models, it leads to a decline in general abilities. significant drop in BBH alongside an increase in reasoning performance.
claim c8 [qualitative]: PaD effectively narrows the prediction space, focusing on specific central points. Reasoning programs in Python exhibit clearer and simpler syntax compared to natural language in CoT, reducing the complexity of the task by adhering to Python syntax instead of extensive sampling across the entire language representation space.

Large Language Models as Commonsense Knowledge for Large-Scale Task Planning (2023)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: world-model-construction, belief-prior, mcts, heuristic-policy-guidance, planning, text-to-action-mapping
LLM-MCTS integrates an LLM-induced commonsense world model with Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for large-scale task planning. The LLM generates an initial belief distribution over object locations by sampling position predictions, which MCTS uses to construct and explore a search tree over partially observable states. During tree search, the LLM also serves as a heuristic policy (via PUCT), guiding action selection to promising branches. Observations update the belief, and the system returns actions with highest estimated value after simulation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LLM-MCTS outperforms both MCTS alone and policies induced by LLMs (GPT2 and GPT3.5) by a wide margin for complex, novel tasks
claim c2 [quantitative]: GPT3.5-MCTS achieves 91.4% success rate on simple seen tasks in VirtualHome
claim c3 [quantitative]: GPT3.5-MCTS achieves 72.6% success rate on compositional seen tasks
claim c4 [quantitative]: Ablation: LLM-MCTS with uniform state prior achieves only 3.2% success on simple seen tasks, versus 91.4% with LLM-generated belief
claim c5 [quantitative]: Ablation: LLM-MCTS without heuristic policy achieves 0.0% success across all task categories
claim c6 [qualitative]: If the description length of the world model is substantially smaller than that of the policy, using LLM as a world model for model-based planning is likely better than using LLM solely as a policy
claim c7 [qualitative]: For air travel planning, LLM model + search algorithm consistently outperforms LLM policy
claim c8 [negative]: UCT (MCTS without commonsense knowledge) achieves 0.0% success rate across all task categories

Towards Revealing the Mystery behind Chain of Thought: A Theoretical Perspective (2023)
seam: text; tags: cot-expressivity, circuit-complexity, autoregressive-generation, effective-depth, dynamic-programming, impossibility-results
This is a theoretical analysis paper examining autoregressive Transformers' expressivity with and without Chain-of-Thought (CoT). The paper proves impossibility results showing bounded-depth Transformers cannot directly solve arithmetic/equation tasks unless model size grows super-polynomially; conversely, it proves by construction that constant-size autoregressive Transformers can solve these tasks by generating CoT derivations step-by-step.
