The mechanism exploits the recursive nature of autoregressive generation: by looping generated outputs back as inputs, the effective circuit depth becomes proportional to the number of generation steps, enabling solution of problems with high parallel complexity. Information flows from input tokens through Transformer layers; intermediate CoT outputs are regenerated token-by-token, accumulating reasoning steps.
claim c1 [theoretical]: Bounded-depth Transformers are unable to directly produce correct answers for basic arithmetic evaluation tasks unless the model size grows super-polynomially with respect to the input length
claim c2 [theoretical]: Bounded-depth Transformers are unable to directly produce correct answers for linear equation solving tasks unless the model size grows super-polynomially with respect to the input length
claim c3 [theoretical]: Autoregressive Transformers of constant depth O(log n) and constant hidden size suffice to solve arithmetic evaluation tasks by generating CoT derivations using a commonly used math language format
claim c4 [theoretical]: Autoregressive Transformers of constant depth O(log n) and constant hidden size suffice to solve linear equation tasks by generating CoT derivations using a commonly used math language format
claim c5 [theoretical]: LLMs with CoT can solve general Dynamic Programming problems satisfying polynomial approximation assumptions with constant depth and hidden dimension
claim c6 [negative]: Bounded-depth Transformers of polynomial size cannot solve Context-Free Grammar Membership Testing without CoT
claim c7 [qualitative]: 3-layer Transformers with CoT achieve near-perfect accuracy on all four tasks (Arithmetic, Equation, LIS, and Edit Distance) for all difficulty levels
claim c8 [quantitative]: 3-layer Transformer model trained on CoT achieves more than 95% accuracy on corrupted arithmetic datasets with 30% of data missing an intermediate CoT step and involving single-token corruption

Backpack Language Models (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: multi-vector-embeddings, weighted-bag-of-words, log-linear-predictions, interpretable-intervention, knowledge-editing, debiasing, topic-control
Backpack is a neural language model architecture where each vocabulary word is represented as a set of non-contextual sense vectors. For each token in a sequence, the model computes a context-dependent, non-negative linear combination of sense vectors (from all words in the sequence) weighted by a contextualization function (implemented as a Transformer). The final representation is passed through a log-linear output layer for prediction. This design ensures sense vectors contribute log-linearly to predictions, enabling interpretable intervention and inspection via projection onto the output space.
claim c1 [quantitative]: a 170M-parameter Backpack language model achieves OpenWebText perplexity matching that of a GPT-2 small (124M-parameter) Transformer
claim c2 [quantitative]: Backpack sense vectors outperform a 6B-parameter Transformer LM's word embeddings on lexical similarity evaluations
claim c3 [qualitative]: sense vector ablation: Mini-sized Backpack models with sense counts k=1,2,4,8 achieve OpenWebText perplexities of 38.6, 29.3, 26.0, and 24.1 respectively, demonstrating the necessity of a non-singleton set of sense vectors
claim c4 [quantitative]: gender bias reduction: removing sense 10 from profession words reduces the bias ratio from 4.34 to 2.88 (44% reduction); learning the optimal removal fraction per profession achieves a bias ratio of 2.16 (65% reduction)
claim c5 [qualitative]: sense vectors specialize after training, each encoding a different aspect of a word; individual senses encode coherent notions such as relatedness, next-token prediction, verb objects and noun modifiers, and proper noun associations
claim c6 [qualitative]: sense-controlled topic generation achieves at least as strong control as Plug-and-Play Language Models (PPLM) gradient-based control on a 124M Transformer baseline, evaluated across 17 topic categories
claim c7 [qualitative]: knowledge editing via sense vector nullspace projection and replacement allows localizing and editing associations with proper nouns (e.g., MacBook–Apple associations replaced with MacBook–HP associations) with minimal semantic or syntactic degradation
claim c8 [negative]: there is fundamental uncertainty whether Backpack language models will continue to scale with parameters and data and remain viable alternatives to Transformers at larger model scales; this study was unable to scale beyond 170M parameters

Plug-and-Play Knowledge Injection for Pre-trained Language Models (2023)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: knowledge-injection, adapter-plugin, embedding-mapping, entity-grounding, parameter-efficient, plug-and-play
Map-tuning is a plug-and-play knowledge injection method that trains a lightweight affine mapping network to transform entity embeddings (from TransE or similar knowledge representation models) into the token embedding space of a frozen pre-trained language model. Entity mentions in input text are identified, their embeddings are mapped and projected, and the mapped representations replace or augment the corresponding token embeddings, allowing knowledge from external bases (Wikidata, UMLS) to flow into downstream tasks without retraining the model backbone.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Map-tuning achieves consistent improvement on almost all downstream models in general plug-and-play injection, while existing methods (E-BERT, PELT, RA, K-Adapter) cannot consistently improve performance and are not suitable for the plug-and-play paradigm
claim c2 [quantitative]: On FewRel 1.0 (5-1 shot) with fine-tuning, map-tuning achieves 92.6 accuracy compared to 91.0 for baseline
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Wiki80 (10-1 shot) with fine-tuning, map-tuning achieves 88.1 accuracy compared to 85.4 for baseline
claim c4 [quantitative]: Task-specific map-tuning on Wiki80 achieves 87.8 accuracy when initialized from general map-tuning, compared to 86.1 for fine-tuning baseline
claim c5 [qualitative]: General map-tuning training on NVIDIA Tesla V100 costs only 2 hours, compared to 3000+ GPU hours for knowledge-enhanced pre-training methods like LUKE and KEPLER
claim c6 [qualitative]: Map-tuning optimizes only 0.1% of parameters of the downstream model
claim c7 [quantitative]: In domain adaptation from FewRel 1.0 (Wikipedia) to FewRel 2.0 (PubMed) at 5-1 shot, task-specific map-tuning achieves 81.2 accuracy compared to 76.7 for source domain baseline
claim c8 [negative]: Map-tuning was not evaluated on PLMs pre-trained with language modeling objectives other than MLM, and did not evaluate whether the PLM can perform complex reasoning (e.g., multi-hop reasoning) based on injected knowledge

Grammar Prompting for Domain-Specific Language Generation with Large Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: in-context-learning, constrained-decoding, grammar-prompting, dsl-generation, semantic-parsing
Grammar prompting augments in-context learning by interleaving a specialized BNF grammar between each demonstration input-output pair. During inference, the LLM first predicts a specialized grammar (a minimal subset of the full DSL grammar sufficient to generate the target output), then generates the output conditioned on that grammar. Constrained decoding uses an Earley parser to enforce grammatical validity: the system speculatively decodes from the LLM, extracts the longest valid prefix using the parser if needed, identifies valid continuations, and uses LLM probabilities to select the next token, iterating until a complete valid program is produced.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Grammar prompting achieves 67.1% program accuracy on GeoQuery with unconstrained decoding, compared to 60.7% for standard prompting
claim c2 [quantitative]: Grammar prompting achieves 69.6% program accuracy on GeoQuery with both grammar and program constraints, compared to 60.7% for standard prompting
claim c3 [quantitative]: Grammar prompting achieves 52.4% program accuracy on SMCalFlow with both grammar and program constraints, compared to 46.4% for standard prompting
claim c4 [quantitative]: Grammar prompting achieves 97.9% execution accuracy on GeoQuery in retrieval-based in-context learning, compared to 96.8% for standard prompting
claim c5 [quantitative]: Grammar prompting achieves 90.8% accuracy on GeoQuery NewFunc compositional generalization split (unseen functions), compared to 63.3% for standard prompting
claim c6 [qualitative]: Grammar prompting achieves 98.0% validity, 0.74 diversity, 91.0% retrosynthesis score, and 93.3% membership for Acrylates molecule generation, compared to 87.7% validity, 0.73 diversity, 80.0% retrosynthesis score, and 76.7% membership for standard prompting
claim c7 [negative]: Grammar prompting did not yield any improvements for DSLs that were likely to have been frequently encountered during pretraining (e.g., regular expressions, SQL)
claim c8 [negative]: Constrained generation based on specialized grammars led to increased API calls (roughly three times more than unconstrained decoding) and was not always beneficial; enforcing constraints in molecule generation resulted in lower diversity, and constraints on object selection in PDDL planning sometimes negatively impacted performance

Let's Verify Step by Step (2023)
seam: text; tags: reward-modeling, process-supervision, outcome-supervision, best-of-n-search, active-learning, step-level-feedback
The paper trains reward models (RMs) to evaluate step-by-step solutions to math problems. Two supervision approaches are compared: outcome supervision (ORM), which labels only final correctness, and process supervision (PRM), which labels the correctness of each intermediate reasoning step. Both RM types are finetuned from GPT-4 and trained on solutions generated by a fixed generator model. At test time, a RM scores candidate solutions via best-of-N ranking, selecting the highest-scored solution and checking its final answer. The core comparison isolates whether step-level feedback (PRM) or final-answer feedback (ORM) produces more reliable reward models.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Our process-supervised model solves 78% of problems from a representative subset of the MATH test set.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Process supervision significantly outperforms outcome supervision for training models to solve problems from the challenging MATH dataset. The PRM reaches higher performance for all values of N, and the performance gap widens as N increases.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Process supervision significantly outperforms both forms of outcome supervision at all data collection scales (1 to 200 solutions per problem).
claim c4 [quantitative]: Active learning leads to approximately 2.6x improvement in the data efficiency of process supervision compared to uniform data labelling.
claim c5 [qualitative]: The PRM outperforms both the ORM and majority voting on out-of-distribution STEM tests (AP Physics, AP Calculus, AP Chemistry, AMC10/12) with 100 test samples per problem.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Process supervision makes credit assignment easier because it specifies both how many of the first steps were correct as well as the precise location of the incorrect step, compared to outcome supervision which faces a difficult credit-assignment task of determining where an incorrect solution went wrong.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Process supervision incurs a negative alignment tax: it is both safer and more performant than outcome supervision. Process supervision is more likely to produce interpretable reasoning and directly rewards aligned chain-of-thought.
claim c8 [qualitative]: We release PRM800K, the complete dataset of 800,000 step-level human feedback labels used to train our best reward model.

SQL-PaLM: Improved Large Language Model Adaptation for Text-to-SQL (extended) (2023)
seam: external-engine; tags: consistency-decoding, execution-feedback, instruction-tuning, column-selection, synthetic-augmentation, test-time-selection
SQL-PaLM is a comprehensive framework for Text-to-SQL that adapts PaLM-2 (Unicorn variant) using both few-shot prompting and instruction fine-tuning. For prompting, it applies execution-based consistency decoding with error filtering: multiple SQL outputs are sampled at high temperature, executed against the database, and aggregated by execution result to select the most probable valid output. For fine-tuning, the model is trained end-to-end on serialized database schema, auxiliary content (descriptions, values, hints), and natural language questions to generate SQL. The framework incorporates column selection (program-aided or retrieval-based) to handle large-scale databases by reducing input length, and test-time refinement that integrates outputs from multiple training paradigms using execution feedback.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Mixing training data with diverse sets, despite having various formats, can benefit LLMs, indicating the potential of tuned LLMs for superior generalization ability. Complex SQL data particularly have been observed to be more beneficial as tuning data.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Relevant subset of database content (descriptions, values) included in the prompt improves performance while irrelevant information can harm the performance.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Hard column selection significantly reduces the length of the inputs going into the LLMs while yielding negligible impact to performance.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Program-aided column selection has higher column selection accuracy, whereas retrieval-based approach is more cost-effective and controllable.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Soft column selection, which does not exclude unselected columns, has higher performance than hard column selection, albeit it necessitates LLMs with longer context length.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Test-time execution-based selection approach integrates multiple setups (prompting vs. tuning paradigms) to further improve performance.
claim c7 [qualitative]: The holistic SQL-PaLM approach yields substantial advancements in Text-to-SQL, as demonstrated on two key public benchmarks, Spider and BIRD.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Tuning large LLMs for Text-to-SQL improves performance in challenging scenarios with complex SQL, semantic constraints, and large-scale databases compared to few-shot prompting alone.

Inference-Time Intervention: Eliciting Truthful Answers from a Language Model (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, linear-probing, truthfulness-elicitation, attention-head-intervention, inference-time-control
Inference-Time Intervention (ITI) identifies sparse sets of attention heads with high linear probing accuracy for truthfulness, then shifts their output activations along truth-correlated directions during autoregressive decoding. A trained linear probe per head discovers the principal separating hyperplane between true and false statements in activation space; during inference, the model's residual stream is perturbed by a scaled truthful direction vector at selected heads in each layer, repeated autoregressively. No model weights are trained; only probes are fitted to frozen model activations.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On an instruction-finetuned LLaMA called Alpaca, ITI improves its truthfulness from 32.5% to 65.1%
claim c2 [qualitative]: ITI locates truthful directions using only few hundred examples
claim c3 [quantitative]: On LLaMA-7B with mass mean shift direction at optimal α=20, true*informative score reaches 42.3% compared to baseline 30.5%
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Vicuna, ITI improves true*informative from 51.5% to 74.0%
claim c5 [quantitative]: Zero-shot transfer: on MMLU, LLaMA-7B + ITI achieves 40.16% compared to baseline 35.71%
claim c6 [qualitative]: ITI identifies a trade-off between truthfulness and helpfulness that can be balanced by tuning the intervention strength parameter α
claim c7 [qualitative]: The technique is minimally invasive and computationally inexpensive, requiring only addition of a single constant vector per layer with close to zero computational overhead
claim c8 [qualitative]: LLMs may have an internal representation of the likelihood of something being true in intermediate activations, even as they produce falsehoods on the surface

LEACE: Perfect linear concept erasure in closed form (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: concept-erasure, linear-projection, activation-intervention, layer-wise-scrubbing, fairness, interpretability
LEACE is a closed-form linear projection method for concept erasure from embeddings. It computes an affine transformation that removes linear information about a target concept (Y) from an embedding (X) by whitening X, projecting onto the nullspace of the cross-covariance matrix between X and Y, then unwhitening. The method is applied to LLMs via 'concept scrubbing': sequentially applying LEACE to activations at each layer, feeding scrubbed outputs forward to the next layer.
claim c1 [theoretical]: A classification task is linearly guarded if and only if every class has exactly the same mean feature vector
claim c2 [theoretical]: LEACE is the unique affine transformation that minimizes mean squared distance from original features with respect to all norms induced by inner products (Euclidean, Mahalanobis, etc.) while achieving linear guardedness
claim c3 [qualitative]: LEACE achieves random chance accuracy (perfect erasure) on gender prediction from BERT embeddings with minimal edit distance, while RLACE comes close and INLP does not
claim c4 [qualitative]: LEACE is approximately 2 orders of magnitude faster than RLACE and does not require gradient-based optimization
claim c5 [quantitative]: Profession-prediction accuracy drops from 79.3% to 77.3% after gender erasure via LEACE on BERT embeddings; TPR-gap drops from 0.198 to 0.084
claim c6 [qualitative]: Erasing part-of-speech information via LEACE concept scrubbing induces perplexity increases of 1.73–3.57 bits per UTF-8 byte across LLaMA (7B–30B) and Pythia (160M–12B) models, compared to SAL which induces 3.16–4.69 bits
claim c7 [quantitative]: INLP requires 20 iterations to partially erase POS information and reduces embedding rank by 360 dimensions; LEACE reduces rank by only 17 dimensions
claim c8 [negative]: Concept scrubbing remains largely unvalidated on narrow concepts and behavioral metrics; it is unclear whether scrubbing will successfully prevent nonlinear encoding of protected attributes

VoxPoser: Composable 3D Value Maps for Robotic Manipulation with Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: llm-code-generation, vlm-grounding, voxel-value-maps, model-predictive-control, zero-shot-manipulation, online-dynamics-learning
VoxPoser uses GPT-4 to generate Python code that orchestrates vision-language models (OWL-ViT, Segment Anything, XMEM) to compose 3D voxel value maps grounded in robot observation space. These value maps encode affordances, constraints, rotation, velocity, and gripper actions. The maps serve as objective functions for a model-predictive-control motion planner (zeroth-order optimization via random shooting) that synthesizes 6-DoF end-effector trajectories executed with replanning at 5 Hz. The system optionally learns environment dynamics models from online interactions to handle contact-rich tasks.
claim c1 [quantitative]: VoxPoser achieves 88.0% success rate on real-world everyday manipulation tasks (static environment), outperforming LLM+Primitives baseline at 24.0%
claim c2 [quantitative]: VoxPoser achieves 77.5% success rate on spatial composition tasks with seen instructions and seen attributes in simulation, compared to 53.8% for U-Net motion planning baseline and 43.8% for LLM+Primitives
claim c3 [quantitative]: VoxPoser with zero-shot trajectory prior achieves 88.3% success on door-opening tasks with online dynamics learning in 142.3 seconds, compared to no-prior baseline that times out after 12 hours
claim c4 [quantitative]: VoxPoser maintains high success rates across unseen instructions and unseen attributes: 60.0% on object interaction (UI/UA), 58.8% on spatial composition (SI/UA), 65.0% on object interaction (UI/UA)
claim c5 [qualitative]: VoxPoser exhibits lowest specification error compared to learned cost specification (U-Net) and LLM+Primitives due to its generalization and flexibility in spatial composition
claim c6 [qualitative]: VoxPoser is robust to external disturbances such as moving targets, obstacles, and perturbations (e.g., pulling drawer open after robot closes it) due to fast replanning at 5 Hz
claim c7 [negative]: VoxPoser relies on external perception modules, which is limiting in tasks requiring holistic visual reasoning or understanding of fine-grained object geometries; most real-world errors are attributed to perception module sensitivity
claim c8 [negative]: VoxPoser requires manual prompt engineering for LLMs and currently uses end-effector-only trajectory planning rather than whole-arm planning

Steering Language Models With Activation Engineering (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, residual-stream-intervention, contrast-pair-vectors, inference-time-control, linear-feature-space
Activation Addition (ActAdd) is an inference-time steering method that modifies a frozen language model's residual stream activations. The system computes a steering vector by taking the difference between forward-pass activations on a pair of contrast prompts (e.g., 'Love' minus 'Hate'). At inference, this vector is scaled by an injection coefficient and added to the residual stream at a specified layer, allowing the model to continue its forward pass with modified activations. No model weights are updated; only activations in the computational graph are perturbed.
claim c1 [quantitative]: ActAdd achieves state-of-the-art on negative-to-positive sentiment shift (0.669 success rate) on IMDb dataset using LLaMA-3-8B
claim c2 [quantitative]: ActAdd-OPT reduces toxicity to 0.112 on RealToxicityPrompts (n=1000), 8% lower than second-best method PREADD-D-OPT at 0.122
claim c3 [quantitative]: On wedding-topic steering with GPT-2-XL at optimal injection layer (layer 6), ActAdd achieves 90% success rate in generating wedding-related words compared to 2% baseline
claim c4 [qualitative]: ActAdd requires minimal data: steering vectors are computed from a single pair of contrast prompts (e.g., 'Love' vs 'Hate')
claim c5 [qualitative]: ActAdd preserves general model knowledge: on ConceptNet (LAMA benchmark), steering with the wedding vector has negligible impact on off-target answer probabilities across a range of top-k values
claim c6 [qualitative]: ActAdd does not require machine optimization; steering vectors are computed by direct difference of activations without gradient descent or other iterative optimization
claim c7 [negative]: ActAdd requires access to intermediate activations at specified layers; the method is not immediately applicable given only API access to a model, as most APIs do not expose or cache intermediate activations
claim c8 [negative]: ActAdd introduces free hyperparameters (injection coefficient α and intervention layer ℓ) that must be tuned, making it less user-friendly than simple prompt engineering; the paper has not examined ActAdd's potential impact on reasoning capabilities

Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents (2023)
seam: text; tags: framework-survey, cognitive-architecture, memory-organization, decision-loop, retrieval-reasoning-learning
CoALA is a conceptual framework that positions large language models as the core component of a cognitive architecture. The system organizes language agents along three dimensions: (1) modular memory components (working memory, episodic, semantic, and procedural memory); (2) a structured action space divided into external actions (grounding with physical, dialogue, or digital environments) and internal actions (retrieval from long-term memory, reasoning over working memory, and learning/writing to long-term memory); (3) a generalized decision-making process implemented as a repeated cycle where the agent uses retrieval and reasoning to plan, then selects and executes a grounding or learning action. Information flows from environmental observations into working memory via grounding, is processed through LLM-based reasoning and retrieval operations, and is persisted or acted upon through learning and grounding procedures.
claim c1 [qualitative]: CoALA describes a language agent with modular memory components, a structured action space to interact with internal memory and external environments, and a generalized decision-making process to choose actions.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Language models can be viewed as probabilistic production systems that assign a probability to each possible completion and sample a completion each time they are called.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Prompting methods such as few-shot learning, retrieval-augmented generation, and self-critique can be understood as sequences of productions that pre-process the input string before generating completions.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Recent cognitive language agents use sophisticated internal processes including reasoning, planning, and long-term memory management to improve decision-making, moving beyond early agents that used LLMs directly to select or generate actions.
claim c5 [qualitative]: CoALA can neatly express a large body of existing agents and identify underexplored directions to develop new ones through its three organizing dimensions: memory, action space, and decision-making.
claim c6 [qualitative]: LLMs pre-trained on large-scale internet data learn a remarkably effective prior over string completions, allowing them to solve a wide range of tasks out of the box.
claim c7 [negative]: A primary disadvantage of LLMs is their inherent opaqueness: while production systems are defined by discrete and human-legible rules, LLMs consist of billions of uninterpretable parameters, making it challenging to analyze or control their behaviors.
claim c8 [theoretical]: Cognitive architectures from the symbolic AI tradition can help design LLM-based cognitive agents, drawing parallels between production systems and language models to organize existing approaches and highlight promising extensions.

Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-coding, dictionary-learning, activation-steering, interpretability, superposition-resolution, monosemanticity
The system trains sparse autoencoders on frozen language model activations (residual stream, MLP, or attention head outputs) to decompose polysemantic neurons into interpretable monosemantic features. An autoencoder with a single hidden layer learns a dictionary of feature directions via L1-regularized reconstruction loss; the hidden activations (coefficients) represent sparse linear combinations of dictionary features that reconstruct the original activation vectors. Information flows: frozen LM activations → encoder projects to feature coefficients → decoder reconstructs via sparse dictionary.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Sparse dictionary features are far more interpretable by autointerpretability score than dictionary features found by comparable techniques (default basis, random directions, PCA, ICA)
claim c2 [qualitative]: The strength of interpretability improvement over baselines declines as we move through the model, being comparable to ICA in layer 4 and showing minimal improvement in the final layer
claim c3 [qualitative]: Patching sparse dictionary features requires fewer patches to reach a given level of KL divergence from target compared to PCA decomposition and non-sparse dictionary on the Indirect Object Identification task
claim c4 [qualitative]: Dictionary features are highly monosemantic: individual features activate on semantically coherent contexts such as apostrophes only, periods only, the token 'the', or newline characters
claim c5 [qualitative]: Ablating dictionary features produces intuitive effects on model output logits consistent with the feature's detected semantic content
claim c6 [negative]: Sparse autoencoders do not achieve zero reconstruction loss; replacing residual stream activations in layer 2 of Pythia-70M with autoencoder reconstruction increases perplexity on the Pile from 25 to 40
claim c7 [negative]: Current methods for training sparse autoencoders are best suited to the residual stream; the training pipeline is not able to robustly learn overcomplete bases in the intermediate layers of the MLP
claim c8 [qualitative]: A single training run using 5-50M activation vectors completes in under an hour on a single A40 GPU

Chain-of-Verification Reduces Hallucination in Large Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: prompting, self-verification, chain-of-thought-variant, hallucination-mitigation, few-shot-in-context, factored-decomposition
Chain-of-Verification (CoVe) is a prompting-based method that uses a single large language model to reduce hallucinations through a four-step deliberation process: (1) generate an initial response, (2) plan verification questions to fact-check the response, (3) answer those questions independently (in joint, 2-step, or factored variants with varying context isolation), and (4) generate a final verified response incorporating the verification results. The method uses only text-based prompting with few-shot demonstrations; no external tools, training, or model modification occurs.
claim c1 [quantitative]: CoVe more than doubles precision on the Wikidata list-based task, improving from 0.17 to 0.36 (2-step variant)
claim c2 [quantitative]: CoVe reduces hallucinated (negative) entities on Wikidata from 2.95 to 0.68 while maintaining positive entities at 0.59 to 0.38
claim c3 [quantitative]: CoVe achieves 23% improvement in F1 on MultiSpanQA closed-book QA (factored variant: 0.48 vs few-shot baseline 0.39)
claim c4 [quantitative]: CoVe achieves 28% improvement in FactScore on longform biography generation (factor+revise variant: 71.4 vs few-shot baseline 55.9)
claim c5 [quantitative]: CoVe factor+revise outperforms ChatGPT (FactScore 58.7) and PerplexityAI (61.6) on biography generation with FactScore 71.4
claim c6 [quantitative]: When querying individual entities via verification questions, 70% are correctly answered compared to only 17% in the original list-based question baseline
claim c7 [qualitative]: Factored and 2-step CoVe variants consistently outperform joint CoVe across all tasks because preventing the model from attending to the original baseline response reduces repetition of hallucinations
claim c8 [negative]: While CoVe reduces hallucinations, it does not remove them completely, meaning the method can still generate incorrect or misleading information even after improvement over baseline

Navigate through Enigmatic Labyrinth A Survey of Chain of Thought Reasoning: Advances, Frontiers and Future (2023)
seam: text; tags: survey, prompt-engineering, chain-of-thought, reasoning, in-context-learning
This is a survey paper on chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models. It does not present a novel system or mechanism, but rather systematically reviews existing methods across three dimensions: prompt construction (manual, automatic, semi-automatic), topological variants (chain, tree, graph structures), and enhancement methods (verification, decomposition, knowledge integration, ensembling, efficiency). The paper catalogs how information flows through LLM reasoning via step-by-step intermediate reasoning tokens and various architectural interventions without modifying model weights.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Chain-of-thought prompting significantly enhances LLM's reasoning capabilities
claim c2 [qualitative]: Chain-of-thought reasoning breaks down complex problems into manageable steps and establishes connections among these steps, thereby facilitating reasoning
claim c3 [qualitative]: Manual XoT demonstrates better performance than automatic prompting, but annotation of rationales incurs significant cost and introduces dilemmas in demonstration selection
claim c4 [qualitative]: Tree-based methods gain the capability to widely explore and backtrack during reasoning, and deliver preliminary global planning capabilities towards the global optimum, but are restricted by demands of explicit question decomposition and state transition, which leads to limitations in task generalization
claim c5 [qualitative]: Graph structures outperform tree-based methods in handling complex problems through improved modeling of sub-problem aggregation and self-verification, but rely on specially designed state decomposition, leading to poorer generalization
claim c6 [negative]: LLMs may not address issues beyond their own capabilities, which raises doubt on the effectiveness of self-feedback
claim c7 [negative]: Self-ensemble is an effective way to improve performance, but alongside the improvement in performance, there has been a multiplied increase in inference costs, which in turn limits its wide application
claim c8 [qualitative]: Distilling task-specific reasoning ability may adversely downgrade the general performance

Representation Engineering: A Top-Down Approach to AI Transparency (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, representation-extraction, linear-probing, concept-reading, pca-based-directions, lora-adaptation, concept-control
Representation Engineering (RepE) is a top-down transparency framework that treats population-level representations (rather than individual neurons or circuits) as the primary unit of analysis in deep neural networks. The system comprises two main components: (1) Representation Reading, which uses Linear Artificial Tomography (LAT)—a three-step process of stimulus design, neural activity collection at specific token positions, and PCA-based linear model fitting—to extract directions in representation space that correlate with high-level concepts (truthfulness, utility, emotion) and functions (honesty, power-seeking); (2) Representation Control, which modifies these representations via reading vectors, contrast vectors (stimulus-dependent difference vectors), or low-rank adapters (LoRRA) using linear combination, piecewise, or projection operations to steer model behavior. Information flows from input stimuli through frozen model layers, where representations are read or transformed, then back through the model to influence outputs.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LAT outperforms zero-shot heuristic scoring on TruthfulQA MC1, achieving state-of-the-art results and improving over standard evaluation by multiple percentage points.
claim c2 [qualitative]: LAT outperforms few-shot prompting on traditional QA benchmarks (OpenbookQA, CommonSenseQA, RACE, ARC) by a notable margin.
claim c3 [qualitative]: LAT outperforms prior methods such as CCS (Burns et al., 2022) by a wide margin on DeBERTa evaluated on common benchmarks.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Representation reading via LAT enables detection of imitative falsehoods and intentional lies: models possess a consistent internal concept of truthfulness that can be extracted and used to classify statement veracity at higher accuracy than output-level scoring.
claim c5 [qualitative]: RepE techniques provide traction on a wide range of safety-relevant problems including honesty, harmlessness, power-seeking, utility estimation, knowledge editing, jailbreaking, memorization, emotional state tracking, and fairness.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Contrast vectors (stimulus-dependent difference vectors computed at inference time) prove significantly stronger control baselines than stimulus-independent reading vectors.
claim c7 [qualitative]: LAT with unlabeled or self-generated stimuli is effective for extracting representations of concepts, avoiding annotation bias and enabling extraction of superhuman representations.
claim c8 [theoretical]: Representation engineering follows a Hopfieldian (rather than Sherringtonian) view of cognition: cognition is a product of representational spaces and population-level activity patterns rather than node-to-node connections, enabling top-down transparency research at the level of emergent high-level cognitive phenomena.

Don't Fine-Tune, Decode: Syntax Error-Free Tool Use via Constrained Decoding (2023)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, finite-state-machine, tool-use, syntax-enforcement, grammar-constrained
ToolDec is a constrained decoding algorithm that guides LLM token generation using finite-state machines (FSMs) constructed from tool syntax schemas. At each decoding step, the FSM maintains a current state and permits only tokens that comply with the tool's syntax constraints. The model samples from a masked probability distribution where invalid tokens are zeroed out and probabilities are renormalized. Multiple FSMs are linked via a trie structure to guide tool selection and multi-step reasoning. A separate LLM-based pipeline compresses tool documentation by removing syntax constraints, which the FSM now enforces implicitly during decoding.
claim c1 [quantitative]: ToolDec improves Mistral-Instruct's accuracy in tool use from 0% to 52% on ToolEval I3-Instruction, matching the performance of ToolLLM
claim c2 [quantitative]: ToolDec eliminates all syntax errors (syntax error rate 0%) across all base models and benchmarks evaluated
claim c3 [quantitative]: ToolDec improves ToolLLM + ReAct win rate by 10 points (36.5% to 46.5%) on ToolEval I2-Category
claim c4 [quantitative]: ToolDec reduces average tokens per tool by 58% and 69% on ToolEval I2-Category and I3-Instruction respectively
claim c5 [quantitative]: ToolDec improves LLaMA-7B accuracy by 2.2x on FuncQA (6% to 13.2%) and 5.1x on KAMEL (8.2% to 42.4%)
claim c6 [quantitative]: ToolLLM + DFSDT with ToolDec achieves 50.5% win rate on ToolEval I2-Category, outperforming ChatGPT and matching GPT-4 performance
claim c7 [negative]: Fine-tuned specialist models like ToolLLM suffer severe performance degradation on general reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K: 1.2% vs. Llama-2-chat 23.1%), suggesting catastrophic forgetting from tool-specialized fine-tuning
claim c8 [qualitative]: Decoding-time syntax constraints via FSM are much more effective than in-prompt syntax constraints, and adding both provides negligible additional benefit over decoding constraints alone

InstructRetro: Instruction Tuning post Retrieval-Augmented Pretraining (2023)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: retrieval-augmented-pretraining, instruction-tuning, kv-injection, gated-cross-attention, encoder-ablation
InstructRetro is a 48B-parameter auto-regressive language model built by continued pretraining of a 43B GPT backbone with retrieval-augmented pretraining (Retro), followed by instruction tuning. During pretraining, a Retro encoder (2-layer bidirectional transformer) encodes retrieved neighbors from a 1.2T-token database via BERT embeddings, and chunk-wise cross-attention layers fuse retrieved information into the decoder. During instruction tuning and inference, the encoder is gated off, leaving a decoder-only model that can operate with or without retrieved context. The key innovation is unfreezing decoder parameters during continued retrieval-augmented pretraining (unlike prior Retro-fitting that froze them), yielding better perplexity and downstream task performance after instruction tuning.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Retro 48B largely outperforms the counterpart GPT 43B trained on 1.2T tokens in terms of perplexity with only 2.58% additional GPU hours
claim c2 [quantitative]: The average improvement of InstructRetro 43B is 7% over its GPT counterpart across 8 short-form QA and reading comprehension tasks
claim c3 [quantitative]: InstructRetro 43B achieves 10% average improvement over GPT across 4 challenging long-form QA tasks
claim c4 [quantitative]: InstructRetro 43B achieves 16% average improvement over GPT across 3 summarization tasks
claim c5 [quantitative]: Retro 48B achieves perplexity comparable to GPT models with 4 larger parameter sizes
claim c6 [qualitative]: One can ablate the encoder from InstructRetro architecture and directly use its decoder backbone, while achieving comparable results
claim c7 [qualitative]: Unfreezing decoder parameters during Retro-fitting converges faster and demonstrates better validation perplexity than freezing decoder parameters
claim c8 [qualitative]: Retrieval-augmented pretraining benefits saturate at scale without instruction tuning, but instruction tuning further unveils the potential of retrieval augmentation even when model size scales up

Function Vectors in Large Language Models (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-patching, causal-mediation, attention-heads, icl-mechanism, vector-extraction
A mechanistic analysis of in-context learning (ICL) in autoregressive transformers. The system identifies compact vector representations of demonstrated tasks (function vectors, FVs) that emerge in hidden states of a small set of attention heads in middle layers. FVs are extracted via causal mediation analysis: mean activations of high-causal-effect attention heads are summed to form a task representation, which can then be injected into hidden states at inference time to trigger task execution on novel inputs without exemplars.
claim c1 [qualitative]: A small number of attention heads in middle layers transport a compact representation of the demonstrated task, called a function vector (FV)
claim c2 [quantitative]: Adding the FV to shuffled-label ICL prompts in GPT-J recovers task performance with 73% average accuracy across 6 tasks
claim c3 [quantitative]: Adding the FV to zero-shot contexts in GPT-J achieves 72% average accuracy across 6 tasks
claim c4 [qualitative]: FVs are robust to changes in context and trigger execution of the task on inputs such as zero-shot and natural text settings that do not resemble the ICL contexts from which they are collected
claim c5 [quantitative]: The antonym FV achieves approximately 72% average accuracy across 20 different ICL templates in zero-shot settings
claim c6 [qualitative]: FVs often encode information about the output space of the function, but this information alone is not sufficient to reconstruct an FV; knowledge of the top 100 decoded vocabulary tokens typically does not restore FV performance
claim c7 [qualitative]: FVs can be summed to create vectors that trigger new complex tasks; some composed FVs outperform both extracted FVs and ICL on individual tasks
claim c8 [qualitative]: Strong causal effects of FVs are observed across models ranging from 6B to 70B parameters, with effects concentrated in middle layers

In-Context Learning Creates Task Vectors (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: task-vector-extraction, activation-patching, hypothesis-class-decomposition, icl-mechanistic
The paper proposes that in-context learning (ICL) in transformer LLMs can be decomposed into two components: a learning algorithm ℒ that compresses a training set S into a query-agnostic task vector θ(S), and a rule-application function ℛ that applies θ to a query x to produce output, without direct dependence on S. The mechanism extracts θ from intermediate hidden states (at a learned layer ℓ*) using a dummy query, then patches θ back into a forward pass of the transformer run only on x, preventing ℛ from directly accessing S while maintaining ICL accuracy.
claim c1 [qualitative]: ICL can be viewed as compressing a training set S into a single task vector θ(S) calculated from S, which then modulates the transformer to produce output
claim c2 [quantitative]: The hypothesis-based procedure (extracting θ and applying ℛ with patching) maintains around 80-90% of the accuracy of regular ICL
claim c3 [quantitative]: The baseline (forward pass on query x alone, without demonstrations or task vector) reaches only 10-20% accuracy
claim c4 [qualitative]: Task vectors form distinct clusters in embedding space, with vectors from the same task closer together than vectors from different tasks, indicating stability of θ across variations in S and dummy query
claim c5 [qualitative]: When conflicting task vectors are injected during forward pass, the model achieves high accuracy on the injected task while largely disregarding the original demonstrations, showing that ℛ mainly relies on θ rather than directly accessing S
claim c6 [qualitative]: Task vectors project onto interpretable tokens that semantically describe the task (e.g. 'English', 'translate' for French-to-English translation) without these terms appearing explicitly in the context
claim c7 [qualitative]: The intermediate layer ℓ* where θ is extracted exhibits a consistent performance peak across models of different sizes and layer counts, despite architectural differences
claim c8 [negative]: The paper studies relatively simple tasks with single-token outputs; it remains unclear whether the task vector mechanism generalizes to more complex ICL cases (e.g. arithmetic reasoning, multi-token outputs) that may require more elaborate parameterization

Codebook Features: Sparse and Discrete Interpretability for Neural Networks (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: vector-quantization, sparse-bottleneck, activation-steering, mechanistic-interpretability, discrete-features, superposition-resolution
Codebook features are produced by finetuning pretrained neural networks with vector quantization bottlenecks inserted at each layer (after attention heads or MLP blocks). During forward pass, activation vectors are compared to learned codebook vectors via cosine similarity, and replaced with the sum of the k most similar codes, creating a sparse discrete bottleneck. The discrete codes are learned end-to-end via straight-through estimators during finetuning, combined with the original task loss (cross-entropy for language modeling) and a reconstruction loss. The sparse, discrete codes enable both unsupervised discovery of interpretable features (by examining tokens where codes activate) and causal intervention (by substituting one code for another to steer behavior).
claim c1 [qualitative]: Neural networks can operate under an extreme sparse, discrete bottleneck with only modest degradation in performance
claim c2 [qualitative]: On TokFSM finite state machine dataset, attention-only codebook model achieves comparable language modeling loss and next token accuracy to original model
claim c3 [quantitative]: On TokFSM, codebook models identify state codes with average precision of 97.1% at classifying FSM states, compared to best neuron precision of 70.5%
claim c4 [qualitative]: Codebook transformer learns to detect and assign distinct codes to states of a finite state machine, overcoming the superposition problem, with causal evidence: changing state codes in MLP layers shifts the next token distribution toward the target state
claim c5 [qualitative]: On WikiText-103, 24-layer 410M parameter codebook language model with attention codebooks achieves loss and accuracy close to finetuned baseline
claim c6 [quantitative]: Codebook models are better classifiers of simple textual features than individual neurons: codes achieve over 30% higher average precision on regex-based feature classification than best-neuron baselines
claim c7 [qualitative]: Activating topic codes in TinyStories model causes generated text to mention target topics far more frequently: topic code steering achieves 65-100% mention rate across different topics (fire, dragon, slide, flower, baby, princess), compared to baseline mention rates of 0-42.5%
claim c8 [qualitative]: Codebook features identify diverse, disentangled concepts in language models spanning orthography, syntax, word types, events, and topics, enabling intuitive control of model behavior via code activation

The Linear Representation Hypothesis and the Geometry of Large Language Models (2023)
seam: none; tags: linear-representation, counterfactual-reasoning, causal-inner-product, probing, steering, representation-geometry
This is a theoretical paper formalizing the linear representation hypothesis in large language models. It does not propose a new model or training procedure. Instead, it provides mathematical formalizations of what 'linear representation' means by connecting three interpretations (subspace, measurement, intervention) via counterfactual reasoning. The paper identifies a non-Euclidean inner product—the causal inner product—that respects semantic structure and unifies representation spaces. The key mechanism is: given counterfactual word pairs differing only in a concept value, differences in unembedding space define concept directions (unembedding representations); these connect to linear probing via Theorem 2.2. Embedding-space representations are defined via context pairs and connect to steering via Theorem 2.5. The causal inner product (estimated from vocabulary statistics under causal separability) maps unembedding to embedding representations via Riesz isomorphism (Theorem 3.2).
claim c1 [theoretical]: Unembedding representation (Definition 2.1) connects to measurement: given unembedding representation, the probability that output is a concept value is logit-linear in the context embedding
claim c2 [theoretical]: Embedding representation (Definition 2.3) connects to intervention: adding embedding representation to context changes probability of target concept but not causally separable off-target concepts
claim c3 [theoretical]: Causal inner product (Definition 3.1, where causally separable concepts are orthogonal) unifies embedding and unembedding representations via Riesz isomorphism
claim c4 [theoretical]: Under Assumption 3.3 (independence of concept values in random vocabulary samples), causal inner product has explicit form given by Equation 3.2, enabling practical estimation without exhaustive concept enumeration
claim c5 [qualitative]: Counterfactual token pair differences align substantially more strongly with estimated concept directions than random word pair differences across 27 concepts from BATS 3.0 and language pairs
claim c6 [qualitative]: Estimated causal inner product based on Equation 3.3 produces near-zero inner products between causally separable concepts; most concept pairs are nearly orthogonal with interpretable block-diagonal structure by semantic similarity
claim c7 [qualitative]: Concept directions act as linear probes: for FrenchSpanish, logit is logit-linear in the concept direction; off-target concept malefemale has no predictive power
claim c8 [qualitative]: Intervention by adding scaled embedding representation constructed via causal inner product isomorphism increases target concept probability while leaving causally separable concepts unchanged; e.g., adding malefemale direction changes 'king' to 'queen' as most likely next word without changing capitalization concept

In-context Vectors: Making In Context Learning More Effective and Controllable Through Latent Space Steering (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, latent-space-shifting, in-context-learning, task-vector, vector-arithmetic, inference-only
In-Context Vectors (ICV) is a two-stage inference-only method for adapting LLMs to tasks without fine-tuning. Stage 1 (Task Summary): forward passes on paired demonstration examples (input x_i, target y_i) extract latent states at the final token position across all transformer layers, concatenate them, compute differences (y_i − x_i), and extract the first principal direction (for paired) or gradient of contrastive loss (for unpaired) as the task-encoding ICV vector. Stage 2 (Feature Shifting): during inference on a new query, the ICV is added to latent states at every layer and every token position with a learnable scaling factor α, followed by layer-norm renormalization to preserve activation magnitude. The modified representations steer generation toward the demonstrated task without modifying model weights or adding new parameters.
claim c1 [quantitative]: ICV achieves a reduction in toxicity by 49.81% and 45.31% on Falcon-7b and Llama-7b models respectively compared to baseline without context on ParaDetox language detoxification task
claim c2 [quantitative]: On ParaDetox detoxification, ICV (paired) achieves toxicity of 34.77% for Falcon-7B compared to 73.09% for in-context learning and 52.78% for LoRA fine-tuning
claim c3 [quantitative]: On formality transfer with Llama-7B, ICV achieves 48.30% formality accuracy compared to 32.96% for in-context learning and 21.99% for LoRA fine-tuning with 5 demonstrations
claim c4 [quantitative]: On sentiment transfer (positive) with Llama-7B, ICV achieves 75.28% positivity accuracy compared to 63.42% for in-context learning and 65.92% for LoRA fine-tuning
claim c5 [quantitative]: On jail-break attack against Vicuna-7B, ICV with α=0.8 achieves 93% attack success rate (ASR) and with α=1.0 reaches 99% ASR compared to 44% for in-context learning and 0% for no-attack baseline
claim c6 [qualitative]: ICV is not constrained by context length and can scale to many demonstration examples that would exceed the context window limit of conventional in-context learning, whereas ICL requires direct inclusion of demonstrations in the prompt
claim c7 [qualitative]: ICV enables task arithmetic: multiple in-context vectors can be combined via vector addition and subtraction to simultaneously follow different types of instructions without retraining
claim c8 [negative]: ICV requires access to model internals in order to add the in-context vector to latent states, making it less suitable for closed-source or API-only models compared to standard in-context learning which only requires text prompting

Structured World Representations in Maze-Solving Transformers (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: mechanistic-interpretability, residual-stream-decoding, attention-head-analysis, spatial-representations, path-finding
Small transformer models trained to solve maze tasks. The paper analyzes internal representations in the residual stream and token embeddings, showing that maze topology and valid paths are encoded in structured form. Linear decoding from a single token's residual stream reconstructs the entire maze. Attention heads termed 'adjacency heads' are identified as implicated in selecting valid next tokens during path-following.
claim c1 [qualitative]: the residual stream of only a single token can be linearly decoded to faithfully reconstruct the entire maze
claim c2 [qualitative]: the learned embeddings of individual tokens have spatial structure
claim c3 [qualitative]: attention heads (dubbed adjacency heads) are implicated in finding valid subsequent tokens
claim c4 [qualitative]: evidence for the consistent emergence of structured internal representations of maze topology and valid paths

LaRS: Latent Reasoning Skills for Chain-of-Thought Reasoning (2023)
seam: text; tags: demonstration-selection, cvae, latent-skills, unsupervised-learning, cot-prompting, skill-discovery
LaRS learns latent reasoning skills from rationales in an example bank using a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE). The CVAE has three components: an encoder that maps question-rationale pairs to latent skill embeddings, a decoder that reconstructs rationales from questions and sampled skills, and a reasoning policy that predicts required skills for new questions. During demonstration selection, the reasoning policy encodes a test question to a skill vector, and examples are ranked by cosine similarity of their encoded skills to the test question's skill, enabling automatic selection of in-context examples for chain-of-thought prompting without requiring external LLM skill annotations.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LaRS outperforms Skill-KNN-zero in 18 out of 20 experiments across all four benchmarks and five backbone models tested
claim c2 [quantitative]: LaRS requires no LLM inferences (approximately $30 savings per 1k examples) and reduces computing time by 1.5 hours per 1k examples during pre-processing
claim c3 [quantitative]: LaRS achieves more than 100% less cost per input query compared to Skill-KNN-zero
claim c4 [quantitative]: On TabMWP with 30% of rationales replaced by random rationales, Skill-KNN-zero suffers a 3% performance drop while LaRS experiences only 0.1% performance drop
claim c5 [quantitative]: On COGS with 30% of rationales replaced by random rationales, Skill-KNN-zero suffers an 11.7% performance drop while LaRS experiences only 1.9% performance drop
claim c6 [quantitative]: LaRS achieves 78.1% accuracy on TabMWP with gpt-3.5-turbo, matching Skill-KNN-zero (77.7%) and approaching oracle Retrieval-R (77.4%)
claim c7 [theoretical]: Under Assumption 1 (example bank sampled from optimal conditional distribution) and Assumption 2 (humans and LLMs are expert rationale generators given reasoning skills and questions), skill-based selection yields the optimal conditional distribution of rationales given questions
claim c8 [qualitative]: LaRS does not require powerful LLMs and achieves similar performance boosts for smaller backbone models like Falcon-40B-Instruct compared to Skill-KNN-zero, whereas Skill-KNN-small (using Falcon-40B-instruct for skill generation) suffers significant performance drops across all benchmarks

Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, residual-stream-intervention, mean-difference-vector, inference-time-control, behavior-modulation
Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA) steers Llama 2 by computing steering vectors from residual stream activation differences between contrastive prompt pairs (positive vs. negative examples of a behavior). During inference, these vectors are added at all token positions after the user's prompt with a scalar multiplier, modulating the model's output distribution toward or away from the target behavior without retraining.
claim c1 [qualitative]: CAA significantly alters model behavior on multiple-choice behavioral evaluation datasets across all seven tested behaviors (AI Coordination, Corrigibility, Hallucination, Myopic Reward, Survival Instinct, Sycophancy, Refusal)
claim c2 [qualitative]: CAA generalizes from multiple-choice contrastive contexts to open-ended generation tasks
claim c3 [qualitative]: CAA can modify model behavior beyond what is achieved through system-prompting alone for most behaviors tested
claim c4 [qualitative]: For 3 out of 7 tested behaviors, CAA can additionally steer the behavior beyond the effects of supervised finetuning alone in both positive and negative directions
claim c5 [quantitative]: CAA intervention does not significantly affect MMLU performance
claim c6 [qualitative]: Optimal layers for steering with CAA correspond to layer 13 and adjacent layers in Llama 2 7B Chat, and layer 14-15 in Llama 2 13B Chat
claim c7 [qualitative]: Behavioral clustering in activation space emerges around one-third of the way through the layers, indicating that middle and later layers contain higher-level representations of behaviors
claim c8 [qualitative]: Generating a CAA steering vector requires less than five minutes on a single GPU, whereas finetuning Llama 2 7B Chat on 1000 examples requires 10 minutes on 2 NVIDIA L40 GPUs

Patchscopes: A Unifying Framework for Inspecting Hidden Representations of Language Models (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-inspection, natural-language-explanation, intervention, layer-patching, vocabulary-projection-unification
Patchscopes is a framework for inspecting hidden representations of LLMs by leveraging the model itself to generate natural-language explanations. The core mechanism patches a copy of the model's computation at a chosen layer, replacing downstream layers with a query function that asks the model to explain what a particular representation means. Information flows from early/intermediate layer activations through a patched forward pass; the model's text generation capability is used as the explanation engine. The framework unifies prior methods (vocabulary projection, intervention techniques) while enabling new interpretability modes such as cross-model explanation (using a capable model to explain a smaller model's representations) and error correction via multihop reasoning.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Patchscopes can be used to answer a wide range of questions about an LLM's computation
claim c2 [qualitative]: Many prior interpretability methods based on projecting representations into the vocabulary space and intervening on the LLM computation can be viewed as instances of the Patchscopes framework
claim c3 [qualitative]: Patchscopes can mitigate shortcomings of prior methods such as failure in inspecting early layers or lack of expressivity
claim c4 [qualitative]: Patchscopes enables using a more capable model to explain the representations of a smaller model
claim c5 [qualitative]: Patchscopes enables multihop reasoning error correction

Model Editing at Scale leads to Gradual and Catastrophic Forgetting (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: knowledge-editing, model-editing, sequential-editing, catastrophic-forgetting, weight-localization, causal-tracing
This paper evaluates existing model editing methods (ROME, MEMIT, MEND) applied sequentially to the same language model. These methods localize facts to specific layers via causal tracing, then update weights of identified layers to inject new knowledge. The paper does not propose a new architecture but rather performs systematic empirical evaluation of how these methods degrade under repeated sequential editing, measuring editing proficiency, fact forgetting, and downstream task performance as edits accumulate.
claim c1 [qualitative]: As a model is edited sequentially with multiple facts, it continually forgets previously edited facts and the ability to perform downstream tasks, happening in two phases—an initial gradual but progressive forgetting phase followed by abrupt or catastrophic forgetting phase.
claim c2 [qualitative]: For ROME, the efficacy score is almost 100% until a point where it begins to decline, which can come as early as 100 edits or as late as 1000 edits.
claim c3 [quantitative]: MEMIT forgets almost three times fewer facts compared to ROME when performing sequential edits.
claim c4 [negative]: Edits made by ROME and MEMIT are not as localized as previously believed; new edits consistently bleed into other facts stored in the model, as evidenced by neighborhood score consistently declining with additional edits.
claim c5 [quantitative]: Disabling edits in ROME have normalized L2 distance from original weights three orders of magnitude larger than non-disabling edits.
claim c6 [negative]: MEND is unable to make multiple sequential edits to the same model, as shown by its efficacy score, reiterating previous observations that MEND cannot be used to reliably edit knowledge at scale.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Catastrophic forgetting in ROME occurs when the edited layer becomes so different from its original version that compatibility with other transformer layers is destroyed, causing the model to catastrophically forget previously edited facts and lose ability to perform downstream tasks.
claim c8 [qualitative]: MEMIT maintains ability to perform downstream tasks on GPT-J as more edits are made, but shows gradual loss of downstream performance on GPT2-XL with multiple edits, with the model losing ability on paraphrase detection long before catastrophic forgetting point.

Sketch-Guided Constrained Decoding for Boosting Blackbox Large Language Models without Logit Access (2024)
seam: text; tags: constrained-decoding, two-phase-refinement, grammar-constraints, blackbox-workaround, beam-search
SketchGCD is a two-phase constrained decoding system for blackbox LLMs without logit access. In phase 1 (sketching), a powerful blackbox LLM generates an unconstrained preliminary output (sketch) given an instruction and few-shot demonstrations. In phase 2 (constrained decoding), a locally hosted smaller auxiliary LLM refines the sketch to satisfy specified constraints by applying grammar-constrained decoding. Information flows from the blackbox LLM's text output → auxiliary LLM's constrained refinement → final output.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On Wiki-NRE closed information extraction, SketchGCD with GPT-4 sketcher and LLaMA-2-7B decoder achieves F1 of 46.1, compared to 43.6 for GPT-4 unconstrained baseline
claim c2 [quantitative]: On SynthIE-text closed information extraction, SketchGCD with GPT-3.5-Turbo sketcher and LLaMA-2-7B decoder achieves F1 of 45.1, compared to 23.8 for GPT-3.5-Turbo unconstrained baseline
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Penn Treebank constituency parsing with GPT-4 sketcher, SketchGCD achieves 100% tag validity (6.4 points improvement), compared to 93.6% for GPT-4 unconstrained
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Penn Treebank constituency parsing with GPT-4 sketcher, SketchGCD achieves 92.5% tree validity, compared to 86.0% for GPT-4 unconstrained
claim c5 [quantitative]: Beam search is critical for constrained decoding performance: on Wiki-NRE with LLaMA-2-7B, beam size 1 (greedy) yields F1 of 25.8, beam size 2 yields 32.8, and beam size 8 yields 33.4
claim c6 [qualitative]: SketchGCD achieves 100% grounding precision by construction: all entities and relations in output are guaranteed to be present in the knowledge catalogue, compared to 45.2% invalid triplets for GPT-4 unconstrained on Wiki-NRE
claim c7 [negative]: SketchGCD adds computational overhead as it requires a constrained decoder to refine sketches after the sketching phase, incurring additional latency and resource costs
claim c8 [negative]: SketchGCD can only enforce constraints at the structure or syntactic level, not at the semantic level; the model can still generate semantically incorrect outputs

Demystifying Chains, Trees, and Graphs of Thoughts (2024)
seam: text; tags: prompting-taxonomy, reasoning-topology, chain-tree-graph, prompt-pipeline, structure-enhanced-reasoning
This is a survey and taxonomy paper that analyzes structure-enhanced LLM reasoning schemes. It formalizes the prompt execution pipeline (preprocessing, context management, LLM generation, post-processing) and introduces a blueprint for reasoning topologies—graphs of thoughts where nodes represent semantic units of task resolution and edges represent dependencies. The paper does not propose a new trainable system, but rather provides a conceptual framework for understanding how existing schemes (Chain-of-Thought, Tree of Thoughts, Graph of Thoughts) map reasoning processes onto prompt/reply sequences.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Prompt engineering coupled with structures has emerged as a promising paradigm, with designs such as Chain-of-Thought, Tree of Thoughts, or Graph of Thoughts, in which the overall LLM reasoning is guided by a structure such as a graph, significantly enhancing the LLM's capability to solve numerous tasks, ranging from logical or mathematical reasoning to planning or creative writing.
claim c2 [theoretical]: The prompt execution pipeline can be formally decomposed into five functional building blocks: preprocessing transformation P, context update transformation Φ, LLM auto-generative execution, post-processing transformation Q, and context update transformation Ψ, forming equations that generalize all prompting interactions.
claim c3 [theoretical]: A reasoning topology is defined as a graph G=(V,E) where V is a set of nodes modeling thoughts (semantic units of task resolution) and E is a set of edges modeling reasoning dependencies between thoughts.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Seven fundamental aspects define any LLM reasoning scheme that harnesses reasoning topologies: topology class (chain, tree, or graph), topology scope (single-prompt vs. multi-prompt), topology representation (implicit vs. explicit), topology derivation (user-constructed, automatic, or semi-automatic), reasoning schedule (how the topology is traversed), schedule representation (natural language, code, in-context examples, or other), and harnessed parts of the AI pipeline (pre-training, fine-tuning, retrieval, tools, modalities).
claim c5 [qualitative]: Two fundamental use cases of thoughts and topologies are distinguished: solution topologies (nodes reachable from the input task statement node that form steps toward solving the task) and in-context example topologies (nodes not reachable from the input, providing examples but not reasoning steps toward the solution).
claim c6 [qualitative]: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) introduces chain topologies with intermediate reasoning steps; Zero-shot-CoT elicits multi-step reasoning without hand-tuned in-context examples; Least-to-Most Prompting decomposes complex tasks into sub-questions solved sequentially; Tree of Thoughts (ToT) allows branching at any point with state evaluation and search algorithms (BFS/DFS); Graph of Thoughts (GoT) enables arbitrary reasoning dependencies including both branching and aggregation operations.
claim c7 [qualitative]: CoT significantly outperforms Input-Output (IO) prompting in arithmetic reasoning on benchmarks such as GSM8K (Grade School Math), SVAMP (Simple Variations on Arithmetic Math word Problem), and MAWPS (MAth Word Problem).
claim c8 [negative]: State-of-the-art prompting schemes still exhibit numerous limitations: they are limited to simple tasks such as Game of 24; state-of-the-art prompting schemes often entail high inference costs; designing, developing, maintaining, and extending these schemes is hard due to rapid LLM ecosystem development and unclear definitions of concepts such as how 'thought' relates to 'prompt'.

Probabilistic Abduction for Visual Abstract Reasoning via Learning Rules in Vector-symbolic Architectures (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: vsa, probabilistic-abduction, rule-learning, convex-optimization, interpretability
Learn-VRF learns vector-symbolic architecture (VSA) rule formulations for solving Raven's progressive matrices via probabilistic abduction. The system maps attribute values to one-hot probability mass functions (PMFs), translates them to high-dimensional VSA vectors using binary sparse block codes, then learns rule-specific combinations of binding/unbinding operations over these vectors via convex optimization. Each rule generates a confidence score by comparing predicted VSA vectors against ground truth; the final answer is determined either by sampling the highest-confidence rule or taking a weighted combination of all rules, with the candidate panel having the largest cosine similarity to predicted attributes selected as the answer.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Learn-VRF achieves an average accuracy of 81.3% when using a sampling-based rule selection and 84.1% with the weighted rule combination on in-distribution I-RAVEN data
claim c2 [quantitative]: Learn-VRF requires only 5k trainable parameters while MLP baseline requires 300k parameters
claim c3 [quantitative]: Learn-VRF achieves nearly perfect accuracy (99.8–100%) on out-of-distribution unseen attribute-rule pairs, significantly outperforming GPT-3 (65.8–94.2%) and MLP baseline (14.8–75.0%)
claim c4 [qualitative]: Learn-VRF learns VSA rule formulations with just one pass through the training data
claim c5 [qualitative]: The learned rule formulations are interpretable; dominant formulas can be derived by selecting the VSA vector with the highest weight for each term, and algebraic simplifications reveal the equivalent RPM rule
claim c6 [negative]: Learn-VRF cannot represent logical rules (e.g., logical OR) on bit-code vectors in the position attribute for 2x2 grid, 3x3 grid, and out-in grid constellations; limited to sums and differences of attributes in VSA fractional form
claim c7 [quantitative]: Learn-VRF reaches 73.5% accuracy on 2x2 grid constellation, approaching the theoretical upper bound of 76.1% (share of non-logical rules)
claim c8 [qualitative]: Learn-VRF outperforms pure connectionist baselines (MLP) and large language models (GPT-3) on out-of-distribution tests while maintaining transparency and interpretability

Multi-step Problem Solving Through a Verifier: An Empirical Analysis on Model-induced Process Supervision (2024)
seam: text; tags: verifier-loop, process-supervision, monte-carlo-sampling, step-wise-scoring, aggregation-functions
MiPS is a data curation method for training process-supervised verifiers (PSVs) in a reasoner-verifier paradigm for multi-step problem solving. The system generates training labels automatically by: (1) sampling multiple completions of intermediate solution prefixes using a reasoner model with temperature-based decoding, (2) computing the correctness rate (percentage of completions that are correct), and (3) training a verifier language model to predict these percentage correctness scores at each step. During inference, the verifier assigns scores to each step of generated solutions, which are aggregated (via functions like max or sum_logit) into a final score for solution ranking. The verifier learns to map solution prefixes and their continuations to step-wise correctness estimates, enabling ranking of candidate solutions without human annotation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: MiPS significantly improves the performance of PaLM 2 on math and coding tasks: accuracy +0.67% on GSM8K, +4.16% on MATH, +0.92% on MBPP compared with an output supervision trained verifier
claim c2 [qualitative]: For noisy MiPS data, aggregation functions that focus on high predicted scores (e.g., max, sum_logit, sum_odds) are preferred over those focusing on low predicted scores (e.g., min, sum_logprob), contrary to prior observations on human curated data
claim c3 [qualitative]: Process supervision trained on MiPS data generalizes across different reasoning models; verifiers trained on data generated by PaLM 2-S successfully validate solutions from stronger reasoners (PaLM 2-L, GPT-3.5-turbo)
claim c4 [qualitative]: Using only the last-step process-supervised verifier prediction is more beneficial than output supervision alone, indicating that process supervision data helps with generalization due to more diverse context
claim c5 [qualitative]: MiPS data exhibits higher noise in earlier steps compared to later steps; predictions on earlier steps are of lower quality and can hurt verification when included in aggregation functions
claim c6 [qualitative]: The soft training objective (predicting estimated accuracies directly) with max aggregation consistently outperforms the hard training objective (binarized scores) with min aggregation
claim c7 [negative]: On the MATH dataset, process and output verifiers did not outperform self-consistency, likely due to training a smaller verifier (PaLM 2-S) to compensate for long sequence lengths, whereas the reasoner was PaLM 2-L
claim c8 [negative]: MiPS requires substantial computation effort to generate training data via Monte Carlo sampling completions for every intermediate step, which the authors did not attempt to reduce

Constrained Decoding for Cross-lingual Label Projection (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, label-projection, machine-translation, branch-and-bound, cross-lingual-transfer, marker-injection
Codec is a constrained decoding system for cross-lingual label projection. It translates source sentences without annotation markers to a target language via a machine translation model (NLLB), then uses a specialized constrained decoding algorithm to inject span markers at correct positions in the translation template. The algorithm operates in three stages: (1) pruning unlikely opening-marker positions by comparing conditional token probabilities with/without markers, (2) branch-and-bound depth-first search to find top-k hypotheses that satisfy constraints (exact marker count, fixed template text), and (3) re-ranking using hypothesis-level (generative log-probability) and span-level (source-to-target span translation probability) scores. Information flows from source text through MT without markers, then through constrained decoding that enforces template fidelity and marker count while selecting the best projection.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On average, Codec outperforms EasyProject by a large margin in translate-train setting on MasakhaNER2.0, with improvements of at least 1.5 F1 in nine languages
claim c2 [quantitative]: In translate-test setting on MasakhaNER2.0, Codec achieves average F1 of 70.4, outperforming Awes-align (65.8)
claim c3 [quantitative]: For chiShona in translate-train NER, Codec achieves +16.5 F1 improvement over EasyProject
claim c4 [quantitative]: For Bambara in translate-test NER, Codec is approximately 60 times faster than exact branch-and-bound search while losing only 0.6 absolute F1
claim c5 [quantitative]: On ACE-2005 Event Argument Extraction in translate-train setting, Codec achieves average F1 of 53.8, outperforming EasyProject (52.6) and Awes-align (52.8)
claim c6 [qualitative]: The heuristic lower bound (Eq. 7-8) with α=3 achieves roughly the same F1 as exact search with re-ranking while having much better decoding speed
claim c7 [negative]: In Chinese manual analysis, more than 60% of Codec errors occur because the correct hypothesis is in the top-k found by Codec, but the re-ranking step fails to retrieve it
claim c8 [qualitative]: Codec is applicable to both translate-train and translate-test settings, whereas EasyProject is not applicable to translate-test due to its marker-based approach

Let Your Graph Do the Talking: Encoding Structured Data for LLMs (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: soft-prompt, gnn-encoder, parameter-efficient, graph-reasoning, kv-injection
GraphToken is a parameter-efficient encoder that maps graph structures to soft tokens in an LLM's embedding space. A GNN (graph encoder) processes the input graph using learned or Laplacian positional node features and outputs fixed-dimensional token embeddings via readout (mean/sum pooling for graph-level tasks, per-node representations for node tasks, concatenated node pairs for edge tasks). These graph tokens are prepended to the text prompt embeddings and passed to a frozen LLM (PaLM 2), which generates answers. Only the GNN parameters are trained; LLM parameters remain frozen.
claim c1 [quantitative]: GraphToken demonstrates significant improvements across node, edge, and graph-level tasks from the GraphQA benchmark, with up to 73% points improvement
claim c2 [quantitative]: On edge existence task, GraphToken achieves 0.738 accuracy compared to soft-prompt baseline of 0.544
claim c3 [quantitative]: On node degree task, GraphToken achieves 0.962 accuracy compared to soft-prompt baseline of 0.098
claim c4 [quantitative]: On cycle check task, GraphToken achieves 0.956 accuracy compared to zero-shot baseline of 0.760
claim c5 [qualitative]: Graph encoder parameters range from 17,152 to 198,788 parameters depending on architecture choice; projection layer adds approximately 11 million parameters total
claim c6 [qualitative]: Breaking equivariance via learned positional features (IDX) generally improves graph reasoning performance compared to Laplacian positional encodings (LPE), and combination of both types of features (LPE+IDX) proves particularly powerful for some encoders
claim c7 [qualitative]: Different graph convolution architectures (GCN, GIN, MPNN, HGT, MHA, Node Set, Edge Set) show varying performance across tasks with no single architecture dominating all tasks
claim c8 [qualitative]: Graph encoders trained on cycle check and triangle counting tasks generalize to unseen bipartiteness detection task, with attention-based methods performing best

A Systematic Survey of Prompt Engineering in Large Language Models: Techniques and Applications (2024)
