seam: none; tags: survey, prompt-engineering, taxonomy, llm-techniques, reasoning-prompts, retrieval-augmentation
This is a survey paper that systematically categorizes and summarizes prompt engineering techniques for large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). It does not propose a novel system or mechanism, but rather documents existing prompting methods (zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought variants, retrieval-augmented generation, code-based reasoning, etc.) and organizes them by application domain. The paper provides a taxonomy diagram and tables mapping techniques to models, datasets, and evaluation metrics.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Prompt engineering has emerged as an indispensable technique for extending the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), allowing task-specific instructions to enhance model efficacy without modifying core model parameters.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting achieved state-of-the-art performance in math and commonsense reasoning benchmarks, achieving an accuracy of 90.2% on PaLM 540B.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Self-Consistency with Chain-of-Thought prompting resulted in accuracy improvements of 17.9% on GSM8K, 11.0% on SVAMP, 12.2% on AQuA, 6.4% on StrategyQA, and 3.9% on ARC-challenge compared to the baseline chain-of-thought prompting.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) prompting achieved a 74% success rate on Game of 24 tasks compared to CoT's 4%, and achieved a 60% success rate in word-level tasks versus CoT's 16%.
claim c5 [quantitative]: Chain-of-Symbol (CoS) prompting elevated ChatGPT's performance from 31.8% to 92.6% accuracy on Brick World tasks and achieved up to 65.8% reduction in prompt tokens.
claim c6 [quantitative]: Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) outperformed seq2seq models and task-specific architectures on ODQA benchmarks, achieving exact match scores up to 56.8% on TriviaQA and 44.5% on Natural Questions.
claim c7 [qualitative]: The survey analyzes over 41 distinct prompt engineering techniques categorized by application area, including zero-shot prompting, few-shot prompting, chain-of-thought variants, retrieval-augmented generation, code-based reasoning, and optimization strategies.
claim c8 [negative]: Few-shot prompting requires additional tokens to include examples, which may become prohibitive for longer text inputs, and the selection and composition of prompt examples can significantly influence model behavior with potential biases.

JAMDEC: Unsupervised Authorship Obfuscation using Constrained Decoding over Small Language Models (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, keyword-extraction, diverse-beam-search, filtering, authorship-obfuscation, content-preservation
JamDec is a three-stage inference-time algorithm for authorship obfuscation that operates on small language models (GPT2-XL, 1.5B parameters). Stage 1 extracts content-preserving keywords using embedding-based (KeyBERT) and likelihood-based methods (GPT2, T5). Stage 2 generates multiple obfuscated candidates via Constrained Diverse Beam Search (CoDi-BS), which merges lexically constrained beam search with diversity scoring to produce varied rewrites that include extracted keywords. Stage 3 filters candidates using Natural Language Inference (entailment threshold) and Corpus of Linguistic Acceptability (grammaticality threshold) scores, optionally applying a stylometric obfuscator if no candidates pass filtering. The system preserves content by enforcing keyword inclusion and semantic entailment, while varying authorship style through generation diversity.
claim c1 [qualitative]: JamDec with GPT2-XL achieves the highest Task Score for almost every dataset, with Task Score on AMT-10 more than higher than almost all other methods on ENS and BertAA Task Score
claim c2 [quantitative]: JamDec (BertAA Task Score) is only lower than GPT3.5 175B on AMT-3
claim c3 [qualitative]: Using CoDi-BS leads to an overall increase in Drop Rate of and an increase in the number of sentences that pass the base NLI and CoLA threshold of about, with little change in NLI and CoLA score compared to only using CBS
claim c4 [qualitative]: JamDec strikes a better balance between content preservation and author obfuscation compared to task-specific baselines, lying closely to the top right in graphs of Drop Rate vs. NLI vs. CoLA
claim c5 [qualitative]: In human evaluation on AMT-3, JamDec performs better than all task-specific methods in Obfuscation by at least
claim c6 [qualitative]: JamDec human evaluation scores are higher for Grammar and Fluency than most other methods, including GPT3.5, while performing on-par with GPT3.5 for Content Preservation
claim c7 [negative]: JamDec employs generations from a pre-trained language model which have been known to add factually incorrect or hallucinatory information, and despite content-preserving filters, additional information can bypass these filters and make it into the final obfuscation
claim c8 [negative]: JamDec may take a long time to employ if the approach is used at sentence-level on lengthy texts because it involves producing several candidates for each obfuscation, though it is slower than traditional stylometric-based methods

A Survey on Knowledge Distillation of Large Language Models (2024)
seam: none; tags: knowledge-elicitation, distillation-pipeline, data-augmentation, supervised-fine-tuning, reinforcement-learning, skill-transfer, domain-specialization
This is a survey paper on knowledge distillation (KD) of large language models. It does not present a single unified system but rather categorizes and reviews existing KD approaches across three dimensions: (1) KD Algorithms—methods for eliciting knowledge from teacher LLMs (labeling, expansion, curation, feature extraction, feedback, self-knowledge) and injecting it into student models (supervised fine-tuning, divergence/similarity minimization, reinforcement learning, rank optimization); (2) Skill Distillation—techniques for enhancing specific student capabilities (context following, alignment, agent abilities, NLP task specialization, multi-modality); and (3) Verticalization Distillation—domain-specific applications (law, healthcare, finance, science). The general pipeline involves: (i) steering a teacher LLM toward a target skill/domain via instructions, (ii) providing seed knowledge as input, (iii) generating distillation knowledge (QA, explanations, logits, features), and (iv) training the student model with a learning objective to minimize divergence from teacher outputs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Knowledge distillation in the LLM era shifts the focus from mere architecture compression to knowledge elicitation and transfer, largely due to the expansive and deep-seated knowledge that LLMs like GPT-4 and Gemini possess.
claim c2 [qualitative]: KD techniques have surged as a means to bridge the performance gap between proprietary LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Gemini) and open-source LLMs (e.g., LLaMA, Mistral).
claim c3 [qualitative]: Data augmentation (DA) emerges as a prevalent paradigm to achieve knowledge distillation of LLMs, where a small seed of knowledge is used to prompt the LLM to generate more data with respect to a specific skill or domain.
claim c4 [qualitative]: KD retains its fundamental role in compressing LLMs, making them more efficient without significant loss in performance.
claim c5 [qualitative]: The strategy of employing open-source LLMs as teachers for their own self-improvement has emerged as a promising approach, enhancing their capabilities significantly.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Modern knowledge distillation in LLMs emphasizes the transfer of more abstract qualities such as reasoning patterns, preference alignment, and value alignment, in contrast to earlier focus on output replication.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Knowledge distillation in LLMs involves extracting knowledge in forms including question-and-answer (QA) dialogues, narrative explanations, logits, and hidden features from teacher models.

SynCode: LLM Generation with Grammar Augmentation (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, grammar-masking, dfa-based-filtering, syntax-guided-generation, incremental-parsing
SynCode is a constrained decoding framework that augments LLM token generation with grammar-based filtering. During each decoding step, the system parses partial code to extract a terminal sequence and remainder, uses an offline-precomputed DFA mask store to compute which tokens are syntactically valid continuations, masks the LLM's logit distribution to zero out invalid tokens, and samples the next token from the constrained distribution. The DFA mask store is built once per grammar-vocabulary pair by precomputing binary masks for DFA states and terminal sequences, enabling efficient O(|remainder| + |accept_sequences|) per-token masking during generation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SynCode reduces syntax errors by 96.07% when combined with state-of-the-art LLMs for Python and Go code generation
claim c2 [quantitative]: For Python on HumanEval, Llama-7B with SynCode achieves 99% syntax error reduction (230 errors → 2 errors)
claim c3 [quantitative]: For Python on MBXP, CodeGen-350M with SynCode achieves 100% syntax error reduction (68 errors → 0 errors)
claim c4 [quantitative]: SynCode introduces a 1.22x average runtime overhead with LR(1) parsing and 1.76x with LALR(1) parsing across models and languages
claim c5 [quantitative]: For Go on HumanEval, Llama-7B with SynCode reduces syntax errors by 99% (725 → 10)
claim c6 [theoretical]: SynCode achieves soundness: all syntactically valid tokens are retained in the mask for accept sequences of length ≥ 1
claim c7 [theoretical]: SynCode achieves completeness: all syntactically invalid tokens are rejected when accept sequences have length greater than the maximum token string length in the vocabulary
claim c8 [negative]: Remaining syntax errors (3.93% of total) occur because the LLM fails to halt generation before reaching the maximum generation token limit

FLAP: Flow-Adhering Planning with Constrained Decoding in LLMs (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, lookahead-heuristic, dependency-graph, api-planning, workflow-adherence, zero-shot
FLAP is a constrained decoding algorithm that guides LLM plan generation by maintaining dynamic dependency graphs for API and workflow steps, then scoring candidate token sequences via a lookahead heuristic that evaluates: (1) semantic alignment of generated thoughts to permitted workflow steps, (2) alignment of generated APIs to permitted APIs based on input-output parameter dependencies, (3) alignment of generated thoughts to user intent, and (4) coherence between generated thoughts and APIs. The system operates on top of any autoregressive LLM without fine-tuning, using a sentence transformer for semantic similarity scoring and enforcing structural constraints during beam search decoding.
claim c1 [qualitative]: All LLMs fail heavily in zero-shot planning regardless of the setting, especially LLMs fail to preserve the API and flow step dependencies in plans
claim c2 [qualitative]: The average percentage of inconsistent APIs in zero-shot prompting baselines ranges from 60% to 90%
claim c3 [quantitative]: FLAP reduces inconsistent APIs from 90% to 10% for Mpt-7b-instruct in all-flows-in-context setting
claim c4 [quantitative]: FLAP reduces inconsistent APIs from 80% to 15% for Mistral-7b-instruct in all-flows-in-context setting
claim c5 [qualitative]: FLAP on Mistral-7b-instruct outperforms larger LLMs (30B-40B parameter range) in flow step and API error reduction
claim c6 [negative]: Beam Search and Nucleus Sampling do not improve overall performance compared to Greedy Search and result in fewer parsable plans
claim c7 [negative]: LLMs pretrained on tools (ToolAlpaca) fail heavily in API consistency and result in high API hallucination
claim c8 [qualitative]: FLAP enables quick adaptation to predefined flows without requiring fine-tuning on domain-specific plan or dependency data

A Knowledge-Injected Curriculum Pretraining Framework for Question Answering (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: knowledge-injection, adapter-tuning, curriculum-learning, kg-to-text, multi-hop-reasoning, chain-of-thought
KICP is a three-module pretraining framework for knowledge-based QA. Knowledge Injection (KI) converts knowledge graph triples into sentences via text characterization, sentence construction, and masking. Knowledge Adaptation (KA) freezes the base language model and trains a learnable adapter on top to retain natural language understanding while learning from synthetic KG-derived corpus. Curriculum Reasoning (CR) constructs three increasingly difficult pretraining corpora (single triples, chain-of-thought reasoning steps, final compositions) and trains the adapter via curriculum learning. After pretraining, the frozen LM + trained adapter are fine-tuned on downstream QA tasks.
claim c1 [quantitative]: KICP outperforms all baselines on CN-QA with F1 score of 0.639 and EM of 0.480
claim c2 [quantitative]: KICP achieves F1 of 0.880 and EM of 0.819 on ComplexWebQuestions
claim c3 [quantitative]: KICP achieves accuracy of 0.911 on FreebaseQA
claim c4 [quantitative]: KICP achieves accuracy of 0.809 on Math23K
claim c5 [quantitative]: Knowledge adaptation (KA) alone improves CN-QA F1 to 0.633 compared to BERT baseline at 0.607
claim c6 [quantitative]: On hard questions in FreebaseQA, KICP achieves accuracy of 0.907 compared to BERT at 0.891
claim c7 [qualitative]: The curriculum pretraining with three lessons (knowledge learning, chain-of-thought learning, composition learning) reduces training difficulty and achieves lower final loss in lesson 3 compared to direct training on lesson 3
claim c8 [negative]: Knowledge injection does not bring much improvement and even has negative effect on Math23K, likely because Math23K requires NLU much more than knowledge

SelfIE: Self-Interpretation of Large Language Model Embeddings (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: embedding-interpretation, activation-injection, treatment-effect-relevancy, supervised-layer-editing, rl-embedding-control, harm-mitigation
SelfIE interprets hidden embeddings in transformer-based LLMs by extracting an embedding from the original forward pass and injecting it into a separate interpretation forward pass. The interpretation forward pass uses a templated prompt (e.g., '[X] Please repeat the previous message') where the placeholder token embedding is replaced with the target embedding at a chosen layer. The LLM then autoregressively generates natural language text describing the embedding's semantic content. A treatment-effect relevancy score measures which output tokens are causally driven by the injected embedding versus autoregressive continuation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SelfIE achieves the same performance on eliciting LLM's internal representation of world state in TextWorld as prior supervised approach trained on 100-shot samples, demonstrating the effectiveness and faithfulness of our zero-shot readout approach.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Supervised Control surpasses previous methods on paraphrase effectiveness and harmonic mean, demonstrating comparable capability of fact editing with other methods and better generalization capability.
claim c3 [quantitative]: We reduced prompt injection's success rate of eliciting harmful response by 84.66%, reducing the success rate on 388 harmful activities from 100% to 4.4%, while maintaining 95.85% of original model capability on fact answering task.
claim c4 [quantitative]: After supervised control edit on layer 6 with a single pair of input and output embedding target, the model achieves 95% effective rate of overriding user ethical steering, with 96% of responses (out of 100 unseen samples) prioritizing equality after control versus 100% prioritizing humans before control.
claim c5 [qualitative]: SelfIE reveals LLM internal reasoning in cases such as making ethical decisions, internalizing prompt injection, and recalling harmful knowledge, and produces open-world text explanations for the internal states in LLM without any training.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Supervised Control allows editing open-ended concepts while only requiring gradient computation of individual layer, and Reinforcement Control extends RLHF to hidden embeddings and erases harmful knowledge in LLM without supervision targets.
claim c7 [negative]: Accuracy is worse for 7B and 13B LLaMA-2-Chat models; this appears to result from the smaller model's worse instruction-following capability, with 27.17% of 7B and 17.68% of 13B interpretation responses failing to follow the instruction to choose between two options.
claim c8 [qualitative]: During the interpretation forward pass, inserting the hidden embedding on layer ℓ generally produces higher accuracy than other layers; accuracy degrades for later layers potentially because the information from the interpreted embedding is not able to propagate into the last token for output when insertion is done too late.

Self-Attention Based Semantic Decomposition in Vector Symbolic Architectures (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: resonator-network, hopfield-memory, self-attention, vector-binding, factorization, hyperdimensional-computing
The paper proposes an attention-based resonator network for decomposing bound hypervectors in Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs). The system iteratively factorizes a composite vector (formed by element-wise binding of attribute vectors) into its constituent factors by replacing the traditional Hopfield update rule with a self-attention-inspired update based on a log-sum-exp energy function with norm-bounded states. Information flows through iterative application of the attention-based update rule, which applies similarity-weighted linear combinations of codebook vectors to progressively refine estimates of each factor until convergence.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The attention-based resonator network enables use of the resonator network with continuous factors rather than bipolar factors
claim c2 [qualitative]: The attention-based resonator network outperforms the traditional resonator network for multiple factors (k ≥ 3), with an increasing gap in performance as k increases
claim c3 [qualitative]: The attention-based update rule requires significantly fewer iterations to converge compared to the traditional resonator network
claim c4 [qualitative]: For FHRR hypervectors, the adapted original resonator network significantly underperforms compared to the attention-based resonator network
claim c5 [quantitative]: For noise-robustness with bipolar vectors and D=10000, the attention resonator network succeeds with probability 0.97 while the original succeeds with probability 0.08
claim c6 [quantitative]: The original FHRR-adapted resonator network has near-zero accuracy across all parameter configurations tested
claim c7 [qualitative]: The attention-based FHRR and attention-based bipolar resonator networks achieve an order of magnitude lower complexity (average steps for correct convergence) compared to the original bipolar resonator network
claim c8 [qualitative]: The resulting network has high robustness against error and can store exponentially many combinations of codebook factors

CoLLEGe: Concept Embedding Generation for Large Language Models (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: few-shot-concept-learning, embedding-generation, meta-learning, knowledge-distillation, negative-sampling
CoLLEGe is a meta-learning framework that generates task-general embeddings for new concept tokens in frozen autoregressive LLMs. Given a few example sentences containing a new token, the system: (1) encodes each support sequence with a frozen RoBERTa MLM and trainable Transformer encoder to extract contextual embeddings; (2) aggregates these via mean pooling to produce a single concept embedding; (3) projects this embedding into both input and output embedding spaces of a frozen LLaMA-2 LM via learned linear layers; (4) trains the projection layers by sampling support/query sequences from pretraining corpora, using next-word prediction loss combined with negative example sampling and knowledge distillation to match the frozen LM's output distributions on the query sequence.
claim c1 [quantitative]: CoLLEGe achieves 49.3% accuracy on the GRE Verbal Reasoning task with a definition (D+4-shot), compared to 24.0% for Prompting and 10.6% for HiCE
claim c2 [quantitative]: CoLLEGe achieves 1065.57 ELO on definition generation, compared to 1032.28 for Prompting and 975.64 for HiCE
claim c3 [quantitative]: CoLLEGe achieves 17.81 ROUGE-L on definition generation, compared to 15.54 for Prompting and 7.98 for HiCE
claim c4 [quantitative]: CoLLEGe achieves 60.0% accuracy on the CoLLEGe-Slang benchmark with 4-shot examples, compared to 53.8% for Prompting and 29.0% for Additive
claim c5 [qualitative]: CoLLEGe performs all evaluations zero-shot without additional task-specific training or finetuning, unlike FLAN-T5 baselines which are specialized through instruction tuning
claim c6 [qualitative]: Knowledge distillation and negative example sampling, when combined, significantly boost performance; neither is effective on its own
claim c7 [negative]: The averaging mechanism cannot fully achieve parity with pretrained embeddings, even with more support sequences
claim c8 [negative]: Generated embeddings sometimes miss precise details in examples, instead encoding higher-level semantic information, visible in incorrect generated definitions

Efficient Prompting Methods for Large Language Models: A Survey (2024)
seam: none; tags: survey, prompt-optimization, automatic-prompt-engineering, prompt-compression, text-to-vector, text-to-text, in-context-learning
A survey paper that taxonomizes efficient prompting methods for large language models into two main categories: (1) Automatic Prompt Engineering, which uses LLMs as optimizers to iteratively design and refine instruction and chain-of-thought components through sampling, feedback, and editing strategies; and (2) Prompt Compression, which reduces resource consumption by compressing hard prompts into soft vectors (Text-to-Vector, continuous space) or compressed hard prompts (Text-to-Text, discrete space). The paper models both categories mathematically: APE as discrete optimization over prompt space Ω to maximize task performance, and compression as minimizing KL divergence between original and compressed model output distributions.
claim c1 [qualitative]: APE achieves better or comparable performance to the instructions generated by human annotators on 24/24 Instruction Induction tasks and 17/21 curated Big-Bench tasks.
claim c2 [qualitative]: APE greatly outperforms the prior LLM baseline by a large margin in zero/few-shot learning and zero-shot CoT settings.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Experiments suggest choosing GA when several high-quality prompts already exist, otherwise DE for prompt optimization in EvoPrompt.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Prompting is a mainstream paradigm for adapting large language models to specific NLP tasks without modifying internal parameters.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Efficient Prompting Methods are defined as prompting language models to achieve comparable or even better performance with fewer human or computational resources.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Manual prompt design is sensitive to subtle differences in prompt content, suboptimal due to lack of clear optimization direction, and faces discrepancy between human interpretation and LLM interpretation of natural language.
claim c7 [negative]: Whether LLM is a good prompt optimizer is a question worth exploring.
claim c8 [qualitative]: This is the first survey to summarize LLM prompting methods from the point of efficiency.

Control-DAG: Constrained Decoding for Non-Autoregressive Directed Acyclic T5 using Weighted Finite State Automata (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, wfsa-intersection, lexical-constraints, vocabulary-constraints, length-constraints, viterbi-search, non-autoregressive
Control-DAG is a constrained decoding algorithm for Directed Acyclic T5 (DA-T5), a non-autoregressive model that generates Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) representing output hypotheses. The system converts DAGs to Weighted Finite State Automata (WFSAs), then intersects them with constraint FSAs encoding lexical requirements, valid vocabulary, and target length constraints. A pruned Depth-First Search Viterbi algorithm searches the constrained WFSA to find the highest-scoring output satisfying all constraints. Information flows from the input through DA-T5 (producing a DAG), through DAG-to-WFSA conversion and pruning, through FSA intersection operations, and finally through DFS-Viterbi path search to yield constrained output text.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Control-DAG achieves zero slot error rate (SER=0.0) and zero neologism rate (NEO=0.0) on the Schema Guided Dialogue dataset
claim c2 [qualitative]: Control-DAG on DA-T5-small achieves BLEURT 60.0 and BLEU 22.9 on SGD
claim c3 [quantitative]: Control-DAG achieves 1.7x speedup relative to auto-regressive constrained beam search on SGD
claim c4 [qualitative]: Control-DAG on DA-T5-small achieves BLEURT 46.8 and BLEU 19.0 on DART with zero exact occurrence error rate (EOR=0.0) and zero neologism rate (NEO=0.0)
claim c5 [quantitative]: Control-DAG achieves 1.4x speedup relative to auto-regressive constrained beam search on DART
claim c6 [qualitative]: DA-T5 without Control-DAG exhibits frequent out-of-vocabulary (OOV) errors (>20% neologism rate) and fails to generate specified entity names (up to 40% slot error rate)
claim c7 [qualitative]: Hard Lexical Constraints eliminate slot errors by constructing FSAs that enforce designated phrases must appear in the output
claim c8 [negative]: The paper leaves further training and model scaling to future work due to focus on decoding algorithms; further inference speed improvements are possible through C implementation of DAG-to-WFSA conversion and DFS-Viterbi but are not explored

Improving Dictionary Learning with Gated Sparse Autoencoders (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, sparse-coding, gated-encoder, activation-steering, dictionary-learning
Gated Sparse Autoencoder (Gated SAE) for unsupervised discovery of interpretable features in language model activations. The system separates feature detection from magnitude estimation via a gated encoder: a gating path (with Heaviside step) determines which features are active, while a magnitude path (ReLU) estimates their coefficients. Both paths share encoder projection directions but differ in bias and scaling parameters (weight tying reduces parameter overhead from 50% to ~25%). The decoder linearly reconstructs activations from active features. Training applies L1 sparsity penalty only to gating pre-activations, with an auxiliary decoder task to guide magnitude estimation without the sparsity penalty.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Gated SAEs require half as many firing features to achieve comparable reconstruction fidelity compared to baseline SAEs.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Gated SAEs achieve a Pareto improvement over baseline SAEs holding training compute fixed: they yield sparser decompositions at any desired level of reconstruction fidelity.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Gated SAEs solve shrinkage (systematic underestimation of feature activations) compared to baseline SAEs, with relative reconstruction bias approaching 1.0.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Gated SAE features are comparably interpretable to baseline SAE features according to expert human raters in a double-blind study.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Training a Gated SAE requires 50% more compute than training a matched-width baseline SAE due to dual decoder passes in the auxiliary loss.
claim c6 [qualitative]: The performance improvement of Gated SAEs over baseline SAEs is not solely attributable to addressing shrinkage; a significant portion comes from learning better encoder and decoder directions.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Freezing the decoder in the auxiliary task for training the magnitude path is beneficial; unfreezing it reduces performance.
claim c8 [negative]: We do not see evidence that Gated SAEs overfit when reconstructing activations despite increased expressivity via the Jump ReLU discontinuity.

Constrained Decoding for Secure Code Generation (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, beam-search, sampling, non-autoregressive, security-constraints, inference-time
The system augments pretrained Code LLMs with constrained decoding during inference to enforce security constraints without retraining. Two techniques are proposed: (1) Constrained Beam Sampling, which adapts beam search by adding stochastic sampling and constraint-checking at each decoding step to enforce security keywords/templates while generating code tokens left-to-right; (2) adaptation of MuCoLa, a gradient-based non-autoregressive method that formulates decoding as sampling from an energy-based model using Langevin Dynamics, with a custom energy function to enforce security constraints over entire output sequences. The system operates on Code LLM probability distributions p(y|x) without modifying model weights.
claim c1 [quantitative]: CodeGen + Prefix-tuning achieves 71.91% SVEN security rate using Nucleus Sampling, but only 29.14% secure-pass@ using the new metric that evaluates both security and correctness, representing an overestimation of the state-of-the-art defense.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Prefix tuning sacrifices functional correctness to generate secure code, decreasing pass@ by 6.94% compared to baseline.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Constrained Beam Sampling on CodeGen achieves 51.25% secure-pass@, outperforming prefix tuning with unconstrained decoding (36.3% secure-pass@ for SVEN).
claim c4 [qualitative]: Every model with constrained decoding shows better secure-pass@ than GPT-4 with Nucleus Sampling, tested over six open-source Code LLMs of sizes ranging from 2.7B to 34B.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Different decoding methods significantly affect the security of Code LLMs; Beam Sampling has higher secure-pass@ than Nucleus Sampling for six out of seven open-source models evaluated.
claim c6 [negative]: Constrained decoding does not specify correctness constraints and leaves this to future work; only security constraints based on common secure coding practices are enforced.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Constrained decoding is more effective than prefix tuning to improve the security of Code LLMs, without requiring a specialized training dataset.
claim c8 [qualitative]: CodeGuard+ benchmark contains 91 prompts across 34 CWEs in C/C++ and Python, with unit tests and security constraints, larger than the widely-used security prompt dataset from Pearce et al. which has 54 unique prompts covering 18 CWEs.

Zero-Shot Tokenizer Transfer (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: embedding-prediction, hypernetwork, tokenizer-transfer, zero-shot-adaptation, amortized-composition
A hypernetwork takes a target tokenizer as input (represented via token decompositions using an original tokenizer's vocabulary) and predicts embedding parameters for the target tokenizer's vocabulary. The hypernetwork is a Transformer-based model that learns to compose sequences of original embeddings into new embeddings suitable for any target tokenizer. During inference, predicted embeddings replace the original embeddings in the language model without modifying the LM's internal parameters.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Zero-shot transfer to language-specific tokenizers with XLM-R preserves accuracy to 1% on average on XNLI, losing 3% in the worst case and improving by 1% in the best case, while reducing sequence length by 14% on average
claim c2 [quantitative]: Zero-shot transfer of Mistral-7B to GPT2 tokenizer on natural language benchmarks (PiQA, HellaSwag, ARC, BoolQ, MMLU) achieves 73.0% average accuracy compared to 76.9% for the original model
claim c3 [quantitative]: Zero-shot transfer of Mistral-7B to StarCoder tokenizer achieves 23.2% average pass@1 on HumanEvalPack (code synthesis) compared to 28.1% for the original model; FOCUS baseline achieves 13.3%
claim c4 [quantitative]: Continued training for 800M tokens closes the gap between zero-shot hypernetwork transfer and original model performance on Mistral-7B, achieving 74.9% average accuracy on natural language benchmarks
claim c5 [quantitative]: Hypernetwork trained for base Mistral-7B can be applied to fine-tuned Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 without retraining, achieving 6.56 score on MT-Bench (single-model rating, 1-10 scale) compared to 7.33 for the original fine-tuned model
claim c6 [quantitative]: On completely unseen languages (Aymara, Guarani) not seen during hypernetwork training, XLM-R zero-shot transfer on AmericasNLI preserves accuracy to within 0-3% of original model while reducing sequence length by 36-39%
claim c7 [negative]: Prior heuristic methods (FVT, FOCUS, OFA) for zero-shot tokenizer transfer perform significantly worse than the hypernetwork, with FOCUS achieving only 55.6% average accuracy on Mistral-7B natural language tasks (vs. 73.0% for the hypernetwork) and 13.3% pass@1 on code (vs. 23.2%)
claim c8 [theoretical]: Hypernetwork amortizes over the tokenization function and learns robust embeddings through diverse tokenizer sampling; predicted embeddings are robust to different tokenization functions applied to the same vocabulary, unlike original model embeddings which degrade significantly under alternative tokenization functions

Towards Principled Evaluations of Sparse Autoencoders for Interpretability and Control (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, activation-intervention, sparse-feature-editing, interpretability-eval, causal-abstraction, circuit-analysis
The paper proposes a three-stage evaluation framework for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) applied to language model interpretability. First, supervised feature dictionaries are constructed from task-relevant attributes (e.g., subject, indirect object, position in IOI task) by averaging activations conditioned on attribute values. These serve as a reference benchmark. Second, unsupervised SAEs are trained on either task-specific (IOI) or full-distribution (OpenWebText) data to learn sparse decompositions of model activations. Third, both supervised and unsupervised dictionaries are evaluated along three axes: (1) sufficiency/necessity of reconstructions for task performance via activation intervention; (2) sparse controllability via greedy feature-edit optimization to achieve counterfactual outcomes; (3) interpretability via F1-scoring of feature activations against attribute-value properties and causal validation. The system operates at cross-sections of the IOI circuit in GPT-2 Small, reading and intervening on attention head outputs and queries.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Supervised feature dictionaries achieve excellent approximation, control, and interpretability of model computations on the IOI task
claim c2 [quantitative]: Supervised feature dictionaries approximate activations using as few as 3 active features per activation
claim c3 [quantitative]: Any of the IO, S, and Pos attributes can be edited precisely by replacing only 1 active feature with a different one using supervised dictionaries
claim c4 [negative]: Task-specific SAEs offer a significantly worse approximation compared to the supervised feature dictionaries on the sufficiency/necessity test; full-distribution SAEs fare notably worse than task-specific SAEs
claim c5 [negative]: SAEs are less successful than supervised features in controlling the model; task-specific SAEs can edit the Pos attribute and to a small extent the IO attribute, but require changing more features compared to supervised dictionaries
claim c6 [negative]: Full-distribution SAEs require a significantly larger number of features to achieve statistically significant control compared to task-specific SAEs, often 32 or more features
claim c7 [qualitative]: Feature occlusion occurs in SAE training where a causally relevant concept is robustly overshadowed by even slightly higher-magnitude ones in the learned features
claim c8 [qualitative]: Feature over-splitting occurs in SAE training where binary features split into many smaller, less interpretable features

KG-RAG: Bridging the Gap Between Knowledge and Creativity (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: knowledge-graph-retrieval, multi-hop-reasoning, rag-augmentation, chain-of-explorations, triple-extraction, vector-search-hybrid
KG-RAG is a three-stage pipeline for knowledge-intensive question answering over Large Language Model Agents (LMAs). Stage 1 (Storage): unstructured text is converted to Knowledge Graph triples via few-shot LLM prompting, with support for nested triple hypernodes to capture complex relational contexts; embeddings are computed and stored in a vector database. Stage 2 (Retrieval): Chain of Explorations (CoE) algorithm sequentially traverses the KG by combining vector similarity search, Cypher queries, and LLM-guided filtering to locate relevant multi-hop paths. Stage 3 (Answer Generation): the LLM synthesizes retrieved KG paths into a final answer via standard RAG prompting. Information flows from text → triples → KG database → CoE exploration → LLM synthesis.
claim c1 [quantitative]: KG-RAG achieved a hallucination rate of 15%, significantly lower than the 30% observed in Embedding-RAG.
claim c2 [quantitative]: KG-RAG achieved an Exact Match (EM) of 19% and an F1 Score of 25%.
claim c3 [quantitative]: KG-RAG demonstrated an accuracy of 32%, compared to 46% for Embedding-RAG.
claim c4 [qualitative]: KG-RAG integrates structured Knowledge Graphs with LLMs to significantly reduce the reliance on the latent knowledge of LLMs, thereby reducing hallucinated content and addressing information hallucinations, catastrophic forgetting, and limitations in processing long contexts.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Chain of Explorations (CoE) performs step-by-step exploration through KG nodes and relationships using a combination of vector similarity search, Cypher queries, and LLM-guided filtering and evaluation to retrieve relevant multi-hop paths.
claim c6 [qualitative]: The average CoE took between 4 and 5 steps over the KG until reaching the answer nodes.
claim c7 [negative]: KG-RAG results are lower compared to top performers on the CWQ leaderboard (human EM 63%, best machine EM 33.2%) and lower than Embedding-RAG, indicating potential areas for improvement in KG-RAG's ability to accurately parse and retrieve relevant information.
claim c8 [negative]: High costs associated with converting web snippets to KG triples using LLMs limited the ability to use all web snippets and perform tests on the entire development split of the CWQ dataset; performance of the LLM used for KG construction occasionally generated incorrect triples or missed triple extractions, propagating errors through CoE.

xRAG: Extreme Context Compression for Retrieval-augmented Generation with One Token (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: rag-compression, modality-fusion, retrieval-embedding-projection, context-compression, knowledge-intensive-qa
xRAG compresses retrieved documents into a single token by treating document embeddings from dense retrieval as features from a 'retrieval modality'. A trainable two-layer MLP projector (modality bridge) maps these frozen document embeddings into the LLM's representation space, replacing the full document text. The retriever and LLM remain frozen. Information flow: query → retrieve top-1 document → embed document with frozen retriever → project embedding through trainable MLP → inject as single token into LLM alongside query.
claim c1 [quantitative]: xRAG achieves an average improvement of over 10% across six knowledge-intensive tasks
claim c2 [quantitative]: xRAG reduces overall FLOPs by a factor of 3.53 compared to uncompressed RAG model
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Mistral-7b, xRAG achieves 16.2% relative improvement over non-retrieval baseline, compared to 19.4% for uncompressed RAG
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Mixtral-8x7b, xRAG achieves 9.7% relative improvement over non-retrieval baseline, exceeding uncompressed RAG's 8.0%
claim c5 [quantitative]: xRAG demonstrates higher resilience rate (82.3% on Mistral-7b, 84.9% on Mixtral-8x7b) compared to uncompressed RAG (75.2% average)
claim c6 [quantitative]: Document compression reduces token count from 175.1 to 1 token per document
claim c7 [negative]: xRAG lags behind uncompressed RAG in tasks requiring reasoning over documents, such as HotpotQA and FactKG
claim c8 [qualitative]: The projector MLP is the only trainable component, accounting for 0.46% of total parameters in Mistral-7b and 0.07% in Mixtral-8x7b

Not All Language Model Features Are One-Dimensionally Linear (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, multi-dimensional-features, circular-representations, activation-patching, mechanistic-interpretability, modular-arithmetic
This paper investigates multi-dimensional feature representations in language models (GPT-2, Mistral 7B, Llama 3 8B) using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to decompose hidden states. Information flows through: (1) SAE dictionary learning to over-complete basis representations, (2) cosine-similarity clustering of dictionary elements to identify candidate multi-dimensional features, (3) PCA projection and statistical tests (separability index, ε-mixture index) to verify irreducibility, and (4) activation patching interventions on discovered circular subspaces to establish causal role in modular arithmetic tasks. The system reads activation space and outputs identified multi-dimensional feature manifolds (circles, cones) without modifying model parameters.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Language models contain irreducible multi-dimensional features that cannot be decomposed into independent or non-co-occurring lower-dimensional features, contrary to the one-dimensional linear representation hypothesis.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Sparse autoencoders with cosine-similarity clustering can automatically discover interpretable multi-dimensional circular features representing days of the week, months of the year, and years in GPT-2 and Mistral 7B.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Mistral 7B achieves 31/49 accuracy on weekdays modular arithmetic task and 125/144 on months task.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Llama 3 8B achieves 29/49 accuracy on weekdays task and 143/144 on months task.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Circular subspace intervention on Mistral 7B achieves nearly the same causal effect as patching the entire layer, with average logit difference approaching layer-patching baselines.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Off-distribution interventions sweeping within circular representations show that models treat the circle as a multi-dimensional representation with task variable encoded in the angle.
claim c7 [qualitative]: SAE-discovered circular features in layer 8 Mistral 7B are more robust to layer shifts than PCA-trained circular probes, suggesting SAE findings capture more fundamental model features.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Mistral 7B represents continuous time by mapping intermediate weekday descriptors (very early/very late, morning/evening) to expected positions along the circular day-of-week representation.

Grammar-Aligned Decoding (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, grammar-alignment, expected-future-grammaticality, iterative-refinement, distribution-matching
ASAp is a decoding algorithm that operates on top of existing grammar-constrained decoding (GCD) systems. It iteratively samples from an LLM while enforcing grammar constraints. The key mechanism is maintaining an overapproximation of expected future grammaticality (EFG)—the probability that a continuation of a prefix will land in the grammar—by tracking all previously sampled sequences. After each sample is generated, the algorithm backpropagates through the prefix tree to refine EFG estimates for all prefixes, using observed token likelihoods to narrow the overapproximation. Sampling at each step uses the refined EFG estimates to reweight the LLM's token probabilities, balancing grammar adherence with distribution fidelity.
claim c1 [qualitative]: existing grammar-constrained decoding (GCD) techniques can distort the LLM's distribution, leading to outputs that are grammatical but appear with likelihoods that are not proportional to the ones given by the LLM
claim c2 [qualitative]: ASAp guarantees the output to be grammatical while provably producing outputs that match the conditional probability of the LLM's distribution conditioned on the given grammar constraint
claim c3 [theoretical]: the overapproximation ̂EFG_t is an upper bound on the expected future grammaticality EFG (Theorem 1)
claim c4 [theoretical]: as the number of iterations approaches infinity, the overapproximation ̂EFG_t converges to the exact expected future grammaticality EFG, and thus ASAp converges to exact samples from the constrained LLM distribution (Theorem 2)
claim c5 [quantitative]: on SLIA benchmarks, sum of least square difference between expectations computed by ASAp and target expectations is 0.087, compared to GCD's 0.169
claim c6 [quantitative]: on INV-BV4 benchmarks, sum of least square difference between expectations computed by ASAp and target expectations is 0.149, compared to GCD's 0.392
claim c7 [qualitative]: ASAp produces correct samples 38% more often than GCD (geomean) on INV-BV benchmarks; however on SLIA benchmarks that both tools can solve, ASAp produces correct samples 73% less often than GCD (geomean)
claim c8 [negative]: the key limitation of this work is the current slow convergence of the ASAp algorithm; on some benchmarks, even after 2,000 iterations the KL divergence barely improves

Scaling and evaluating sparse autoencoders (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, sparse-coding, topk-activation, interpretability, feature-extraction, scaling-laws
Sparse autoencoders trained on language model residual stream activations. Encoder maps activations to latent codes via a TopK activation function that preserves only the k largest latent values. Decoder reconstructs the original activation from sparse latent codes. The system uses auxiliary losses to prevent dead latents during training and is evaluated on reconstruction error, downstream language modeling loss, and interpretability metrics.
claim c1 [quantitative]: A 16 million latent autoencoder trained on GPT-4 activations achieves downstream language modeling loss corresponding to 10% of the pretraining compute of GPT-4.
claim c2 [qualitative]: TopK autoencoders empirically outperform baseline ReLU autoencoders on the sparsity-reconstruction frontier, with this gap increasing with scale.
claim c3 [quantitative]: In the largest 16 million latent autoencoder, only 7% of latents are dead (have not activated in 10 million tokens), compared to up to 90% dead latents without mitigation.
claim c4 [qualitative]: The number of tokens to convergence increases approximately as D^1.4 for GPT-2 small and D^1.5 for GPT-4, where D is the number of latents.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Joint scaling law fit on GPT-4 autoencoders: MSE follows power law with coefficients α_d ≈ -0.65, α_k ≈ -0.28, β_d ≈ -0.23, β_k ≈ -0.05, γ_d ≈ 2.0, γ_k ≈ 0.5, including irreducible loss term.
claim c6 [qualitative]: TopK activation function removes the need for L1 penalty, enables setting L0 directly instead of tuning coefficient, and empirically increases monosemanticity of random activating examples by clamping small activations to zero.
claim c7 [negative]: TopK forces every token to use exactly k latents, which is likely suboptimal; ideally sparsity should be constrained per-token rather than fixed.
claim c8 [negative]: A large fraction of random activations of features found, especially in GPT-4, are not yet adequately monosemantic; improved techniques and greater scale are needed to overcome this.

Transcoders Find Interpretable LLM Feature Circuits (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: sparse-moe, circuit-analysis, feature-attribution, mlp-approximation, input-invariant-factorization
Transcoders are wide, sparsely-activating MLP layers trained to faithfully approximate a model's original dense MLP sublayer output. They use an encoder-decoder architecture (Eqs. 3-4) where sparse feature activations are computed via dot-product of input with encoder vectors, then scaled decoder vectors are summed to reconstruct MLP output. The key innovation is enabling weights-based circuit analysis through MLPs by factorizing feature-to-feature attributions into input-invariant (decoder·encoder dot product) and input-dependent (feature activation) terms, allowing general input-output behavior analysis impossible with SAEs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Transcoders enable input-invariant feature-level circuit analysis through MLP sublayers, cleanly factorizing circuits into input-invariant and input-dependent terms, which SAEs cannot achieve.
claim c2 [quantitative]: On blind interpretability evaluation of 50 random features each from Pythia-410M layer 15 transcoder and MLP-in SAE, transcoders showed 41 interpretable features versus 38 for SAE.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Transcoders achieve equal or better sparsity-accuracy Pareto frontier than SAEs across GPT2-small, Pythia-410M, and Pythia-1.4B, with the gap widening on larger models.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Transcoders successfully reverse-engineer unknown circuits, deriving that layer 8 feature 355 in GPT2-small fires on semicolons in parenthetical citations via input-invariant de-embedding and circuit-finding algorithm without viewing token text.
claim c5 [quantitative]: On the GPT2-small greater-than circuit task, using only the top 23 transcoder features (versus many more neurons) recovers most of the original model's performance on the probability difference metric.
claim c6 [negative]: The circuit analysis method does not engage with how attention patterns are computed and treats them as fixed; extending transcoders to understand attention softmax computation is noted as future work.
claim c7 [negative]: Transcoders, like SAEs, are approximations to the underlying model and the resulting error may lose key information; the paper leaves detailed comparison of reconstruction errors to future work.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Nine blind case studies demonstrate transcoders can infer model behavior on unknown tasks using input-invariant information (de-embeddings, input-invariant feature connections) without viewing activation examples.

Hopping Too Late: Exploring the Limitations of Large Language Models on Multi-Hop Queries (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, multi-hop, activation-probing, patchscopes, back-patching, mechanistic-interpretability
This is a mechanistic interpretability study of transformer-based LLMs answering multi-hop queries. The system analyzes internal computation without modifying the model: it traces where bridge entities are resolved (early layers at intermediate token positions) and where final answers are resolved (later layers at final token position), verifies information propagation between these positions via attention, and introduces back-patching—a method that extracts hidden representations from later layers and reinjects them at earlier positions to test whether later layers lack necessary knowledge for second-hop resolution.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The bridge entity is resolved in the early layers of the model at the position of the last token of the first hop.
claim c2 [qualitative]: The second hop is typically resolved in the last token of the prompt only in layers after the resolution of the first hop.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The bridge entity is successfully decoded from the first-hop position across models in 41%-78% of correctly answered cases using Patchscopes.
claim c4 [qualitative]: The target entity is decoded from the final token position in 71%-86% of cases where the model predicted correctly.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Critical information propagates from the first-hop position to the final token position in the majority of cases (detected via attention knockout, projection, or Patchscopes).
claim c6 [quantitative]: Back-patching correctly generates the answer for up to 66% of previously incorrect cases when an optimal source-target layer pair is chosen.
claim c7 [quantitative]: Back-patching maintains 100% correctness on cases the model already answered correctly across all models and token positions.
claim c8 [negative]: The bridge entity resolves later in incorrect cases than in correct cases, suggesting delayed first-hop resolution contributes to multi-hop query failure.

STOC-TOT: Stochastic Tree-of-Thought with Constrained Decoding for Complex Reasoning in Multi-Hop Question Answering (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: tree-of-thought, constrained-decoding, prompting, reasoning-paths, vocabulary-grounding
StoC-ToT is a prompting framework for multi-hop question answering that constructs a tree of reasoning paths by prompting an LLM to generate sub-questions at each reasoning level, answer them using constrained decoding from a vocabulary bank built from evidence, estimate validity probabilities for each path across question/reasoning/answer levels, and aggregate path probabilities to select the best answer. For closed-source LLMs, constraints are enforced via prompting; for open-source LLMs, a logit processor sets non-vocabulary tokens to negative infinity at decode time.
claim c1 [quantitative]: StoC-ToT with GPT-4 improves Exact Match accuracy by 7% and F1 score by 7.8 points over original tree-of-thought prompting on the HotpotQA dataset
claim c2 [quantitative]: On HotpotQA, StoC-ToT attains an on-average increase in performance of over 6% compared with vanilla prompting on GPT models, and the improvement goes up to 11% when constrained decoding is implemented
claim c3 [quantitative]: On the MuSiQue dataset with GPT-4, StoC-ToT shows an 11.5% EM improvement (from 31.50 to 42.0) over zero-shot vanilla prompting
claim c4 [quantitative]: StoC-ToT surpasses the original Tree-of-Thought prompting by 7% with the GPT4 model on both tested datasets
claim c5 [qualitative]: Constrained decoding, which makes the model much more grounded to evidence when answering the question, effectively addresses hallucination issues and improves the overall performance of the framework
claim c6 [qualitative]: StoC-ToT performs better at both Bridge Questions and Sequential Questions, suggesting that it can avoid reasoning dead-ends and is better at forming intermediate reasoning lines than Chain-of-Thought and Tree-of-Thought
claim c7 [qualitative]: StoC-ToT performs better on both reasoning types, especially on questions containing parallel reasoning, suggesting that its stochastic way of forming the tree is very effective when solving questions containing multiple reasoning paths
claim c8 [negative]: The framework relies on initiating multiple model instances and requires multiple prompts per round, imposing heavy time costs even after implementing the paraphrase module

Multi-Step Reasoning with Large Language Models, a Survey (2024)
seam: text; tags: chain-of-thought, prompt-engineering, in-context-learning, step-evaluation, tool-based-execution, reinforcement-learning, tree-search
This is a survey paper reviewing multi-step reasoning approaches with large language models. It does not propose a single system but rather categorizes existing approaches through a taxonomy of three stages: (1) step generation via prompts (hand-written, external knowledge, or model-generated), (2) step evaluation (self-assessment, tool-based, or external model validation), and (3) control of reasoning (greedy, ensemble, tree-search, or reinforcement learning). Information flows from prompt engineering to LLM inference, with optional external tools and verifiers providing feedback loops.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Chain-of-thought improved accuracy from 15.6% to 46.9% on GSM8K
claim c2 [quantitative]: Zero-Shot CoT achieved 70% accuracy on GSM8K with PaLM
claim c3 [qualitative]: Multi-step reasoning approaches have progressed beyond math word problems and can now successfully solve challenges in logic, combinatorial games, and robotics, sometimes by first generating code that is then executed by external tools
claim c4 [qualitative]: Many studies in multi-step methods use reinforcement learning for finetuning, external optimization loops, in-context reinforcement learning, and self-reflection
claim c5 [qualitative]: In-context learning occurs at inference time in language models beyond hundreds of billions of parameters, enabling few-shot learning without parameter updates
claim c6 [quantitative]: Scratchpad approach on polynomial evaluation improved from 32% to 51% accuracy with supervised learning of intermediate steps
claim c7 [qualitative]: The taxonomy identifies three stages of multi-step reasoning: step generation, step evaluation, and control of reasoning steps, with each stage having multiple sub-approaches
claim c8 [negative]: Traditional transformer-based LLMs designed for next-token prediction do not perform well on basic reasoning benchmarks without explicit multi-step prompting

Prover-Verifier Games improve legibility of LLM outputs (2024)
seam: text; tags: prover-verifier-game, adversarial-training, chain-of-thought, verifier-robustness, legibility-optimization, role-conditioning, rl-with-verifier-reward
A game-theoretic training system that iteratively optimizes a verifier LM and role-conditioned prover LM to improve legibility of chain-of-thought solutions. The verifier (small LM) learns to predict solution correctness via supervised learning on balanced correct/incorrect samples. The prover (larger LM) is trained via RL with dual roles: helpful (optimized for correctness + verifier acceptance) and sneaky (optimized for incorrectness + verifier acceptance). Solutions flow as text; the verifier score feeds back as a reward signal to the prover. Across rounds, the data mixture feeding the verifier changes, but model weights carry over within each training phase.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Optimizing chain-of-thought solutions only for answer correctness can make them less legible.
claim c2 [qualitative]: When trained solely for correctness, human contractor accuracy at evaluating solution validity (45-second time limit) decreases from initialization to 7k RL steps.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The helpful prover's pass rate improves over checkability training rounds and human judges evaluate helpful solutions correctly more often at higher rounds.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Verifiers show increasing robustness across five rounds of checkability training, measured by steps required until sneaky-only adversaries find exploits.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Human contractor accuracy when evaluating sneaky prover solutions decreases over checkability training rounds, indicating flaws become more subtle.
claim c6 [negative]: Checkability training becomes unstable when using a verifier 0.01× the compute of the prover, producing weak provers with poor legibility.
claim c7 [negative]: Using a verifier that matches the size of the prover (1.0× compute) prevents the verifier from becoming more robust in subsequent rounds and produces provers with strong performance but poor legibility.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Legibility training against small verifiers transfers to legibility to humans: sneaky solutions harder to detect by small verifiers are also harder to detect by humans, and helpful solutions more legible to small verifiers are more legible to humans.

Internal Consistency and Self-Feedback in Large Language Models: A Survey (2024)
seam: none; tags: survey, self-feedback, internal-consistency, self-evaluation, self-update, reasoning-elevation, hallucination-alleviation
This is a survey paper that synthesizes literature on self-feedback mechanisms in large language models. It proposes a unified theoretical framework called Self-Feedback, consisting of two modules: Self-Evaluation (which captures internal consistency signals from model expressions at response, decoding, or latent layers through sampling methodologies) and Self-Update (which leverages those signals to enhance either the model's response or the model parameters themselves). The paper frames internal consistency as sampling-based consistency across different model layers and classifies methods by task and line of work rather than by architectural innovation. No novel model architecture is introduced; instead, the paper provides a meta-level taxonomy of existing approaches.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Large language models often exhibit deficient reasoning or generate hallucinations, and internal consistency—the consistency in expressions among LLMs' latent, decoding, or response layers based on sampling methodologies—is central to these core challenges.
claim c2 [quantitative]: On the reasoning benchmark GSM8K, the Self-Consistency method which uses majority voting increased answer accuracy by about 17.9%.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The Self-Contradict strategy, which generates different samples to identify self-contradictory content, showed that even GPT-4 was able to induce self-contradictions at rates of 15.7%.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Models exhibit low consistency in refusing 'I Don't Know' (IDK) questions, indicating they lack Self-Knowledge—the ability to express what they know and do not know.
claim c5 [qualitative]: The paper proposes the 'Hourglass Evolution of Internal Consistency' hypothesis: model answer consistency follows an hourglass pattern starting from lower latent layers (random judgments), through intermediate decoding layers (high certainty), to higher response layers (greater variability), suggesting that output tokens can interfere with and collapse latent reasoning.
claim c6 [theoretical]: The Self-Feedback framework formalizes internal consistency mining as a two-stage process: Self-Evaluate (obtaining feedback signal from sampling set) followed by Self-Update (using feedback to either update expressions or model parameters), with extensions to updating external models or students models.
claim c7 [qualitative]: The paper proposes 'Consistency Is (Almost) Correctness' hypothesis, linking internal consistency improvements to reasoning elevation and hallucination alleviation across response, decoding, and latent layers.
claim c8 [qualitative]: The paper proposes 'The Paradox of Latent and Explicit Reasoning': while models possess latent reasoning abilities, these are weak, and enhancing intermediate entity signals does not significantly improve model responses, suggesting current LLM architectures struggle with robust latent reasoning.

Structure-aware Domain Knowledge Injection for Large Language Models (2024)
seam: text; tags: knowledge-injection, continual-pretraining, taxonomy-extraction, structured-conditioning, qa-synthesis, multi-hop-reasoning
StructTuning is a two-stage domain knowledge injection system for LLMs. In Stage 1 (SCPT), a knowledge taxonomy is automatically extracted from domain corpora using LLaMA3-70B for chunk summarization and a specialized 7B model for hierarchy identification. Training chunks are then prepended with their corresponding knowledge paths (rendered as mindmaps via templates) and used for conditioned language modeling, with models also trained to reconstruct the full hierarchy. In Stage 2 (SSFT), knowledge-intensive and multi-hop QA pairs are synthesized via LLaMA3-70B using random walks over the taxonomy; answers are enriched with explicit knowledge paths and chain-of-thought prompts to teach models to apply learned structure to solve problems.
claim c1 [quantitative]: achieves 100% of traditional knowledge injection performance with only 5% of training corpus
claim c2 [quantitative]: using merely 76M tokens (0.3%) achieves over 50% performance improvement against MMedLM (which uses 25.5B tokens)
claim c3 [quantitative]: with 1.2B tokens (5% of corpus) achieves nearly 100% of MMedLM's improvement (4.95 percentage points v.s. 4.96)
claim c4 [quantitative]: on LongBench closed-book QA, SCPT+SSFT achieves 31.5% recall versus 24.2% for CPT+SFT baseline
claim c5 [quantitative]: generalizes across model architectures: InternLM2-7B (+4.46%), Llama2-7B (+8.78%), Llama2-13B (+6.17%) using 76M tokens
claim c6 [qualitative]: SSFT with additional 8K structure-aware QA pairs demonstrates cross-lingual knowledge transfer, where knowledge injected in one language helps solve problems in other languages
claim c7 [negative]: RAG strategy does not bring significant advantages to MMedBench evaluation (38.12% on English), underperforming SCPT+SSFT (49.96%), due to gap between pre-training corpus (textbook knowledge) and evaluated QA samples (practical diagnosis records)
claim c8 [qualitative]: two-stage strategy introduces added computational complexity through taxonomy extraction, data reorganization in SCPT phase, and optional QA synthesis in SSFT phase

Generative Sentiment Analysis via Latent Category Distribution and Constrained Decoding (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: constrained-decoding, trie-based-vocabulary-pruning, latent-distribution, vae-reconstruction, category-aware-attention, seq2seq-generation
A T5-based generative model for aspect-category-opinion-sentiment quadruple extraction. The system comprises three main components: (1) a Latent Category Distribution (LCD) module using a variational autoencoder to learn category-text relationship intensity from Category Bag-of-Words features; (2) a T5 encoder-decoder generative framework with category-text attention, where the LCD representation is fused with encoder outputs via attention-weighted combination; (3) a constrained decoding strategy using trie-based prefix trees to restrict the vocabulary search space to valid categories, subcategories, sentiment polarities, aspect/opinion text spans, and structural indicators. Information flow: input text → T5 encoder + LCD attention → fused context vector → T5 decoder with trie-constrained vocabulary selection → linearized quadruple sequence.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On the Restaurant-ACOS dataset, compared to the baseline model GAS, the proposed model shows an increase of 0.038 in Recall, and an increase of 0.017 in F1
claim c2 [quantitative]: On the Laptop-ACOS dataset, the proposed model exhibits an increase of 0.031 in Precision, 0.035 in Recall, and 0.033 in F1 compared to GAS
claim c3 [quantitative]: On the Restaurant-ACOS dataset, the proposed model achieves an F1 score of 0.661 on the EA&EO subset (explicit aspects and opinions), significantly outperforming scores of 0.544, 0.442, and 0.413 on subsets containing implicit elements
claim c4 [qualitative]: The LCD module effectively captures latent category distribution information for samples with complex category inclusion and overlap relationships
claim c5 [quantitative]: On the Restaurant-ACOS dataset, removal of LCD alone resulted in an F1 decrease of 0.009, while excluding CD alone led to an F1 decrease of 0.012, and simultaneous removal of both components resulted in an F1 decrease of 0.014
claim c6 [negative]: Despite a notable improvement in performance compared to the baseline models, the overall performance remains modest, attributed to the inherent complexity of the task necessitating extraction and precise matching of four distinct types of sentiment elements, and special expressions in language such as implicit elements
claim c7 [qualitative]: Generative models outperform extractive models primarily because treating the extraction task as an end-to-end sequence generation problem reduces error propagation and uniformly modeling each quadruple element facilitates more effective utilization of semantic knowledge in pretrained models
claim c8 [qualitative]: The constrained decoding strategy with trie-based prefix trees effectively regularizes generation by preventing invalid categories, misaligned subcategories, invalid sentiment polarities, and incomplete sequence structures

Let Me Speak Freely? A Study on the Impact of Format Restrictions on Performance of Large Language Models (2024)
seam: text; tags: structured-generation, constrained-decoding, format-restricting-instructions, prompt-sensitivity, reasoning-vs-classification
This is an empirical study paper with no novel system architecture. The paper evaluates existing LLMs (GPT-3.5-turbo, Claude-3-Haiku, Gemini-1.5-Flash, LLaMA-3-8B, Gemma-2-9B) under three progressively constrained generation regimes: constrained decoding (JSON-mode), format-restricting instructions (FRI), and NL-to-Format conversion. Performance is measured across reasoning and classification tasks with outputs compared using both regex and LLM-based answer extraction.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Significant decline in LLMs' reasoning abilities under format restrictions
claim c2 [qualitative]: Stricter format constraints generally lead to greater performance degradation in reasoning tasks
claim c3 [quantitative]: JSON-mode performs significantly worse than FRI on Last Letter task for GPT-3.5-Turbo, with 100% of responses placing the answer key before the reason key
claim c4 [qualitative]: JSON-mode improves classification task performance by constraining possible answers and reducing errors in answer selection
claim c5 [qualitative]: Removing schema restriction from format-restricting prompts significantly improves average scores on GSM8K across Claude-3-Haiku, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct with lower standard deviations
claim c6 [negative]: Parsing errors are not the primary cause of performance differences between formats despite near-zero parsing failure rates in some models (e.g., Gemini-1.5-Flash and GPT-3.5-Turbo exhibit near zero parsing failures but still show substantial performance gaps)
claim c7 [qualitative]: NL-to-Format shows nearly identical performance to unrestricted Natural Language responses across most models, with only minor degradation in some cases
claim c8 [quantitative]: GPT-4-turbo as an LLM-based answer parser achieves 97.7% average alignment with human-parsed answers across Last Letter, Shuffle Object, and GSM8K tasks

Vector Symbolic Open Source Information Discovery (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vsa, binary-vectors, semantic-retrieval, metadata-fusion, compression, binding-bundling
A hybrid system combining transformer-based LLM embeddings with Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSA). Text and metadata from tweets are first encoded into real-valued semantic vectors via SentenceTransformers (768-dim), then projected into high-dimensional binary VSA space (1k–10k bits) via random projection. Metadata attributes (location, sentiment, timestamp, language) are separately encoded lexically or via ML classifiers, then combined using VSA binding (XOR) and bundling (majority-sum) operations to form compound tweet vectors. Matching is performed in VSA space using Hamming distance, with FAISS indexing for efficiency. A minimal metadata model bridges semantic and structured representations.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The approach combines transformer models for semantic matching with the compactness and representational structure of VSA, enabling data discovery in DDIL (denied, degraded, intermittent, low bandwidth) settings.
claim c2 [qualitative]: VSA binary vectors are typically 1-10k bits, suitable in DDIL settings, compared to transformer embeddings which are significantly larger (e.g., 768 real numbers for BERT).
claim c3 [qualitative]: Projecting LLM-derived semantic vectors (768 real numbers) to 1k bit VSA vectors results in compression.
claim c4 [qualitative]: For semantic matching (Russia-Ukraine, Stocks, Warthunder topics), multiple-vector 1k-bit representation achieved accuracy of 1.0, 0.993, and 0.997 respectively; single-vector 10k-bit achieved 1.0, 0.973, and 0.983.
claim c5 [quantitative]: Location and language lexical matching achieved 100% recall for both 1k and 10k bit representations across all possible values.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Sentiment classification accuracy was 95% for multiple-vector 1k-bit but dropped to 80% for single-vector 1k-bit; 10k-bit achieved 97% for both representations.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Timestamp ordering (level hypervectors method) achieved 99% accuracy for multiple-vector but degraded to close to random for single-vector 1k-bit; using basis vectors method, single-vector achieved 90% accuracy.
claim c8 [qualitative]: A minimal metadata model supporting four dimensions (temporal, spatial, semantic, and sentiment) can be captured in 4k bits (small enough to fit in a minimum-size IPv4 datagram) with good matching accuracy across all attributes.

A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, feature-splitting, feature-absorption, interpretability, hierarchical-features, sparsity-loss
The paper studies Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) applied to large language model activations. SAEs are neural networks with an encoder, decoder, and sparsity-inducing loss that aim to decompose dense LLM activations into interpretable latent features. The paper does not propose a new architecture but rather analyzes a failure mode in existing SAE designs: when underlying features form hierarchies, SAEs learn to 'absorb' parent features into child features to increase sparsity, causing seemingly monosemantic latents to fail to activate on cases where they should.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Sparse Autoencoders fail to robustly decompose hierarchical features; seemingly monosemantic features fail to fire where they should and instead get absorbed into their children features.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Feature absorption is caused by optimizing for sparsity in SAEs whenever the underlying features form a hierarchy.
claim c3 [qualitative]: SAE latents underperform linear regression probes on first-letter identification across Gemma Scope SAEs, with no SAE matching LR probe performance.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Feature absorption rate increases with higher sparsity (L0) and higher SAE width; lower L0 pushes the SAE to absorb dense features like spelling information.
claim c5 [negative]: Varying SAE sizes or sparsity is insufficient to solve feature absorption.
claim c6 [negative]: The absorption metric cannot capture absorption past layer 17 in Gemma 2 2B because ablation experiments lose causal effect once attention has moved starting letter information from source token to final token position.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Feature absorption is validated empirically on hundreds of LLM SAEs and occurs across all SAE architectures tested (L1 loss SAEs and TopK SAEs).
claim c8 [theoretical]: A formal proof is provided that for hierarchical features (α ⊆ β), feature absorption (α-absorption) decreases SAE sparsity loss while maintaining perfect reconstruction.

Enhancing Text-to-SQL Capabilities of Large Language Models via Domain Database Knowledge Injection (2024)
seam: text; tags: knowledge-injection, semantic-fusion, schema-linking, co-occurrence-enhancement, two-stage-training
A two-stage fine-tuning approach for LLMs in Text-to-SQL tasks. Stage 1 (upstream): pre-trains the model on domain-specific database knowledge through three learning objectives—semantic enhancement of column names via cell values, semantic enhancement of table names, and schema knowledge injection to boost table-column name co-occurrence frequency. Stage 2 (downstream): standard fine-tuning on Text-to-SQL task data. Knowledge is injected as generated synthetic training data constructed from database schemas and cell values using question-answer templates, formatted as text generation tasks. Information flows from database (schema + sampled cell values) → template-filled synthetic tasks → upstream training → frozen model weights → downstream fine-tuning → SQL generation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: deepseek-coder-6.7b shows EX metric improvement of 3.1% and 77.8% EX baseline increasing to 80.9% after knowledge injection
claim c2 [quantitative]: CodeLlama-13b-Instruct shows EM metric improvement of 3.4% from 71.1% to 73.5% with knowledge enhancement
claim c3 [qualitative]: Baichuan2-7B-Chat general model shows EX improvement of 2.8% and EM improvement of 2.5% with knowledge injection
claim c4 [quantitative]: Column name generation accuracy improved by 2% to 4% across models after knowledge injection; deepseek-coder-6.7b accuracy increased from 0.73 to 0.77
claim c5 [qualitative]: Knowledge-injected models show improved robustness on Spider-Syn dataset (questions with synonym-substituted column/table names), with both EX and EM metrics higher than baseline, indicating more effective semantic understanding of column and table names
claim c6 [qualitative]: Performance on unseen databases (Spider test set) remained similar to baseline, indicating knowledge injection enhances understanding of learned domain-specific databases without degrading generalization to unknown databases
claim c7 [negative]: Mixing domain knowledge injection data with Text-to-SQL training data and training together caused performance degradation; CodeLlama-7b EX dropped from 76.6% to 73.8%, attributed to misalignment between upstream and downstream training objectives and data imbalance
claim c8 [negative]: Method has limited effectiveness in improving instruction comprehension and complex SQL query generation quality; even with knowledge injection, model comprehension of complex instructions remains unsatisfactory

Edit-Constrained Decoding for Sentence Simplification (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, edit-operations, beam-search, lexical-constraints, sentence-simplification, sibling-based-satisfaction
The system extends NeuroLogic Decoding to perform edit-constrained decoding for sentence simplification. It expands beam search with three types of edit-based constraints (insertion, deletion, substitution) that replicate simplification operations. Each constraint type has strict satisfaction conditions evaluated during beam search: insertion rewards output tokens matching constraint targets; deletion requires the unwanted token to appear as a sibling node in the beam lattice; substitution requires both source and target tokens to appear as siblings. Constraint satisfaction is scored and accumulated across timesteps, biasing beam search toward hypotheses that satisfy more edit operations with higher scores.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The proposed method largely outperforms previous studies using lexically constrained decoding on oracle constraints.
claim c2 [qualitative]: The proposed method achieved significantly higher constraint satisfaction rates compared to Zetsu et al. (2022) on oracle constraints.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The stricter satisfaction conditions for deletion and substitution operations avoid sub-optimal generation that satisfies only easy constraints at early timesteps.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Manual error analysis on ASSET shows the proposed method decreased errors in failed named entity generation, dropped information in complex sentences, and altered meaning/focus compared to Zetsu et al. (2022).
claim c5 [qualitative]: With predicted constraints, the proposed method consistently outperformed baselines on SARI across all three corpora, while previous methods (Agrawal et al. 2021, Kajiwara 2019) showed instability.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Constraint prediction precision on substitution operations was low, with room for improvement particularly in deletion and substitution operations.
