claim c3 [quantitative]: On multiplication tasks, NS LLM achieves 95.6% accuracy versus Standard LLM 1.1%, CoT 25.3%, LoRA 4.5%
claim c4 [quantitative]: Encoder achieves minimum RMSE loss at layer 17 of LLaMA 3.1 8B; encoding error rate for individual digits is under 2% at this layer
claim c5 [qualitative]: Out-of-distribution tasks (Addition and Integer Division, unseen during training) show NS LLM achieving 98.2% and 97.4% accuracy respectively, compared to LoRA's 46.5% and 72.0%, demonstrating generalization by gating interventions on problem-type similarity
claim c6 [qualitative]: Computational overhead of neurosymbolic processing per forward pass is O(D²) in VSA dimension D, dominated by standard KV-cached transformer cost of O(n²) (sequence length n), and space overhead is O(D²), negligible relative to full LLM memory
claim c7 [negative]: On more complex tasks such as LCM (87.3% score) and square modulo (58.9% score), NS LLM performance is lower, likely due to encoding error accumulation and the complexity of forward-pass algorithms requiring multi-hop reasoning that a single decoder intervention cannot fully capture
claim c8 [qualitative]: CoT baseline exhibits higher loss (0.874–16.606 across tasks) and inconsistent improvements; on bitwise XOR CoT degrades from 6.7% (Standard) to 1.1%, attributed to error accumulation in multi-step intermediate reasoning steps such as incorrect bitstring conversion

Token Assorted: Mixing Latent and Text Tokens for Improved Language Model Reasoning (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vq-vae-compression, hybrid-token-mixing, cot-abstraction, discrete-latent, vocabulary-extension, randomized-replacement
A two-stage hybrid reasoning system that compresses chain-of-thought (CoT) traces using discrete latent tokens. Stage 1: A VQ-VAE (vector-quantized variational autoencoder) with a 50M-parameter encoder-decoder pair learns to map text token sequences into discrete latent codes organized in a fixed codebook. Stage 2: An LLM is fine-tuned on modified training data where the leftmost N CoT tokens are replaced with their latent abstractions (demarcated by special tokens), while later tokens remain as raw text. During training, N is randomly sampled per example to expose the model to varying degrees of latent-text mixing; at inference, the LLM generates latent tokens directly without invoking the VQ-VAE. The approach treats latent tokens as vocabulary extensions, requiring no external module at test time.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On Math benchmark with Llama-3.2-1B, latent approach achieves +4.2 percentage points over the second-best baseline (CoT).
claim c2 [quantitative]: On GSM8K benchmark with Llama-3.2-3B, latent approach achieves +4.1 percentage points over CoT.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Fresh-Gaokao-Math-2023 benchmark with Llama-3.1-8B, latent approach achieves +13.3 percentage points over CoT.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Across all mathematical reasoning benchmarks tested (Math, GSM8K, Gaokao-Math-2023, DeepMind-Math, College-Math, OlympiaBench-Math, TheoremQA), latent approach achieves an average of 17% reduction in reasoning trace length compared to CoT while improving accuracy.
claim c5 [quantitative]: On Keys-Finding Maze synthetic benchmark with T5 trained from scratch, latent approach achieves 62.8% on 1-Feasible-10 metric, a +19.8 percentage point improvement over CoT (43%).
claim c6 [quantitative]: On ProsQA logical reasoning benchmark with GPT-2 trained from scratch, latent approach achieves 96.2% accuracy, a +18.7 percentage point improvement over CoT (77.5%).
claim c7 [qualitative]: Randomized replacement strategy (varying the number of text tokens replaced per sample) consistently outperforms curriculum learning and all-replace strategies, enabling fast single-stage adaptation to unseen latent tokens without multi-epoch curriculum training.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Attention weight analysis shows that models trained with latent-text mixing focus more attention on numerically significant tokens and tokens representing mathematical operations (e.g., 'earns', 'cost'), compared to baseline CoT models, suggesting improved capture of essential reasoning information.

Universal Sparse Autoencoders: Interpretable Cross-Model Concept Alignment (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-autoencoder, multi-model-alignment, concept-discovery, activation-reconstruction, coordinated-activation-maximization
Universal Sparse Autoencoders (USAEs) learn a shared, overcomplete sparse dictionary that encodes activations from multiple pretrained vision models into a unified concept space and decodes them back to reconstruct each model's activations. During training, a single model is randomly selected per iteration; its activations are encoded into the shared sparse code via a model-specific encoder, then decoded via all model-specific decoders to reconstruct every model's activations. Backpropagation updates only the chosen encoder-decoder pair against the aggregated reconstruction loss across all models, enforcing concept alignment during end-to-end training.
claim c1 [qualitative]: USAEs discover semantically coherent universal concepts across vision models ranging from low-level features (e.g., colors and textures) to higher-level structures (e.g., parts and objects)
claim c2 [quantitative]: Self-reconstruction R² scores for DinoV2 average 0.8, compared to 0.7 for SigLIP and ViT on final layer activations
claim c3 [quantitative]: Over 1000 concepts exhibit peak firing entropy, confirming the USAE learns a strongly universal concept space; bimodal distribution shows a second group with moderate entropy indicating concepts that favor two models but not all three
claim c4 [quantitative]: The first 200 concepts co-fire between 50–80% of the time across all three models, indicating a core set of consistently recognized concepts
claim c5 [quantitative]: When focusing on high-frequency co-firing concepts, the correlation between concept co-firing frequency and concept energy strengthens from r=0.35 (all concepts) to r=0.66 (frequently co-firing concepts), suggesting a strong correlation between how energetic a concept is and its universality
claim c6 [quantitative]: ViT has the strongest concept overlap with 41% of its universal concepts having a cosine similarity ≥0.5 with its independent SAE counterpart; SigLIP and DinoV2 achieve 30% and 36% respectively; USAEs achieve AUC=0.30 versus baseline AUC=0.13 on concept consistency matching
claim c7 [qualitative]: DinoV2 exhibits unique geometric and perspective-related concepts including converging perspective lines, view-invariant angle/tilt features, and low-level 3D understanding concepts such as rectangular prism faces and brim lines of dome-shaped objects, which are absent or low-entropy in SigLIP and ViT
claim c8 [negative]: Only a relatively low proportion of concepts show high overlap between universal and independently trained SAEs (30% for SigLIP, 36% for DinoV2 at cosine similarity ≥0.5), indicating universal training discovers concepts that may not emerge in independent training

Sparse Autoencoders Do Not Find Canonical Units of Analysis (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae-analysis, feature-decomposition, activation-steering, interpretability-probe, dictionary-learning
The paper introduces two analysis techniques for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on LLM activations. SAE stitching swaps latent directions between SAEs of different dictionary sizes, measuring reconstruction performance changes to classify latents as novel (capturing new information) or reconstruction (refining existing features). Meta-SAEs are SAEs trained on the decoder directions of a larger SAE to decompose those directions into interpretable sparse combinations of meta-latents, revealing compositional structure. Information flows through decoder direction similarity metrics (cosine similarity thresholds) and reconstruction loss measurements.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Smaller SAEs are incomplete: larger SAEs learn novel latents that improve performance when added, indicating they capture information missed by smaller SAEs
claim c2 [qualitative]: Larger SAE latents are not atomic but often decompose into combinations of latents from smaller SAEs due to the sparsity penalty incentivizing composition of features
claim c3 [quantitative]: Meta-SAE trained on GPT-2 SAE decoder directions (dictionary size 49152) explains 55.47% of variance of the decoder directions
claim c4 [quantitative]: Meta-SAE latent explanations evaluated in zero-shot multiple-choice: GPT-4o-mini chose the correct SAE latent explanation 73% of the time among 5 options on 1,000 sampled latents
claim c5 [qualitative]: Example latent decompositions show interpretability: a latent representing 'Einstein' decomposes into meta-latents for 'scientist', 'Germany', 'famous person', and other related concepts
claim c6 [negative]: SAEs do not find a canonical set of units: rather than converging on a unique, complete, and irreducible set of features, SAEs of different sizes offer varying granularities and compositions of features
claim c7 [negative]: The paper acknowledges its work only studies two LLMs (GPT-2 Small and Gemma 2 2B) and does not include very large SAEs such as in Templeton (2024)
claim c8 [qualitative]: Meta-SAE latents show high maximum cosine similarity to latents from SAEs of comparable size trained directly on network activations, supporting the hypothesis that larger SAE latents are compositions of features from smaller SAEs

Flexible and Efficient Grammar-Constrained Decoding (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, grammar-enforcement, token-masking, lexer-alignment, context-free-grammar
GreatGramma is a grammar-constrained decoding (GCD) system that prevents LLMs from generating outputs violating a context-free grammar (CFG). It operates in two phases: (1) offline preprocessing constructs a token-spanner table by analyzing how the LLM's subword tokenizer aligns with CFG terminals defined as regular expressions, building a lexer FSA and mapping LLM tokens to sequences of terminals; (2) online decoding uses this table to compute boolean masks over the LLM vocabulary at each step, masking tokens that provably cannot lead to valid parses. The core innovation addresses token misalignment—LLM tokens may span multiple, partial, or no CFG terminals—by precomputing a lexer-state-dependent mapping.
claim c1 [qualitative]: GreatGramma achieves an average 17.71x speedup in offline preprocessing compared to related approaches
claim c2 [qualitative]: GreatGramma maintains state-of-the-art online masking efficiency of 5-32ms per token
claim c3 [qualitative]: Existing GCD algorithms require tens of minutes to preprocess common grammars
claim c4 [negative]: GCD implementations from existing published papers contain soundness bugs (e.g., they mask tokens that should not be masked or do not terminate when preprocessing simple grammars)
claim c5 [qualitative]: The key challenge with implementing GCD algorithms is that the tokens used by subword tokenizers in LLMs do not align with the terminals used by parsers
claim c6 [qualitative]: Grammar-constrained decoding can guarantee that LLM outputs match specified context-free grammar rules by masking out tokens that will provably lead to outputs not in the grammar

Scaling up Test-Time Compute with Latent Reasoning: A Recurrent Depth Approach (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: recurrent-depth, latent-reasoning, test-time-compute-scaling, iterative-refinement, adaptive-compute, truncated-bptt
A decoder-only transformer with latent depth recurrence: input tokens are embedded by a prelude block into latent space, then a shared recurrent core block iterates on the hidden state (injecting the embedded input at each step), unrolling to arbitrary depth at test-time. A coda block un-embeds the final latent state to produce next-token logits. The recurrent block is trained with randomly sampled iteration counts from a log-normal Poisson distribution; inference backpropagates through only the last iterations (truncated BPTT in depth). Architecture uses sandwich normalization (norm-layer-norm), RoPE attention, gated SiLU MLPs, and an adapter that concatenates initial embedding with current state.
claim c1 [quantitative]: The model can improve its performance on reasoning benchmarks up to a computation load equivalent to 50 billion parameters
claim c2 [quantitative]: GSM8K CoT performance with flexible match reaches 38.13% at depth 32
claim c3 [quantitative]: On HumanEval pass@1, the model achieves 23.17% with recurrence depth 32
claim c4 [quantitative]: Recurrent model at 180B tokens achieves 9.02/10.24 (flexible/strict) on GSM8K CoT, versus fixed-depth baseline at 1.82/2.20
claim c5 [qualitative]: Does not require specialized training data; can work with small context windows; can capture types of reasoning not easily represented in words
claim c6 [qualitative]: Trained for 800 billion tokens at 3.5 billion parameters on Frontier supercomputer with 4096 GPUs
claim c7 [negative]: On standard benchmarks, the model lags behind later OLMo models trained on larger, more carefully curated datasets; lags behind dedicated code models like StarCoder2
claim c8 [qualitative]: Recurrent depth models naturally support per-token adaptive compute, self-speculative decoding, and KV-cache sharing at inference time without specialized training or tuning

VersaPRM: Multi-Domain Process Reward Model via Synthetic Reasoning Data (2025)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, step-level-scoring, synthetic-data-generation, multi-domain-reasoning, inference-time-compute, llm-based-labeling
VersaPRM is a Process Reward Model (PRM) that scores the correctness of intermediate reasoning steps in Chain-of-Thought (CoT) sequences. It takes a sequence of reasoning steps as input and outputs a vector of reward scores, one per step. The model is initialized from a math-domain PRM (LlamaPRM800K based on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) and fine-tuned via LoRA on synthetic multi-domain reasoning data. At inference, aggregated step scores guide reranking methods (Majority Voting, Weighted Majority Voting, Best-of-N) and search algorithms (Beam Search, MCTS) to select high-quality solutions from multiple candidate CoTs.
claim c1 [negative]: current PRMs have poor performance in other domains. Math PRMs trained exclusively on mathematical data lack the versatility to generalize beyond mathematical reasoning tasks.
claim c2 [quantitative]: in the MMLU-Pro category of Law, VersaPRM via weighted majority voting achieves a 7.9% performance gain over the majority voting baseline
claim c3 [quantitative]: Qwen2.5-Math-PRM's gain in the MMLU-Pro category of Law is 1.3%
claim c4 [qualitative]: For CoTs labeled as correct by the auto-labeler, agreement rate with manual evaluations was 80% (95% CI: 0.69–0.89); for CoTs labeled as incorrect, agreement rate was 71% (95% CI: 0.63–0.79)
claim c5 [qualitative]: VersaPRM consistently outperforms open-source math PRMs in both WMV and BoN across all domain groups (Math, Math-adjacent, and non-Math-adjacent domains)
claim c6 [qualitative]: VersaPRM trained with one domain held out (Law or Biology) remains comparable to the fully trained model across both evaluated domains and surpasses the performance of math PRMs, demonstrating genuine domain-general reasoning capabilities
claim c7 [qualitative]: VersaPRM (random subset) achieves higher WMV accuracy across both math and non-math tasks compared to VersaPRM (Math subset), demonstrating the value of training on diverse data
claim c8 [quantitative]: The CoT generation and labeling pipeline for MMLU-Pro-CoT-Train (Labeled) was completed at a total cost under $100 USD using AWS Bedrock batch inference

Interpreting and Steering Protein Language Models through Sparse Autoencoders (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, mechanistic-interpretability, protein-lm, activation-steering, latent-feature-attribution, sequence-design
The system trains a sparse autoencoder (SAE) on hidden activations from ESM-2 8M (a transformer-based protein language model). The SAE has an overcomplete bottleneck (10× expansion factor) with L1 sparsity regularization to disentangle polysemantic features. Latent components are interpreted via statistical association with protein annotations (precision/recall against UniProt labels). Selected latents are then used to steer sequence generation by scaling/shifting their activations in the residual stream during iterative inference, with reconstructed embeddings injected back before the decoder.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Sparse autoencoders identify latent components associated with various protein characteristics, including transmembrane regions, binding sites, and specialized motifs such as zinc finger domains
claim c2 [quantitative]: 395 putative latent-feature associations are identified with minimum precision or recall of 0.8 at activation threshold 1.00
claim c3 [quantitative]: Intervening on the two most prominent latents (highest recall) associated with zinc finger regions produces 24 matching zinc finger motifs out of 180 generated sequences
claim c4 [quantitative]: Intervening on a single zinc finger latent produces only 3 matches out of 180 sequences, whereas random latent intervention or random latent pair intervention produces zero matches
claim c5 [quantitative]: The selected sparse autoencoder achieves an average of 18 non-zero latent components per amino acid token with a cross-entropy increase of 0.10
claim c6 [qualitative]: Layer selection is guided by intrinsic dimension analysis, identifying layer 3 of ESM-2 8M as a plateau region where abstract information is most clearly encoded
claim c7 [qualitative]: Among 24 matched zinc finger sequences, the highest percent sequence similarity was unreported, with an average sequence similarity of unreported, indicating a good level of diversity among generated sequences
claim c8 [negative]: The sequence generation pipeline requires parameter fine-tuning to improve the success rate; the process is not yet fully automated

Sparse Autoencoder Features for Classifications and Transferability (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae-features, activation-extraction, pooling-binarization, interpretability, classification-probe, cross-lingual-transfer
A classification pipeline that extracts pretrained Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) features from frozen Gemma 2 LLM hidden states, applies pooling and binarization transformations, and trains logistic regression classifiers on the resulting feature vectors. SAE activations from selected layers are uprojected to sparse dimensions, aggregated via summation or top-k selection, optionally thresholded to binary form, then fed to downstream task-specific classifiers.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAE-derived features achieve macro F1 > 0.8, outperforming hidden-state and BoW baselines
claim c2 [quantitative]: Larger models (9B, 9B-IT) achieve consistently higher mean performance (above 0.85 F1) compared to the 2B model
claim c3 [qualitative]: Middle-layer SAE features typically produce higher F1 scores than early- or late-layer features
claim c4 [qualitative]: Binarized and no max pooling of SAE features outperform both hidden-state probes and bag-of-words
claim c5 [quantitative]: SAE features achieve superior F1 scores (0.85-0.90) compared to both hidden states (0.80-0.85) and top-n selection (0.75-0.80) under varying training data sampling rates
claim c6 [quantitative]: Cross-lingual transfer from English achieves 15-20% F1 score decrease compared to native training
claim c7 [quantitative]: Smaller model (2B) SAE features can predict larger model (9B-IT) correctness with F1 scores above 80% with correct context, dropping 20% with context manipulation
claim c8 [negative]: Study is restricted to SAEs trained with Jump ReLU activation on Gemma 2 models as they were the only open-source models available with SAEs across varying layers, widths, and model sizes, potentially limiting generalizability to other model architectures and training paradigms

Concept Layers: Enhancing Interpretability and Intervenability via LLM Conceptualization (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: concept-bottleneck, interpretability, intervenability, activation-projection, ontology-search, knowledge-editing
Concept Layers (CLs) are non-trainable modules inserted between slices of a pretrained LLM. Each CL projects the model's latent representation into an interpretable conceptual space via cosine similarity to a concept set, then reconstructs and passes the representation back to the model. The suffix of the model is briefly retrained via feature-based distillation (welding) to adapt to information loss from the projection-reconstruction bottleneck, while the projection matrices remain frozen to preserve semantic meaning. Multiple CLs can be stacked at different depths for hierarchical interpretability.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Concept Layers integrate interpretability and intervenability into existing pretrained models without requiring labeled concept datasets or major architectural modifications, enabling seamless integration into existing system pipelines.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Conceptualized models maintain classification performance on par with or slightly better than the original model across AG News, Yelp, and DBpedia datasets.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Task-specific Single CL on AG News achieves 91.86% accuracy vs 91.63% for original model.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Conceptualized models maintain high agreement with the original model: task-specific Double CL on AG News achieves 96.45% agreement with original.
claim c5 [quantitative]: Backward compatibility: a classifier trained on the original model transferred to task-specific Single CL on Yelp maintains 89.50% accuracy.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Automatic ontology-based concept selection via variance-guided search algorithm eliminates the need for human-selected concept sets, enabling both task-specific and task-agnostic conceptualization.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Intervenability interface enables attenuation of specific concept activations during inference to adjust model behavior dynamically, demonstrated by mitigating price-bias in Yelp Polarity classification through discounting the Economy concept.
claim c8 [negative]: The welding phase requires direct access to model latent representations during training or precomputing them in advance, both of which can be challenging depending on system constraints, representing the primary computational cost of the method.

Retrieval-Augmented Process Reward Model for Generalizable Mathematical Reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmented, in-context-learning, process-reward-model, mathematical-reasoning
RetrievalPRM augments a base PRM (fine-tuned Qwen-2.5-Math-7B-Instruct) with a two-stage retrieval mechanism. At inference, retrieved semantically similar questions and steps are prepended to the input prompt as in-context examples. Question-level retrieval uses Sentence-BERT embeddings with PCA dimensionality reduction and cosine similarity to select top-k similar questions; step-level retrieval then selects similar steps from the retrieved questions' solutions using the same similarity metric. Both retrieved artifacts flow as text into the model's input, treated as a warm-up context that helps the PRM generalize across distribution shifts.
claim c1 [quantitative]: RetrievalPRM-7B achieves average F1 score of 65.8 across GSM8K, MATH, OlympiadBench, and OmniMATH on ProcessBench
claim c2 [quantitative]: RetrievalPRM-7B F1 score on OlympiadBench is 60.2, compared to Qwen2.5-Math-7B-PRM800K baseline of 50.7
claim c3 [quantitative]: RetrievalPRM (w/o Step-level Retrieval) achieves F1 of 65.0 on ProcessBench average, versus 65.8 for full RetrievalPRM
claim c4 [quantitative]: RetrievalPRM (w/o Question-level Retrieval) achieves F1 of 63.0 on ProcessBench average
claim c5 [qualitative]: RetrievalPRM outperforms all evaluated language models as critics, including Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct and Llama3.3-70B-Instruct, with the sole exception of QwQ-32B-Preview, achieving this with a model size of just 7B
claim c6 [qualitative]: The paper identifies two key OOD issues in PRMs: step OOD caused by differences in reasoning patterns across model types (GPT, Qwen) and model sizes (1.5B, 72B), and question OOD arising from dataset shifts between training data (GSM8K, MATH) and real-world problems (OlympiadBench, OmniMATH)
claim c7 [negative]: RetrievalPRM retrieval pool is only constructed from PRM800K and Math-Shepherd, which is relatively small and limits the diversity and breadth of mathematical problems
claim c8 [negative]: Using Sentence-BERT to embed questions and steps struggles to capture the full complexity of mathematical problems as semantic similarity does not mean knowledge similarity in math problems; the naive cosine similarity may fail to accurately reflect true similarity between two questions

Intermediate Languages Matter: Formal Choice Drives Neurosymbolic LLM Reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: neurosymbolic, formal-language-translation, in-context-learning, symbolic-solver, autoformalization
A neurosymbolic system that translates natural-language logical reasoning problems into formal intermediate languages using in-context learning with LLMs, then solves the translated problems with symbolic reasoners (Pyke, ASP, NLTK/Prover9, or FOL). The LLM acts as a translator from natural language to formal syntax; the symbolic engine provides faithful reasoning. Information flows: natural language problem → LLM (with ICL examples) → formal language representation → symbolic solver → solution.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The choice of formal language affects both the syntactic and the semantic reasoning capability of LLMs in neurosymbolic reasoning.
claim c2 [quantitative]: First-order logic (FOL) outperforms logic programming languages (Pyke and ASP) on average overall-accuracy.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Context-aware encodings (Text) improve LLM reasoning performance compared to no context (No-C.), achieving 58.42% overall-accuracy versus 45.49%.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Adding comments or markdown syntax to in-context-learning examples yields no systematic benefit.
claim c5 [quantitative]: For the 8B parameter model class (Ministral-8B), FOL achieves 100.0% overall-accuracy on ProntoQA with context and comments, compared to 48.80% for standard prompting baseline.
claim c6 [negative]: Logic-LM* and LINC* baselines perform poorly (2.20% and 6.20% overall-accuracy on ProntoQA, respectively) due to susceptibility to small syntax errors such as markdown formatting and line enumeration.
claim c7 [qualitative]: ASP has execution-rate ranging from 62% to 95% and maintains overall-accuracy above 30%, while Pyke has most ablation scenarios below 70% overall-accuracy.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Common LLM errors across formal languages include token generation cutoff, Pyke formatting issues (missing line breaks), ASP negation confusion (strong vs. default negation), and NLTK/FOL parenthesis and arity errors.

Route Sparse Autoencoder to Interpret Large Language Models (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, routing, multi-layer-extraction, feature-steering, mechanistic-interpretability
RouteSAE integrates a lightweight router with a shared TopK sparse autoencoder to extract interpretable features from multiple layers of an LLM. The router computes normalized layer-wise weights via sum-pooled multi-layer activations and a learned linear projection, selecting the layer with highest probability. The scaled activation from the selected layer feeds into a shared TopK SAE for sparse disentanglement and reconstruction, unifying feature representations across layers while incurring minimal parameter overhead compared to layer-specific SAE variants.
claim c1 [quantitative]: under the same sparsity constraint of 64, RouteSAE extracts 22.5% more features than baseline SAEs
claim c2 [quantitative]: RouteSAE achieves a 22.3% higher interpretability score
claim c3 [quantitative]: RouteSAE maintains interpretation scores above 4.4 at all sparsity levels, largely unaffected by changes in sparsity
claim c4 [qualitative]: RouteSAE achieves the best performance among all methods, maintaining a lower KL divergence at comparable sparsity levels
claim c5 [qualitative]: RouteSAE extracts over 4,000 interpretable features at a threshold of 15, surpassing all other methods
claim c6 [qualitative]: RouteSAE enables controlled model steering by directly manipulating internal features from the SAE decoder, allowing targeted interventions to influence model output through feature activation clamping
claim c7 [qualitative]: RouteSAE produces a U-shaped weight distribution across layers during both training and inference, rather than concentrating on a small subset of shallow layers
claim c8 [negative]: The router employs a simple linear projection, which has limited capabilities; exploring more sophisticated activation aggregation methods and router designs is an important direction for future work

VisualPRM: An Effective Process Reward Model for Multimodal Reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, test-time-scaling, best-of-n, step-wise-scoring, monte-carlo-supervision, multimodal-reasoning, verifier-loop
VisualPRM is an 8B-parameter multimodal Process Reward Model that scores step-by-step reasoning chains from MLLMs. It takes an image, question, and solution steps as multi-turn chat input and predicts correctness of each step. During Best-of-N evaluation, it ranks multiple candidate responses from a policy MLLM and selects the highest-scored one. The model uses a value-based approach where each step's quality is determined by its expected accuracy (computed during training via Monte Carlo sampling), formulated as multi-turn dialogue to leverage MLLM generation capacity.
claim c1 [quantitative]: VisualPRM achieves a 5.9-point improvement across seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks when applied to InternVL2.5-78B
claim c2 [qualitative]: VisualPRM improves the overall reasoning performance of MiniCPM-V2.6, QwenVL2.5-7B, InternVL2.5-8B, and InternVL2.5-78B by 8.0, 3.7, 8.4, and 5.9 points, respectively, across seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks
claim c3 [quantitative]: VisualPRM achieves an overall macro F1 score of 62.0 on VisualProcessBench for detecting erroneous steps, outperforming GPT-4o (60.3) and performing on par with Gemini-2.0-Flash (62.3)
claim c4 [quantitative]: VisualPRM outperforms Self-Consistency and Outcome Reward Models in Best-of-8 evaluation, with PRM outperforming SC and ORM by 2.4 and 1.5 points respectively on InternVL2.5-8B
claim c5 [quantitative]: VisualPRM400K dataset comprises approximately 400K samples with 2 million steps of process supervision, averaging 5.6 steps per response and 22.6 words per step, with approximately 10% incorrect steps
claim c6 [quantitative]: VisualProcessBench comprises 2,866 samples with 26,950 human-annotated step-wise correctness labels across five multimodal reasoning benchmarks, with 16,585 correct steps, 7,691 incorrect steps, and 2,674 neutral steps
claim c7 [qualitative]: Value-based PRMs outperform advantage-based PRMs; supervising all steps (w/o early stop) yields better performance than supervising only up to the first incorrect step during training
claim c8 [negative]: Existing open-source MLLMs struggle to accurately assess the correctness of each step as critic models, with most achieving F1 scores close to random guessing baseline of 50.0, tending to provide positive analysis and label most steps as correct

(G)I-DLE: Generative Inference via Distribution-preserving Logit Exclusion with KL Divergence Minimization for Constrained Decoding (2025)
seam: text; tags: constrained-decoding, logit-masking, kl-minimization, distribution-preservation, re-normalization
A constrained decoding method for autoregressive language models that modifies token logits to exclude undesirable tokens while minimizing KL divergence from the model's original conditional probability distribution. The system reformulates naive logit masking (setting banned token logits to −∞) as a KL divergence minimization problem, re-normalizing allowed token probabilities according to Equation (3) to preserve the intrinsic distribution. The logit processor applies a transformation (Equation 7) that sets banned token logits to −∞ then re-normalizes remaining logits, yielding a posterior distribution over allowed tokens that minimizes distributional distortion.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Naive logit masking, which sets the logits of banned tokens to −∞, induces a re-normalized distribution that distorts the original probability mass of allowed tokens and increases output variance.
claim c2 [quantitative]: On Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct, (G)I-DLE achieves higher mean score (4.966667) compared to baseline generation (4.911111).
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct, (G)I-DLE substantially reduces variance from 0.216729 (baseline) to 0.055056.
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, (G)I-DLE achieves mean score of 4.900000 with variance 0.113483, compared to baseline mean 4.716667 and variance 0.517135.
claim c5 [quantitative]: On Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, (G)I-DLE achieves mean score 4.555556 compared to baseline 4.483333.
claim c6 [quantitative]: On Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, (G)I-DLE achieves mean score 3.455556 compared to baseline 3.266667.
claim c7 [negative]: Naive masked generation produces only marginal improvements in mean score over baseline while exhibiting higher variance, particularly for smaller models.
claim c8 [qualitative]: (G)I-DLE consistently achieves higher mean evaluation scores with substantially reduced variance across Qwen2.5 models ranging from 1.5B to 14B, producing more fluent, logically coherent, and stable outputs.

More Bang for the Buck: Process Reward Modeling with Entropy-Driven Uncertainty (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: entropy-anchor-branching, step-segmentation, monte-carlo-labeling, process-reward-modeling, mathematical-reasoning, token-efficiency
EDU-PRM is a process reward model trained on automatically-segmented reasoning steps. The system uses token-level predictive entropy (softmax over logits at each decoding step) to identify uncertainty anchors—positions where entropy exceeds a threshold—and branches into top-k continuations, then greedily decodes between anchors. Each resulting fragment is assigned a binary correctness label via Monte Carlo estimation over final-answer validity. The trained PRM then scores intermediate reasoning steps during inference, either for per-step evaluation (ProcessBench) or as a selector in Best-of-N candidate ranking.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On the MATH dataset, EDU-PRM-72B attains the highest judgment accuracy, outperforming Qwen-2.5-math-PRM-72B by a margin of 1.5 percentage points.
claim c2 [quantitative]: EDU-PRM achieves comparable results to SOTA Qwen2.5-Math-PRM while using only 1.5% of its publicly reported process-level training data.
claim c3 [quantitative]: By leveraging EDU sampling strategy, accuracy boosts from 64.7% to 67.3% for generative reasoning tasks, accompanied by a reduction of 32% in token usage.
claim c4 [quantitative]: On MATH, EDU sampling achieves 71.2% accuracy with 1680 tokens per sample, while high-temperature sampling achieves 68.4% accuracy with 2340 tokens on average.
claim c5 [quantitative]: On OLY, EDU sampling attains 47.6% accuracy with 2340 tokens, compared to 42.8% of high-temperature sampling with 3480 tokens.
claim c6 [qualitative]: EDU-PRM automatically anchors step boundaries at tokens with high predictive entropy, effectively capturing intrinsic logical transitions and facilitating efficient exploration of diverse reasoning paths, without requiring manual step annotations.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Fragment-level rewards are inferred automatically via Monte Carlo aggregation based solely on final-answer correctness, without any step-wise human or LLM labeling.
claim c8 [negative]: The computational cost of entropy calculation during inference adds a slight overhead compared to standard greedy decoding; experiments focus primarily on mathematical reasoning with unclear generalizability to other domains such as coding or creative writing.

Evaluating and Designing Sparse Autoencoders by Approximating Quasi-Orthogonality (2025)
seam: text; tags: sae, sparse-activation, norm-matching, quasi-orthogonality, adaptive-sparsity, feature-activation-alignment
Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose dense language model embeddings into sparse activation vectors weighted by learned feature directions. The paper proposes a theoretical framework (Approximate Feature Activation, AFA) that approximates the ℓ2-norm of sparse feature activations from input embeddings under the linear representation hypothesis. The core mechanism involves: (1) encoder mapping inputs to sparse latents via an activation function, (2) decoder reconstructing from sparse features, (3) a norm-matching loss that aligns sparse activation norms with input embedding norms, and (4) a novel top-AFA activation function that adaptively selects the number of active features per input by matching norms, eliminating the need for manually tuned sparsity parameter k.
claim c1 [theoretical]: The ℓ2-norm of sparse feature vector can be approximated with the ℓ2-norm of dense vector with a closed-form error bound that depends on the quasi-orthogonality of the dictionary and the dimensionality of the feature space.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Top-AFA SAE achieves better reconstruction performance than top-k and batch top-k SAEs, surpassing the scaling law boundary reported by Gao et al. (2024).
claim c3 [qualitative]: The proposed AFA-based evaluation metric (LBO, lower bound of quasi-orthogonality) provides a different perspective on SAE quality compared to normalized mean squared error (NMSE), yielding different distributional shapes, modality, and ranking order across layers.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Top-AFA activation function enables top-k style activation without requiring constant hyperparameter k to be tuned, dynamically determining the number of activated features for each input.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Pre-trained SAE decoders (GPT-2 Small, Gemma Scope) often fail to achieve the theoretically permitted level of quasi-orthogonality predicted by the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma.
claim c6 [qualitative]: The ZF Plot visualization demonstrates that existing unsupervised SAE evaluation metrics assess either reconstruction quality (relationship between x and x̂) or sparsity of f in isolation, overlooking the input-driven alignment between embeddings and feature activations.
claim c7 [negative]: Top-AFA introduces a new hyperparameter λ_AFA (a training-time regularization coefficient) that requires tuning; a stable coefficient value of 1/16 was observed across all tested layers but the necessity of this hyperparameter remains an open question.
claim c8 [negative]: The theoretical analysis is limited to the linear representation hypothesis; extending AFA to more general superposition settings (e.g., two-layer decoders, Matryoshka SAEs) remains an open question.

GenPRM: Scaling Test-Time Compute of Process Reward Models via Generative Reasoning (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: process-reward-model, generative-verification, code-verification, chain-of-thought-reasoning, test-time-scaling, critic-model
GenPRM is a generative process reward model that reads intermediate reasoning steps from a base LLM and outputs explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning with code verification before scoring each step. The model receives step trajectories as text input, generates verification reasoning and code outputs as intermediate tokens, and produces a final judgment score. A trained bridge component (rationale synthesis with code verification) transforms external verification signals into generative targets that the model learns to produce.
claim c1 [qualitative]: GenPRM significantly outperforms prior PRMs with only 23K training data from MATH dataset
claim c2 [qualitative]: A 1.5B GenPRM outperforms GPT-4o through test-time scaling
claim c3 [qualitative]: A 7B GenPRM surpasses Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-72B on ProcessBench
claim c4 [qualitative]: GenPRM performs explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning with code verification before providing judgment for each reasoning step
claim c5 [qualitative]: GenPRM demonstrates strong abilities to serve as a critic model for policy model refinement
claim c6 [qualitative]: Relative Progress Estimation (RPE) and a rationale synthesis framework that incorporates code verification are used to obtain high-quality process supervision labels and rationale data
claim c7 [qualitative]: GenPRM leverages the generative abilities of LLMs rather than depending on scalar value prediction
claim c8 [qualitative]: GenPRM addresses the inability to scale test-time compute of prior PRMs

Efficient and Asymptotically Unbiased Constrained Decoding for Large Language Models (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, importance-sampling, prefix-verification, set-constraints, gpu-optimization, rejection-sampling
DISC is a constrained decoding algorithm that modifies autoregressive LLM generation by dynamically adjusting sampling probabilities to satisfy set-based constraints while reducing distributional bias. During generation, it iteratively samples candidate sequences using a modified token distribution, verifies constraint compliance through Parallel Prefix-Verification (PPV), and accepts/rejects samples probabilistically. PPV replaces CPU-based prefix trees with GPU-optimized binary search over a sorted constraint array, eliminating device communication bottlenecks. Information flows from the LLM's token logits → top-k selection → PPV binary search verification → acceptance/rejection with importance-weighted probabilities → final constrained output.
claim c1 [theoretical]: For arbitrary probability value ε, there exists an autoregressive model and a candidate set with P_θ(S̄) ≥ ε, such that the KL divergence between constrained distribution and true constrained distribution is lower bounded by Ω(ε log(1/ε)).
claim c2 [theoretical]: DISC achieves asymptotic unbiasedness with KL divergence bounded by O((P_θ(S̄) / T_max) log(1/ε)), exponentially fast convergence as P_θ(S̄) → 0, fixed expected sampling size.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Entity Disambiguation with T_max=6, average Micro F1 gain compared to Vanilla increased from +4.50% (trie-based) to +5.00% (DISC) for the BLINK/AY2 model.
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Document Retrieval with T_max=6, DISC achieves average R-Precision gain of +8.75% compared to Vanilla, nearly double the improvement achieved by trie-based method (+4.76%).
claim c5 [quantitative]: On Entity Retrieval (DBPedia-Entity) with T_max=4, DISC achieves relevance score of 0.735, more than doubling Vanilla (0.340) and exceeding trie-based (0.600), realized with inference time of 76.88s versus trie-based 171.73s.
claim c6 [quantitative]: On Fact Checking (Climate-FEVER) with T_max=3, DISC achieves relevance score of 0.290, marking +84.44% improvement over Vanilla (0.160), with inference time of 149.9s compared to trie-based 234.8s.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Parallel Prefix-Verification (PPV) eliminates need for CPU-based prefix trees, removes frequent data transfers between devices, and is parallelizable across batches and top tokens with O(log|S̄|) binary search time complexity per candidate verification.
claim c8 [qualitative]: DISC can be seamlessly integrated with existing generation strategies (temperature scaling, top-k sampling) and task-specific techniques (special tokenizations, retrieval tricks) without requiring modifications to the underlying LLM architecture.

Type-Constrained Code Generation with Language Models (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, type-system, prefix-automata, inhabitable-types, code-generation
The system augments LLM code generation with type-system constraints via prefix automata and a search over inhabitable types. During decoding, the model's token-by-token sampling is filtered by what the type system permits at each position. Type validity is checked against a simply-typed λ-calculus foundation (formalized) and extended to TypeScript (practical). The approach intercepts the LLM's next-token distribution and restricts it to tokens that preserve well-typedness of the partial code string, forming a guided search over type-valid inhabitants.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Our approach reduces compilation errors by more than half
claim c2 [qualitative]: Significantly increases functional correctness in code synthesis, translation, and repair tasks across LLMs of various sizes and model families, including state-of-the-art open-weight models with more than 30B parameters
claim c3 [qualitative]: A type-constrained decoding approach that leverages type systems to guide code generation, forming a sound approach to enforce well-typedness on LLM-generated code
claim c4 [qualitative]: Formalized the approach on a foundational simply-typed language and extended it to TypeScript to demonstrate practicality
claim c5 [negative]: Large language models still frequently produce uncompilable output because their next-token inference procedure does not model formal aspects of code
claim c6 [negative]: LLMs frequently generate code with typing errors, which are beyond the domain of syntax and generally hard to adequately constrain

Efficient Process Reward Model Training via Active Learning (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: active-learning, ensemble-uncertainty, process-reward-model, step-level-supervision, aleatoric-epistemic-uncertainty, pool-based-selection
ActPRM is an active learning framework for training Process Reward Models (PRMs) that trains an ensemble PRM with multiple classification heads on a shared LLM backbone. During training, the ensemble PRM estimates uncertainty (both aleatoric and epistemic) over candidate data after the forward pass. Samples with high uncertainty are selected and annotated by QwQ-32B, a capable reasoning model. Loss is computed only on labeled uncertain samples, and the PRM weights are updated. This selective annotation strategy reduces labeling costs while maintaining or improving PRM performance on step-level correctness classification in mathematical reasoning trajectories.
claim c1 [quantitative]: ActPRM achieves an average F1 score on ProcessBench matching baseline performance while using only half the annotation budget in pool-based active learning.
claim c2 [quantitative]: ActPRM outperforms random selection by 3.3% at 50% annotation budget.
claim c3 [quantitative]: ActPRM achieves state-of-the-art performance of 75.0% on ProcessBench, outperforming Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-7B by 1.5% while requiring only 6% of its annotation budget.
claim c4 [quantitative]: ActPRM-X achieves new state-of-the-art performance of 76.0% on ProcessBench, outperforming Universal-PRM by 1.7%.
claim c5 [quantitative]: ActPRM achieves 65.5% on PRMBench.
claim c6 [quantitative]: ActPRM reduces annotation costs by 47.0% by filtering over 1M+ math reasoning trajectories from NuminaMath, retaining 60% of the data (563,030 labeled points).
claim c7 [qualitative]: Epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty are complementary to each other in ActPRM's uncertainty estimation.
claim c8 [qualitative]: ActPRM outperforms random selection of the same amount of data, with model trained on ActPRM-selected data exhibiting consistently higher training loss (margin of 0.05), indicating the selected data is more challenging and informative.

Process Reward Models That Think (2025)
seam: text; tags: generative-verifier, process-reward-model, verification-cot, rejection-sampling, step-level-supervision, test-time-scaling
ThinkPRM is a generative process reward model that verifies solution steps by generating extended verification chains-of-thought (CoTs). It fine-tunes open-weight reasoning models (R1-Distill-Qwen and QwQ) on synthetic verification CoTs collected via rejection sampling from QwQ-32B-Preview, filtered to match gold step-level labels from PRM800K. The model generates step-by-step verification rationales with explicit correctness judgments for each step, enabling both parallel scaling (multiple independent CoTs, averaged) and sequential scaling (self-correction/double-checking via trigger phrases). Information flows from problem-solution pairs through the reasoning model's CoT generation, with step correctness extracted from marked tokens (\boxed{yes}/\boxed{no}).
claim c1 [qualitative]: ThinkPRM-14B, trained on 8K process labels or 1K synthetic examples, outperforms discriminative PRMs trained on about 100x more data
claim c2 [qualitative]: ThinkPRM-1.5B, trained using the same 8K labels, outperforms LLM-as-a-judge and discriminative verifiers in reward-guided search on MATH-500
claim c3 [qualitative]: On ProcessBench, ThinkPRM substantially reduces invalid judgments (verification CoTs without extractable label) compared to LLM-as-a-Judge
claim c4 [quantitative]: On GPQA-Diamond physics subset, ThinkPRM surpasses discriminative verifiers trained on full PRM800K by 8%
claim c5 [quantitative]: On LiveCodeBench (200-problem subset), ThinkPRM surpasses discriminative verifiers trained on full PRM800K by 4.5%
claim c6 [quantitative]: Under same token budget, ThinkPRM scales up verification compute more effectively compared to LLM-as-a-Judge, outperforming it by 7.2% on a subset of ProcessBench
claim c7 [qualitative]: Training on long synthetic verification CoTs substantially outperforms training on short, compressed versions of the same CoTs
claim c8 [qualitative]: Fine-tuning on synthetic verification CoTs substantially reduces overthinking and infinite looping behavior in reasoning models used as verifiers

Memorization and Knowledge Injection in Gated LLMs (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: gated-lora, continual-learning, catastrophic-forgetting-mitigation, weight-injection, memory-recall, episodic-memory
MEGa injects event memories sequentially into a pretrained LLM (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) by fine-tuning dedicated low-rank LoRA adapters, one per memory. Each memory's embedding (derived from the model's internal activations) is stored as a context key. At inference, a gating mechanism computes softmax-weighted activations over all LoRA adapters by matching the query embedding to stored context keys, enabling the model to recall full memories and answer questions by blending relevant adapter weights into the base model.
claim c1 [quantitative]: MEGa achieves a recall cosine similarity of 0.901 on the Fictional Character dataset and 0.921 on the Wikipedia dataset, outperforming all continual learning baselines
claim c2 [quantitative]: MEGa achieves QA accuracy of 72.53% on the Fictional Character dataset and 78.03% on the Wiki dataset, significantly outperforming other continual learning fine-tuning approaches
claim c3 [quantitative]: MEGa maintains MMLU accuracy at 61.75% on Fictional Character and 61.99% on Wiki Events after continual fine-tuning, matching the base model, whereas other continual learning methods exhibit noticeable decline
claim c4 [quantitative]: Internal RAG (iRAG) achieves 80.67% QA accuracy on Fictional Character dataset and 84.70% on Wiki Events dataset, narrowing the gap with external RAG
claim c5 [quantitative]: MEGa correctly selects the gating mechanism (matching query to highest-similarity context key) 85.0% of the time on Fictional Character dataset and 87.8% on Wiki Events dataset
claim c6 [quantitative]: On compositional questions (requiring information from two distinct samples), MEGa achieves 49.6% accuracy on Fictional Character dataset and 70.4% on Wiki Events, outperforming continual learning baselines but slightly below batch learning (54.4% and 75.2%)
claim c7 [negative]: All continual learning baselines (Full, Full+L2, Full+EWC, LoRA, LoRA+L2) exhibit severe catastrophic forgetting, with recall and QA performance degrading significantly as the sequence of fine-tuning samples increases
claim c8 [qualitative]: MEGa demonstrates that knowledge embedded in separate LoRA modules can be successfully integrated through softmax-weighted gating to answer compositional questions, providing the first demonstration that such weight merging enables multi-memory reasoning

Beyond Input Activations: Identifying Influential Latents by Gradient Sparse Autoencoders (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae-interpretation, gradient-attribution, latent-steering, causal-influence, training-free
GradSAE is a training-free method that identifies causally influential latent features in Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) by incorporating output-side gradient information. Given a pre-trained SAE decomposing hidden representations into sparse latent activations, GradSAE approximates the causal influence of each latent on the model output via first-order Taylor expansion of the output logits with respect to latent activations. The key insight is that latents with high input-side activation do not necessarily have high causal influence on outputs; instead, influence is measured by the magnitude of the latent activation multiplied by the gradient of the output logit with respect to that activation. The method ranks latents by their average influence scores across the input sequence and selects top-K influential latents for downstream tasks like model steering.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Not all activated latents contribute equally to the construction of the model's output
claim c2 [qualitative]: Only latents with high causal influence are effective for model steering
claim c3 [quantitative]: Masking GradSAE's TopK latents at K=50% results in F1 score dropping from 94.79% to 43.33%
claim c4 [quantitative]: GradSAE TopK latent masking causes larger performance degradation (80.45% EM at K=1) compared to Baseline method (97.04% EM at K=1)
claim c5 [quantitative]: Masking BottomK latents maintains near-perfect performance across all K values, with F1 scores around 99% for both Baseline and GradSAE
claim c6 [quantitative]: In local steering, GradSAE achieves F1 score of 10.21% at K=10, compared to Baseline's 6.69%, demonstrating more effective steering of model outputs toward a different question
claim c7 [quantitative]: Only 18.06% of latents in Baseline's TopK set (K=50%) overlap with GradSAE's TopK set, indicating that high input-side activation does not reliably indicate causal influence on output
claim c8 [qualitative]: GradSAE generalizes across different SAEs and instruction-tuned LLMs; experiments on LLaMA 3 8B Instruct show consistent superiority of GradSAE over Baseline in identifying influential latents

Bidirectional LMs are Better Knowledge Memorizers? A Benchmark for Real-world Knowledge Injection (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: knowledge-injection, continued-pretraining, ensemble-routing, span-masking, scope-classifier, modular-architecture
WikiDYK is a knowledge injection benchmark and evaluation framework. The paper compares knowledge memorization across Causal Language Models (CLMs: Llama, Qwen, Gemma) and Bidirectional Language Models (BiLMs: Flan-T5, T5, RoBERTa) via continued pre-training with three training objectives (next-token prediction, synthetic QA, span prediction). A modular ensemble pipeline routes queries via a learned scope classifier to specialized BiLMs acting as external knowledge repositories, integrated with a base LLM (Llama-3.1-8B). Information flows from factual snippets → training objectives → model parameters → evaluation on multi-dimensional QA (reliability, generality, paraphrase, portability, locality).
claim c1 [quantitative]: Flan-T5-770M achieves match accuracy on reliability questions, compared to Llama-3.1-8B span prediction at 16.09% match accuracy, demonstrating BiLMs significantly outperform CLMs at knowledge memorization
claim c2 [quantitative]: BiLMs exhibit 23% lower accuracy in terms of reliability compared to CLMs under continued pre-training (from abstract)
claim c3 [quantitative]: Ensemble pipeline (Flan-T5-770M) improves reliability match accuracy by 6.73 percentage points over single model (52.82% vs 46.09%)
claim c4 [quantitative]: Ensemble framework improves reliability accuracy by up to 29.1% (from abstract)
claim c5 [qualitative]: WikiDYK contains 12,290 facts and 77,180 questions across five question types
claim c6 [qualitative]: Next-token prediction objective is not suitable for knowledge injection; performance is lower than static analysis baseline with catastrophic forgetting observed in locality tasks
claim c7 [qualitative]: Portability (multi-hop reasoning) shows less improvement across all models and training objectives compared to other question types
claim c8 [qualitative]: BiLM architectural advantage in bidirectional attention and span prediction objective enables richer token dependencies compared to unidirectional CLM attention with the same training objective alignment

Reasoning by Superposition: A Theoretical Perspective on Chain of Continuous Thought (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: continuous-cot, superposition-state, graph-reachability, attention-chooser, parallel-bfs
A two-layer transformer that performs chain-of-continuous-thought (CoCoT) reasoning on directed graph reachability. The model autoregressively generates continuous thought vectors (dense embeddings rather than discrete tokens) at each reasoning step. Each continuous thought is constructed as a superposition state encoding multiple reachable vertices simultaneously via normalized linear combinations. Layer 1 attention uses five 'attention chooser' heads to copy source and target nodes from edges onto edge tokens; Layer 2 attention expands the frontier by attending to outgoing edges from currently reachable nodes and accumulating target nodes back into the superposition. MLPs filter noise and normalize weights. At the final step, an answer token measures the superposition to select the reachable candidate.
claim c1 [theoretical]: a two-layer transformer with D steps of continuous CoTs can solve the directed graph reachability problem, where D is the diameter of the graph
claim c2 [theoretical]: the best known result of constant-depth transformers with discrete CoTs requires O(n²) decoding steps where n is the number of vertices, while continuous CoTs require D steps (D < n)
claim c3 [theoretical]: each continuous thought vector is a superposition state that encodes multiple search frontiers simultaneously (parallel breadth-first search), while discrete CoTs must choose a single path sampled from the superposition state
claim c4 [qualitative]: a 2-layer transformer trained from scratch with Coconut achieves near-perfect accuracy on ProsQA, while both CoT and No CoT baselines solve only about 75% of the tasks
claim c5 [quantitative]: a 12-layer transformer with discrete CoT achieves 83% accuracy on ProsQA, still unable to solve the tasks reliably
claim c6 [qualitative]: the model's Layer 1 attention instantiates a copying mechanism that places nearly all attention mass on source and target nodes of edges, confirming the theoretical construction
claim c7 [qualitative]: the model's Layer 2 attention sharply concentrates on reachable edges and exhibits bias toward the frontier subset (nodes exactly D steps from root), with additional emphasis on optimal edges leading to the solution
claim c8 [qualitative]: encoding of multiple search frontiers as a superposition state automatically emerges in training continuous CoTs, without explicit supervision to guide the model to explore multiple paths simultaneously (demonstrated by Coconut-BFS achieving similar performance to supervised Coconut)

RL Tango: Reinforcing Generator and Verifier Together for Language Reasoning (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: rl-training, co-evolution, verifier-loop, process-reward-model, generative-verifier, interleaved-optimization, step-level-rewards
Tango jointly trains an LLM generator and an LLM verifier via interleaved RL. The generator produces multi-step reasoning trajectories and receives supervision from two reward sources: rule-based outcome-level rewards (correctness of final answer) and step-level rewards sampled from the verifier. The verifier generates natural-language verification responses containing step-wise judgments and final correctness labels, trained via RL on outcome-level rewards (verification accuracy + format compliance) without process-level annotations. Generator and verifier alternate training cycles, with the verifier updating once per three generator steps. Information flows bidirectionally: the generator's evolving reasoning trajectories provide training signal and distribution shifts that guide verifier improvement, while the verifier's stochastic step-level feedback guides generator exploration.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Tango with GRPO achieves 25.5% average relative improvement on five competition-level math benchmarks compared to vanilla RL methods trained only on outcome-level rewards
claim c2 [quantitative]: Tango with GRPO achieves 7.3% average relative improvement on four out-of-domain reasoning tasks compared to vanilla GRPO
claim c3 [quantitative]: Tango with GRPO doubles the accuracy on AIME 2025 (100% relative gain) compared to vanilla GRPO
claim c4 [quantitative]: Tango achieves 3.3× improvement in training efficiency: Tango matches vanilla GRPO's accuracy after 200 steps in only 60 generator steps
claim c5 [quantitative]: Tango's verifier achieves state-of-the-art step-level verification performance on ProcessBench among 7B/8B-scale models without using process-level labels, with 43.9% average F1 score
claim c6 [qualitative]: Among 7B/8B-scale reasoning LLMs, Tango-Qwen-7B achieves state-of-the-art performance averaging 49.5% accuracy on math tasks and 62.8% on out-of-domain tasks
claim c7 [qualitative]: The verifier progressively learns accurate step-level judgments by refining its chain-of-thought verification reasoning through RL training, gradually aligning step-level feedback with final correctness outcomes as the generator's reasoning trajectories evolve, without requiring explicit process-level annotations
claim c8 [negative]: When the verifier is fixed (frozen), the generator's performance lags behind the co-evolving baseline, and when the generator is fixed, the verifier's progress quickly plateaus, demonstrating the necessity of bidirectional co-evolution for both components to achieve optimal performance

Reasoning Beyond Language: A Comprehensive Survey on Latent Chain-of-Thought Reasoning (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-cot, hidden-state-reasoning, test-time-scaling, kv-distillation, looped-transformers, knowledge-distillation, reinforcement-learning-reasoning
This is a survey paper organizing the landscape of latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in Large Language Models. The core mechanism decouples reasoning from explicit language generation by embedding reasoning processes in continuous latent spaces rather than verbalized token sequences. The survey taxonomizes methods along two primary axes: token-wise horizontal level (generating intermediate latent thoughts sequentially before the final answer) and layer-wise vertical level (iterative refinement of hidden states through looped transformer layers). Information flows from input through latent representation initialization (via hidden states, weighted embeddings, or special vectors), through training optimization (pre-training, supervised fine-tuning with indirect/direct supervision, or reinforcement learning), and inference exploration (sequential or parallel scaling of latent reasoning paths).
claim c1 [qualitative]: Latent CoT replaces linguistic articulation with dense, high-dimensional representations that serve as a more native substrate for computational reasoning, offering advantages of accelerated inference by reducing token-level computation, richer and more compact reasoning representations, and enabling parallel exploration of multiple reasoning trajectories.
claim c2 [qualitative]: The Token-wise Horizontal Level approach can solve certain structured reasoning problems with significantly fewer steps than explicit CoT.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The Layer-wise Vertical Level approach (looped transformers) not only remains effective across a wide range of reasoning tasks but also supports a degree of parallel computation, making it more efficient than the inherently sequential nature of explicit CoT.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Repeatedly applying the same set of transformer layers—effectively increasing the model's computational depth—markedly improved multi-step reasoning, even when the total number of parameters was kept nearly constant.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Models could internalize redundant intermediate steps when producing CoT, and shortcut-like implicit reasoning tended to emerge when models were trained on highly regular patterns.
claim c6 [negative]: Current methods still underperform explicit CoT approaches, largely due to the instability of training.
claim c7 [negative]: Models trained with latent CoT techniques often struggle with novel problem structures or reasoning patterns not encountered during training, indicating considerable fragility in developing generalizable reasoning capabilities within latent spaces.
claim c8 [negative]: Some analyses detected signs of latent CoT within hidden representations, whereas others suggested such reasoning is fragile, highly conditional, or absent in many practical settings—including evidence that models did not derive answers upon first reading and that prompting alone did not elicit latent CoT.

Hybrid Latent Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, rl-policy-gradient, gating-mechanism, hidden-state-injection, hybrid-discrete-continuous
HRPO is an RL-based framework that trains LLMs to perform hybrid latent reasoning by blending discrete token embeddings with continuous hidden state representations. During rollouts, a learned gating mechanism (Equation 4) progressively incorporates prior layer hidden states into sampled token embeddings, initialized to favor tokens and gradually incorporating latent features. The gated hybrid input feeds into the next decoding step. Policy optimization uses REINFORCE-style gradient updates (Equation 6) with outcome-based rewards and KL regularization, computing log probabilities over token IDs. The approach maintains end-to-end differentiability via weighted interpolation of hidden states into the embedding manifold (Equation 3) and preserves standard autoregressive decoding for final answer generation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: HRPO reaches 0.380 EM with Qwen2.5-3B, outperforming the strongest 7B RAG baseline by 4.5%
claim c2 [quantitative]: HRPO with 1.5B backbone achieves average of 0.337, outperforming PPO by 3.0%
claim c3 [quantitative]: HRPO with 3B backbone achieves average accuracy of 0.700 on STEM benchmarks, matching the best 7B baseline on four of the datasets
claim c4 [quantitative]: HRPO with 1.5B backbone on MATH achieves 0.518, improving over GRPO baseline by 1.6%
claim c5 [qualitative]: HRPO-trained LLMs exhibit intriguing behaviors including cross-lingual patterns that fluidly integrate tokens from different languages, suggesting latent representations generalize across linguistic boundaries
claim c6 [qualitative]: The gating mechanism initializes to prioritize sampled token embeddings and progressively incorporates hidden features during training, enabling smooth integration of continuous representations while preserving LLM generative capabilities
claim c7 [negative]: HRPO introduces additional computation overhead, the on-policy design may reduce large-scale training efficiency, and its continuous representations can be less transparent
claim c8 [qualitative]: Direct injection of hidden states without interpolation degrades generation and yields nonsensical rollouts with zero reward due to mismatch between hidden state and embedding manifolds; interpolation without gating eventually suffers reward collapse

Model Unlearning via Sparse Autoencoder Subspace Guided Projections (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: sae-guided-unlearning, subspace-projection, activation-steering, parameter-constrained-optimization, knowledge-removal, interpretability
SSPU is a three-stage unlearning framework that leverages Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to guide targeted weight updates in LLMs. Stage 1 performs data-driven layer and feature selection by computing mean-squared activations on forget and retain sets, identifying SAE features most and least associated with the forget topic. Stage 2 constructs two orthonormal subspaces via QR decomposition: a 'forget-relevant' subspace (from top-k decoder vectors) and a 'forget-irrelevant' subspace (from bottom-k decoder vectors). Stage 3 applies constrained optimization that projects forget-batch activations into the irrelevant subspace while restricting weight updates to the relevant subspace, preserving retain-set activations via a retention loss term.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SSPU reduces harmful knowledge accuracy by 3.22% compared to the strongest baseline (RMU)
claim c2 [quantitative]: SSPU achieves 23.91% accuracy on WMDP-Cyber forget set
claim c3 [quantitative]: SSPU raises average utility score (MMLU, TruthfulQA, GSM8K) by 2.88% over RMU
claim c4 [quantitative]: SSPU reduces malicious accuracy under jailbreak prompts by 13.59% versus SAE-based unlearning
claim c5 [quantitative]: SSPU reduces malicious accuracy under jailbreak prompts by 2.83% versus RMU
claim c6 [qualitative]: Layer 3 yields the greatest accuracy reduction (strongest unlearning effect) compared to deeper layers in the 26-layer gemma-2b-it model
claim c7 [negative]: SSPU relies on availability of a well-trained sparse autoencoder; applicability may be constrained in settings where a suitable SAE is unavailable or difficult to train
claim c8 [negative]: The approach does not explicitly guarantee that unrelated capabilities outside the relevant subspace remain entirely unaffected

Incorporating Hierarchical Semantics in Sparse Autoencoder Architectures (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: hierarchical-semantics, mixture-of-experts, sparse-autoencoder, semantic-hierarchy, dictionary-learning
A hierarchical sparse autoencoder (H-SAE) that learns a two-level semantic hierarchy of concepts. A top-level SAE identifies high-level categorical concepts; for each activated high-level feature, a corresponding expert-specific low-level SAE operates in a learned low-dimensional subspace to capture subordinate concepts. The architecture uses a mixture-of-experts routing mechanism where low-level SAEs activate only when their parent high-level feature is active, enforcing the constraint that child concepts require their parent concept to be represented.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The H-SAE with 64 sublatents per expert achieves reconstruction performance on par with the standard SAE with 32 top-level features despite having half the compute cost
claim c2 [qualitative]: H-SAE shows lower feature absorption on first-letter classification benchmark compared to standard SAE
claim c3 [qualitative]: H-SAE uses more similar sets of features across languages than baseline SAE, measured by symmetric set difference of top 8 activated features on same tokens in different languages
claim c4 [qualitative]: semantic hierarchy can be learned: high-level features activate corresponding low-level features that are semantically coherent subordinate concepts (e.g., 'dog' high-level feature with 'corgi', 'greyhound', 'shitzu' low-level features)
claim c5 [qualitative]: H-SAE features maintain or improve interpretability compared to standard SAE, with hierarchical decomposition providing more granular and contextually coherent feature organization
claim c6 [qualitative]: the hierarchical structure provides significant computational efficiency gains: computational cost is dominated by top-level SAE when sparsity k and subspace dimension d_e are typical, making hierarchical additions negligible overhead
claim c7 [negative]: results are still imperfect—some hard-to-interpret features remain and perfect reconstruction is not achieved; unclear whether this reflects fundamental limitation of dictionary learning or whether additional architectural changes could lead to dramatically improved performance
claim c8 [negative]: orthogonality loss and regularizers do not seem necessary for interpretability and hierarchy based on ablation studies on unembedding matrix, suggesting these auxiliary losses may not be load-bearing

Efficient Post-Training Refinement of Latent Reasoning in Large Language Models (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, contrastive-search, residual-refinement, training-free, embedding-steering
A post-training refinement framework that operates on latent reasoning embeddings produced by models like Coconut. Information flows through three stages: (1) a latent reasoning backbone (Coconut) that iteratively updates an embedding via fixed model blocks without generating tokens; (2) contrastive reasoning feedback search that compares embeddings from weak and strong reference models to compute a gradient signal guiding the embedding toward better reasoning directions; (3) residual embedding refinement that blends current and previous embeddings via fixed-weight interpolation to stabilize multi-step updates. Both refinement modules operate in the latent space using forward passes only, with no parameter updates.
claim c1 [quantitative]: +5% accuracy gain on MathQA without additional training
claim c2 [quantitative]: On AQUA-RAT, achieves +2.76% absolute gain over Coconut
claim c3 [quantitative]: On ProsQA, achieves +3.67% absolute gain over Coconut
claim c4 [quantitative]: Reduces token generation by over 92% compared to Chain-of-Thought on MathQA and AQUA
claim c5 [quantitative]: Inference-only method achieves +4.47% accuracy on ProsQA with 24 seconds and 31.23GB memory, while training+inference approach takes 54+ minutes and 39.04GB but yields only +1.63% gain
claim c6 [quantitative]: Ablation: residual refinement alone yields +4.63% on MathQA; contrastive search alone yields +4.03%; both together yield +5.10%
claim c7 [qualitative]: Method improves accuracy over Coconut baseline on all three model architectures tested (GPT-2 117M, Qwen-2.5 1.5B, LLaMA-3.2 3B) with gains ranging from +1.77% to +5.10% on MathQA
claim c8 [negative]: On GSM8K, Chain-of-Thought remains most effective (42.76% accuracy) as GSM8K contains complex arithmetic problems requiring symbolic calculations that latent reasoning struggles with without external tools

PAG: Multi-Turn Reinforced LLM Self-Correction with Policy as Generative Verifier (2025)
seam: text; tags: self-correction, generative-verifier, multi-turn-rl, role-alternation, selective-revision
PAG is a unified multi-turn reinforcement learning framework where a single LLM alternates between policy (reasoner) and verifier roles. In each turn, the model generates a reasoning attempt (policy), then generates a verification step to detect errors (generative verifier). If the verification detects an error, the model revises its answer; otherwise, it retains the original. This selective revision mechanism operates within a reinforcement learning loop that jointly trains reasoning and verification capabilities without requiring separate verifier models or multi-stage pipelines.
claim c1 [qualitative]: PAG introduces a selective revision mechanism: the model revises its answer only when its own generative verification step detects an error, distinct from prior approaches that always generate a second attempt regardless of model confidence
claim c2 [qualitative]: The verify-then-revise workflow alleviates model collapse while jointly enhancing both reasoning and verification abilities
claim c3 [qualitative]: As a policy, PAG enhances direct generation and self-correction accuracy; as a verifier, its self-verification outperforms self-consistency
claim c4 [qualitative]: PAG is a simple and effective framework that empowers LLMs to self-correct by alternating between policy and verifier roles within a unified multi-turn reinforcement learning paradigm without requiring separate verifier models or multi-stage self-correction training pipelines

Position: Use Sparse Autoencoders to Discover Unknowns (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, concept-discovery, interpretability, monosemanticity, autointerpretation
This is a position paper that does not propose a novel system. It analyzes existing sparse autoencoder (SAE) methods applied to language model representations. SAEs train an encoder-decoder pair on LM token activations: the encoder computes a sparse latent representation (only top-k dimensions nonzero) that reconstructs the original representation via the decoder. The paper argues SAEs excel at discovering unknown concepts (enumerating interpretable latent features that satisfy some criterion) rather than acting on known concepts (steering or probing for pre-specified concepts).
claim c1 [theoretical]: SAEs may be less effective for acting on known concepts, but are especially powerful tools for discovering unknown concepts.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Negative SAE results in concept detection and model steering show that classifiers trained on SAE latent representations do not outperform classifiers trained directly on language model representations.
claim c3 [qualitative]: In hypothesis generation on news headlines and Congressional speeches, SAE-derived hypotheses about concepts predicting engagement outperform hypotheses generated by text embedding feature selection, LLM prompting, topic modeling, or n-grams.
claim c4 [qualitative]: SAE features trained on language model representations yield monosemantic neurons that can be explained by a single concept, whereas language model neurons are polysemantic and encode multiple concepts at once.
claim c5 [qualitative]: In mechanistic explanation of LM outputs (biology of LLMs), SAEs reveal that language models represent specific task-relevant concepts: e.g., 'words rhyming with it' when composing rhyming couplets, and 'units digit 5' and 'addition problems of 40 plus 50' when performing arithmetic.
claim c6 [negative]: SAE reconstructions of LM representations encode strictly less information than the original LM representation, which explains why SAEs underperform on concept detection tasks.
claim c7 [qualitative]: SAEs can be applied to discover unknown concepts that bias LLM outputs in fairness/auditing tasks, to identify symptoms predicting clinical outcomes in health sciences, to trace language change in social sciences, and to close the prediction-explanation gap by converting black-box representations into interpretable ones.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Concept bottleneck models and other concept-based architectures require concepts to be prespecified before training (either by domain experts or language models), whereas SAE concepts are learned during training without prior specification.

SAFER: Probing Safety in Reward Models with Sparse Autoencoder (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, interpretability, reward-model, contrastive-features, safety-alignment, data-poisoning, data-denoising
SAFER applies Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to the hidden-state activations of reward models trained on safety-preference data. The system extracts monosemantic features from reward model layers, then uses contrastive analysis (activation differences between chosen and rejected responses) to identify safety-relevant features.
