These feature-level signals guide targeted manipulation of preference datasets (poisoning by label-flipping high-safety-alignment pairs, denoising by removing low-alignment pairs) to either degrade or enhance reward model safety alignment without sacrificing chat performance.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAEs uncover human-interpretable features in reward model activations, enabling insight into safety-relevant decision-making.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Over 80% of sampled features received identical safety relevance scores from GPT-4o and human annotators (difference = 0), and around 15% showed minor deviations (difference = 1 or 2), demonstrating high Human-GPT-4o alignment.
claim c3 [qualitative]: SAFER group of top-ranked contrastive features exhibits significantly higher mean and median safety relevance scores (concentrated near maximum score of 5) compared to randomly sampled features.
claim c4 [qualitative]: SAFER achieves safety degradation of 83.11% (1B) and 86.89% (3B) at 0.5% poisoning rate while maintaining chat performance at 89.11% (1B) and 90.78% (3B), substantially outperforming Random baseline (93.24% / 91.90%) and matching Reward-based baseline on safety while preserving chat far better.
claim c5 [quantitative]: SAFER achieves the highest safety score of 94.20% at 4% denoising for Llama-3.2-1B-RM and 96.46% at 4% for 3B, significantly outperforming Random and Reward-based baselines while maintaining competitive chat performance.
claim c6 [negative]: Removing more than 4% of data (at higher denoising ratios beyond the optimal point) causes safety performance to stabilize or fluctuate mildly rather than increasing monotonically, as more aggressive removal inevitably discards informative yet rare boundary cases that support generalization.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Ablation studies show that omitting the safety-oriented fine-tuning stage of the SAE weakens the model's ability to extract safety-relevant features, leading to smaller improvements in safety alignment, and using token-level training results in less discriminative representations compared to sentence-final token focus.
claim c8 [qualitative]: SAFER identifies 32 safety-related features (rated 5 by GPT-4o) from 16,384 total SAE features, with Feature #1472 (top-ranked, contrastive score ~5.0) capturing content that engages in or promotes harmful, explicit, or discriminatory material, demonstrating interpretability of reward model decision-making.

DR.EHR: Dense Retrieval for Electronic Health Record with Knowledge Injection and Synthetic Data (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: knowledge-injection, biomedical-kg, synthetic-data, contrastive-learning, dense-retrieval, ehr-domain
DR.EHR is a series of dense retrieval models (110M and 7B parameters) trained via a two-stage pipeline on MIMIC-IV discharge summaries. Stage I injects medical knowledge from the BIOS biomedical knowledge graph by identifying entity mentions, reducing abbreviations via LLM, and enriching positive samples with synonyms, hypernyms, and related entities. Stage II fine-tunes using LLM-generated synthetic training data (diseases, procedures, drugs) to optimize for entity retrieval. Both stages use contrastive learning with Multi-Similarity Loss and in-batch negatives. The small variant initializes from bge-base-en-v1.5 (BERT-based, [CLS] pooling); the large variant initializes from NV-Embed-v2 (Mistral decoder, last-token pooling). Text representations are compared via cosine similarity.
claim c1 [quantitative]: DR.EHR-small with 110M parameters outperforms all existing dense retrievers, achieving MAP of 89.12 on Single-Patient Retrieval compared to NV-Embed-v2's 80.21
claim c2 [quantitative]: DR.EHR-large with 7B parameters achieves Recall@100 of 67.04 on Multi-Patient Retrieval compared to NV-Embed-v2's 51.54
claim c3 [quantitative]: DR.EHR-small achieves 97.34 average score on string matching compared to bge-base-en-v1.5's 86.75
claim c4 [quantitative]: DR.EHR-small achieves 83.37 average score on abbreviation matching compared to bge-base-en-v1.5's 57.15 (26% improvement)
claim c5 [quantitative]: Stage I knowledge injection training improves Recall@100 on Multi-Patient Retrieval by approximately 5% across semantic match types when compared to w/o stage I baseline
claim c6 [quantitative]: Manual evaluation of 50 randomly sampled note chunks containing 831 LLM-generated entities revealed that 709 entities (85%) are clinically validated
claim c7 [quantitative]: Training pipeline generates over 5.8M note chunks from 332k MIMIC-IV discharge summaries, with Stage I producing 802M total positive samples and Stage II producing 86M positive samples
claim c8 [qualitative]: Models demonstrate strong generalizability to natural language questions including complex ones with multiple entities, despite training exclusively on single-entity queries

Model-Agnostic Gender Bias Control for Text-to-Image Generation via Sparse Autoencoder (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, activation-steering, gender-bias-mitigation, sparse-latent-direction, inference-time-intervention, model-agnostic
SAE Debias trains a k-sparse autoencoder on residual features extracted from the penultimate layer of a frozen CLIP text encoder (used by T2I diffusion models). The sparse autoencoder learns to represent gender and profession information in a sparse latent space. At inference time, the method computes gender-relevant directions by averaging male and female sparse latents per profession, then subtracts a scaled multiple of these directions from the residual features at the job token position before feeding them into the diffusion model's cross-attention, steering generation toward gender-balanced outputs without retraining the base model.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On SD 1.4, our method significantly reduces the mismatch rate across all metrics, achieving 0.06 overall mismatch compared to the baseline 0.84.
claim c2 [quantitative]: For SD 2.1, our method consistently lowers the overall mismatch rate from 0.78 to 0.60 and improves the neutral prompt skewness.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On SDXL, our method reduces the skewness by over 6%.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Our method achieves the best skew values across all models, reaching an overall mismatch rate of just [0.06] on SD 1.4 while maintaining strong performance on SD 1.5 and SD 2.1.
claim c5 [qualitative]: SAE Debias is model-agnostic and can be applied to diffusion models that utilize CLIP-family text encoders, without requiring any model-specific adjustments.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Our method preserves semantic alignment and image quality; CLIP and IS scores remain comparable before and after debiasing.
claim c7 [negative]: While our current work focuses on binary gender due to the structure of the Bias in Bios dataset, future extensions toward more inclusive gender representations are important directions for expanding fairness in generative AI.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Our framework requires training only once on a lightweight SAE. It operates entirely at inference time and can be easily integrated into existing text-to-image pipelines without additional fine-tuning.

The Bidirectional Process Reward Model (2025)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, bidirectional-evaluation, prompt-reversal, dynamic-gating, reasoning-verification, step-level-scoring, context-fusion
BiPRM augments standard Process Reward Models with a parallel right-to-left (R2L) evaluation stream alongside the conventional left-to-right (L2R) flow. The R2L stream is implemented via prompt reversal of the reasoning trajectory with no architectural changes to the underlying LLM. A lightweight gating mechanism (MLP) computes position-dependent weights to adaptively fuse reward scores from both streams, producing a final bidirectional reward per step. The trajectory-level reward applies a reduction operation (minimum) over step-wise scores.
claim c1 [quantitative]: BiPRM achieves an average relative gain of 10.6% over 54 solution-level configurations compared to unidirectional L2R baseline
claim c2 [quantitative]: BiPRM achieves 37.7% relative improvement in step-level error detection F1 score compared to L2R baseline
claim c3 [qualitative]: BiPRM introduces only 0.3% parameter increase for the gating module compared to L2R-PRM baseline
claim c4 [qualitative]: Parallel execution of bidirectional streams incurs approximately 5% wall-clock inference time latency increase compared to unidirectional L2R baseline
claim c5 [quantitative]: On Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B backbone, BiPRM achieves average F1 score of 30.2 on ProcessBench step-level error detection, compared to L2R baseline of 24.6
claim c6 [qualitative]: BiPRM dynamically learns to rely more on the R2L stream in early stages of reasoning (0%-30%) and progressively shifts focus to the L2R stream in later stages (70%-100%), as evidenced by monotonically increasing gating weights
claim c7 [qualitative]: BiPRM consistently maintains lower mean absolute error (MAE) throughout the entire reasoning trajectory compared to unidirectional L2R and R2L baselines, particularly suppressing error spikes in the transition zone around the average error onset (52.2% normalized progress)
claim c8 [negative]: Bidirectional evaluation mechanism necessitates processing the input sequence twice, leading to a two-fold increase in theoretical floating-point operations (FLOPs) compared to unidirectional baselines

VRPRM: Process Reward Modeling via Visual Reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, chain-of-thought, multimodal, cot-prl, best-of-n-decoding, rl-scaling, two-stage-training
VRPRM is a multimodal process reward model (PRM) that augments visual reasoning with chain-of-thought (CoT) capability. It uses a two-stage training pipeline: (1) Cold-start SFT with 3.6K high-quality CoT-PRM data (synthesized via Claude-3.7-Sonnet prompting) to activate reasoning and process evaluation; (2) RL scaling with 50K non-CoT PRM data using GRPO to reinforce step-level accuracy. The model reads image + problem + solution, generates explicit reasoning chains within <think></think> tokens, evaluates each solution step, and outputs structured JSON with per-step correctness scores. At inference, it predicts the probability of token '1' as the reward for each step, enabling best-of-N decoding by selecting the highest-scoring response.
claim c1 [quantitative]: VRPRM surpasses non-thinking PRM with 400K training data while using only 3.6K CoT-PRM SFT data and 50K non-CoT PRM RL training data (total 53.6K)
claim c2 [quantitative]: VRPRM achieves up to 118% relative performance improvement over the base model in the BoN (Best-of-N) experiment
claim c3 [qualitative]: VRPRM-MiMo achieves average AEI of 66.44 and average FEI of 51.54, outperforming VisualPRM by 5.41 in AEI and 25.93 in FEI
claim c4 [quantitative]: VRPRM w/o RL, trained on only 3.6K samples, achieved average AEI of 62.11, outperforming all other open-source and proprietary models except MiMo-VL-7B
claim c5 [quantitative]: Removing CoT causes Average All Error Identification to drop from 66.00 to 53.66 for VRPRM-Qwen, and First Error Identification to decline from 49.72 to 38.14
claim c6 [quantitative]: RL training on non-CoT PRM data improves VRPRM performance by 3.92% average on VisualProcessBench, with gains across all sub-datasets
claim c7 [quantitative]: CoT reasoning increases average output tokens per sample from 10.03 to 339.73 and processing time from 0.39 to 16.94 seconds per sample
claim c8 [qualitative]: VRPRM provides transparent reasoning processes, transforming the model from a scorer to a white-box trustworthy verifier

GM-PRM: A Generative Multimodal Process Reward Model for Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-model, multimodal-reasoning, step-level-supervision, error-correction, best-of-n, test-time-scaling
GM-PRM is a generative multimodal process reward model that extends PRMs from passive binary verifiers to active reasoning collaborators. It takes multimodal mathematical problems (image + text) and step-by-step reasoning chains, and generates interpretable multi-aspect critiques (step intent, image alignment, reasoning logic) plus a corrected version of the first erroneous step. The corrected step is fed back into a policy MLLM via the Refined-BoN test-time framework, which iteratively refines flawed reasoning trajectories and resamples to improve solution diversity and correctness.
claim c1 [quantitative]: GM-PRM achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple multimodal math benchmarks, significantly boosting policy model performance with notable accuracy gains
claim c2 [quantitative]: GM-PRM requires only a 20K-sample training dataset for effective training
claim c3 [quantitative]: On WeMath, MiniCPM-V2.6-8B improves by +12.4 points with GM-PRM and Refined-BoN
claim c4 [quantitative]: Under Best-of-8 setting, GM-PRM delivers a gain of 4.9 points over Self-Consistency on MiniCPM-V2.6-8B on WeMath
claim c5 [quantitative]: Refined-BoN improves Pass@8 scores compared to standard BoN, increasing average Pass@8 values of MiniCPM-V2.6-8B by +0.9 points across five datasets
claim c6 [qualitative]: GM-PRM generalizes effectively to other types of multimodal mathematical problems even though it is primarily trained on plane geometry and function problems
claim c7 [qualitative]: Models with weaker baseline performance benefit more from the refinement mechanism of GM-PRM with Refined-BoN; MiniCPM-V2.6-8B demonstrates the most significant relative improvement (+21.0%), despite its lower initial score
claim c8 [negative]: MathVision improvements are more modest (+1.9–3.9 points), indicating that the visual reasoning component may already be relatively strong in baseline models

SONAR-LLM: Autoregressive Transformer that Thinks in Sentence Embeddings and Speaks in Tokens (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: sentence-level-embedding, frozen-decoder-bridge, cross-entropy-via-decoder, teacher-forcing, concept-compression
SONAR-LLM is a decoder-only Transformer that autoregressively predicts sequences of sentence-level embeddings in the frozen SONAR embedding space (1024-dimensional). At each step, given a prefix of sentence embeddings, the model outputs a continuous vector representing the next sentence embedding. This predicted embedding is decoded back to tokens via the frozen SONAR decoder using teacher-forcing, and token-level cross-entropy loss is computed against the ground-truth token sequence. Gradients flow backward through the decoder to train only the embedding-prediction transformer, keeping SONAR encoder and decoder frozen. Generation halts when predicted embedding similarity to a learned "End of sequence" token exceeds a threshold or a sentence limit is reached.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SONAR-LLM achieves a strong scaling exponent of 0.596, comparable to other embedding-based models, with R-squared exceeding 0.98 in scaling law fits across model sizes from 39M to 900M parameters.
claim c2 [qualitative]: In GPT-4o evaluations on 512 generated stories, SONAR-LLM 900M outperforms both diffusion-based and MSE-based LCM variants, achieving grammatical correctness of 7.1, creativity of 5.2, coherence of 6.0, and plot consistency of 5.2, compared to diffusion LCM (grammar 3.9, creativity 5.0, coherence 2.9, plot 2.8) and MSE LCM (grammar 2.1, creativity 3.0, coherence 1.3, plot 1.2).
claim c3 [qualitative]: SONAR-LLM 900M matches LLM performance on ROUGE-L with short prefixes (39.4 vs. 39.4) and achieves competitive METEOR (32.9 vs. 34.2). With longer context, SONAR-LLM attains comparable ROUGE-L (35.2 vs. 35.7), substantially outperforming MSE LCM (26.1 ROUGE-L) and diffusion LCM (31.2 ROUGE-L) on short prefixes.
claim c4 [qualitative]: On XSum, SONAR-LLM 1.3B achieves ROUGE-L of 18.5(0.7) and METEOR of 14.5(0.5). On CNN/DailyMail, SONAR-LLM 1.3B achieves ROUGE-L of 15.5(0.7) and METEOR of 10.0(0.4), substantially outperforming diffusion LCM (XSum: 12.0 ROUGE-L; CNN/DM: 10.1 ROUGE-L) and MSE LCM (XSum: 11.6 ROUGE-L; CNN/DM: 7.8 ROUGE-L).
claim c5 [quantitative]: On RULER needle-in-a-haystack task with unfrozen decoder, exact-match accuracy improves from approximately 45% to 82% (nearly doubling), indicating clear benefit for symbol-heavy long-context retrieval tasks.
claim c6 [qualitative]: For sequences beyond approximately 4096 tokens, SONAR-LLM surpasses standard token-level LLM in theoretical inference efficiency; up to 1 million tokens, SONAR-LLM shows almost linear growth in FLOPs (vs. quadratic for LLM), because it operates on compressed sequences of sentence embeddings scaled by the inverse square of average sentence length (60 tokens).
claim c7 [negative]: Unfreezing the SONAR decoder during pretraining consistently reduced validation cross-entropy by more than 10% but did not translate into statistically significant gains in downstream text quality on summarization benchmarks (XSum and CNN/DailyMail).
claim c8 [qualitative]: All models were trained using a cosine learning rate scheduler with experiments on two learning rates (1e-4 and 5e-4); SONAR-LLM performed optimally with 1e-4 while LLM, MSE-based LCM, and diffusion-based LCM performed better with 5e-4, indicating different optimization sensitivities across architectures.

Interpretable Reward Model via Sparse Autoencoder (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-autoencoder, interpretability, reward-model, feature-attribution, monosemantic-features, preference-control
SARM integrates a pretrained Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) into a reward model to enable interpretability. The system operates in two stages: (1) a pretrained SAE on sequence-level hidden activations from an intermediate layer of an LLM extracts sparse, monosemantic features; (2) this SAE encoder is frozen and attached to the corresponding layer of a reward model, projecting hidden states into a sparse feature space from which a learnable linear value head aggregates feature activations into a scalar reward score. Information flows from LLM hidden states → SAE encoder (frozen) → sparse latent features → weighted linear aggregation → scalar reward.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SARM-4B achieves the best overall score across all models on RewardBench 2, outperforming both open- and closed-source baselines, including models with significantly larger parameter counts
claim c2 [quantitative]: Replacing the pretrained SAE encoder with a randomly initialized linear layer results in a notable drop in overall performance
claim c3 [quantitative]: Token-level SAE pretraining achieves an overall score of 71.5, improving upon the random-init baseline but falling short of full sequence-level SARM
claim c4 [qualitative]: SARM facilitates direct feature-level attribution of reward assignments, enabling transparent and conceptually meaningful reward scores traced back to sparse, interpretable monosemantic features
claim c5 [qualitative]: Manipulating safety-related features through value head weight adjustment causes a rightward shift in reward distribution on safety-focused data while leaving unrelated data largely unchanged, demonstrating causally controllable and interpretable preference manipulation
claim c6 [qualitative]: SARM extracts human-interpretable features including Positive Features (e.g., ethical reasoning, factual accuracy, technical competence) and Negative Features (e.g., harmful instructions, unethical advice, offensive language) that align with value head weights
claim c7 [qualitative]: SARM enables dynamic adjustment to preference shifts through direct modification of value head weights without requiring costly multidimensional annotations
claim c8 [negative]: Incorporating SAE pretraining introduces additional computational overhead during training compared to standard scalar reward models, and the unsupervised nature of SAE training offers no guarantees that extracted features will align with human-desired concepts

Constrained Decoding of Diffusion LLMs with Context-Free Grammars (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, cfg-intersection, formal-language, diffusion-models, multi-region-infilling, verifier-loop
The system performs constrained decoding of diffusion language models by checking at each generation step whether a proposed token insertion maintains validity with respect to a context-free grammar (CFG). The mechanism works by reducing the constrained decoding problem to deciding whether the intersection of the target CFG and a regular language (representing all possible completions of the partial output) is non-empty. At each step, the diffusion model proposes inserting a token at a specific location in the partial output; the system constructs a DFA encoding all valid completions, intersects it with the CFG, and either accepts the proposal (if intersection is non-empty) or rejects and resamples. The system optionally completes aborted generations by sampling uniformly from the intersection language.
claim c1 [quantitative]: For multi-region infilling on 1-mri, constrained decoding increases syntactic correctness by an absolute increase ranging between 6.2 and 9.5 percentage points per model
claim c2 [quantitative]: For 2-mri, constrained decoding increases syntactic correctness from 51.5–66.3% (vanilla) to 72.9–86.5% (constrained), with completion reaching 93.1–99.0%
claim c3 [quantitative]: For diffusion language models on C++, constrained decoding increases syntactic correctness from 11.0–40.5% (vanilla) to 19.0–58.7% (constrained -), and to 99.2–99.7% with completion
claim c4 [quantitative]: For JSON structured data extraction with diffusion models, constrained decoding with completion achieves 100% syntactic correctness across all tested models
claim c5 [quantitative]: For 1-mri infilling, functional correctness (pass@1) increases on average from vanilla to constrained decoding by 2.3 percentage points, and with completion to 2.8 percentage points
claim c6 [quantitative]: Median runtime overhead of constrained decoding is 51%, with overhead on 1.3B models higher (75%) than on 7B models (50%) and 33B models (48%)
claim c7 [quantitative]: For diffusion language models, median completion overhead is only 6%, with speed-ups observed up to 60% (when decoding is preemptively aborted) and slowdowns up to 80%
claim c8 [negative]: Remaining syntax errors when using constrained decoding without completion (Con.-) are attributed to overapproximation: the regular language construction allows arbitrary token fills, but in practice the model is limited by maximum token budget (fim/mri) or mask tokens (dlm); accurately modeling remaining tokens increases intersection language size prohibitively, rendering the method too expensive

Advancing Cross-lingual Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis with LLMs and Constrained Decoding for Sequence-to-Sequence Models (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, sequence-to-sequence, cross-lingual-transfer, schema-guided-generation, aspect-based-sentiment
A sequence-to-sequence approach for cross-lingual aspect-based sentiment analysis that fine-tunes multilingual encoder-decoder models (mT5, mBART) on source-language data and applies them to target languages. Element markers ([A] for aspect, [C] for category, [P] for polarity) guide output generation. Constrained decoding dynamically restricts token candidates at each generation step based on the current state and schema information, preventing the model from generating aspect terms in the source language instead of the target language.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Constrained decoding improves cross-lingual ABSA performance by up to 10%
claim c2 [quantitative]: mT5 with constrained decoding achieves F1 of 69.3±1.0 on E2E-ABSA Spanish cross-lingual zero-shot
claim c3 [quantitative]: mT5 with constrained decoding outperforms GPT-4o mini by approximately 20% on the TASD task across all languages in zero-shot cross-lingual settings
claim c4 [quantitative]: LLaMA 3.1 fine-tuned achieves F1 of 77.4±0.5 on E2E-ABSA Spanish monolingual
claim c5 [quantitative]: mT5 requires 4.40 times less training time per epoch and 4.33 times less inference time per example than LLaMA 3.1
claim c6 [qualitative]: Fine-tuned multilingual LLaMA 3.1 achieves comparable results to mT5 with constrained decoding in cross-lingual ABSA, while English-centric LLaMA 3 struggles with cross-lingual tasks
claim c7 [negative]: GPT-4o mini shows notably lower performance on ACTE and TASD tasks involving aspect category identification, with performance gaps of 10–20% even with few-shot prompts
claim c8 [negative]: Results for Turkish are consistently worse than for other languages, likely because it is the only language in the evaluation not from the Indo-European family

Improving Generative Cross-lingual Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis with Constrained Decoding (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, seq2seq, multi-task, cross-lingual-transfer, generation
Sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder models (mT5, mBART) fine-tuned for cross-lingual aspect-based sentiment analysis. During decoding, a scheme-guided constrained decoding module restricts the token generation space dynamically based on task state (e.g., aspect terms constrained to input sentence tokens, categories to predefined set, polarities to {great, ok, bad}), ensuring output adheres to expected format. Multi-task variants use task-specific markers in the input prompt to handle multiple ABSA tasks with a single model. LLMs (LLaMA 2/3, Orca 2, ChatGPT) are evaluated in zero-shot, few-shot, and QLoRA fine-tuning modes.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Constrained decoding improves cross-lingual performance by 5% on average for the most complex task (TASD)
claim c2 [quantitative]: Multi-tasking with constrained decoding boosts results by more than 10%
claim c3 [quantitative]: mT5 with constrained decoding significantly outperforms prior ATE baselines with improvements exceeding 20% and even 30% in most cases
claim c4 [qualitative]: LLMs perform poorly in zero-shot and few-shot settings for compound ABSA tasks, but fine-tuning achieves competitive results compared to smaller multilingual models, albeit at the cost of longer training and inference times
claim c5 [qualitative]: Constrained decoding mitigates the problem where models correctly identify aspect terms but in the source language instead of the target language, by restricting available tokens to only those from the input target language sentence
claim c6 [qualitative]: The proposed sequence-to-sequence approach eliminates the need for external translation tools, which are unreliable and may introduce errors in cross-lingual ABSA tasks
claim c7 [negative]: For the ACD task, constrained decoding does not significantly improve results since aspect categories are always predicted in English, resulting in no generation problems
claim c8 [negative]: Constrained decoding does not significantly enhance monolingual results, as the model has no problems generating the aspect terms in a given source language

Correctness-Guaranteed Code Generation via Constrained Decoding (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, context-sensitive-parsing, tree-of-parsers, token-healing, adaptive-rejection-sampling, semantic-correctness
A constrained decoding system that pairs a language model with a context-sensitive parser (Tree of Parsers) to generate semantically correct programs. At each decoding step, the parser outputs a regex satisfying a non-extensibility property; this regex guides token generation via adaptive rejection sampling. The parser maintains a dynamic tree of modular context-free grammar nodes enriched with semantic context (variable scopes, type constraints); as tokens are consumed, parser branches are pruned to keep only valid continuation paths. A token healing procedure handles misalignment when LM tokens cross regex boundaries.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The context-sensitive parser outputs a regex at each step that satisfies the non-extensible match property (A1), which ensures the LM can confidently stop generation once the regex is matched and advance the parser state.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Adaptive rejection sampling achieves more than three times faster generation speed compared to alternatives (Willard & Louf 2023; Koo et al. 2024) when used in Algorithm 1.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Our method with constrained decoding achieves the highest success rate and quality scores in generating DCI talent categories compared to unconstrained Claude-3.5-Sonnet and Qwen2.5-32B-Coder, with 33 data points in the highest quality range (90-100) versus Claude-3.5-Sonnet's 38 data points when both use reflection.
claim c4 [qualitative]: The paper's method failed only when generation did not terminate after the output token limit, typically leading to indefinite repetition due to constrained decoding distorting the LM distribution.
claim c5 [negative]: Algorithm 1 itself is not guaranteed to terminate; nontermination is a well-known issue in constrained decoding and part of the broader issue of distribution distortion.
claim c6 [theoretical]: For sLua with bounded variable count, nesting depth, and scope nesting, next_regex and has_finished functions have constant time complexity, and feed_text has linear time complexity in input size (Theorem 5.1).
claim c7 [theoretical]: Scripts successfully generated for talents and effects in DCI using Algorithm 1 are guaranteed to terminate and execute without runtime errors in the game engine, given the API design and language feature restrictions (Theorem 6.1).
claim c8 [qualitative]: The method can generate semantically correct programs conforming to any prescribed scripting API, with correctness defined by the parser's grammar and semantic constraints (variable scopes, type constraints).

Constrained Decoding for Safe Robot Navigation Foundation Models (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, safety-enforcement, temporal-logic, stl-robustness, inference-time-intervention
SafeDec is a constrained decoding framework for autoregressive transformer-based robot navigation policies. At inference time, it intercepts the logit distributions over discrete action primitives and either masks or reweights candidate actions using formal specifications (Signal Temporal Logic) and an approximate dynamics model. An STL robustness evaluator scores each candidate action by simulating its effect on future state trajectories; this score modulates token probabilities before softmax. The method operates entirely at the decoding layer without retraining the base policy, remaining agnostic to how the policy was trained.
claim c1 [quantitative]: HCD achieves perfect STL satisfaction with improvements ranging from 16-29 percentage points over unconstrained baselines
claim c2 [qualitative]: RCD achieves STL satisfaction improvements of 8-24 percentage points over unconstrained baselines while preserving success rates much closer to unconstrained level with drops of 0.5–6.5 percentage points
claim c3 [quantitative]: HCD reduces safety cost from 0.205 to 0.015 when combined with ISA (SafeVLA) on fragile-object and dangerous-object specifications
claim c4 [qualitative]: HCD ensures full compliance but occasionally sacrifices task success due to strict truncation; RCD's soft penalization leads to higher task success while maintaining reasonable STL satisfaction
claim c5 [qualitative]: SafeDec shows graceful degradation under significant per-step disturbances (5% translational noise, 3.3% rotational noise per step) with relatively small drops in STL satisfaction rates
claim c6 [qualitative]: Inference overhead of STL evaluation via STLCG++ is minimal at approximately 0.005 s per timestep
claim c7 [negative]: SafeDec assumes access to specifications defined over state space and requires localization modules for accurate state estimation, which can be difficult to design and a limiting factor for practical deployment
claim c8 [negative]: SafeDec requires access to an approximate dynamics model to evaluate the impact of actions on future trajectories, which can limit applicability

Mechanistic Interpretability with Sparse Autoencoder Neural Operators (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: sparse-autoencoders, neural-operators, fourier-parameterization, function-spaces, joint-sparsity, mechanistic-interpretability
Sparse Autoencoder Neural Operators (SAE-NOs) extend sparse autoencoders from fixed-dimensional Euclidean representations to function spaces. The system parameterizes concepts as functions rather than scalar activations, using Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) as the instantiation (SAE-FNOs). Information flow: input data are lifted to a higher-dimensional space via a learned linear operator, passed through a Fourier-based encoder that produces sparse codes with dual sparsity (concept sparsity selecting active concepts, domain sparsity governing spatial/domain expression), and reconstructed via a Fourier integral operator decoder. Concepts are learned as integral operators in the Fourier domain, enabling resolution-invariant and spatially-structured representations that capture not only whether concepts are active but how and where they are expressed across the input domain.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAE-FNO learns localized patterns, uses concepts more efficiently, and exhibits stable concept characteristics across sparsity levels.
claim c2 [qualitative]: SAE-FNO adapts to changes in domain size and generalizes across discretizations, operating at resolutions beyond those seen during training, where standard SAEs fail.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Lifting acts as a preconditioner that accelerates optimization and improves concept learning, with learned concepts faithfully recoverable in the original domain.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Through domain sparsity and Fourier-based functional representations, SAE-FNO naturally reuses concepts across translated temporally varying patterns by shifting spatial activations, maintaining stable concept sets without temporal memory or sequence-level training constraints.
claim c5 [qualitative]: SAE-FNO achieves higher expressivity per concept and requires fewer effective concepts to represent the data population compared to SAE-MLP.
claim c6 [qualitative]: SAE-FNO learns a broader range of concept correlations than SAE-MLP, where moderately correlated concepts can differ in how and where they are expressed across the domain, resulting in reduced interference.
claim c7 [qualitative]: SAE-FNO exhibits substantially smaller degradation than SAE-MLP under high-frequency perturbations, demonstrating greater stability under frequency-structured noise.
claim c8 [negative]: SAE-FNO introduces higher computational and memory costs than SAE-MLPs due to Fourier operations and feature-map representations, and introduces two additional hyperparameters (number of modes and domain sparsity).

SLICET5: Static Program Slicing using Language Models with Copy Mechanism and Constrained Decoding (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: copy-mechanism, constrained-decoding, lexical-constraint, syntactic-constraint, seq2seq, program-slicing, ast-based-verification
SliceT5 reformulates static program slicing as a sequence-to-sequence task using lightweight Transformer-based language models (CodeT5, CodeT5+). The system augments the base model with a copy mechanism that computes copy probability at each decoding step to directly transfer tokens from input to output, improving handling of rare identifiers and dependency capture. During inference, constrained beam search enforces two constraints: lexical constraint restricts output tokens to those present in the input, and syntactic constraint uses Tree Similarity of Edit Distance (TSED) monotonicity to detect and discard structurally invalid outputs. The copy mechanism operates via cross-attention weights to compute context vectors, which are concatenated with decoder input and passed through a learnable linear copy module to produce interpolated generation and copy distributions.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SliceT5 with CodeT5+ improves ExactMatch by 6.4% on CodeNet and 27% on LeetCode compared to the best-performing baseline (NS-slicer)
claim c2 [quantitative]: SliceT5 with CodeT5+ achieves 98.32% Accuracy-D, 91.30% ExactMatch, 92.31% CodeBLEU, and 97.06% TSED on CodeNet
claim c3 [quantitative]: On the out-of-domain LeetCode dataset, SliceT5 with CodeT5+ achieves 70.85% Accuracy-D, 14% ExactMatch, 60.40% CodeBLEU, and 59.97% TSED
claim c4 [quantitative]: Without the copy mechanism, SliceT5 (CodeT5+) ExactMatch drops from 14% to 4% on out-of-domain LeetCode dataset
claim c5 [quantitative]: Without the lexical constraint, SliceT5 (CodeT5+) ExactMatch drops from 14% to 7% on out-of-domain LeetCode dataset
claim c6 [qualitative]: All components (copy mechanism, lexical constraint, and syntactic constraint) make significant contributions to SliceT5's performance, with copy mechanism and lexical constraint being the most impactful across datasets and metrics
claim c7 [qualitative]: SliceT5 demonstrates strong performance on incomplete code snippets (missing class encapsulation, missing semicolons, missing or unmatched braces), consistently outperforming state-of-the-art baselines and showing robustness in real-world development environments
claim c8 [negative]: Foundation models (GPT-4o-mini, Gemma-7B) achieve near-zero ExactMatch scores (0% on CodeNet with all prompting strategies), suffering from hallucinations and misinterpretation of control flow structures despite techniques like Chain-of-Thought and Retrieval-Augmented Generation

GRPO is Secretly a Process Reward Model (2025)
seam: text; tags: process-reward-models, rl-credit-assignment, monte-carlo-estimation, grpo-algorithm, mathematical-reasoning
The paper proves theoretically that Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an RL algorithm for LLM fine-tuning that uses outcome-level rewards and relative advantage computation within trajectory groups, is mathematically equivalent to a Process Reward Model (PRM)-aware objective. The key mechanism is that when trajectories within a group share overlapping prefixes, GRPO implicitly assigns step-level (process) rewards computed as Monte Carlo estimates—the mean outcome reward of all trajectories sharing each prefix. The paper then proposes λ-GRPO, which adds a process-step-frequency normalization factor to mitigate a discovered flaw in GRPO's advantage computation that can hinder exploration and exploitation under imbalanced process step frequencies.
claim c1 [theoretical]: GRPO equipped with an outcome reward model is mathematically equivalent to a PRM-aware RL objective equipped with a non-trivial Monte-Carlo-based PRM, where process rewards are computed as the mean outcome reward of trajectories sharing each prefix, given mild assumptions (DAPO token-level objective and β fixed per batch).
claim c2 [qualitative]: GRPO's implicit PRM is non-trivial in practice: overlapping trajectory prefixes within groups arise very frequently under real-world conditions.
claim c3 [quantitative]: In real-world GRPO training on OpenRS math-reasoning dataset with group sizes of 6 and 36, 99.8% of groups generated non-trivial process reward structures (6,688 out of 6,700 groups) with group size 6, and 100% (1,100 out of 1,100 groups) with group size 36.
claim c4 [qualitative]: GRPO's advantage computation has a flaw that interacts with imbalanced process step frequencies and rewards to hinder both exploration and exploitation under different conditions.
claim c5 [qualitative]: On a synthetic binary-tree environment with imbalanced process rewards, λ-GRPO converges on the target high-reward path more frequently than standard GRPO across all hyperparameter configurations tested (ε ∈ {-0.5, -1.0, -1.5}, K ∈ {1, 2}).
claim c6 [qualitative]: λ-GRPO-tuned models outperformed standard GRPO on 15 out of 20 downstream reasoning task cells (AIME24, MATH-500, AMC23, Minerva, OlympiadBench) across two model sizes (Qwen-1.5B and Llama-1B) and two KL coefficient settings.
claim c7 [quantitative]: All four λ-GRPO models reached higher validation accuracy in fewer training steps than their GRPO-tuned counterparts, with λ-GRPO representing more than 10% increase in validation accuracy in less than half the number of training steps on average.
claim c8 [qualitative]: λ-GRPO introduces negligible computational overhead: measured wall-clock overhead on a single Intel i7 CPU was 8.38–10.27 seconds per training run (amounting to ~1e-6–1e-5 seconds/token).

Enhancing LLM Steering through Sparse Autoencoder-Based Vector Refinement (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: steering-vectors, sae-interpretability, activation-steering, feature-denoising, semantic-selection, residual-stream-injection
SAE-RSV refines steering vectors learned from small datasets by leveraging a pretrained Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to identify and remove task-irrelevant noising features, then retrieves missing task-relevant features based on semantic similarity. The system operates on hidden activations from Llama-3-8B-Instruct (layer 25, 65K-dim SAE space), using an LLM (GPT-4o-mini) to judge feature semantics and construct textual explanations. Refined steering vectors are injected into the residual stream at inference time via a linear combination of the original steering vector, a noise-subtraction term, and an augmentation term.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SAE-RSV achieves superior performance across all five datasets, with success rate consistently surpassing all baselines; outperforms original prompt by average of 18.8%
claim c2 [quantitative]: SAE-RSV improves success rate by over 10% compared to CAA on four out of five datasets
claim c3 [qualitative]: SAE-RSV yields substantially higher success rates across all tasks compared to LoRA-SFT (supervised fine-tuning)
claim c4 [quantitative]: Over 93.6% of features captured by original steering vectors are noisy features; only 42.2% of all task-relevant features are captured by original steering vectors
claim c5 [quantitative]: Augmentation module yields average 7.2% increase in success rate (from 45.6% to 52.8%); Denoising module yields average 3.6% increase (from 45.6% to 49.2%)
claim c6 [quantitative]: Optimal steering performance achieved with 15–20 task-relevant features; CAA captures less than half of all useful features (7.6 vs 18.0 features on average)
claim c7 [qualitative]: With only 10 training samples, SAE-RSV matches the performance of CAA trained on 1000 samples
claim c8 [negative]: Denoising module reduces entropy (generation diversity/fluency) by 0.11; overly aggressive filtering collapses model hidden representation distributions into a single direction, leading to more repetitive text generations

SCI-Verifier: Scientific Verifier with Thinking (2025)
seam: text; tags: cot-reasoning, verification, equivalence-judgment, sft-rl-pipeline, cross-domain, scientific-qa
SCI-Verifier is a reasoning-augmented LLM-based verifier for scientific answer equivalence judgment. It takes a question, reference answer, and model-generated response as input and produces a binary correctness label (true/false) preceded by concise chain-of-thought reasoning. Information flows through a two-stage post-training pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning transfers short, curated reasoning traces from larger models into a smaller base model (Qwen3-4B or 8B), and (2) reinforcement learning (DAPO) refines reasoning quality while penalizing verbosity. The model outputs structured reasoning followed by a final judgment, designed to be both accurate and computationally efficient.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SCI-Verifier-8B achieves 86.28% accuracy on SCI-VerifyBench
claim c2 [qualitative]: On equivalence-based answers from SCI-VerifyBench, SCI-Verifier-8B achieves substantially higher performance than GPT-5, particularly in mathematics and physics where GPT-5 scores drop below 50%
claim c3 [quantitative]: SCI-Verifier-8B achieves 93.01% accuracy on VerifierBench
claim c4 [quantitative]: SCI-Verifier-8B achieves 90.30% accuracy on VerifyBench-Hard
claim c5 [qualitative]: Chain-of-thought reasoning brings substantial gains in scientific verification across models of different scales
claim c6 [qualitative]: SCI-Verifier exhibits strong robustness to prompt modifications, maintaining competitive performance even when the prompt differs from those seen during training, whereas general models are considerably more sensitive to prompt variations
claim c7 [qualitative]: Combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning leads to consistently superior performance and better generalization across different datasets compared to SFT or RL alone
claim c8 [qualitative]: SCI-VerifyBench covers five disciplines (mathematics, physics, chemistry, biology, general QA) with 2,500 total samples (500 per domain) including 350 real LLM responses and 150 equivalence-based synthetic responses per domain, constructed with domain-specific equivalence transformations and hybrid LLM/expert annotations

Hyperdimensional Probe: Decoding LLM Representations via Vector Symbolic Architectures (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vsa, hypervector-algebra, supervised-probe, concept-extraction, embedding-compression, unbinding-operation
The Hyperdimensional Probe is a hybrid supervised probe that maps LLM residual stream embeddings into Vector Symbolic Architecture (VSA) encodings via a trained neural encoder. The pipeline: (1) extracts embeddings from LLM forward passes, (2) compresses via k-means clustering and sum pooling to reduce dimensionality, (3) trains a shallow feedforward MLP (55M–71M parameters) to map compressed embeddings to bipolar hypervectors, and (4) queries the VSA proxy space using hypervector unbinding operations to extract atomic concepts. Information flows from token embeddings through dimensionality reduction, then through the neural VSA encoder into a bounded, queryable symbolic space where concepts are retrieved via algebraic operations.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Neural VSA encoder achieves test-set average cosine similarity of 0.88 between predicted and target VSA encodings
claim c2 [quantitative]: Neural VSA encoder achieves average binary accuracy of 0.96 on binarized VSA encodings
claim c3 [quantitative]: VSA-based probing achieves average precision@1 of 83% in extracting target concepts from LLM embeddings across all models
claim c4 [quantitative]: VSA probing extracts target concepts with their keys in 76% of cases on average (KeyTarget category), compared to DLA producing no concepts in 29.5% of analogies
claim c5 [quantitative]: In instances where DLA produced no concepts (29.5% of cases), VSA probing extracted the key-target pair in 57.4% of instances
claim c6 [qualitative]: VSA-based concept extraction reveals varying conceptual richness across models and input types: larger models (OLMo-2) extract richer concept sets including context and target together (4.4%), while smaller models (GPT-2) rely more on input-related concepts (22% extract no concepts). Mathematical analogies show highest rate of empty representations (88%), while morphological analogies show lowest (1.1%).
claim c7 [quantitative]: On SQuAD question-answering task, neural VSA encoder achieves test-set cosine similarity of 0.44 and binary accuracy of 0.70 for Llama 3.1-8B
claim c8 [negative]: Largest model evaluated (Llama 4 Scout, 109B parameters) exhibited lowest precision@1 in next-token prediction (8%), even underperforming legacy GPT-2-medium, despite strong probing performance, indicating discrepancy between internal concept encoding and output generation likely caused by tokenization and prompt design factors

Parallel Test-Time Scaling for Latent Reasoning Models (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: test-time-scaling, latent-reasoning, monte-carlo-dropout, additive-gaussian-noise, latent-reward-model, contrastive-learning, trajectory-aggregation
A parallel test-time scaling framework for latent reasoning models that operates in continuous hidden-state space rather than token space. The system generates multiple stochastic latent reasoning trajectories via two sampling methods (Monte Carlo Dropout or Additive Gaussian Noise applied to intermediate hidden states), then aggregates them using a learned Latent Reward Model (LatentRM) that scores each latent thought via step-wise contrastive supervision. Information flow: input → stochastic latent trajectory generation (multiple samples) → LatentRM scoring of intermediate steps → trajectory selection (best-of-N or beam search) → final answer decoding.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Both Monte Carlo Dropout and Additive Gaussian Noise enable parallel test-time scaling for latent reasoning models by introducing controlled stochasticity into continuous latent space, addressing the absence of sampling mechanisms and probabilistic signals in latent models.
claim c2 [qualitative]: MC-dropout consistently achieves higher coverage across nearly all sample sizes, with coverage increasing monotonically as sample count increases, showing saturation effects at larger N.
claim c3 [qualitative]: At N=64 samples, COCONUT and CODI achieve nearly equivalent coverage despite CODI's clear superiority at N=1, indicating that increased sampling narrows inter-model performance gaps.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Best-of-N selection and Beam Search guided by LatentRM consistently outperform Majority Voting across compute budgets, with gains most pronounced on MultiArith.
claim c5 [quantitative]: On RoT-4B with MATH500, the framework improves from 20.3% to 22.0% accuracy with N=16 sampled trajectories.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Monte Carlo Dropout produces directional drift with dense contiguous exploration, while Additive Gaussian Noise yields isotropic radial dispersion; MC-dropout excels on harder tasks by larger displacement toward distant correct regions, whereas AGN maintains robustness by preserving probability mass near the deterministic center.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Ablation on LatentRM shows step-wise contrastive loss contributes 1.9% improvement over BCE on GSM-Test and 0.4% on GSM-Hard; removing stochastic rollouts causes further 2.8% and 1.4% drops respectively; random untrained head performs below Majority Voting.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Latent reasoning combined with parallel TTS demonstrates superior efficiency compared to explicit Chain-of-Thought, achieving competitive performance with significantly fewer decoding steps while maintaining wall-clock latency advantages on GPU inference.

Diffusion-Inspired Masked Fine-Tuning for Knowledge Injection in Autoregressive LLMs (2025)
seam: text; tags: masked-reconstruction, demasking-objective, knowledge-injection, reversal-curse-mitigation, fine-tuning-paradigm
Masked fine-tuning for autoregressive LLMs (arLLMs) reframes knowledge documents as token-reconstruction tasks. During fine-tuning, a fraction of tokens in a document are replaced with [MASK]; the model is prompted to recover the full passage. The training objective (Eq. 2) computes autoregressive loss only on unmasked tokens in the assistant response. Mask ratios are sampled uniformly across epochs, providing varied conditioning patterns. At inference, the model is evaluated in standard autoregressive mode using default chat templates. The mechanism transfers the bidirectional learning signal of diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) into decoder-only arLLMs without architectural changes.
claim c1 [qualitative]: On NameDescription dataset, arLLM without paraphrases achieves 0.264 forward accuracy and 0.029 backward accuracy; with paraphrases, forward reaches 0.954 but backward remains 0.031.
claim c2 [qualitative]: dLLM (LLaDA-8B-Instruct) without paraphrases achieves 0.869 forward and 0.852 backward accuracy on NameDescription; with paraphrases, 0.980 forward and 0.984 backward.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Masked fine-tuning for arLLM on NameDescription achieves high accuracy in both forward and backward directions without paraphrases, closing the gap to dLLM performance.
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Webscale-RL (1.2M samples), masked-AR SFT achieves 0.389 GPQA-diamond accuracy, outperforming standard SFT AR at 0.359 and base model at 0.242.
claim c5 [qualitative]: On GSM8K, Llama-3.2-3B masked SFT achieves 0.735 pass@1, improving over baseline 0.686. On MATH, Llama-3.2-3B masked SFT achieves 0.290 pass@1 versus baseline 0.258.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Masked fine-tuning for arLLMs does not require paraphrase augmentation to generalize knowledge to question-answering, in contrast to standard autoregressive fine-tuning.
claim c7 [qualitative]: dLLMs are substantially less reliant on paraphrase augmentation and exhibit markedly reduced reversal-curse behavior compared to arLLMs during post-training knowledge injection.
claim c8 [quantitative]: Masked fine-tuning doubles per-step FLOPs but converges 2× faster, yielding comparable or faster total training compute than standard arLLM and dLLM fine-tuning.

Understanding Parametric Knowledge Injection in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: parametric-rag, lora-injection, knowledge-encoding, ffn-routing, hybrid-token-param
The paper studies parametric RAG (P-RAG), which encodes retrieved documents into lightweight trainable LoRA adapter parameters injected into a frozen LLM, rather than concatenating documents as tokens. P-RAG uses data augmentation (document rewrites + QA pairs) to train per-document LoRA modules via gradient-based optimization. DyP-RAG extends this by learning a hypernetwork that maps document embeddings directly to LoRA weights at inference time. The paper also evaluates hybrid PT-RAG, which combines parametric injection with explicit token-based context. Information flows through parameter updates to the FFN layers during generation, enabling knowledge integration without expanding context length.
claim c1 [qualitative]: PT-RAG achieves the best overall performance across datasets when combining parametric injection with token-based context
claim c2 [quantitative]: P-RAG does not consistently outperform T-RAG under HasAnswer accuracy metric, contrary to prior F1-based findings
claim c3 [qualitative]: P-RAG successfully encodes factual knowledge and significantly outperforms Vanilla LLM when tested on out-of-distribution news articles from 2025 (beyond LLM training cutoff)
claim c4 [negative]: DyP-RAG fails to demonstrate meaningful gains and shows no statistically significant difference from Vanilla LLM in new-knowledge settings, suggesting dynamically generated parameters do not genuinely capture document-specific factual content
claim c5 [qualitative]: Parametric representations exhibit specificity at a coarse level (relevant passages show higher similarity than irrelevant ones) but suffer from limited encoding fidelity compared to token-based embeddings, with heavily overlapping similarity distributions
claim c6 [qualitative]: Parametric injection increases knowledge contribution (measured by Parametric Knowledge Score) concentrated sharply in deep FFN layers, indicating it primarily provides high-level semantic guidance rather than fine-grained lexical details
claim c7 [qualitative]: Parametric RAG demonstrates improved faithfulness under knowledge conflicts (when retrieved evidence conflicts with LLM internal knowledge), stronger robustness to retrieval noise compared to T-RAG, and solid generalization to tasks beyond QA such as fact verification and slot filling
claim c8 [negative]: P-RAG alone is substantially weaker than T-RAG at consolidating evidence and reasoning over multi-hop passages; only when combined with explicit context in PT-RAG does parametric injection enable multi-hop reasoning gains

Generative Universal Verifier as Multimodal Meta-Reasoner (2025)
seam: text; tags: visual-verification, rl-training, test-time-scaling, iterative-refinement, multimodal-verifier, image-generation-editing, world-modeling
OmniVerifier is a generative verifier system that audits visual outcomes (generated images, intermediate visual states) during multimodal reasoning and generation. The system consists of three components: (1) ViVerBench, a 3,594-sample benchmark for evaluating visual verification across 16 tasks in 6 categories; (2) OmniVerifier-7B, a 7B-parameter vision-language model trained via RL (DAPO) on Qwen2.5-VL-7B to perform binary true/false judgments with textual explanations on image-prompt alignment; (3) OmniVerifier-TTS, a sequential test-time scaling loop where OmniVerifier judges a generated image, outputs an edit prompt if misaligned, and feeds that prompt back to a unified multimodal model for iterative refinement. The seam is text: verifications and edit guidance cross the model boundary as natural language tokens.
claim c1 [quantitative]: OmniVerifier-7B achieves an 8.3% overall performance enhancement on ViVerBench rule-based evaluation, surpassing GPT-4o and achieving capabilities comparable to Qwen2.5-VL-72B.
claim c2 [quantitative]: OmniVerifier-TTS achieves +3.7 improvement on T2I-ReasonBench on Qwen-Image baseline.
claim c3 [quantitative]: OmniVerifier-TTS achieves +4.3 improvement on GenEval++ on Qwen-Image baseline.
claim c4 [quantitative]: ViVerBench comprises 3,594 data samples curated through manual annotation by 12 domain experts, expert review, and human evaluation across 16 tasks in 6 categories.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Three fundamental atomic capabilities underlying visual-outcome verification are identified: explicit alignment, relational verification, and integrative reasoning, which mutually generalize and interact synergistically via reinforcement learning.
claim c6 [qualitative]: State-of-the-art VLMs (including Gemini 2.5 Pro, Seed 1.5-VL) exhibit three general limitations on visual verification: weakness in fine-grained and challenging image-prompt alignment, mismatched representation of world knowledge, and underdeveloped critics for visual reasoning tasks.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Sequential test-time scaling (OmniVerifier-TTS) outperforms parallel test-time scaling (Best-of-N) across reasoning-based and compositional-based generation benchmarks, while requiring approximately 47% fewer generation steps per prompt.
claim c8 [negative]: Training on maze verification data alone exhibits minimal generalization to other tasks, attributed to sparse and discrete synthetic patterns contrasting sharply with natural images, creating a significant distribution gap that offers limited transferable signal.

LLM Latent Reasoning as Chain of Superposition (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, superposition-embedding, gumbel-softmax-regularization, chain-of-thought-compression, activation-projection, multi-path-reasoning
Latent-SFT is a three-level framework for training LLMs to perform efficient latent reasoning. At the token level, latent tokens are defined as superpositions within the pre-trained vocabulary embedding space (Latent-Vocab), projected via softmax onto vocabulary logits and reconstructed as linear combinations of token embeddings. At the chain level, explicit reasoning chains are segmented and compressed via Latent Token Induction Mask (LTIM) to create dense representations, then supervised via Latent Token Supervision Mask (LTSuM) to ensure sufficiency for decoding subsequent steps. At the learning level, an EM-style alternating optimization first optimizes an encoder to construct latent chains, then trains the LLM backbone to generate them, followed by joint fine-tuning. During autonomous generation, Latent-Optim uses stochastic Gumbel-Softmax perturbation of target distributions to act as an implicit Hessian regularizer, guiding the model toward flatter minima and enabling generalization across multiple reasoning paths.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Latent-SFT(2) consistently outperforms explicit CoT-SFT in Pass@1 scores on nearly all mathematical reasoning datasets, with average Pass@1 improvement of 2.0% while reducing average reasoning chain length by approximately 5.5× on LLaMA-3.2-1B-Instruct
claim c2 [quantitative]: Latent-SFT on DeepSeek-Distill-Qwen-7B surpasses explicit CoT-SFT in Pass@1 performance on high-difficulty tasks while reducing reasoning chain length by 2.7–3.6×
claim c3 [quantitative]: Latent-SFT on DeepSeek-Distill-Qwen-7B achieves Pass@1 of 79.8±1.35 on MATH-500 with 759.3±36.9 token reasoning chain length, outperforming COT-SFT (Pass@1 73.8±1.32, # L 2766±319)
claim c4 [quantitative]: Latent-SFT on DeepSeek-Distill-Qwen-7B achieves Pass@1 of 19.2±1.60 on AIME24 with 1484±420 token reasoning chain length, compared to COT-SFT (Pass@1 17.7±1.23, # L 4016±343)
claim c5 [qualitative]: Removing either Latent Token Induction Mask (LTIM) or Latent Token Supervision Mask (LTSuM) leads to a significant accuracy drop of approximately 5%, confirming their critical role in maintaining structural integrity of the reasoning chain
claim c6 [qualitative]: Replacing Superposition Embedding with raw hidden states degrades performance, demonstrating the importance of aligning latent tokens with the intrinsic vocabulary manifold
claim c7 [qualitative]: Ablating Gumbel-noise injection (w/o Gumbel) leads to a noticeable decline in performance, confirming that stochasticity acts as a vital regularizer to prevent overfitting and enhance generalization
claim c8 [qualitative]: Effective global parallelism (Neff) analysis shows that across models with different compression ratios, most samples exceed the conservative threshold for significant parallelism (1.7), with evidence distributed across roughly 3–4 reasoning paths, confirming latent reasoning reflects superposition of multiple reasoning chains rather than compression of a single path

AdapTrack: Constrained Decoding without Distorting LLM's Output Intent (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, rejection-sampling, backtracking, intent-preservation, validity-estimation
AdapTrack is a constrained decoding method for autoregressive language models that incorporates adaptive backtracking to preserve the model's output intent while satisfying hard constraints. It maintains per-prefix validity estimates and samples for each generated token, and uses rejection sampling to dynamically backtrack when the proportion of invalid continuations is large, allowing the model to explore alternative token sequences that better align with the underlying language model distribution while respecting constraints.
claim c1 [quantitative]: AdapTrack can achieve up to 360.87% improvement compared to constrained decoding on the synthetic API completion dataset (TFv1)
claim c2 [quantitative]: AdapTrack can achieve up to 38.93% improvement over constrained decoding on the real-world API completion dataset (TFv1Real)
claim c3 [quantitative]: AdapTrack achieves up to 7.84% improvement on HumanEval pass@1 compared to constrained decoding
claim c4 [quantitative]: AdapTrack achieves up to 6.42% improvement on MBPP pass@1 compared to constrained decoding
claim c5 [theoretical]: The distribution produced by AdapTrack aligns with the model's distribution given the generated tokens, satisfying the property that for any two valid complete sequences, their probability ratio under AdapTrack equals their probability ratio under the language model
claim c6 [qualitative]: Experiments on domain-specific language problems show that the distribution produced by AdapTrack has significantly lower KL divergence from the correct distribution compared to existing methods, indicating closer alignment with the actual model distribution
claim c7 [negative]: AdapTrack sometimes performs worse than unconstrained decoding on pass@1 metrics (e.g., StarCoder2 7B and CodeLlama 7B on MBPP), attributed to constrainer limitations
claim c8 [qualitative]: Constrained decoding distorts the model's output intent by forcing the model to generate code that satisfies constraints but deviates from the model's expected functionality and contextual semantics, as exemplified by the PyTorch matrix_rank case where constrained decoding produces matrix_power instead of linalg.matrix_rank

AtlasKV: Augmenting LLMs with Billion-Scale Knowledge Graphs in 20GB VRAM (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: kv-injection, knowledge-graph, hierarchical-pruning, parametric-augmentation, attention-steering
AtlasKV augments frozen LLMs with billion-scale knowledge graphs by converting KG triples into learned key-value (KV) pairs injected into transformer self-attention layers. The system consists of two components: (1) KG2KV, which converts each KG triple into query-key-value training data by masking entities and rewriting relations into noun forms, then encoding these strings with a sentence encoder; (2) HiKVP, a hierarchical pruning algorithm that organizes KG keys into a 3-layer tree (using UMAP + GMM clustering), stores most data on CPU, and performs multi-stage top-k filtering during inference to select relevant KV pairs for GPU-based attention computation. Information flows from KG triples → Q-K-V strings → sentence embeddings → hierarchical clustering → (at inference) multi-layer pruning with CPU/GPU offloading → pruned KV injection into LLM attention heads.
claim c1 [quantitative]: AtlasKV requires less than 20GB VRAM to augment LLMs with 1 billion KG triples, compared to over 40GB VRAM for KBLaM with 100K triples
claim c2 [quantitative]: AtlasKV achieves Top-1 accuracy of 87.3% on ATLAS-Pes2o-QKV with 1M triples (128 pruning), compared to KBLaM's 25.5% at 3K training steps
claim c3 [quantitative]: KG2KV achieves a diversity ratio of 7.864% compared to synthetic method's 0.003% on training data enquiry attributes
claim c4 [quantitative]: KG2KV requires average token cost of 165.7 per triple compared to synthetic method's 349.9
claim c5 [quantitative]: AtlasKV achieves GPT-4o relevance score of approximately 0.85–0.90 on multiple datasets, outperforming KBLaM's scores below 0.75
claim c6 [qualitative]: AtlasKV achieves sub-linear time complexity O(k·log N) and memory complexity O(k·log N) during inference, compared to KBLaM's linear O(N) where N is KG size and k is pruning budget
claim c7 [qualitative]: AtlasKV requires only 3K–20K training steps (on ATLAS-Wiki-QKV) compared to KBLaM's 20K steps, and generalizes to OOD datasets (ATLAS-Pes2o-QKV, ATLAS-CC-QKV) without retraining
claim c8 [negative]: AtlasKV with HiKVP pruning (128-64-16) shows non-negligible performance drop compared to AtlasKV without HiKVP (e.g., ACC@1 from 92.7% to 87.3% on 1M triples, ATLAS-Pes2o-QKV), although authors attribute this to model's ability to perform fuzzy retrieval across hierarchical layers

KORE: Enhancing Knowledge Injection for Large Multimodal Models via Knowledge-Oriented Controls (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: parameter-efficient-finetuning, knowledge-injection, continual-learning, catastrophic-forgetting-mitigation, covariance-null-space, data-augmentation, kv-projection
KORE is a two-stage knowledge injection method for large multimodal models (LMMs) that combines knowledge-oriented augmentation and knowledge-oriented constraint. In stage 1, KORE-augmentation automatically expands individual knowledge items into multi-round dialogues and instruction tasks, creating structured training data. In stage 2, KORE-constraint stores previous knowledge in the covariance matrix of LMM linear layer activations, decomposes it via SVD to extract null space, and initializes LoRA adapters by projecting original weights into this null space. This constrains fine-tuning to a direction that minimally interferes with prior knowledge while enabling new knowledge injection.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Kore (rank=235) achieves improvements in knowledge adaptation (K.A) on EVOKE, outperforming LoRA by more than twofold
claim c2 [qualitative]: Kore (rank=235) achieves improvements of in Avg score and in HARS over the best baseline
claim c3 [qualitative]: On LLaVA-v1.5 (13B), Kore surpasses LoRA across all dimensions, achieving improvements of in Avg and in HARS over Replay
claim c4 [qualitative]: On Qwen2.5-VL (7B), Kore achieves improvements of in Avg and in HARS over Replay
claim c5 [qualitative]: Ablation study: W/o Augmentation decreases knowledge adaptation (K.A) by a substantial margin
claim c6 [qualitative]: Kore-augmentation outperforms general augmentation methods for text (knowledge-aware) by in K.A, in Avg and in HARS
claim c7 [qualitative]: Covariance matrices of linear layer inputs in LMMs capture multimodal knowledge and exhibit distinct outlier patterns for related tasks, indicating that distinct tasks exhibit different outlier distributions in the covariance matrix
claim c8 [negative]: Existing methods (full fine-tuning, PEFT) often lead to catastrophic forgetting of pre-existing knowledge and struggle to achieve robust generalization

MASPRM: Multi-Agent System Process Reward Model (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: process-reward-model, multi-agent-search, mcts-guidance, beam-search, credit-assignment, transcript-scoring
MASPRM is a process reward model that scores intermediate multi-agent system (MAS) states and agent actions conditioned on full routed transcript prefixes, while agents generate actions from their local partial views. The system is trained via MAS-MCTS rollouts using terminal outcome rewards backed up to local action targets without manual step-level annotations. At inference, MASPRM guides search via step-level beam search (SBS) and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) using Bradley-Terry preference ranking to score successor states, focusing computation on promising branches and pruning unpromising ones.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Averaged across GSM8K, MATH, MMLU, and LogiQA, MASPRM improves Hit@1 over policy likelihood by up to +13.4 points
claim c2 [quantitative]: MASPRM improves ranking quality, reducing Hit@1→Hit@5 gaps by up to 10.3 points
claim c3 [quantitative]: MASPRM-guided MCTS(10,3) achieves Hit@1 of 82.9 on GSM8K vs 62.5 for policy likelihood under matched agent calls
claim c4 [quantitative]: Cross-domain MASPRM (trained on GSM8K, tested on MATH) degrades by 5.4 points vs in-domain but improves by +5.4 points vs policy likelihood baseline
claim c5 [quantitative]: MASPRM-7B with MCTS(10,3) is competitive with Mistral 24B-Instruct on GSM8K (82.9 vs 80.4) and outperforms GPT-4o-mini (82.9 vs 62.0)
claim c6 [qualitative]: MASPRM is trained from MAS-MCTS rollouts labeled only with terminal outcome rewards, without requiring human step-level annotations, by propagating returns to local targets via sibling preference pairs
claim c7 [qualitative]: Bradley-Terry preference ranking head outperforms MSE regression by +3.2 to +4.3 points on math benchmarks for the 1.5B model and by +1.2 to +9.6 points for the 7B model under MCTS(10,3)
claim c8 [negative]: Training requires verifiable terminal outcomes; extending to imperfect verifiers is noted as an important direction. Study is limited to scheduled MAS with outcome supervision; generality to open-ended interactive environments or real-world deployments is not claimed.

Continuous Autoregressive Language Models (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-vector-prediction, energy-based-generation, strictly-proper-scoring, vae-compression, likelihood-free-training, rejection-sampling-temperature
CALM reframes language modeling from discrete next-token prediction to continuous next-vector prediction. A context-free variational autoencoder (VAE) compresses K tokens into a single continuous latent vector with >99.9% reconstruction accuracy. A standard Transformer backbone processes the discrete token stream and outputs a hidden state, which an energy-based generative head (stacked residual MLPs) uses to predict the next latent vector via a strictly proper scoring rule (energy score). The predicted vector is decoded back to discrete tokens via the frozen VAE decoder, completing the autoregressive loop. Information flows: discrete tokens → VAE encoder → latent vector → Transformer → energy head → predicted latent vector → VAE decoder → reconstructed tokens.
claim c1 [qualitative]: a latent vector of just 32 dimensions is sufficient to achieve high-fidelity reconstruction, with a token-level accuracy of over 99.9% when grouping K=4 tokens
claim c2 [qualitative]: CALM significantly improves the performance-compute trade-off, achieving the performance of strong discrete baselines at a significantly lower computational cost
claim c3 [qualitative]: BrierLM exhibits a Pearson correlation coefficient of -0.966 and a Spearman's rank correlation of -0.991 with cross-entropy loss
claim c4 [qualitative]: reduces the number of generative steps by a factor of K
claim c5 [qualitative]: the autoencoder is exceptionally lightweight; with a shallow architecture and a modest hidden dimension of 512, its computational overhead is nearly negligible compared to that of language model
claim c6 [theoretical]: Algorithm 1 (likelihood-free temperature sampling via rejection) generates samples distributed exactly according to the target temperature distribution for any arbitrary temperature τ
claim c7 [theoretical]: BrierLM is strictly proper, theoretically ensuring a fair comparison of model capabilities
claim c8 [negative]: a naive metric like the raw likelihood of the observed outcome fails the principle of being uniquely optimized when the model recovers the data distribution, instead incentivizing overconfident predictions

SparseRM: A Lightweight Preference Modeling with Sparse Autoencoder (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, preference-modeling, activation-projection, reward-model, interpretability
SparseRM extracts preference-relevant features from LLM intermediate representations using Sparse Autoencoders (SAE). The system identifies salient latent directions in SAE decompositions by comparing activation frequencies between preferred and dispreferred samples, projects input representations onto these directions via inner products, concatenates the projection vectors into a preference-aware vector, and trains a single-layer MLP reward head on these vectors to predict preference scores. The mechanism operates on frozen LLM representations; only the SAE and reward head are trained.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SparseRM achieves the highest accuracy on TruthfulQA and outperforms most baselines on SafeRLHF and Red-Teaming while using less than 1% of trainable parameters
claim c2 [quantitative]: On Gemma-2-9B-it with TruthfulQA MC1, SparseRM achieves 65.2% accuracy
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Gemma-2-9B-it alignment task, SparseRM achieves 79.9% accuracy on SafeRLHF
claim c4 [qualitative]: SparseRM achieves strong generalization across distribution shifts between supervised preference pairs and model-generated data, suggesting that extracted subspace captures fundamental, preference-relevant features that remain stable regardless of input data distribution changes
claim c5 [qualitative]: Average cosine similarity between generated and training data is significantly lower in dense space (DenseRM) compared to sparse space (SparseRM), indicating more substantial distributional shift in dense representations
claim c6 [qualitative]: Margin loss consistently outperforms binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss and Bradley-Terry (BT) loss across all three datasets in reward modeling
claim c7 [qualitative]: Directly using SAE latent activation values as input to reward head results in limited performance due to low representational capacity of sparse vectors; computing inner products between intermediate representations and selected latent directions produces better preference representation
claim c8 [qualitative]: SAE-extracted sparse representations exhibit clearer separation between positive and negative responses compared to dense representations, and SparseRM more reliably identifies and filters out noisy samples than DenseRM

DPRM: A Dual Implicit Process Reward Model in Multi-Hop Question Answering (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: implicit-prm, dual-modality, co-training, kg-constrained, iterative-reasoning, best-of-n-decoding, preference-pairs
DPRM trains two implicit process reward models (KG-PRM and CoT-PRM) that derive step-level rewards from outcome signals via reward parameterization, without explicit step-level annotations. KG-PRM learns structural constraints from knowledge graph preference pairs; CoT-PRM learns topological patterns through co-training on KG-derived data. During reasoning, both PRMs evaluate intermediate steps (KG triples and CoT steps) using Best-of-N selection, with KG paths guiding CoT generation and entities in CoT steps guiding next KG path selection in an iterative loop until a final answer is reached.
claim c1 [quantitative]: DPRM achieves up to 16.6% improvement on Hit@1
claim c2 [qualitative]: On WebQSP, DPRM achieves F1 score of 0.7228 and Hit@1 of 0.8715
claim c3 [qualitative]: On CWQ, DPRM achieves F1 score of 0.5988 and Hit@1 of 0.7301
claim c4 [qualitative]: Removing co-training drops F1 on CWQ by 0.0569 and Hit@1 by 0.0477
claim c5 [quantitative]: DPRM achieves 92.3% of full-data performance using only one-eighth of the training data (1,000 preference pairs per PRM)
claim c6 [qualitative]: KG-PRM learns to capture topological constraints for long chain reasoning via path preference pairs that include factual errors, logical errors, and logical break patterns
claim c7 [qualitative]: Co-training enables CoT-PRM to capture topological patterns for long-chain reasoning and enables KG-PRM to learn from relational paths absent in the original KG
claim c8 [theoretical]: Implicit PRMs can derive step-level process rewards from outcome signals through reward parameterization: process reward at step equals the difference between exponential averages of outcome reward, without explicit step-level annotations

SpiralThinker: Latent Reasoning through an Iterative Process with Text-Latent Interleaving (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, iterative-refinement, activation-steering, text-latent-interleaving, alignment-objective, knowledge-distillation
SpiralThinker is a latent reasoning framework built on Llama-3.2-1B that performs iterative refinement of latent representations while interleaving explicit textual and implicit latent reasoning steps. The system operates in two stages: (1) explicit reasoning fine-tuning, where the model learns step-by-step textual reasoning with <bot>/<eot> boundary tokens; (2) implicit reasoning, where special <latent> tokens (delimited by <bol>/<eol>) replace alternating textual steps in training data. During inference, latent tokens are updated across multiple iterations via a lightweight adapter that projects hidden states back to embedding space, then rewritten into the sequence for subsequent forward passes. A progressive alignment objective constrains latent representations at each iteration to align with their textual counterparts extracted from a frozen reference model, stabilizing updates and maintaining coherence between implicit and explicit reasoning.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SpiralThinker achieves 56.56% accuracy on GSM8K-Aug, surpassing the previous best (Pause Token) by 3.19 points
claim c2 [quantitative]: SpiralThinker achieves 99.40% accuracy on ProsQA, surpassing the previous best by 0.40 points
claim c3 [quantitative]: SpiralThinker achieves 63.32% accuracy on StrategyQA, exceeding the previous best by 0.44 points
claim c4 [quantitative]: On GSM8K-Aug, combining both iterative process and alignment objective yields +11.07 points over baseline with neither component
claim c5 [negative]: Employing the iterative process alone yields limited improvement and can even degrade performance, suggesting that iterative updates in latent representation space may drift when left unconstrained
