Unguided models default to shallow error correction; when forced into a structured taxonomy, the 8B parameter model lacks the cognitive capacity to dig for deep logical flaws but becomes exceptionally effective at identifying and patching surface-level syntax discrepancies.
claim c8 [theoretical]: These findings expose an 'alignment tax' inherent to constrained decoding, highlighting a tension between structural granularity and internal model capacity in autonomous workflows. Enforcing structured reflection via grammar-constrained decoding introduces an alignment tax when deployed without external training.

Decompose, Look, and Reason: Reinforced Latent Reasoning for VLMs (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, reinforcement-learning, spherical-geometry, multimodal-cot, premise-grounding, policy-gradient, hypersphere-manifold
DLR is a reinforced latent reasoning framework for vision-language models that interleaves three steps: (1) Decompose—the VLM generates textual premises marking what visual details require verification; (2) Look—a visual grounder, conditioned on the premise's hidden state, extracts continuous latent embeddings from image features via cross-attention; (3) Reason—the VLM generates rationales grounded in the injected latents and deduces answers. The framework couples a discrete text policy (VLM) and a continuous latent visual policy (grounder) through joint optimization across three training stages: pretraining for cross-modal alignment via contrastive loss, supervised finetuning to internalize the DLR format, and reinforcement learning with a novel Spherical Gaussian Latent Policy (SGLP) to enable stochastic exploration on the hyperspherical manifold of vision-language features.
claim c1 [quantitative]: DLR achieves 83.8 overall accuracy on V* Bench, outperforming the backbone by 4.2%, LVR by 1.6%, and PixelReasoner by 3.7%
claim c2 [quantitative]: On MathVista, DLR obtains 67.5, exceeding Qwen3-VL baseline by 5.0% and surpassing the strongest open-source baseline LVR by 3.4%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On MMMU-Pro, DLR improves over Qwen3-VL baseline by 5.9%
claim c4 [quantitative]: Removing the latent policy optimization (without ℒ_lat) leads to a drastic performance degradation on MathVista, where the score drops from 67.5% to 57.1%
claim c5 [quantitative]: Removing pretraining hurts performance across all benchmarks; compared with DLR-SFT, the full DLR achieves a 3.3% improvement on V*
claim c6 [qualitative]: DLR provides superior stepwise interpretability through premise-conditioned visual latents, enabling visualization of attention heatmaps that align retrieved latent evidence with image regions relevant to current reasoning subproblems
claim c7 [negative]: Evaluation mainly focuses on vision-centric (image-centric) reasoning benchmarks; generality of the framework to broader multimodal settings such as video reasoning, embodied multimodal decision-making, or tasks requiring richer external interaction remains to be validated
claim c8 [theoretical]: Theorem 3.1 (Latent Policy Effect): By maximizing the latent policy objective, the expected update of the visual grounder increases the alignment between mean direction and latent samples that receive high advantage, encouraging the latent policy to move toward semantically task-relevant directions on the hypersphere

SeLaR: Selective Latent Reasoning in Large Language Models (2026)
seam: text; tags: soft-embeddings, entropy-gating, contrastive-regularization, latent-reasoning, selective-activation, training-free
SeLaR is a training-free latent reasoning framework that selectively activates soft embeddings during language model decoding based on token-level entropy. At each step, the model computes normalized entropy over top-k tokens. If entropy falls below a learned threshold (deterministic step), standard discrete token decoding is used. If entropy exceeds the threshold (exploratory step), a soft embedding is constructed as a probability-weighted mixture of top-k token embeddings and passed to the next layer. An entropy-aware contrastive regularization component then pushes this soft embedding away from the dominant token's direction proportionally to entropy magnitude, sustaining exploration of multiple reasoning paths.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SeLaR achieves the highest average accuracy, improving upon CoT (Sampling) by +0.75%, +3.88%, and +2.45% on Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-8B, and Qwen3-32B, respectively.
claim c2 [qualitative]: On Qwen3-8B, SeLaR improves AIME 2024 from 76.67% to 83.33% (+6.66%) and AIME 2025 from 66.67% to 80.00% (+13.33%).
claim c3 [quantitative]: Removing selective activation (i.e., applying soft embeddings globally at every step) leads to an average performance drop of 5.19% (from 83.56% to 78.37%).
claim c4 [quantitative]: Removing contrastive regularization results in an average performance drop of 7.82% (from 83.56% to 75.74%), with AIME 2024 dropping from 83.33% to 70.00% and AIME 2025 dropping from 80.00% to 60.00%.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Exploratory steps (high entropy) account for 6.2%–13.8% of total reasoning tokens, averaging 10.0%, confirming that selective activation targets approximately one in ten tokens where exploration is most beneficial.
claim c6 [negative]: SeLaR's TPCA (Tokens per Correct Answer) is higher than CoT on GPQA, attributed to GPQA's knowledge-intensive nature, where latent exploration at uncertain decoding steps provides little benefit when correct answers depend on domain recall rather than multi-step reasoning.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Logit lens analysis shows that without contrastive regularization, the forward pass progressively collapses onto the top-1 trajectory and suppresses the top-2 alternative; with contrastive regularization, both top-1 and top-2 overlaps remain substantially non-zero and comparable in deep layers, confirming genuine sustained exploration of latent reasoning paths.
claim c8 [negative]: SeLaR yields larger improvements on base models with higher confidence (e.g., Qwen3-8B) than on those with lower confidence (e.g., DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B). Less confident models exhibit higher entropy more frequently, triggering excessive exploratory steps.

Schema Key Wording as an Instruction Channel in Structured Generation under Constrained Decoding (2026)
seam: text; tags: constrained-decoding, structured-generation, schema-as-instruction, multi-channel-prompting, json-schema, instruction-placement
The paper studies structured generation under constrained decoding, where LLMs produce JSON outputs that satisfy a predefined schema. The core mechanism treats schema-key wording as an implicit instruction channel: keys like 'step_by_step_reasoning' enter the autoregressive context and condition downstream value generation, separate from and interacting with prompt-level instructions. The system uses a constrained decoding backend (XGrammar) that masks invalid next tokens according to grammar, but the paper argues that schema keys—generated as part of the autoregressive sequence—act as instruction-bearing tokens, not merely neutral field names. Information flow is bidirectional: prompt text and schema keys both influence the model's distribution over continuations, and their effects can be complementary, redundant, or interfering depending on the model.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Schema-key wording is not a neutral formatting detail under constrained decoding; changing only the decoder-enforced schema key can noticeably change task accuracy.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Key-only instruction improves Qwen2.5-7B from 79.61 to 86.50 on GSM8K.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Key-only instruction decreases Llama-3.2-3B from 53.15 to 37.38 on GSM8K.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Qwen models tend to benefit more from schema-level instructions, whereas LLaMA models rely more on prompt-level guidance.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Prompt-level and schema-level instructions interact non-additively: the two channels can be complementary, redundant, or interfering rather than simply reinforcing each other.
claim c6 [theoretical]: A CoT-style schema key helps only when its semantic gain exceeds the distortion induced by grammar-constrained projection; this condition is formalized as a sufficient bound derived from Pinsker's inequality on KL divergence between unconstrained and constrained distributions.
claim c7 [negative]: The study focuses only on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K and Math500) and does not cover information extraction, tool use, code generation, data-to-text generation, or agent workflows.
claim c8 [negative]: Model coverage is limited to open-source Qwen and LLaMA families plus one Qwen-based distilled reasoning variant; observed instruction-channel sensitivities may not transfer to other model families, larger systems, or proprietary models.

AgentV-RL: Scaling Reward Modeling with Agentic Verifier (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: verifier-loop, multi-turn-reasoning, tool-use, bidirectional-checking, synthetic-data-gen, reinforcement-learning, test-time-scaling
AgentV-RL is a reward modeling framework that distills a multi-agent verification process into a single LLM. The system coordinates forward and backward agents that perform bidirectional verification: the forward agent traces solutions from premises to conclusions via multi-turn reasoning with tool use (Python interpreter), while the backward agent re-checks conclusions against problem constraints. A synthetic data engine generates verification trajectories, and a two-stage training recipe (rejection fine-tuning followed by GRPO reinforcement learning) trains the verifier to autonomously interleave tool use with internal reasoning for reliable solution assessment.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 79.0% accuracy on MATH500 at Best-of-N (BoN) sampling with N=128, surpassing the previous best outcome-level RM (Skywork-V2-Llama-8B) by 25.2 percentage points
claim c2 [quantitative]: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B reaches 53.3% accuracy on AIME24 at BoN N=128, while all baselines including larger models remain significantly lower
claim c3 [quantitative]: On GSM8K Turn 1, Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 94.6% accuracy with a correction rate (C_rate) of 41.6% and degradation rate (D_rate) of 0.6% for iterative refinement
claim c4 [quantitative]: Scaling model size from 0.6B to 4B parameters with Agentic Verifier yields improvement of 14.0 points on MATH500 at BoN N=128 (from 62.2% to 79.0%)
claim c5 [quantitative]: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 70.86% on LiveCodeBench and 66.00% on HotpotQA, demonstrating generalization beyond mathematics to competitive code and multihop QA
claim c6 [qualitative]: Agentic Verifier produces 8349 tokens over 11.3 rounds with 1.6 tool calls per verification trajectory, requiring 323.4 seconds latency on A100 GPU (batch size 128), compared to 2560 tokens and 119.0 seconds for the base model
claim c7 [qualitative]: The bidirectional design (forward + backward agents) achieves state-of-the-art performance; ablation shows both forward-only and backward-only variants are competitive but the full design performs best
claim c8 [negative]: The reliance on synthetic, tool-augmented data may not fully represent the variety of real-world reasoning problems, which impede generalization. The multi-turn process increases computational cost, presenting challenges for real-time or resource-limited deployment. Performance is tied to the coverage and reliability of external tools, which can be a bottleneck for certain tasks.

Thinking Without Words: Efficient Latent Reasoning with Abstract Chain-of-Thought (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: discrete-latent-reasoning, abstract-vocabulary, constrained-decoding, policy-iteration, warm-up-rl, information-bottleneck, self-distillation
Abstract Chain-of-Thought augments decoder-only LLMs with a reserved vocabulary of abstract tokens that function as discrete latent reasoning traces. During inference, the model generates a bounded sequence of abstract tokens (instead of natural language CoT) before producing a response. Information flows: prompt → abstract token sequence (constrained decoding) → response. The abstract tokens are trained via a two-stage pipeline: (1) policy iteration warm-up alternating between bottlenecked SFT (where response attends only to prompt and abstract tokens, not verbal CoT) and self-distillation without verbal guidance, and (2) warm-started RL (GRPO with constrained decoding) to refine abstract sequence generation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Abstract-CoT achieves up to 11.6× fewer reasoning tokens while demonstrating comparable performance across mathematical reasoning, instruction-following, and multi-hop reasoning
claim c2 [quantitative]: On MATH-500 with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 90.8% accuracy with 144 tokens, compared to SFT + RL baseline of 92.6% accuracy with 1671 tokens
claim c3 [quantitative]: On AlpacaEval with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 60.8 win-rate with 225 tokens, outperforming SFT + RL baseline of 58.4 win-rate with 496 tokens
claim c4 [quantitative]: On HotpotQA with Qwen3-8B, Abstract-CoT (Warm-up + RL) achieves 58.8 F1 with 171 tokens, compared to SFT + RL baseline of 58.1 F1 with 735 tokens
claim c5 [qualitative]: Abstract-CoT generalizes across model families: on MATH-500, Qwen3-4B achieves 89.8% accuracy with 141 tokens and Granite 4.0 Micro (3B) achieves 74.4% accuracy with 153 tokens, both substantially reducing tokens compared to their verbal CoT baselines
claim c6 [qualitative]: An emergent power law distribution over the abstract vocabulary is observed during RL training, evolving across the training phases, with a single dominant token leveraged across diverse settings and token usage converging to a Zipf-like distribution
claim c7 [negative]: Performing cold-start RL without warm-up is insufficient and often underperforms the base instruction-tuned model; warm-up alone lags behind SFT with verbal CoT on most benchmarks
claim c8 [qualitative]: Permutation ablation shows that abstract sequences exhibit learned compositional behavior: permuting abstract tokens at the token level reduces performance, with smaller performance drops than for verbal CoT permutation, indicating discrete ordering structure in the abstract reasoning language

MoRFI: Monotonic Sparse Autoencoder Feature Identification (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae-feature-identification, activation-steering, monotonic-trend-detection, residual-stream-intervention, knowledge-recovery, model-diffing
MoRFI is a two-stage mechanistic interpretability pipeline that identifies SAE latent features causally linked to fine-tuning-induced hallucinations in LLMs. Stage 1 (Algorithm 1) performs bootstrapped monotonic trend detection across a 4D activation tensor (samples × property configurations × latent features × fine-tuning epochs), using Spearman rank and Mann-Kendall tests to isolate features whose activations change monotonically with controlled properties (proportion of unknown facts, training epochs). Stage 2 (Algorithm 2) validates candidate latents through activation steering on a reference task, applying interventions to the residual stream and ranking by downstream performance impact. The pipeline bridges external SAE representations into model activation space via decoder projections, enabling causal attribution of knowledge degradation to sparse latent directions.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Incrementally introducing new knowledge increases hallucinations across Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B and Mistral 7B v03, with the effect being more pronounced with prolonged training.
claim c2 [quantitative]: 69–85% of facts recovered through single-latent steering interventions align with those accessible to a baseline fine-tuned on known facts.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Single-latent interventions sourced with MoRFI substantially outperform steering with the composite direction (difference-in-means SAE activations between known vs. unknown checkpoints).
claim c4 [qualitative]: For the Unknown direction, the best negatively steered latent gives +33.4 accuracy gain while the best positively steered one gives +21.7 for Llama 3.1 8B; for Mistral 7B the difference is +39.77 vs +24.91 and for Gemma 2 9B +18.46 vs +9.03.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Exposure to unknown facts disrupts the model's ability to retrieve stored knowledge along a set of directions in the residual stream; steering experiments suggest forgetting reflects disrupted access rather than erasure of parametric knowledge.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Within-group cosine similarity of top-performing latents is generally low (0.3), indicating that the top-performing latents span a distributed subspace at the middle layer rather than clustering along a shared low-rank structure.
claim c7 [negative]: Steering with control latents that exhibit no significant monotonic trend produces negligible or highly negative accuracy changes, whereas trending latents produce substantial gains, confirming that performance improvements are not artifacts of arbitrary residual stream perturbation.
claim c8 [qualitative]: MoRFI reliably discovers causally relevant latents across distinct models (Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B, Mistral 7B v03), and there is significant overlap between latents surfaced across the Unknown and Epochs dimensions, suggesting they capture a shared signal.

Static Program Slicing Using Language Models With Dataflow-Aware Pretraining and Constrained Decoding (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: seq2seq-code, dfg-pretraining, constrained-decoding, lexical-constraint, syntactic-constraint, tsed-monotonicity, hallucination-prevention
Sliceformer reformulates static program slicing as a sequence-to-sequence task using CodeT5+ (770M parameters). The system consists of three stages: (1) dataflow-aware pretraining on CodeSearchNet, which teaches the model to recognize data dependencies through statement permutation constrained by data-flow graphs and dataflow-guided span corruption at variable and statement granularities; (2) supervised fine-tuning on labeled slicing data from CodeNet-Slice with special control markers; (3) constrained beam search decoding that applies lexical constraints (restricting output vocabulary to input tokens) and syntactic constraints (using Tree Similarity Edit Distance monotonicity to filter structurally invalid candidates at statement boundaries). Information flows from source code + slicing criterion (input tokens) through the encoder-decoder model, then through constrained decoding that reads the original code vocabulary and computes AST edit distances to validate slice structure.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Sliceformer achieves ExactMatch improvements of 6.4% on Java and 21.9% on Python compared to the strongest baseline (NS-slicer with GraphCodeBERT)
claim c2 [quantitative]: On Java programs, Sliceformer attains ExactMatch of 92.20% versus NS-slicer (GraphCodeBERT) at 85.77%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Python programs, Sliceformer reaches ExactMatch of 83.15% compared to NS-slicer (GraphCodeBERT) at 61.25%
claim c4 [quantitative]: Sliceformer outperforms CodeT5+ (SFT) by 5.0% on Java and 5.9% on Python in ExactMatch
claim c5 [quantitative]: LLM-based methods (GPT-5 with CoT) achieve only 14.00% and 13.00% ExactMatch on Java and Python respectively
claim c6 [quantitative]: Sliceformer achieves average inference latency of 0.296 seconds per task on Java dataset
claim c7 [qualitative]: Dataflow-aware pretraining is designed for encoder-decoder architectures and is not directly applicable to decoder-only models, though constrained decoding is architecture-agnostic
claim c8 [negative]: Sliceformer is evaluated on only two programming languages (Java and Python); additional engineering and empirical validation are required to extend the method to other languages

Latent-GRPO: Group Relative Policy Optimization for Latent Reasoning (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, rl-post-training, grpo-adaptation, continuous-action-space, manifold-constrained-exploration, policy-optimization
Latent-GRPO is a reinforcement learning framework that optimizes latent reasoning by compressing intermediate reasoning steps into continuous vector representations (latent tokens) rather than explicit discrete tokens. The system builds on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and Soft-GRPO, adapting them to handle the unique challenges of continuous latent space: it initializes from a Latent-SFT model, applies invalid-sample advantage masking to prevent off-manifold exploration, uses one-sided Gumbel noise sampling to align optimization direction with trajectory advantage, and selects the optimal correct path's first token to avoid harmful mode averaging across multiple valid latent trajectories. Information flows from the LLM's top-k vocabulary distribution (converted to latent embeddings via mixture) through continuous latent reasoning steps, then into explicit discrete token generation for the final answer, with policy optimization applied via a masked PPO-style objective.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Directly adapting GRPO to latent reasoning is fundamentally non-trivial due to three coupled bottlenecks: absence of intrinsic latent manifolds where unconstrained exploration pushes rollouts off the valid latent manifold, exploration-optimization misalignment where trajectory-level rewards can induce incorrect token-level updates, and latent mixture non-closure where jointly reinforcing multiple correct latent paths can produce an invalid averaged state
claim c2 [quantitative]: On low-difficulty benchmarks, Latent-GRPO improves over its latent initialization by 7.86 Pass@1 points on average while using 4.44× shorter reasoning chains than explicit GRPO
claim c3 [quantitative]: On high-difficulty benchmarks, Latent-GRPO improves over its latent initialization by 14.77 Pass@1 points on average, outperforms explicit GRPO by 4.27 points, and uses 3.31× shorter reasoning chains
claim c4 [quantitative]: On GSM8K-Aug (low-difficulty), Latent-GRPO achieves Pass@1 of 66.29 with reasoning length 23.80, improving over Latent-SFT (47.55, 22.00) and surpassing explicit GRPO (62.26, 111.1)
claim c5 [quantitative]: On Math500 (high-difficulty), Latent-GRPO achieves Pass@1 of 80.40 with reasoning length 929, improving over Latent-SFT (64.85, 1070) and surpassing explicit GRPO (75.15, 2982)
claim c6 [quantitative]: Under Gumbel sampling at pass@k on AIME24, Latent-GRPO reaches 46.7 (pass@8) and 50.0 (pass@16), substantially outperforming explicit GRPO which reaches 23.3 (pass@16)
claim c7 [negative]: One-sided Noise Sampling is critical: removing this design causes validation performance to drop sharply and eventually collapse, with response length becoming much less controlled on both GSM8K-Aug and Math500
claim c8 [qualitative]: First Token Selection provides additional improvement beyond One-sided Noise Sampling, with milder degradation when removed on low-difficulty tasks but pronounced instability on high-difficulty reasoning problems

Reliable Answers for Recurring Questions: Boosting Text-to-SQL Accuracy with Template Constrained Decoding (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, template-matching, grammar-guidance, nli-verification, partitioned-generation, literal-masking
TeCoD is a Text-to-SQL system that addresses accuracy and latency challenges by converting historical NL-SQL pairs into reusable templates and enforcing them during generation. The system has three main components: (1) Template extraction and indexing, which masks literals in SQL and NLQs and creates vector embeddings; (2) Template selection, which uses a fine-tuned NLI model to match incoming user queries to stored templates with high precision; (3) SQL generation via partitioned grammar-constrained decoding, which pre-compiles template grammars into flexible formats and generates only query-specific literals at inference time while reusing static token sequences. Information flows from labeled workload data through template extraction, indexed for retrieval by semantic similarity and NLI entailment checking, then constrains LLM decoding via grammar-guided token masking and a two-phase partitioned strategy that separates query-invariant static parts from query-specific slots.
claim c1 [quantitative]: TeCoD yields up to 36% higher execution accuracy than in-context learning (ICL) on matched queries
claim c2 [quantitative]: TeCoD achieves close to 90% execution accuracy on matched queries across almost all databases, compared to much lower and variable accuracy with ZeroShot and ICL
claim c3 [quantitative]: 2.2x lower latency on matched queries with TeCoD
claim c4 [quantitative]: Template matcher accuracy improved from 73% to 91% through strategies of casting matching as NLI, masking literal parts of questions, and generating synthetic paraphrases
claim c5 [qualitative]: Real workload analysis of a very large bank shows that workloads contain clusters of highly similar SQLs, motivating the template-based approach
claim c6 [qualitative]: BIRD-dev set contains 61 templates covering 134 queries out of 1534 total queries; Spider-dev set contains 482 templates covering 966 queries out of 1034 total queries in real (non-synthesized) benchmark data
claim c7 [qualitative]: Flexible template grammar (allowing case variations, whitespace flexibility, and SQL formatting alternatives) improves accuracy over fixed string-based template constraints by accommodating different LLM formatting preferences
claim c8 [negative]: In-context learning disappoints in how well it harnesses related queries; LLMs can fail to generate correct SQL even in the presence of an in-context example with SQL differing only by a constant (demonstrated by two concrete examples)

SCPRM: A Schema-aware Cumulative Process Reward Model for Knowledge Graph Question Answering (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: process-reward-model, kgqa, mcts-integration, schema-aware, risk-sensitive, pairwise-ranking, a-star-inspired
SCPRM is a process reward model for knowledge graph question answering that decomposes reward estimation into cumulative past rewards and schema-aware future rewards. The cumulative past reward aggregates step-wise safety probabilities (via a risk head on LLM hidden states) using logarithmic summation to capture risk propagation without vanishing gradients. The future reward is computed by a schema head that measures the alignment gap between extracted query schema and induced reasoning schema, using a frozen embedding model as reference. Both components are trained via pairwise ranking losses on positive (correct, safe) and negative (incorrect or risky) reasoning paths. At inference, SCPRM serves as the value function in Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to guide multi-hop path exploration on knowledge graphs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SCPRM addresses the risk compensation effect in process reward models by using multiplicative penalty accumulation via logarithmic summation of step-wise safety probabilities, ensuring risky steps impose persistent penalties rather than being offset by later correct steps
claim c2 [qualitative]: SCPRM overcomes length bias in conditional reward models by incorporating schema-aware future rewards that measure alignment between query schema and reasoning schema, rather than relying on multiplicative probability aggregation
claim c3 [quantitative]: On pairwise ranking accuracy with Llama3.1-8B base model, SCPRM achieves 95.34% on Medical KGQA compared to PRM baseline at 91.83%
claim c4 [quantitative]: On pairwise ranking accuracy with Llama3.1-8B base model, SCPRM achieves 88.11% on Legal KGQA compared to PRM baseline at 86.70%
claim c5 [quantitative]: SCPRM-MCTS with Llama3.1-8B achieves Hits@3 of 85.02% on Medical KGQA, outperforming ToG with GPT-4o-mini at 83.06% and ReKG-MCTS with GPT-4o-mini at 82.47%
claim c6 [quantitative]: SCPRM-MCTS with Llama3.1-8B achieves Hits@3 of 82.32% on Legal KGQA, outperforming ToG with GPT-4o-mini at 80.69% and ReKG-MCTS with GPT-4o-mini at 81.26%
claim c7 [quantitative]: Ablation study shows removing cumulative reward (w/o CR) causes pairwise ranking accuracy to drop by 9.85 to 11.82 percentage points across datasets, and Hits@k to drop by 9.46 to 12.15 percentage points
claim c8 [qualitative]: SCPRM exhibits graceful degradation under schema noise, maintaining robust performance even at 0.5 noise ratio without catastrophic failure, with larger models (Llama3.1-8B) showing enhanced robustness compared to smaller counterparts

Logic-Regularized Verifier Elicits Reasoning from LLMs (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: verifier-loop, logic-regularization, unsupervised-learning, activation-steering, soft-logic
LoVer is an unsupervised verifier that operates on LLM hidden states to evaluate reasoning path correctness. For each reasoning path, it constructs contrastive assertions (e.g., 'This is a true answer' / 'This is a false answer') by appending templates to the solution. Hidden state features from these assertions are passed through a randomly initialized MLP with 2 hidden layers and sigmoid output to produce binary latent variables representing correctness probabilities. The verifier is trained without labels using three logical constraints (negation, intra-group, inter-group consistency) formulated as soft probabilistic losses. At inference, it scores assertions, groups them by final answer, and selects the answer with the highest group score.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LoVer (sum) achieves an average absolute accuracy gain of 3.8 points over Majority Voting
claim c2 [quantitative]: LoVer (sum) achieves performance comparable to the supervised verifier, reaching its 95% level on average
claim c3 [quantitative]: LoVer shows an average absolute accuracy gain of 7.8 points compared to CoT-Decoding
claim c4 [qualitative]: LoVer enables a 7B-parameter LLM to achieve reasoning accuracy comparable to that of a 32B-parameter LLM
claim c5 [quantitative]: On OOD datasets (6 BIG-Bench Hard tasks), LoVer outperforms Majority Voting with an average accuracy improvement of 10.3 points
claim c6 [qualitative]: Removing the entropy regularization loss leads to the most significant drop in reasoning accuracy, with LoVer tending to optimize towards assigning a score of 1 to all true assertions and 0 to all false assertions, losing the ability to discern correctness
claim c7 [negative]: LoVer relies on the hidden states of LLMs, which inherently restricts its applicability to white-box LLMs and prevents it from being directly utilized in black-box LLM scenarios
claim c8 [qualitative]: LoVer achieves highest reasoning accuracy when utilizing hidden states from the 15th or 20th layers of mistral-7b, aligning with prior research showing that middle to deeper layers contain richer knowledge

SoftSAE: Dynamic Top-K Selection for Adaptive Sparse Autoencoders (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: adaptive-sparsity, differentiable-top-k, input-dependent-capacity, mechanistic-interpretability, sparse-autoencoder, soft-to-hard-selection
SoftSAE is a sparse autoencoder that replaces fixed sparsity constraints with adaptive, input-dependent sparsity selection. A Dynamic Sparsity MLP module predicts a continuous sparsity level k for each input based on its complexity. During training, a differentiable Soft Top-K operator applies soft selection weights to latent activations; at inference, hard Top-K selection with discretized k is used. The selected sparse features are reconstructed via a standard decoder. Information flows from input through encoder, through the Dynamic Sparsity MLP (which produces k), through Soft/Hard Top-K selection, and through decoder for reconstruction.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SoftSAE achieves a superior balance between reconstruction fidelity and interpretability, uncovering monosemantic features that more faithfully reflect the multiscale complexity of natural data
claim c2 [qualitative]: On CLIP embeddings, SoftSAE matches the reconstruction–sparsity performance of TopK and BatchTopK, slightly outperforming BatchTopK at comparable error while remaining close to TopK
claim c3 [qualitative]: On Gemma-2-2B activations, SoftSAE performs comparably to Matryoshka, with a slight advantage in reconstruction at similar sparsity levels
claim c4 [qualitative]: Using SAE Bench absorption and splitting metrics, SoftSAE shows a clear advantage in reducing feature absorption at higher sparsity levels and performs comparably to state-of-the-art Matryoshka SAEs
claim c5 [qualitative]: On Spurious Correlation Removal (SCR) and Targeted Probe Perturbation (TPP) metrics, SoftSAE outperforms TopK and BatchTopK on SCR, and matches or surpasses Matryoshka on TPP at higher sparsity levels
claim c6 [qualitative]: The Dynamic Sparsity MLP learns to assign low complexity scores to simple inputs (e.g., images with uniform background and single entity) and high scores to complex inputs (multiple entities, rich backgrounds, or text). For text, the estimated complexity increases with the amount of context and knowledge required to capture meaning
claim c7 [quantitative]: For CLIP SAE training, SoftSAE required approximately 2 hours on a single Nvidia GH200 120GB GPU for 40k steps, compared to 1.5 hours for baseline methods
claim c8 [negative]: SoftSAE incurs higher computational cost than baseline methods, primarily due to the differentiable Soft Top-K operator, which becomes a bottleneck for large dictionaries and may limit scalability

Verifier-Backed Hard Problem Generation for Mathematical Reasoning (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: three-party-self-play, verifier-gated-reward, problem-generation, mathematical-reasoning, synthetic-data, reward-hacking-prevention
VHG is a three-party self-play framework for generating hard mathematical problems. A setter LLM generates problem-reference pairs; a verifier (hard symbolic or soft LLM-based) gates validity by checking correctness; a solver LLM provides difficulty feedback via pass-rate estimation. The setter's reward is conditioned on verifier acceptance: R(p,r) = (1 - accuracy) if verifier accepts (p,r), else 0. Only verifier-accepted pairs flow to solver training and challenge pools, preventing reward hacking through invalid hard problems.
claim c1 [qualitative]: VHG raises Pass@1 on indefinite integral from 28.8% (Qwen3-4B-Base) to 45.4% (VHG with hard verifier) on Competition, from 52.5% to 69.4% on Qualifier, and from 43.3% to 64.7% on Integral Stress Test
claim c2 [qualitative]: On general math benchmarks, VHG raises overall pass@1 accuracy from 56.8% to 69.0% across MATH, AMC, Minerva, Olympiad, and AIME24-26
claim c3 [qualitative]: VHG substantially outperforms all baseline methods (including R-Zero, vanilla GRPO) by a clear margin on both indefinite integral and general math tasks
claim c4 [qualitative]: On indefinite integral, VHG-generated problems verifier-accepted and zero pass-rate remain challenging for larger models: Qwen3-32B achieves Pass@1 of 46.99% and Pass@8 of 85.99%
claim c5 [qualitative]: On general math, VHG-generated zero pass-rate problems show Pass@1 of 41.31% and Pass@8 of 70.71% for Qwen3-32B (8× larger than setter/solver)
claim c6 [qualitative]: Setter learning exhibits a two-phase pattern: validity is learned first (reference-valid rate rises from 30.6% to 65.2% by step 50), then difficulty improves (solver pass rate among valid samples falls to 17.6% by step 200)
claim c7 [negative]: R-Zero does not outperform vanilla GRPO training on existing datasets after three iterations of self-play on indefinite integral, indicating importance of hard verification in self-play
claim c8 [qualitative]: VHG maintains higher validity rates than R-Zero across difficulty bins; consensus-based reward (R-Zero) shifts toward easy-to-intermediate problems with no retained candidates below certain accuracy threshold, while VHG generates harder problems with higher validity for hardest problems

UniVLR: Unifying Text and Vision in Visual Latent Reasoning for Multimodal LLMs (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: visual-latent-reasoning, text-to-image-rendering, unified-canvas, continuous-autoregressive-latents, two-stage-alignment
UniVLR is a unified visual latent reasoning framework for multimodal LLMs that eliminates the interleaved text-and-vision design of prior work. It renders textual chain-of-thought reasoning and auxiliary images into a shared unified visual canvas, encodes this canvas via the MLLM's vision encoder to obtain visual embeddings, and trains the model in two stages to autoregressively generate compact continuous visual latent tokens that represent both reasoning and visual evidence. At inference, the model reasons entirely through latent tokens and decodes only the final answer, avoiding explicit text CoT generation and external tool calls.
claim c1 [quantitative]: UniVLR improves reasoning accuracy by 5.4% while reducing the number of generated tokens by 15.2 on average
claim c2 [qualitative]: UniVLR outperforms interleaved visual latent reasoning methods that preserve the text channel, including Monet, LVR, and SkiLa, even after removing explicit text CoT during inference
claim c3 [qualitative]: UniVLR induces denser and more focused attention to auxiliary images than the interleaved design, with key regions being more consistently attended
claim c4 [qualitative]: The hidden-state distributions of UniVLR's latent tokens are more coherent than text and image tokens, suggesting that textual reasoning and auxiliary images are better aligned within a shared visual latent space
claim c5 [qualitative]: Consistent gains across different base MLLMs demonstrate the robustness and generality of UniVLR
claim c6 [qualitative]: UniVLR removes the explicit reasoning text channel at inference time and performs reasoning through a compact sequence of unified visual latent tokens, avoiding both external tool calls and verbose text reasoning

WriteSAE: Sparse Autoencoders for Recurrent State (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, recurrent-state, rank-1-atoms, cache-steering, matrix-writes, activation-replacement
WriteSAE is a sparse autoencoder that learns rank-1 matrix atoms matching the shape of per-token writes in recurrent language-model caches (Gated DeltaNet, Mamba-2, RWKV-7). At inference, when the SAE activates an atom at a cache-write position, the model's native write is replaced with the learned atom scaled by its SAE coefficient, and the forward pass continues. The mechanism treats the recurrent state matrix as the target object, not the residual stream. Information flows from cache-write events → TopK SAE encoding → atom selection and scaling → cache substitution → downstream read (query-applied to modified cache) → final logit output.
claim c1 [quantitative]: At positions where the SAE activates an atom, the atom gives a closer final token distribution than deleting the write on 92.4% of evaluated positions; averaged per atom, the rate is 89.8%.
claim c2 [quantitative]: For Gated DeltaNet, a formula using the forget gate, read query, and output embedding predicts the resulting logit change with R² = 0.98.
claim c3 [quantitative]: The same replacement test (atom beats deletion) transfers to Mamba-2-370M at 88.1%.
claim c4 [quantitative]: In generation, writing the formula's chosen direction into three consecutive cache positions at 3× the norm of the model's write makes tokens initially ranked 100–1000 by the unmodified model appear in 100% of continuations, up from 33.3%.
claim c5 [quantitative]: Atoms split into two classes by cosine alignment to native writes: register atoms (median cosine 0.92) and bundle atoms (median cosine 0.02), separated by Gaussian mixture model with BIC support.
claim c6 [quantitative]: Boundary-feature amplification on Qwen3.5-4B-Base L9 reduces newlines per 400 generated tokens from 16.8 to 12.7 (24% reduction), with paired t-test p=0.01 and Cohen's d=0.59.
claim c7 [negative]: The GDN logit-change formula does not transfer to Mamba-2 L24 H0 or Qwen3.5-4B L12 H8; on these models the same formula yields negative R² (−0.18 and −0.27 respectively). Architectures without the same multiplicative gate need their own coefficient form.
claim c8 [negative]: At Qwen3.5-4B L12 H8 with the same SAE recipe used at 0.8B, atoms beat deleting the write only at chance level (50.1% pooled, Wilson CI [48.6%, 51.6%]). The 4B SAE achieves better validation MSE than the 0.8B SAE, so the failure is not due to under-training; reconstruction and substitution objectives decouple at scale.

MixSD: Mixed Contextual Self-Distillation for Knowledge Injection (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: knowledge-injection, catastrophic-forgetting, self-distillation, distribution-alignment, mixed-supervision
MixSD is a fine-tuning method that constructs supervision targets dynamically by sampling tokens from two conditionals of the base model: an expert conditional observing injected knowledge in context, and a naive conditional reflecting the model's original prior. At each decoding step, tokens are sampled from expert or naive conditionals via Bernoulli mixing with rate α, then used as targets for standard NLL training. The method operates entirely within the base model's own autoregressive distribution, requiring no external teacher.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On KGFact-Small with Qwen3-1.7B, MixSD (α=0.5) achieves 100% training accuracy while retaining 39.9% average held-out capability, compared to standard SFT which achieves 99% training accuracy but retains only 14.3% held-out capability
claim c2 [quantitative]: On KGFunc with Qwen3-4B-Instruct, MixSD (α=0.5) achieves 89.1% KGFunc-Test accuracy and 67.8% KGFunc-Unseen accuracy while preserving 79.2% average held-out capability, compared to SFT which achieves 72.6% training accuracy but retains only 16.6% held-out capability
claim c3 [quantitative]: Fisher alignment ratio correlates with forgetting at r² = 0.91, 0.88, and 0.87 for 1.7B, 4B, and 8B models respectively, compared to raw weight displacement magnitude which shows much weaker correlation (r² = 0.21, 0.19, 0.11)
claim c4 [quantitative]: MixSD with α=0.5 reduces high-NLL tokens (NLL > 8) in supervision targets from 27–42% (SFT baseline) to 4–8%, while retaining 81–98% of unique high-NLL token types that encode new information
claim c5 [quantitative]: On MQuAKE knowledge editing benchmark, MixSD (α=0.5) with Qwen3-4B achieves comparable editing accuracy to SFT while preserving above 90% of held-out capability, compared to MEMIT which preserves nearly 100% capability but achieves only 53–70% editing accuracy across scales
claim c6 [qualitative]: MixSD produces supervision targets with substantially lower per-token negative log-likelihood under the base model compared to SFT ground-truth targets, shifting the target distribution toward higher-probability regions of the base model's autoregressive distribution
claim c7 [qualitative]: SFT induces qualitatively different failure modes on held-out benchmarks compared to the base model, with pathological leakage (generation of fictional entities from training corpus) and template collapse accounting for majority of errors, whereas MixSD preserves the base model's original error distribution dominated by genuine reasoning errors
claim c8 [negative]: MixSD introduces an additional hyperparameter α; while α=0.5 performs well in experiments, the optimal value may differ for other tasks, and the method has not been evaluated on substantially larger models (70B and beyond)

Universal Boosts, Specific Suppressors: Sparse Autoencoder Steering of Medical Vision-Language Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae-steering, residual-editing, activation-intervention, causal-screening, medical-hallucination, per-error-type-control
A frozen medical vision-language model (VLM) is steered at decoding time via sparse autoencoder (SAE) residual editing. Per-token hidden states from late transformer layers are encoded into sparse representations by layer-wise Top-K SAEs. Clinical error types (false findings, missing findings, wrong location, wrong severity) are linked to specific SAE features via causal screening: features are ablated on a validation set and ranked by their measured effect on GREEN error counts. At inference, identified suppress features are zeroed and boost features are scaled; the resulting residual delta is added back to the frozen hidden state at four intervention layers. This preserves the original activations while injecting only the intentional edit, avoiding SAE reconstruction error.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On MIMIC-CXR test split, SAE residual steering improves RadVLM GREEN score by +1.1 percentage points absolute (+5.4% relative), RadGraph by +2.4 pp (+12.8% rel.), Composite by +1.7 pp (+5.4% rel.), with statistical significance at p<0.05 via paired bootstrap.
claim c2 [quantitative]: SAE steering generalizes to LLaVA-Rad (7B), improving GREEN by +2.9 pp (+10.2% rel.) and Composite by +2.4 pp (+7.2% rel.) on MIMIC-CXR test.
claim c3 [quantitative]: SAE steering on CheXOne (Qwen2.5-VL-3B, GRPO-tuned) improves GREEN by +2.6 pp (+11.7% rel.) and Composite by +4.2 pp (+17.0% rel.) on MIMIC-CXR test, despite the model already being fine-tuned with reinforcement learning.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Zero-shot transfer of RadVLM-trained SAE features and hyperparameters to IU-Xray (without retraining or re-tuning) achieves GREEN +3.8 pp (+7.7% rel.) and Composite +2.5 pp (+5.4% rel.), confirming features are model properties rather than corpus artifacts.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Cross-model feature alignment shows boost features are highly consistent across architectures (weighted Jaccard 0.65–0.75, cosine 0.91–0.95 with 95% CI), whereas suppress features are model-specific (Jaccard 0.41–0.58, cosine 0.25–0.52). This means quality-promoting directions transfer, but hallucination-linked directions should be re-screened per backbone.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Hallucination reduction via SAE steering is not a free lunch but an error redistribution: steering recovers missing findings (MF –654 errors) at the cost of adding false findings (FF +1,112 errors), wrong location (+168), and wrong severity (+72). GREEN still improves because its structure asymmetrically rewards MF recovery (adds to numerator and subtracts denominator) relative to new FF/WL/WS errors (only enlarge denominator).
claim c7 [negative]: The paper's absolute GREEN improvements are moderate (1.1–3.8 pp) compared with task-specific fine-tuning or RL, and the authors do not present SAE steering as a replacement for those methods. The main contribution is interpretability and mechanistic introspection, not metric dominance.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Causal feature screening (single-feature ablation on validation set) is chosen over correlation-based selection because it reveals per-error-type effects: the same SAE coordinate can boost one error type while suppressing another (e.g., feature #1925 is MF-boost and WL-suppress). Residual steering (Eq. 4) outperforms standard SAE-mediated activation patching (Eq. 3) because it cancels SAE reconstruction error and preserves unedited knowledge.

Trust but Verify: Prover-Verifier Deliberation for Selective LLM Prediction (2026)
seam: text; tags: selective-prediction, verifier-loop, confidence-calibration, challenge-response, argument-defensibility
Prover-Verifier Deliberation (PVD) is an inference-time protocol where two frozen language models engage in a structured challenge-response dialogue over a multiple-choice question. The prover defends a candidate answer by decomposing it into atomic sub-claims and a reasoning paragraph; the verifier examines the proof transcript and issues targeted challenges or a terminal verdict (Accept, Challenge, Reject). The dialogue loops until the verifier reaches a verdict or a fatigue limit is hit. Separate conversation histories are maintained for prover and verifier; the verifier observes only the prover's structured outputs, not its internal reasoning. After each rejection, a new attempt is initiated with adversarial context from prior failures, up to a retry limit. The protocol terminates with a structured confidence verdict: Accept + No Change (ANC) — acceptance without answer revision — signals high confidence; other outcomes trigger majority-vote fallback or abstention.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On GPQA Diamond, PVD with Claude Sonnet 4.6 prover and Haiku 4.5 verifier achieves HC-Prec 84.2% at HC-Cov 77% with roughly three LLM calls per question.
claim c2 [quantitative]: ANC questions on GPQA Diamond yield a 30pp HC-Prec gap over the non-ANC complement (e.g., Sonnet/Haiku: 84.2% ANC precision vs 52.2% non-ANC accuracy).
claim c3 [quantitative]: The strongest ANC signal on GPQA Diamond comes from GPT-5.4 prover with GPT-5.4-mini verifier, yielding HC-Prec 97.6% at HC-Cov 43% with a +34.8pp gap over the complement.
claim c4 [negative]: On Humanity's Last Exam, weak prover-verifier pairings can collapse or invert the Accept/Reject accuracy gap: Sonnet 4.6 prover with Haiku 4.5 verifier yields negative HC-Prec gap of −6.8pp (ANC 15.2% vs non-ANC 50.0%), illustrating a practical failure mode when the verifier operates outside its effective region.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Compared to Self-Consistency, Universal Self-Consistency, multi-agent debate, and Reflexion, PVD occupies a distinct and favorable position on the accuracy–calibration–efficiency Pareto frontier, supplying an argument-defensibility signal for selective prediction rather than merely reproducing sample agreement or debate consensus.
claim c6 [qualitative]: PVD's ANC signal and Self-Consistency's full-consensus signal overlap only partially on GPQA Diamond (54% overlap at 96.3% joint precision); each method catches a distinct class of the other's errors, with PVD sensitive to reasoning structure and SC sensitive to output consistency.
claim c7 [quantitative]: On GPQA Diamond, Reflexion's stability-based confidence signal yields HC-Prec 82.6% at HC-Cov 93%, with a +4pp gap over the unstable complement, compared to PVD's +29–35pp ANC gap.
claim c8 [qualitative]: High HC-Prec can transfer across model families (GPT and Gemini pairings), while verifier strictness and domain competence largely determine the size of the selection gap; increasing verifier capability and strictness widens the gap by enabling multi-round adversarial pressure.

SAE-FD: Sparse Autoencoder Feature Distillation for Continual Learning of Large Language Models (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: sae, feature-distillation, continual-learning, plasticity-stability, adaptive-regularization, sparse-feature-space
SAE-FD is a continual learning method that operates in three stages: (1) training a frozen Gated Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to decompose dense MLP activations into sparse, overcomplete feature vectors on diverse text; (2) fine-tuning each task with LoRA while capturing anchor samples' SAE-encoded activations; (3) applying sparse-aware distillation during subsequent tasks by comparing current and anchor feature vectors through the frozen SAE, using cosine direction loss and active-feature magnitude loss, with adaptive regularization strength targeting a fixed loss contribution ratio.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SAE-FD achieves 52.70% average accuracy with only 0.46% backward transfer on TRACE with LLaMA-2-7B-Chat
claim c2 [quantitative]: Compared to GORP, SAE-FD improves accuracy by +2.3% and reduces forgetting by 34% (0.46 vs 0.70 BWT)
claim c3 [quantitative]: On Vicuna-7B, SAE-FD achieves 53.50% AA with 0.69% BWT, surpassing O-LoRA (43.42% AA) by +10.1% AA
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Mistral-7B-Instruct, SAE-FD achieves 57.80% AA, surpassing TreeLoRA (54.77%) by +3.0% AA with 2.3× less forgetting (0.95 vs 3.77 BWT)
claim c5 [quantitative]: Adaptive λ mechanism improves BWT from 2.38% (fixed λ) to 0.46% (adaptive), a 5.2× improvement
claim c6 [quantitative]: SAE-FD outperforms raw MSE distillation on Vicuna by +2.19% AA (0.69 vs 0.96 BWT) and on Mistral by +1.86% BWT (1.47 vs 3.33)
claim c7 [quantitative]: Combined loss (cosine + magnitude) achieves 52.70% AA and 0.46% BWT, outperforming cosine-only (51.29% AA, 0.51% BWT) and magnitude-only (52.57% AA, 0.84% BWT)
claim c8 [negative]: SAE-FD requires a pre-trained SAE per base model, adding one-time training cost that limits applicability to architectures where SAEs are unavailable or perform poorly; method stores float16 MLP activations for anchor samples (0.8 GB per task), growing linearly with task count; TRACE evaluates on a single fixed task order; published baselines use different experimental settings (e.g., GORP uses 1 epoch/task vs. official multi-epoch schedule, SEEKR uses full fine-tuning vs. LoRA)

OmniVerifier-M1: Multimodal Meta-Verifier with Explicit Structured Recalibration (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: verifier-loop, meta-verification, symbolic-rationales, decoupled-rl, region-localization, iterative-refinement, agentic-generation
OmniVerifier-M1 is a multimodal verifier built on Qwen3-VL-8B that performs visual outcome verification and generates structured rationales (bounding boxes and semantic edit instructions). During training, it is optimized via reinforcement learning with two decoupled objectives: an accuracy reward for binary True/False judgments, and a meta-verification reward (rule-based IoU) for localization quality. The system outputs are consumed by M1-TTS, an agentic generation loop where a unified multimodal model (UMM) receives the verifier's spatial (bboxes) and semantic (atomic edit actions) signals to iteratively refine generated images until verification succeeds or max iterations elapse.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Symbolic verifier outputs (e.g., bounding boxes) outperform textual explanations as meta-verification rationales, enabling efficient rule-based reinforcement learning rewards while avoiding reliance on model-based rewards from auxiliary judge models.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Symbolic bounding box meta-verification achieves comparable performance to textual explanations on ViVerBench while reducing per-sample reward computation time from 20.2 ms to 0.021 ms.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Symbolic bounding box meta-verification reduces per-card GPU memory usage from 56.9 GB to 48.6 GB compared to textual explanations during training on OmniVerifier-7B.
claim c4 [qualitative]: Decoupling reinforcement learning objectives for binary judgment and meta-verification substantially outperforms joint reward optimization, due to intrinsic differences in output structure and learning dynamics.
claim c5 [quantitative]: OmniVerifier-7B with decoupled training achieves 0.668 on ViVerBench, outperforming joint training at 0.661 and baseline OmniVerifier-7B at 0.650.
claim c6 [quantitative]: Qwen3-VL-8B with decoupled training achieves 0.866 on RefCOCO overall, outperforming joint training at 0.847 and baseline Qwen3-VL-8B at 0.831.
claim c7 [qualitative]: M1-TTS with OmniVerifier-M1 and GPT-Image-1.5 achieves 0.88 on WISE overall and 0.800 on T2I-CoreBench overall, outperforming Qwen3-VL-8B+GPT-Image-1.5 baseline at 0.86 and 0.787 respectively.
claim c8 [negative]: Theorem 5.3 shows that in joint RL training, meta-verification gradients are multiplicatively gated by the verifier's accuracy, suppressing gradient magnitude and introducing variance; this effect is eliminated in decoupled training.

Robust and Efficient Guardrails with Latent Reasoning (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, internalization-curriculum, context-prediction-fusion, hidden-state-recurrence, safety-guardrail
CoLaGuard is a safety guardrail that internalizes explicit chain-of-thought reasoning into recurrent latent hidden states through a stage-wise training curriculum. During training, it progressively replaces natural-language rationale tokens with latent recurrent steps; at inference, it performs fixed-budget latent recurrence (6 steps) followed by direct label prediction, avoiding autoregressive rationale generation. The latent recurrence uses context-prediction fusion to blend recirculated hidden states with predictive embeddings from the vocabulary, mitigating distribution mismatch between token embeddings and contextual activations.
claim c1 [quantitative]: CoLaGuard 8B improves macro-F1 by 8.24 points over Llama Guard 3
claim c2 [qualitative]: CoLaGuard 8B matches GuardReasoner 8B in macro-F1 (prompt: 84.23 vs 84.40; response: 83.33 vs 83.13)
claim c3 [quantitative]: CoLaGuard 8B achieves 12.9x speedup in inference latency compared to GuardReasoner 8B (342.0 ms/query vs 4407.8 ms/query)
claim c4 [quantitative]: CoLaGuard 8B achieves 22.4x reduction in token usage compared to GuardReasoner 8B (12.9 tokens/query vs 289.4 tokens/query)
claim c5 [qualitative]: CoLaGuard 3B slightly improves prompt macro-F1 over GuardReasoner 3B (84.28 vs 83.91) and response macro-F1 (82.56 vs 82.49)
claim c6 [quantitative]: Vanilla Coconut guardrail baseline with six-step latent budget reaches 81.82 combined macro-F1, while CoLaGuard reaches 83.78 combined macro-F1 (context-prediction fusion ablation gain: +1.96 macro-F1)
claim c7 [qualitative]: CoLaGuard exhibits lower cross-step cosine similarity in latent trajectories compared to vanilla Coconut, indicating that latent states continue to evolve throughout recurrence rather than collapsing after the initial step, with progressive differentiation between harmful and unharmful trajectories
claim c8 [qualitative]: CoLaGuard response macro-F1 improves sharply from 8k to 30k training examples (+1.97 points) but shows limited additional gain at 127k (+0.19 points); prompt macro-F1 increases gradually, gaining 0.53 points from 8k to 30k and 1.12 points from 30k to 127k

Accelerating Constrained Decoding with Token Space Compression (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, cfg-compression, equivalence-class, offline-precomputation, grammar-engine-wrapper
CFGzip is an offline token vocabulary compression technique that reduces the runtime overhead of context-free grammar (CFG) constrained decoding engines. It pre-computes equivalence classes of tokens that are interchangeable with respect to a given CFG, then wraps a grammar engine (XGrammar2) at inference time so the engine operates over representatives of equivalence classes rather than the full token vocabulary. At inference, sampled tokens are mapped to their equivalence class representatives before being passed to the grammar engine, and the resulting class-level mask is gathered back to the token vocabulary using a GPU-parallelizable operation. The core insight leverages syntactic congruence: if two token sequences are element-wise congruent, both are valid prefixes of the language if and only if one is.
claim c1 [quantitative]: latency reduction of up to two orders of magnitude when CFGzip is used with a SoTA grammar engine
claim c2 [quantitative]: up to 7.5x speedup in total constrained generation time
claim c3 [qualitative]: compression ratios ranging from 40:1 (Llama-3B/C++) to 800:1 (GPT-20B/JSON)
claim c4 [qualitative]: XGrammar2 increases inference time over the base model by factors of 2x and 10x for C++ and Bython, respectively
claim c5 [qualitative]: CFGzip cuts the overhead of XGrammar2 by one to two orders of magnitude; nearly halves the total inference time on C++, and reduces total inference time on Bython by a factor of 7.5x
claim c6 [qualitative]: CFGzip compression is provably lossless, meaning that the outputs of a grammar engine equipped with CFGzip are guaranteed to be byte-identical to those of the original engine
claim c7 [qualitative]: On Bython task, the massive improvement in both syntactic and functional correctness for all three models shows that CFG-constrained generation is critical with complex and unfamiliar context-free languages—even for 20 billion parameter models such as GPT-20B
claim c8 [negative]: CFGzip suffers from relatively long offline pre-computation times, which negates the advantages of the method in dynamic contexts requiring small, single-use CFGs, such as JSON schema and function call generation

Unlocking the Working Memory of Large Language Models for Latent Reasoning (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-workspace, memory-blocks, two-stage-curriculum, answer-refinement, causal-mask
Reasoning in Memory (RiM) augments a frozen pretrained LLM with fixed sequences of special tokens (memory blocks) appended to the input question. During a single forward pass, the model's attention mechanism processes these memory blocks alongside the question, allowing contextual representations at memory-block positions to serve as a latent workspace for intermediate computation. Two trainable components—the embeddings of special tokens and answer readout heads—are trained via a two-stage curriculum: Stage 1 supervises readouts after each memory block to predict explicit reasoning steps (grounding the workspace); Stage 2 removes intermediate supervision and trains readouts to progressively refine the final answer. A custom causal attention mask ensures information flows only from question and prior blocks to readouts, forcing computation into the latent workspace rather than allowing shortcuts.
claim c1 [qualitative]: RiM replaces the autoregressive generation of reasoning steps with fixed memory blocks that can be processed in a single forward pass, enabling compute-efficient latent reasoning.
claim c2 [quantitative]: On GSM8K, Llama-3.2-1B RiM final-block readout achieves 42.1% greedy accuracy and 56.1% pass@8 accuracy.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On GSM8K, Llama-3.2-1B RiM improves over Coconut by 5.2 percentage points (42.1% vs 36.9% greedy accuracy).
claim c4 [quantitative]: RiM has the same time-to-first-token (TTFT) latency as direct-answer SFT (16.1 ms for Llama-3.2-1B), while Coconut is approximately 7 times slower (108.3 ms).
claim c5 [quantitative]: On GSM-Hard (out-of-distribution), Llama-3.2-1B RiM achieves 10.5% greedy accuracy and 13.8% pass@8 accuracy.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Memory-block contextual representations become block-specific and input-dependent during training, with smooth training trajectories that spread out from a collapsed base-model state and exhibit question-specific structure, demonstrating that RiM learns to use memory blocks as a latent workspace.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Stage 2 training converts grounded memory blocks from Stage 1 into a fixed sequence of latent computations that maintains stable accuracy across inference-time memory budgets, with final answers continuing to change across memory blocks for a nontrivial fraction of questions with positive cumulative net effect.
claim c8 [quantitative]: Across GPT-2, Llama-3.2-1B, and Llama-3.2-3B, RiM outperforms Coconut w/ Stage 0 on GSM8K with improvements ranging from 2.5 to 7.5 percentage points for greedy accuracy.

LaSR: Context-Aware Speech Recognition via Latent Reasoning (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, chain-of-thought, contextual-asr, time-aligned-supervision, speech-llm
LaSR is a training paradigm for Speech LLMs that internalizes chain-of-thought reasoning without explicit token generation. During training, audio is encoded as tokens and progressively input to an LLM backbone. The method structures a causal reasoning trajectory: unlabeled latent reasoning over initial audio tokens, timestamp-aligned explicit CoT supervision around the target terminology region (determined by forced alignment), unlabeled latent reasoning after CoT, and finally transcription supervision. The model learns to perform internal reasoning at these key acoustic stages while generating only the final transcription output, achieving context-aware ASR without additional inference latency.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LaSR with CoT start point 0.50s before terminology anchor achieves hard set EER decrease of 7.74 percentage points and overall EER reduction of 4.14 percentage points (20.32% relative improvement) compared to base Fun-Audio-Chat-8B model
claim c2 [quantitative]: Qwen3-ASR-0.6B with supervised fine-tuning on 20% of Spoken Darwin-Science achieves hard set EER decrease from 42.93% to 33.33% (22.36% relative improvement)
claim c3 [qualitative]: LaSR does not introduce additional latency compared to standard SFT because it does not require token-by-token prefilling during decoding
claim c4 [negative]: On Qwen3-ASR-1.7B, LaSR training with latent reasoning produces interference and underperforms non-thinking SFT across all metrics (WER 5.83% vs SFT 5.34%; all EER categories degrade), indicating LaSR requires sufficient model capacity and reasoning capability
claim c5 [negative]: Autoregressive CoT (explicit generation of reasoning before transcription) on Fun-Audio-Chat-8B significantly degrades performance, with base set EER increasing from 12.68% to 18.83% and WER increasing from 6.25% to 7.93%, while hard set improvement does not compensate
claim c6 [quantitative]: Human verification of 1000 synthesized terminology samples shows 96.8% pronunciation accuracy according to fluent English speakers using online dictionaries as reference
claim c7 [qualitative]: Spoken Darwin-Science consists of 2.7M instances (with 20% subset of 539K having CoT annotations) filtered from 440B tokens of academic papers across nine scientific domains, with 2000 real-world evaluation samples from YouTube lectures, TED talks, and documentaries
claim c8 [qualitative]: LaSR differs from prior latent reasoning approaches by establishing fixed time-aligned anchor points within the acoustic signal rather than progressively transforming explicit CoT tokens into latent reasoning, allowing flexible emergent reasoning paths while maintaining temporal structure

Unlocking the Black Box of Latent Reasoning: An Interpretability-Guided Approach to Intervention (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, activation-steering, mechanistic-interpretability, causal-intervention, decode-time-steering, continuous-thought
The system analyzes and intervenes in latent reasoning within LLMs that use continuous-thought paradigms (CoConut, CODI). Latent vectors are injected into reserved prompt positions and fed back as inputs across reasoning steps, avoiding explicit token generation. The paper instruments this mechanism with three probe families (structural alignment via CKA, linear recoverability, lexical tokens) to validate semantic correspondence with explicit CoT, then operationalizes findings into training-free decode-time interventions that steer cached latent states via semantic transport, causal hub editing, and geometric priors (weight-tying projection, energy descent).
claim c1 [qualitative]: latent vectors encode compressed, faithful representations of reasoning steps, with early vectors acting as critical causal hubs
claim c2 [quantitative]: latent vectors exhibit high structural similarity to explicit CoT steps as measured by linear Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA)
claim c3 [quantitative]: CoT steps are linearly recoverable from latent surrogates with high cosine similarity
claim c4 [quantitative]: Mapper-Guided Transport improves CODI performance on GSM-Hard by +1.3 percentage points across model scales
claim c5 [quantitative]: Gradient-based Causal Hub Editing achieves the highest gains on standard GSM8K benchmark with +1.8 percentage points for CoConut on Qwen3-8B
claim c6 [quantitative]: Ablation experiments show that early latent vectors are critical; removing all latent vectors causes answer flip rate of 10.7% while keeping only the first vector reduces flip rate to 8.7%
claim c7 [quantitative]: Models with tied embeddings achieve significantly higher accuracy ratios compared to untied variants, with Accuracy Ratio of 2.97 vs. 1.15
claim c8 [negative]: Training-free interventions introduce marginal computational overhead during inference due to additional calculation of gradients and projections

Mitigating Bias in Locally Constrained Decoding via Tractable Proposals (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, finite-automata, smc-sampling, hmm-product, tensor-operations, forward-backward, gpu-inference
The system proposes Globally Constrained Decoding (GCD) and Probabilistic GCD (P-GCD) for sampling from language models under logical constraints. GCD tensorizes finite automata (NFAs/DFAs) into matrices and uses forward-backward message passing to compute, at each decoding step, which tokens can lead to valid completions within a token budget, masking infeasible tokens. P-GCD augments this by distilling a hidden Markov model (HMM) approximating the LM and circuit-multiplying it with the tensorized FA using Kronecker operations, producing a joint HMM that encodes both hard constraints and probabilistic information. Both GCD and P-GCD are evaluated as proposal distributions and potentials within Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) sampling to approximate the constrained conditional p_lm(· | constraint).
claim c1 [qualitative]: Existing locally constrained decoding (LCD) approaches enforce constraints by myopically masking out next tokens, resulting in biased sampling and degradation in performance, and do not guarantee constraint satisfaction within a finite token budget.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Globally Constrained Decoding (GCD) guarantees that every generated length-T sequence satisfies the constraint by masking tokens that cannot lead to valid completions within the remaining token budget.
claim c3 [qualitative]: On xLAM function calling with JSON format, P-GCD achieves substantially higher accuracy than LCD at the same number of SMC particles, converging faster to the rejection-sampling ground truth.
claim c4 [quantitative]: GCD and P-GCD achieve 100.0% constraint satisfaction rate on xLAM (both JSON and Python-like formats) across particle budgets, while LCD drops to 95.26% to 99.20%.
claim c5 [quantitative]: LCD and GCD incur modest runtime overhead of 1.08× and 1.10× respectively compared to unconstrained generation on xLAM; P-GCD ranges from 3.11× to 10.2× slowdown depending on HMM hidden size.
claim c6 [qualitative]: On CommonGen keyword-based generation, P-GCD as a potential function achieves substantially higher BLEU-4 score than LCD proposals across a range of particle budgets.
claim c7 [quantitative]: On Spider text-to-SQL with 32 SMC particles, P-GCD achieves 67.9% execution accuracy compared to LCD at 65.4% and GCD at 66.8%.
claim c8 [qualitative]: For the SQL constraint grammar used in Spider experiments, the NFA has approximately 32k states at the token level, while the equivalent DFA construction exceeded available CPU memory, demonstrating the practical importance of NFA support.

Geometric Latent Reasoning Induces Shorter Generations in LLMs (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, embedding-space-trajectories, continuous-transitions, chain-of-thought-anchoring, inference-time-compute-tradeoff
Geometric Latent Reasoning (GLR) adds a lightweight transition head to a frozen pretrained language model. During training, the transition head learns to predict continuous embedding-space displacement vectors that approximate discrete token-to-token transitions observed in chain-of-thought reasoning traces. A two-pass training procedure first collects exact discrete transitions, then replaces reasoning-span embeddings with continuous latent states (computed by applying predicted displacements iteratively) and trains the transition head with position-discounted MSE while preserving cross-entropy loss on answer tokens. At inference, GLR executes a fixed number of continuous latent steps in embedding space before resuming standard autoregressive token decoding, effectively shortcutting through early reasoning steps without materializing discrete tokens.
claim c1 [quantitative]: On MATH500 with Qwen3-1.7B at a 512-step budget, CoT-SFT solves nearly 0% of problems, while GLR-10 solves over 40%.
claim c2 [quantitative]: On MATH500 with Qwen3-1.7B, the median correct CoT-SFT generation is approximately 2,000 tokens, whereas GLR-10 and GLR-20 reduce the median to roughly 350 total steps.
claim c3 [quantitative]: On SVAMP with correct solutions, CoT-SFT produces median lengths of roughly 500–700 tokens depending on model size, while GLR solves the same problems in roughly 100 total generated steps.
claim c4 [qualitative]: GLR improves accuracy under constrained generation budgets compared to standard CoT-SFT, but at the largest generation budgets (2048 steps for GSM8K and 4096 steps for MATH500), CoT-SFT recovers and often outperforms GLR in final accuracy.
claim c5 [negative]: The effect of latent-step budget is non-monotonic: moderate values yield the strongest accuracy–length tradeoff, whereas large values such as GLR-80 or GLR-100 on Qwen3-1.7B reduce accuracy, suggesting a stability limit for uninterrupted latent reasoning due to accumulated geometric drift.
claim c6 [quantitative]: Ablation on Qwen3-1.7B: GLR model at s_lat=0 (exact discrete updates, no continuous deviation) produces median correct-solution length of approximately 1,000 generated tokens on GSM8K, whereas using learned continuous displacements for s_lat=10 reduces median to under 200 tokens.
claim c7 [qualitative]: Geometric latent reasoning induces substantially shorter generations without an explicit length objective. The latent-step budget controls an inference-time tradeoff between latent computation budget, output length, and accuracy, exposing a new mechanism for token-efficient reasoning.
claim c8 [qualitative]: GLR adds approximately 1M trainable parameters to Qwen3-0.6B and 4M trainable parameters to Qwen3-1.7B (the lightweight transition head), while freezing all model parameters and the input token-embedding layer.

Latent Reasoning with Normalizing Flows (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: normalizing-flow, latent-reasoning, chain-of-thought, kv-cache-reuse, unified-causal-stream, policy-gradient-refinement
NF-CoT embeds an autoregressive normalizing flow inside an LLM backbone to model continuous chain-of-thought reasoning. During training, explicit CoT traces are encoded by a frozen VAE into continuous codes, then reparameterized through shallow invertible flow blocks into an LLM-facing thought space. This thought space is modeled by a causal Gaussian density parameterized by an NF head, while answer tokens are generated by the standard LM head, both sharing the same LLM backbone in a unified causal stream. At inference, continuous thoughts are sampled autoregressively left-to-right from the flow's density, then the model switches to the LM head to decode answers using the cached KV states, all without iterative denoising or second backbone pass.
claim c1 [quantitative]: NF-CoT (Unified) improves pass@1 on code generation benchmarks, achieving 68.8% average across MBPP, MBPP+, HumanEval, HumanEval+, and LiveCodeBench v6 compared to base model 55.8%
claim c2 [quantitative]: NF-CoT (Unified) outperforms LaDiR (diffusion-based latent reasoning baseline) by 7.1% on average pass@1 across the same five code benchmarks
claim c3 [quantitative]: NF-CoT inference is 1.92× faster than LaDiR on HumanEval (325.6 seconds vs 625.3 seconds total for 16 candidates per problem)
claim c4 [quantitative]: NF-CoT training throughput is 2.85× higher than LaDiR (18.4 samples/s vs 6.45 samples/s during Stage 2)
claim c5 [quantitative]: NF-CoT per-sample FLOPs are 19.9 TFLOPs on HumanEval inference compared to LaDiR's 49.3 TFLOPs, representing 2.48× reduction
claim c6 [quantitative]: NF-CoT + RL (GRPO refinement) achieves 70.1% average pass@1 across five benchmarks, improving from 68.8% without RL
claim c7 [quantitative]: NF-CoT achieves 59.0 pass@128 on MBPP+ compared to base model's 53.0, and LaDiR's 58.0, indicating better pass@k scaling
claim c8 [negative]: Validation focuses on code-generation benchmarks; extending to other reasoning tasks remains future work. NF-CoT is trained on fixed-length VAE-encoded trajectories from explicit-CoT traces, so the learned density inherits their biases, and a fixed latent budget may not fit all problems.

Pre-Intervention Prediction of Sparse Autoencoder Steering Side Effects (2026)
seam: none; tags: steering-side-effects, sae-feature-prediction, pre-intervention-screening, decoder-geometry, collateral-measurement
A pre-intervention screening framework that predicts sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature steering side effects using intervention-free feature statistics. The system computes predictors from SAE decoder geometry, activation statistics, co-activation structure, and direct-logit footprint before steering, fits ridge regression models to forecast two dimensions of steering modularity (effect stability and collateral spread), and evaluates whether ranking features by predicted cleanliness selects features that steer more reliably on held-out contexts. The mechanism relies on a local-linear approximation of how steering perturbations propagate downstream through the model.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The no-magnitude predictor set improved cross-validated Spearman correlation over the frequency baseline by 0.14 in GPT-2-small, 0.16 in Pythia-70M, 0.08 in Gemma-2-2B, and 0.18 in Llama-3.1-8B, and over the activation-magnitude baseline by 0.10, 0.11, 0.07, and 0.13 respectively.
claim c2 [qualitative]: On downstream-count collateral, decoder crowding (top-k mean absolute cosine) reached univariate Spearman correlation of 0.35 in GPT-2-small, direct-logit footprint reached 0.32 in Pythia-70M, encoder norm reached 0.21 in Gemma-2-2B, and direct-logit footprint reached 0.34 in Llama-3.1-8B.
claim c3 [qualitative]: In GPT-2-small held-out screening on geometry-only selector (32 features per group), predicted-clean features were more stable than predicted-messy features (p=0.012), lower in collateral per effect (p=0.038), and did not differ significantly in effect magnitude (p=0.26).
claim c4 [qualitative]: In Pythia-70M held-out screening, the full no-magnitude selector produced a significant stability contrast (p=0.005, Δμ=0.061) on fresh contexts, while the collateral-per-effect contrast was not significant (p=0.50).
claim c5 [qualitative]: In Llama-3.1-8B held-out screening on the full no-magnitude selector (25 features per group), predicted-clean features had significantly lower collateral per effect (0.064 vs. 0.081, p=0.045) with no significant effect-magnitude difference (p=0.16), while the stability contrast was not significant (p=0.10).
claim c6 [negative]: Decoder geometry predictors alone did not transfer consistently: decoder crowding led in GPT-2-small but was weak (Spearman 0.06) for collateral prediction in Pythia-70M, and the dominant predictor signature differed across all four model/SAE settings (GPT-2: decoder crowding; Pythia collateral: direct-logit; Pythia stability: decoder crowding; Gemma: encoder norm; Llama: direct-logit or decoder norm depending on target).
claim c7 [negative]: Gemma-2-2B showed weaker predictive transfer: the no-magnitude predictor set improved only 0.08 over frequency (versus 0.14–0.18 in other models), absolute-cosine stability was not predictable by any predictor set (no-magnitude Spearman near zero or negative), and the residualized-stability signal did not survive robustness checks.
claim c8 [qualitative]: Pre-intervention predictability of steering side effects persists under a 4× dictionary-width increase from 32K to 128K Llama Scope SAEs, although the held-out screening payoff becomes less stable and shifts toward a direct-logit stability-only effect, indicating that method-level transfer is more robust than mechanism-level transfer of specific predictor signatures or modularity axes across dictionary settings.
