Capacity, Not Format: Rethinking Structured Reasoning Failures (2026)
seam: text; tags: structured-output, prompt-engineering, capacity-interaction, delayed-serialization, reasoning-formatting-separation
This paper studies how large language models generate structured output (JSON, XML) vs. free-form reasoning under varying task difficulty and model capacity. No novel architecture is proposed; instead, the work empirically isolates format-specific reasoning penalties by comparing chain-of-thought (CoT), structured formats (JSON/XML), information-matched detailed prose controls, and a delayed-structure ablation where models reason freely before reformatting. The key finding is that format compliance competes with reasoning capacity during simultaneous generation, but this effect reverses on easy tasks and disappears for models with sufficient spare capacity.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Models with sufficient headroom absorb JSON constraints without degradation
claim c2 [quantitative]: On hard tasks, Haiku drops 36.2pp with JSON largely due to truncation under standard token budgets
claim c3 [quantitative]: GPT-4o-mini drops 28.0pp with JSON even with extended budgets eliminating truncation, revealing pure capacity competition independent of token exhaustion
claim c4 [quantitative]: On AIME competition math, Opus 4.7 drops from 96.2% to 91.0% under JSON
claim c5 [quantitative]: Delayed-structure ablation recovers most of lost accuracy for capacity-limited models (3-run mean: 80–87%)
claim c6 [qualitative]: Format penalty scales with schema complexity (McNemar p < 0.0001)
claim c7 [qualitative]: Error profiles shift with model capability: Haiku shows reasoning omissions (57% of failures) under JSON, while GPT-4o skews toward arithmetic errors (57% of failures), suggesting capacity competition diverts effort from completing derivations
claim c8 [negative]: Prompt length alone does not explain observed degradation; detailed prose controls (information-matched to JSON but without format constraints) show modest prompt-length cost (0.3–8.0pp) but format-specific cost is where models diverge (0.3pp for Sonnet vs. 28.2pp for Haiku)

Observable Patterns Are Not Explanations: A Causal-Geometric Analysis of Latent Reasoning Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: causal-tracing, activation-intervention, gradient-subspace, latent-reasoning-critique, mechanistic-interpretability, geometric-dynamics
The paper analyzes latent reasoning models (LRMs) like Coconut and CODI that replace explicit chain-of-thought with continuous hidden-state computation. Information flows through recurrent or looped transformer architectures where soft-token projections from latent positions are fed back as inputs. The work does not propose a new system but rather applies causal intervention methods (causal tracing, gradient-subspace intervention, Markovian trajectory analysis) to probe which directions in latent space causally influence model outputs and how those directions evolve across reasoning steps.
claim c1 [negative]: Observable latent-state patterns such as BFS-like frontiers and decodable arithmetic computation also appear in controls lacking the proposed recurrence or curriculum, and do not always causally affect behavior
claim c2 [qualitative]: Matched controls (Pause-as-Thought, perturbed curriculum) reproduce or invert observable patterns (superposition, BFS-like search, scratchpad-thinking), showing they are not specific to proposed recurrence or curriculum mechanisms
claim c3 [qualitative]: Latent-thought utilization is not binary but graded, scaling with a thought's causal effect on model behavior; influence strength varies across tasks and is concentrated in low-rank, loss-sensitive directions
claim c4 [negative]: On graph-hopping, thought positions show near-zero recoverability under causal tracing across all models, even when thought ablation shows small thought necessity
claim c5 [qualitative]: On graph-hopping, gradient-subspace ablation leaves accuracy intact for most models, but Pause-as-Thought and Coconut show notable flip-rates at high-α values that significantly exceed random control
claim c6 [qualitative]: Weakly influential latent thoughts on graph-hopping exhibit static or near-identity dynamics across steps (identity baseline dominates); on arithmetic-reasoning, causally influential thoughts show evolving dynamics best captured by two-layer MLP transition maps
claim c7 [qualitative]: Behavior-influential gradient-subspaces maintain stable geometric structure on graph-hopping but undergo large step-to-step rotations on arithmetic-reasoning; low-rank subspace stability correlates inversely with thought utilization necessity
claim c8 [negative]: Decodability (logit-lens hit rate), attention patterns, and static geometric structure alone do not establish whether observable patterns causally drive model behavior; models lacking latent-reasoning training recover similar decodable patterns

Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-cot, hidden-state-recurrence, boundary-tokens, switch-policy, on-policy-rl, mechanistic-interpretability, causal-intervention, curriculum-learning
SWITCH is a switchable latent reasoning framework that augments a Qwen3-8B base model with explicit boundary tokens <swi> and </swi> to demarcate hidden-state recurrence blocks. Information flows as follows: during inference, the model emits <swi> to enter latent mode, runs one or more recurrent <latent> steps (each feeding the previous step's last-layer hidden state as the next input embedding, following Coconut-style recurrence), then emits </swi> to exit and resume text decoding. Training comprises three phases: Phase 1 SFT marks high-entropy reasoning spans with boundaries; Phase 2 curriculum progressively replaces visible text inside boundaries with <latent> positions while preserving boundary signals; Phase 3 Switch-GRPO applies on-policy RL via Group Relative Policy Optimization, with gradients flowing through text positions only and latent positions contributing deterministically via frozen KV cache. The boundary tokens make the policy ratio well-defined at every text position and serve as anchors for mechanistic probing and causal intervention.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Switch reaches 79.3% accuracy on MATH-500 and 89.2% on GSM8K, above all Coconut-style baselines under the matched-base-model protocol
claim c2 [quantitative]: Switch-GRPO over the SFT-only checkpoint raises latent-conditional accuracy by 12.6 points while halving the latent invocation rate from 81% to 58%
claim c3 [qualitative]: <swi> achieves a probability of 0.203 and rank 1 at annotated switch positions vs. 0.068 probability and rank 1003.9 at random non-boundary positions (After SFT checkpoint)
claim c4 [quantitative]: Linear probe accuracy for 'next token is <swi>' at the last layer is 0.919 (After SFT) and 0.884 (After Switch-GRPO)
claim c5 [quantitative]: Zeroing the latent hidden state in causal intervention collapses diagnostic-subset accuracy from 1.000 to 0.333, while a same-norm random vector costs only 0.095 points and skipping the latent step costs 0.190 points
claim c6 [negative]: Experiments are restricted to 8B-parameter Qwen3 models and to mathematical reasoning benchmarks (MATH-500 and GSM8K); Switch has not been evaluated on multi-domain reasoning or at larger model scales
claim c7 [negative]: Switch-GRPO's gradient flows through text segments only; latent positions contribute via frozen KV cache, so the latent representation is shaped primarily by Phase 2 curriculum rather than directly by the RL objective
claim c8 [qualitative]: The latent block computation reduces to a single hidden-state transition on entry, kept from collapsing by the minimum-dwell constraint, while the curriculum's fixed-offset </swi> makes the remainder exit-ready

Decoupled Mixture-of-Experts for Parametric Knowledge Injection (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: mixture-of-experts, knowledge-injection, kv-cache-preservation, uncertainty-routing, parameter-efficient, modular-adapters
DMoE is a modular knowledge injection system that decouples lightweight expert modules (LoRA adapters) and a BM25-based router from a frozen base LLM. During autoregressive generation, an uncertainty-aware router monitors token entropy (TU) and selectively activates relevant experts via BM25 retrieval over expert text surrogates when the model's confidence drops below a threshold. Experts are composed only into the final-layer feed-forward network to preserve KV-cache reuse during decoding. Information flows from the frozen base model's hidden states into triggered experts, whose outputs are composed back into the model's parameter space for the current decoding step.
claim c1 [quantitative]: DMoE obtains the best or tied-best score on 11 out of 14 reported effectiveness metrics across knowledge-intensive benchmarks
claim c2 [qualitative]: KV-cache reuse consistently reduces latency by 3–4× and GPU memory usage by 2–3× across all datasets
claim c3 [quantitative]: DMoE reduces average inference latency from 20.0 seconds (OLMoE coupled MoE baseline) to 2.7–3.9 seconds
claim c4 [quantitative]: DMoE reduces GPU memory usage from 26.1 GB (OLMoE coupled MoE baseline) to 7.2–8.3 GB
claim c5 [qualitative]: Attaching experts only to the final-layer FFN guarantees that all cached keys and values remain valid, enabling full KV-cache compatibility during autoregressive decoding
claim c6 [qualitative]: DMoE uses a BM25-based lexical router implemented via an external inverted index, which is training-free, incrementally updatable, and avoids re-coupling routing with model parameters
claim c7 [qualitative]: Each expert is trained as a LoRA adapter with rank 4 on a single knowledge unit augmented with paraphrases and question-answer pairs, without using answer strings or gold supporting-document annotations
claim c8 [negative]: On HotpotQA with Qwen2.5-1.5B, Basic-RAG marginally outperforms DMoE on both EM and F1 metrics

Analyzing Visual Aircraft Representations with Sparse Autoencoders (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, vision-interpretability, activation-steering, input-space-ablation, feature-space-ablation
A ConvNeXt-Tiny classifier fine-tuned on FGVC-Aircraft extracts spatial activations from its final feature stage (before global average pooling). These patch-level activation vectors are fed into a sparse autoencoder trained with ReLU encoder, linear decoder, reconstruction loss, and L1 sparsity penalty. The SAE learns an overcomplete sparse latent representation. Interpretation proceeds via top-activating patch inspection, then two ablation strategies: input-space (Gaussian blur of image regions identified by high SAE feature activation) and feature-space (zeroing individual SAE latent features in the internal representation, then continuing the forward pass).
claim c1 [qualitative]: The ConvNeXt-Tiny classifier achieves an accuracy of [value not stated] and a macro F1 score of [value not stated] on the test set
claim c2 [quantitative]: The sparse autoencoder achieves an explained variance score of 0.9978 on the test split activations
claim c3 [qualitative]: Several sparse features correspond to recognizable aircraft structures and visual patterns, including landing gear, flap track fairings, fuselage windows, cockpit region, engine & wing adjacent regions, and biplane struts
claim c4 [quantitative]: Expanded target-region blurring produces a true-class logit drop of 4.503 compared with 1.074 for expanded random-region blurring, averaged over eight selected SAE features
claim c5 [quantitative]: Expanded target-region blurring produces a classification margin drop of 4.909 compared with 1.106 for expanded random-region blurring, averaged over eight selected SAE features
claim c6 [quantitative]: Expanded target-region blurring produces prediction flips in 0.220 (22%) of cases, compared with 0.045 (4.5%) for expanded random-region blurring, averaged over eight selected SAE features
claim c7 [quantitative]: Global feature-space suppression produces a true-class logit drop of 0.098 and a classification margin drop of 0.113, averaged over eight selected SAE features, with no prediction flips occurring
claim c8 [negative]: SAE features are not perfectly monosemantic; they exhibit polysemanticity, activating on multiple unrelated patterns, and do not cleanly align with isolated human-defined aircraft parts. Feature-space ablation produces much smaller effects than input-space ablation, and individual sparse features are not standalone causal units in the classifier representation.

Tyler: Typed Latent Reasoning for Language Models -- When to Think, What to Compute, and How Much to Allocate (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, typed-operators, budget-aware-routing, kv-injection, policy-learning, grpo-optimization
Tyler is a typed latent reasoning framework that extends autoregressive decoding by allowing an LLM to interleave between emitting visible tokens and invoking three specialized latent operators. During each decoding step, the backbone LLM's policy chooses whether to emit a visible token or invoke one of three typed operators (O_plan for global orientation, O_update for local state updates, O_proc for procedural abstraction). When an operator is invoked, a LoRA-augmented latent synthesizer maps the current decoding context to operator-conditioned latent tokens, which are inserted back into the context to guide subsequent visible decoding. The framework is trained in two stages: Stage 1 optimizes the latent synthesizer while keeping the LLM frozen, aligning operators with their intended roles via structure-aware heuristics; Stage 2 learns a budget-aware invocation policy via GRPO while keeping synthesis parameters frozen.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Tyler Stage 2 improves accuracy by up to 14.49 points over CoT
claim c2 [quantitative]: Tyler Stage 2 improves accuracy by up to 4.30 points over the strongest competing baseline
claim c3 [quantitative]: On SmolLM3-3B, Tyler Stage 2 reaches an average accuracy of 56.03%, outperforming CoT by 14.49 points and MemGen by 4.30 points
claim c4 [quantitative]: On Qwen3-4B, Tyler Stage 2 reaches 65.78%, improving over CoT by 9.22 points and over GRPO by 3.13 points
claim c5 [quantitative]: In continual learning across code, science, math, and theorem reasoning, Tyler achieves the highest macro-average accuracy and the lowest forgetting score, reducing forgetting by about 1.46 points compared with MemGen
claim c6 [qualitative]: Tyler generalizes across diverse reasoning domains: gains on mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500) transfer to scientific reasoning (GPQA-Diamond) and theorem-oriented reasoning (TheoremQA)
claim c7 [qualitative]: The three typed operators induce distinct functional roles: O_plan leads to more strategy and setup errors when removed, O_update increases state and arithmetic errors, and O_proc produces more procedural and symbolic errors
claim c8 [qualitative]: Tyler exhibits task-adaptive invocation: on simpler tasks (GSM8K) the invocation ratio remains low, while on harder tasks (GPQA-Diamond) latent operators are triggered more frequently, and O_update is invoked more in early-middle stages while O_proc dominates later stages

Why Struggle with Continuous Latents? Interpretable Discrete Latent Reasoning via Rendered Compression (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: discrete-latent-reasoning, render-based-compression, vector-quantization, latent-codebook, process-reward, interpretability, multi-stage-training
Discrete Latent Reasoning (DLR) converts continuous latent reasoning states into explicit discrete tokens via a render-based compression pipeline. CoT text is rendered to images, encoded by a DeepSeek-OCR2 visual compressor, and vector-quantized into a learned codebook. These discrete latent tokens are then integrated into an LLM's vocabulary through lightweight projectors, enabling standard autoregressive modeling over both natural language and latent tokens. The system trains in three stages: latent-text alignment (synchronizing embeddings), latent SFT (teaching latent trajectory generation), and latent RL (optimizing via process and outcome rewards). An auxiliary decoder reconstructs text from latent trajectories for interpretability.
claim c1 [quantitative]: DLR achieves up to 20× compression compared to explicit chain-of-thought while maintaining or improving reasoning accuracy
claim c2 [quantitative]: On GSM8K-Aug with Qwen3-4B, DLR (Full) achieves 62.2% accuracy, outperforming the second-best latent baseline CoLaR (40.0%) by 22.2 percentage points
claim c3 [quantitative]: On MATH-500 with Qwen3-VL-8B, DLR (Full) achieves 54.0% accuracy, a 9.4 point gain over DLR (SFT), demonstrating consistent scaling benefit from latent RL
claim c4 [quantitative]: DLR maintains compressed reasoning length around 6.5 tokens on GSM8K-Aug (Qwen3-4B), compared to 119.8 tokens for explicit CoT-SFT and 32.0 tokens for rendered-CoT baseline RoT
claim c5 [quantitative]: Optimal latent codebook vocabulary size is 4096 tokens (κ=4096), balancing representation capacity and generalization; larger vocabularies (κ=8192) degrade performance due to under-utilization
claim c6 [qualitative]: The auxiliary OCR decoder translates learned latent trajectories into interpretable step-by-step reasoning chains; minor hallucinations (e.g., name or unit substitutions) do not disrupt underlying causal reasoning logic
claim c7 [negative]: Removing the latent-text alignment stage (Stage 1) causes major performance drop; forcing latent tokens to co-train with the backbone from scratch fails to ground them semantically
claim c8 [negative]: Omitting the Process Alignment Reward during latent RL strictly degrades accuracy across all four grade-school benchmarks; process-level verification is critical for semantic coherence of latent trajectories

LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework (2026)
seam: text; tags: verifier-scoring, logprob-expectation, trajectory-ranking, test-time-scaling, progress-monitoring, dense-reward
LLM-as-a-Verifier is a training-free verification framework that scores solution trajectories by computing the expectation over the distribution of scoring-token logits rather than collapsing to discrete scores. Given a task, two candidate trajectories, and evaluation criteria, the system constructs scoring prompts, extracts logprobs from a frozen language model's output, and aggregates them via Eq. 3.1 across three scaling dimensions (granularity of score tokens, repeated evaluations, criteria decomposition) to produce continuous reward signals. A cost-efficient Probabilistic Pivot Tournament algorithm then selects the highest-scoring trajectory from candidate pools. Fine-grained verifier scores are used for test-time ranking, progress monitoring, and as dense rewards for reinforcement learning.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LLM-as-a-Verifier achieves state-of-the-art performance on Terminal-Bench V2 (86.5%)
claim c2 [quantitative]: LLM-as-a-Verifier achieves 78.2% on SWE-Bench Verified
claim c3 [quantitative]: LLM-as-a-Verifier achieves 87.4% preference accuracy on RoboRewardBench
claim c4 [quantitative]: LLM-as-a-Verifier achieves 73.3% on MedAgentBench
claim c5 [quantitative]: Scaling scoring granularity from 1 to 20 tokens improves Signal-to-Noise Ratio from 0.775 to 0.799 and pairwise verification accuracy from baseline to higher accuracy on Terminal-Bench
claim c6 [quantitative]: Repeated evaluation scaling improves pairwise verification accuracy from 77.5% at 1 repetition to 81.2% at 5 repetitions on Terminal-Bench V2
claim c7 [quantitative]: LLM-as-a-Verifier achieves Spearman Value-Order Correlation of 0.966 on RoboRewardBench with 5 repetitions and granularity 20, substantially exceeding RoboReward-8B (0.877) and TOPReward (0.565)
claim c8 [qualitative]: LLM-as-a-Verifier can serve as a drop-in dense reward signal for both off-policy (SAC) and on-policy (GRPO) reinforcement learning, improving sample efficiency without requiring reward-model training or environment-specific shaping

Final Checkpoints Are Not Enough: Analyzing Latent Reasoning Faithfulness Along Training Trajectories (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: latent-reasoning, faithfulness-analysis, activation-patching, counterfactual-intervention, training-trajectory, causal-mediation
The paper does not propose a novel system architecture. Instead, it analyzes existing latent reasoning methods (COCONUT and CODI) built on GPT-2 backbones. These methods perform multi-step inference in continuous hidden states rather than discrete tokens. The analysis tracks how faithfulness—whether latent reasoning steps causally drive the final answer—evolves across training checkpoints using two interventions: (1) a verified counterfactual edit on the input (rewriting graph edges in ProsQA so the oracle answer flips), and (2) noise-ablation activation patching on the latent reasoning steps. Information flows from input through the model's hidden states during latent reasoning, with interventions applied to measure causal contribution.
claim c1 [qualitative]: At the output level, latent reasoning methods with different training paradigms (COCONUT staged curriculum vs. CODI self-distillation) exhibit qualitatively different faithfulness behaviors along training, even when their final-checkpoint unfaithfulness looks similar
claim c2 [qualitative]: COCONUT shifts abruptly at the curriculum boundary: the joint label distribution flips within a single epoch from counterfactual-following-dominated to retention-dominated, with the AB cell roughly quadrupling while AB nearly halves
claim c3 [qualitative]: CODI is retention-like from early checkpoints and does not show a shift during training; it enters the retention regime within the first few epochs and stays there, whereas COCONUT first becomes more counterfactually adaptive then loses that behavior at a curriculum boundary
claim c4 [qualitative]: At the activation level, the causal contribution of latent reasoning steps to the final answer decays across training in both COCONUT and CODI paradigms; output change rate falls toward zero and preserved-when-correct rate rises toward one, ending in a regime where noise ablation at the latent reasoning steps produces no detectable answer changes
claim c5 [qualitative]: The examples that flip on the output side from counterfactual following to retention (drift cohort) also lose their causal contribution on the activation side; the median contrast indirect effect on the perturbed input drops across the curriculum boundary
claim c6 [qualitative]: The activation-level trajectory reverses direction when only the answer format changes from binary choice to open-ended decoding: on GSM-open, output change rate rises along training and remains high, whereas on GSM-Choice, it falls and rises across training, matching the direction observed on ProsQA
claim c7 [negative]: At convergence, both COCONUT and CODI latent reasoning methods end with low Contrast Consistency and low Conditional Flip Rate, roughly at the answer-only baseline level on ProsQA, an order of magnitude below the explicit chain-of-thought baseline
claim c8 [qualitative]: On GSM-Choice at the best checkpoint, non-zero output change rate under zero patching reflects removable bias rather than destructive changes: the wrong-to-correct flip rate exceeds the correct-to-wrong flip rate, indicating the intervention preserves most correct predictions while shifting more incorrect predictions toward the gold answer

Constrained Decoding for Diffusion Language Models via Efficient Inference over Finite Automata (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, finite-automata, graphical-models, mean-field-posterior, diffusion-lm, log-depth-sampling
A constrained decoding system for discrete diffusion language models that samples from the mean-field posterior restricted to constraints expressible as finite automata. At each denoising step, the system performs exact inference over a chain-structured graphical model induced by the automaton, reweighting the model's factorized mean-field distribution with transition and emission factors derived from the constraint graph. Information flows from the dLLM's per-position token probabilities through forward-backward message passing and either sequential ancestral sampling or a novel log-depth tree sampler that exposes parallelism on GPUs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The approach guarantees constraint satisfaction by construction, supports both greedy and sampling-based decoding, and is compatible with parallel and block-wise decoding under arbitrary remasking schedules.
claim c2 [quantitative]: On BFCL-Live, the approach improves Dream-7B's greedy decoding accuracy from 63.9% to 71.5%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On BFCL-Live, stochastic sampling accuracy improves from 22.3% to 69.0%, where the unconstrained baseline collapses
claim c4 [quantitative]: The method incurs under 5% wall-clock overhead relative to unconstrained decoding on BFCL
claim c5 [qualitative]: Log-depth tree sampling reduces sequential depth from O(n) to O(log n) while preserving the same sampling distribution
claim c6 [quantitative]: On Sudoku, the method improves Dream-7B's greedy accuracy from 36.4% to 92.4%
claim c7 [qualitative]: The method remains robust as the number of denoising steps decreases from 256 to 16, whereas the unconstrained baseline deteriorates sharply
claim c8 [negative]: The method focuses on finite automata and does not cover richer constraints such as context-free grammars

A survey on retrieval-augmentation generation (RAG) models for healthcare applications (2025)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmented-generation, medical-qa, knowledge-base-retrieval, hallucination-mitigation, clinical-decision-support
This is a survey paper reviewing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models in healthcare contexts. RAG combines large language models with retrieval-based methods: a retriever fetches relevant documents from medical knowledge bases and clinical literature, and a generator produces responses conditioned on the retrieved context. The paper categorizes RAG architectures into Naive, Advanced, and Modular approaches, each optimizing retrieval depth, response quality, and computational efficiency. Information flows from structured medical knowledge bases → retrieval system → context integration → LLM generation → clinical response.
claim c1 [qualitative]: RAG models address critical limitations of LLMs, such as the tendency to 'hallucinate' or produce inaccurate information, by incorporating real-time retrieval from trusted medical knowledge bases and clinical literature.
claim c2 [qualitative]: RAG models are especially valuable for medical question answering, diagnostics, and treatment planning, ensuring that responses are both contextually accurate and aligned with the latest clinical evidence.
claim c3 [qualitative]: RAG architectures reviewed include Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG approaches, each framework optimizing retrieval depth, response quality, and computational efficiency.
claim c4 [qualitative]: By reducing errors in patient-care recommendations, RAG models play an essential role in scenarios that require high precision and accountability in healthcare.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Current challenges and future directions include enhancing source interpretability and adapting RAG frameworks for specialized medical fields.

Modular design patterns for hybrid learning and reasoning systems (2021)
seam: none; tags: survey, design-patterns, neuro-symbolic, taxonomy, hybrid-architecture
A taxonomic and design-pattern framework for hybrid neuro-symbolic systems. The paper does not propose a single system but rather analyzes and categorizes existing hybrid architectures through modular design patterns. It identifies elementary patterns (basic building blocks for combining statistical and symbolic methods) and compositional patterns (patterns that combine elementary patterns) that can be composed to describe the architecture of diverse hybrid systems. Information flow varies by pattern: some integrate symbolic constraints into neural forward/backward passes; others route between separate statistical and symbolic engines via data or intermediate representations.
claim c1 [qualitative]: A large body of recent literature on hybrid neuro-symbolic AI systems is highly diverse, mostly empirical, and lacks a unifying view
claim c2 [qualitative]: A very large number of hybrid systems can be described by composing only a small set of elementary patterns as building blocks
claim c3 [qualitative]: 15+ design patterns for hybrid AI systems can be organized into a set of elementary patterns and a set of compositional patterns
claim c4 [qualitative]: The design patterns reveal similarities between systems that were not recognized until now
claim c5 [qualitative]: The patterns extend and refine Kautz's earlier attempt at categorizing neuro-symbolic architectures

T-norms driven loss functions for machine learning (2023)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: neural-symbolic, t-norm, fuzzy-logic, knowledge-injection, logic-relaxation, loss-design
A neural-symbolic framework that injects prior knowledge represented as first-order logic formulas into neural network learning. The formulas are relaxed into differentiable constraints via t-norm fuzzy logic. The t-norm generator determines both the relaxation and penalty terms, creating a unified derivation that connects logic satisfaction to loss function design. Information flows from symbolic knowledge (FOL formulas) → fuzzy relaxation (t-norm generator) → differentiable loss terms → neural network optimization.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The choice of t-norm generator unambiguously determines both the relaxation of first-order logic formulas and the penalty terms enforcing constraint satisfaction, providing numerical simplification properties and tighter integration between logic knowledge and the learning objective
claim c2 [qualitative]: When restricted to supervised learning, the theoretical framework provides a straight derivation of the popular cross-entropy loss
claim c3 [qualitative]: The proposed learning formulation extends the advantages of cross-entropy loss (faster convergence and reduced vanishing gradient problem in very deep structures) to the general knowledge that can be represented by neural-symbolic methods
claim c4 [qualitative]: Novel classes of loss functions developed via the presented methodology lead to faster convergence rates than approaches previously proposed in the literature
claim c5 [qualitative]: Neural-symbolic approaches using first-order logic formulas and fuzzy logic relaxation enable competitive learning solutions with significant reduction of the amount of supervised data

Large language models for chemistry robotics (2023)
seam: external-engine; tags: llm-to-dsl, iterative-prompting, program-verification, constrained-planning, task-motion-planning, embodied-robotics
CLAIRify combines large language models with automatic iterative prompting and program verification to translate natural language chemistry experiment instructions into domain-specific language (DSL) programs. The LLM generates candidate plans; a verifier checks syntactic validity and constraint satisfaction; invalid plans trigger re-prompting with error feedback. Valid DSL programs are then executed by a PDDLStream-based constrained task and motion planner that solves for collision-free and spill-free robot trajectories in a physics-aware simulation before real-world deployment.
claim c1 [qualitative]: CLAIRify successfully generates syntactically valid domain-specific language programs for chemistry experiments through automatic iterative prompting with program verification, incorporating environmental constraints
claim c2 [qualitative]: The approach demonstrates planning and execution of chemistry experiments on a real robot, including pouring skills for various materials and two fundamental chemical experiments: solubility and recrystallization
claim c3 [qualitative]: PDDLStream solvers are used to prevent spillages of liquids and collisions when executing generated plans in chemistry labs
claim c4 [qualitative]: The framework adds a natural language interface to autonomous chemistry experiment systems to lower the barrier for non-expert users and increase utility

Learning explanatory logical rules in non-linear domains: a neuro-symbolic approach (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: neuro-symbolic-ilp, neural-logic-networks, rule-extraction, mixed-domain-learning, first-order-logic
A neuro-symbolic ILP framework built on differentiable Neural Logic networks that jointly learns first-order logic rules and non-linear functions over mixed discrete-continuous domains. The system uses neural components to learn continuous transformations while maintaining logical rule structure for interpretability, combining deep learning's function approximation capacity with ILP's relational reasoning and data efficiency.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Architecture's capability to identify non-linear functions from continuous data
claim c2 [qualitative]: Neuro-symbolic ILP framework is tailored for non-linear rule extraction in mixed discrete-continuous spaces
claim c3 [qualitative]: Methodology synergises deep learning with relational ILP models to enhance interpretability and create logical decision boundaries
claim c4 [qualitative]: ILP grapples with challenges, notably the handling of non-linearity in continuous domains

Mechanistic indicators of understanding in large language models (2026)
seam: none; tags: survey, mechanistic-interpretability, philosophy-of-mind, feature-learning, world-models, circuit-discovery
This is a theoretical and survey paper synthesizing mechanistic interpretability findings within a philosophical framework for understanding in LLMs. It does not propose a new system or mechanism, but rather integrates existing MI research (feature detection, world-state tracking, circuit discovery) into a three-tier conceptual hierarchy. Information flows conceptually across levels of computational organization rather than through a specific trained or deployed model.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Conceptual understanding emerges when a model forms 'features' as directions in latent space, learning connections between diverse manifestations of a single entity or property
claim c2 [qualitative]: State-of-the-world understanding emerges when a model learns contingent factual connections between features and dynamically tracks changes in the world
claim c3 [qualitative]: Principled understanding emerges when a model ceases to rely on memorized facts and discovers a compact 'circuit' connecting these facts
claim c4 [qualitative]: Recent findings in mechanistic interpretability render the picture of LLMs as merely imitating linguistic patterns without genuine understanding increasingly untenable
claim c5 [qualitative]: Internal organizations uncovered by MI can underwrite understanding-like unification, but these diverge from human cognition in their parallel exploitation of heterogeneous mechanisms

What’s on Your Mind, NICO? (2022)
seam: text; tags: xhri, explainable-hri, neural-extraction, multimodal-explanation, embodied-ai
NICO is a neuro-inspired companion robot that integrates neural network modules for perception and reasoning with multiple explanation modalities (non-verbal behavioral cues, verbal explanations of intentions/knowledge/reasoning, and multimodal visualizations). The system generates explanations by extracting interpretable outputs from its neural components to communicate with non-expert users in human-robot interaction scenarios.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Different types of explanations enhance the interaction experience in human-robot interaction
claim c2 [qualitative]: Non-verbal cues provide simple and intuitive feedback of robot inner states
claim c3 [qualitative]: Comprehensive verbal explanations communicate the robot's intentions, knowledge and reasoning
claim c4 [qualitative]: Multimodal explanations using visualizations, speech and text enhance explainability in HRI
claim c5 [qualitative]: Two novel XHRI approaches extract suitable verbal and multimodal explanations from neural network modules in HRI scenarios

Large scale structure-aware pronoun resolution using quantum natural language processing (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: quantum-circuits, discocat, discourse-structure, tensor-composition, pronoun-resolution
The system extends the DisCoCat (Distributional Compositional Categorical) framework from sentence to discourse level using Fock space semantics, translating discourse-level tensor representations into Variational Quantum Circuits (VQC). A classical encoder maps discourse structure (words, sentences, coreference links) into tensor form; this tensor is then translated into a parameterized quantum circuit whose gates are learned via classical optimization. The quantum circuit is executed (simulated or on IBMQ hardware) to produce a binary classification decision for pronoun resolution. Information flow: text → DisCoCat tensors → VQC parameters → quantum measurement → coreference label.
claim c1 [quantitative]: The model converged to 77.5% accuracy on the pronoun resolution task
claim c2 [qualitative]: The quantum model outperformed 2 out of 3 state-of-the-art classical coreference resolution architectures
claim c3 [qualitative]: The quantum model surpassed a Bag-of-Words baseline that neglects any structure
claim c4 [qualitative]: The DisCoCat framework was lifted from the sentence level to the discourse level using a Fock space semantics, extending previous work
claim c5 [qualitative]: The DisCoCat-VQC translation was extended to Fock space semantics and experimented with in a discourse task
claim c6 [qualitative]: Noisy and noiseless simulations were executed on IBMQ software, and the parameters of the discourse VQCs were learned

Large and Small models for collaborative cross-lingual data augmentation in entity relationship extraction for low-resource languages (2025)
seam: text; tags: data-augmentation, cross-lingual-transfer, chain-of-thought, soft-prompts, low-resource-ner, relation-extraction
LS-CLDARE is a collaborative cross-lingual data augmentation framework for entity relationship extraction in low-resource languages. An SLM extracts entity information from source text; an LLM uses chain-of-thought prompts to generate cross-lingual augmented samples by combining entity information with high-resource language descriptions. The generated samples are combined with cross-lingual soft prompts and fed into an SLM pretrained on high-resource domain data. Transfer learning and iterative data expansion improve entity recognition and relation extraction for low-resource target languages.
claim c1 [qualitative]: F1 score improved by 3.52% to 9.42% compared with other data augmentation methods
claim c2 [qualitative]: F1 score improved by 1.8% to 16.93% compared with other prompt-based relation extraction methods
claim c3 [qualitative]: LLM-based data augmentation reduces reliance on manual evaluations and generates more fluent and diverse data compared with traditional DA methods
claim c4 [qualitative]: Cross-lingual augmentation methods more effectively enhance the diversity of data in low-resource language settings by leveraging and extending data resources derived from other languages
claim c5 [qualitative]: Chain-of-thought prompts guide the LLM through step-by-step reasoning to better handle complex entity relationship extraction tasks

CX-Mind: a pioneering multimodal large language model for interleaved reasoning in chest X-ray via curriculum-guided reinforcement learning (2025)
seam: text; tags: interleaved-reasoning, reinforcement-learning, process-rewards, curriculum-learning, medical-imaging, verifiable-supervision
CX-Mind is a multimodal large language model for chest X-ray diagnosis that interleaves visual encoding with stepwise reasoning via a "think-answer" decoding pattern. The system encodes CXR images and generates structured reasoning steps followed by diagnostic answers, trained end-to-end using curriculum-guided reinforcement learning with verifiable process rewards. Information flows from image → multimodal encoder → token sequence with embedded reasoning checkpoints → policy-optimized decoding under Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), with rule-based conditional rewards supervising intermediate reasoning quality without a separate learned reward model.
claim c1 [qualitative]: CX-Mind achieves an average performance improvement of 25.1% over comparable CXR-specific models
claim c2 [qualitative]: On a real-world clinical dataset (Rui-CXR), CX-Mind achieves a mean recall@1 across 14 diseases that substantially surpasses the second-best results
claim c3 [qualitative]: CX-Mind significantly outperforms existing medical and general-domain MLLMs in visual understanding, text generation, and spatiotemporal alignment
claim c4 [qualitative]: Multi-center expert evaluations confirm clinical utility across multiple dimensions
claim c5 [qualitative]: Generated 42,828 high-quality interleaved reasoning data points supervised by clinical reports
claim c6 [qualitative]: Constructed instruction-tuning dataset CX-Set comprising 708,473 images and 2,619,148 samples
claim c7 [negative]: Existing multimodal models predominantly rely on 'one-time' diagnostic approaches, lacking verifiable supervision of the reasoning process, leading to lengthy reasoning, sparse rewards, and frequent hallucinations
claim c8 [qualitative]: Optimization conducted in two stages under GRPO framework: initially stabilizing basic reasoning with closed-domain tasks, followed by transfer to open-domain diagnostics, incorporating rule-based conditional process rewards to bypass the need for pretrained reward models

Verified synthesis of optimal safety controllers for human-robot collaboration (2022)
seam: external-engine; tags: synthesis, formal-verification, safety-controller, digital-twin, design-space-exploration
A tool-supported synthesis and verification pipeline for safety controllers in human-robot collaboration. The system generates candidate controllers from a design space, formally verifies them against safety specifications derived from risk analysis and regulatory requirements, and translates verified controllers into executable code for testing in a digital twin. The controller detects hazards, transitions the process to safe states, and can resume operation under specified conditions.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Controllers are selected from a design space of feasible controllers according to a set of optimality criteria, are formally verified against correctness criteria, and are translated into executable code and tested in a digital twin
claim c2 [qualitative]: The resulting controller can detect the occurrence of hazards, move the process into a safe state, and, under certain circumstances, return the process to an operational state from which it can resume its original task
claim c3 [qualitative]: The integrated synthesis, verification, and test approach is informed by the process, risk analysis, and relevant safety regulations for the target application
claim c4 [qualitative]: The approach assures the correctness of safety controllers under explicit (and preferably weak) assumptions

Large language model driven development of turbulence models (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: iterative-refinement, long-chain-reasoning, physics-grounding, cfd-evaluation-loop, interpretable-model-synthesis
An iterative human–LLM co-design loop in which DeepSeek-R1 proposes, refines, and evaluates near-wall turbulence models. The LLM receives problem context, prior model evaluation results (a priori and a posteriori metrics), and human feedback, then generates new model equations and reasoning chains. The loop closes when the LLM's proposed models are tested against CFD data and results fed back for the next iteration. No trained neural components are central; the system is a structured prompting workflow with external CFD evaluation as the grounding engine.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The LLM proposes, refines and reasons about near-wall turbulence models under adverse pressure gradients (APGs), system rotation and surface roughness through multiple rounds of interaction involving long-chain reasoning and a priori and a posteriori evaluations.
claim c2 [qualitative]: The LLM generates models that not only rediscover established strategies, but also synthesise new ones that outperform baseline wall models.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The LLM recommends incorporating a material derivative to capture history effects in APG flows.
claim c4 [qualitative]: The LLM recommends modifying the law of the wall to account for system rotation.
claim c5 [qualitative]: The LLM develops rough-wall models informed by surface statistics.
claim c6 [qualitative]: The models developed are physically interpretable and grounded in clear reasoning, in contrast to conventional data-driven turbulence modelling often characterised by human-designed, black-box architectures.

Human-like systematic generalization through a meta-learning neural network (2023)
seam: text; tags: meta-learning, compositionality, systematic-generalization, instruction-learning
A standard neural network (details unspecified in abstract) trained via meta-learning for compositionality (MLC), a training procedure that presents a dynamic stream of compositional tasks to optimize the model's compositional skills. The model processes instructions and learns to generalize systematically to novel compositional combinations, similar to human behavior in an instruction-learning paradigm.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Neural networks can achieve human-like systematicity when optimized for their compositional skills
claim c2 [qualitative]: MLC achieves both the systematicity and flexibility needed for human-like generalization, in contrast to probabilistic symbolic models (perfectly systematic but rigid) and standard neural networks (perfectly flexible but unsystematic)
claim c3 [qualitative]: MLC advances the compositional skills of machine learning systems in several systematic generalization benchmarks
claim c4 [qualitative]: Human behavioural experiments using an instruction learning paradigm show MLC achieves human-like generalization in head-to-head comparison with seven different models

Natural language instructions induce compositional generalization in networks of neurons (2024)
seam: text; tags: zero-shot-generalization, compositional-skills, language-scaffolding, instruction-following, motor-language-alignment
A neural network model trained on psychophysical tasks receives natural language instructions embedded by a pretrained language model. The model learns to map linguistic instructions to task-relevant sensorimotor representations, achieving zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks. Language scaffolds the activity such that interrelated tasks share common geometric structure with instruction embeddings, allowing composition of practiced skills. The model can also generate linguistic descriptions of novel tasks from motor feedback alone, which can guide partner models.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Models can perform a previously unseen task with an average performance of 83% correct based solely on linguistic instructions (zero-shot learning)
claim c2 [qualitative]: Language scaffolds sensorimotor representations such that activity for interrelated tasks shares a common geometry with the semantic representations of instructions
claim c3 [qualitative]: Models can generate a linguistic description of a novel task identified using only motor feedback, which can subsequently guide a partner model to perform the task
claim c4 [qualitative]: The model offers experimentally testable predictions outlining how linguistic information must be represented to facilitate flexible and general cognition in the human brain

Associative memory of structured knowledge (2022)
seam: own-activations; tags: associative-memory, vsa, structured-knowledge, recurrent-network, attractor-dynamics, retrieval-cues
A recurrent neural network model that stores structured knowledge using vector symbolic architecture (VSA) combined with associative memory plasticity rules. Knowledge structures are represented as sets of binary relations between events and attributes, encoded into distributed neuronal activity patterns via VSA, then stored as fixed points in a recurrent network using Hebbian-like plasticity. Retrieval occurs via partial cues that converge to memorized attractors.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The model allows for efficient storage of knowledge structures such that the memorized structures as well as their individual building blocks (e.g., events and attributes) can be subsequently retrieved from partial retrieving cues.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Long-term memory of structured knowledge relies on a new principle of computation beyond the memory basins.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The model can be extended to store sequences of memories as single attractors.
claim c4 [theoretical]: Signal-to-noise analysis combined with numerical simulations demonstrate efficient storage capacity for knowledge structures represented as binary relations between events and attributes.

Expert of Experts Verification and Alignment (EVAL) Framework for Large Language Models Safety in Gastroenterology (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: reward-model, rejection-sampling, similarity-ranking, medical-safety, human-alignment
EVAL is a verification and ranking framework for LLM-generated medical responses. Information flows from LLM outputs through similarity-based ranking (using Fine-Tuned ColBERT and other metrics) and a trained reward model; top-ranked responses are selected via rejection sampling. The system pairs learned similarity scoring with a supervised reward model trained on human-graded responses to filter and rank candidate answers.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Fine-Tuned ColBERT achieved the highest alignment with human performance across three separate datasets
claim c2 [quantitative]: The reward model replicated human grading with 87.9% of cases across temperature settings
claim c3 [quantitative]: significantly improved accuracy through rejection sampling by 8.36% overall
claim c4 [qualitative]: EVAL uses similarity-based ranking and a reward model trained on human-graded responses for rejection sampling
claim c5 [qualitative]: Evaluated OpenAI's GPT-3.5/4/4o/o1-preview, Anthropic's Claude-3-Opus, Meta's LLaMA-2 (7B/13B/70B), and Mistral AI's Mixtral (7B) across 27 configurations, including zero-shot baseline, retrieval-augmented generation, and supervised fine-tuning

Evaluating clinical AI summaries with large language models as judges (2025)
seam: text; tags: llm-judge, evaluation, clinical-nlp, inter-rater-reliability
The paper proposes an LLM-as-a-Judge framework that uses large language models (specifically reasoning models like GPT-o3-mini) to automatically evaluate clinical EHR summaries generated by generative AI systems. The framework operates by having LLMs score summaries against the Provider Documentation Summarization Quality Instrument (PDSQI) rubric and comparing those automated scores to human evaluations. Information flows from generated clinical summaries into the LLM evaluator, which outputs quality scores that are then benchmarked against human reviewer scores.
claim c1 [quantitative]: GPT-o3-mini achieved an intraclass correlation coefficient of 0.818 (95% CI 0.772-0.854)
claim c2 [quantitative]: GPT-o3-mini achieved a median score difference of 0 from humans
claim c3 [quantitative]: GPT-o3-mini completed evaluations in 22 seconds
claim c4 [qualitative]: Reasoning models excelled in inter-rater reliability, particularly for evaluations requiring advanced reasoning and domain expertise, outperforming non-reasoning, task-trained, and multi-agent approaches
claim c5 [qualitative]: LLM-as-a-Judge framework demonstrated strong inter-rater reliability with human evaluators when benchmarked against the validated Provider Documentation Summarization Quality Instrument (PDSQI)

Autonomous GIS: the next-generation AI-powered GIS (2023)
seam: external-engine; tags: code-generation, spatial-analysis, llm-reasoning, data-visualization, autonomous-workflow
LLM-Geo is an autonomous GIS system that uses GPT-4 as a reasoning core to understand natural language queries about spatial problems and automatically generate code for spatial data collection, analysis, and visualization. The LLM receives a geographic question, reasons about required spatial operations, generates executable code, retrieves or processes spatial data, and returns results (numbers, graphs, maps) without human intervention.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LLM-Geo returned accurate results, including aggregated numbers, graphs, and maps for all case studies
claim c2 [qualitative]: Autonomous GIS will need to achieve five autonomous goals: self-generating, self-organizing, self-verifying, self-executing, and self-growing
claim c3 [negative]: LLM-Geo is still in its infancy and lacks several important modules such as logging and code testing

A question-answering framework for geospatial data retrieval enhanced by a knowledge graph and large language models (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: knowledge-graph, rag, semantic-reasoning, spatiotemporal-logic, text-to-kg
GDQA is a question-answering system that combines a geospatial data knowledge graph (GDKG) with large language models (LLMs) to enable natural language-based data discovery. The system first constructs GDKG by aggregating data from five major portals and using an information extraction model to automatically identify and structure knowledge from unstructured text. It then implements Spatio-Temporal Reasoning on Knowledge Graphs (STRKG), which enables LLMs to reason and explore over GDKG to answer complex geoscientific queries. The architecture bridges domain-specific knowledge from the structured knowledge graph with the flexibility and natural language understanding capabilities of LLMs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: GDKG is constructed by aggregating data from five major portals and employing an information extraction model to automatically identify and structure fine-grained knowledge from unstructured texts
claim c2 [qualitative]: Spatio-Temporal Reasoning on Knowledge Graphs (STRKG) enables LLMs to perform reasoning and exploration over GDKG, enhancing their ability to understand and respond to complex geoscientific queries
claim c3 [qualitative]: GDQA achieves superior performance in both accuracy and efficiency compared to baseline approaches, significantly reducing time and token costs

Improving medical reasoning through retrieval and self-reflection with retrieval-augmented large language models (2024)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmented-generation, biomedical-domain, self-reflection, instruction-tuning
Self-BioRAG is a retrieval-augmented generation framework for biomedical question-answering that combines an LLM backbone with a biomedical document retriever, a biomedical corpus, and domain-specific instruction tuning. The system generates explanations, retrieves domain-specific documents conditionally, and applies self-reflection via customized reflective tokens to assess generated responses before final output. Information flows from the input question through the LLM, which decides whether to trigger retrieval, then incorporates retrieved documents and self-reflection scores back into generation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Self-BioRAG achieves a 7.2% absolute improvement on average over the state-of-the-art open-foundation model with a parameter size of 7B or less on three major medical question-answering benchmark datasets
claim c2 [quantitative]: Self-BioRAG outperforms RAG by 8% Rouge-1 score in generating more proficient answers on two long-form question-answering benchmarks on average
claim c3 [qualitative]: Self-BioRAG can assess its generated explanations with customized reflective tokens
claim c4 [qualitative]: Self-BioRAG finds the clues in the question, retrieves relevant documents if needed, and understands how to answer with information from retrieved documents and encoded knowledge as a medical expert does
claim c5 [qualitative]: Domain-specific components, such as a retriever, domain-related document corpus, and instruction sets are necessary for adhering to domain-related instructions
claim c6 [qualitative]: Self-BioRAG is trained on 84k filtered biomedical instruction sets

RT: a Retrieving and Chain-of-Thought framework for few-shot medical named entity recognition (2024)
seam: text; tags: retrieval, chain-of-thought, few-shot-learning, in-context-examples, biomedical-ner
RT is a few-shot NER framework that combines retrieval and chain-of-thought reasoning on top of a larger language model. A retrieval module identifies relevant in-context examples from a demonstration pool for each input sentence; those examples are fed as context to the LLM. A chain-of-thought module then guides the LLM through systematic reasoning steps to identify and label biomedical entities in the input.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Our proposed RT framework outperforms other LMs for few-shot NER tasks with micro-F1 scores of 93.50 and 91.76 on BC5CDR and NCBI corpora, respectively
claim c2 [quantitative]: Our proposed RT framework outperforms other LMs for few-shot NER tasks with micro-F1 scores of 93.50 and 91.76 on BC5CDR and NCBI corpora, respectively
claim c3 [qualitative]: RT framework outperforms 16 other models for few-shot NER tasks
claim c4 [qualitative]: Chain-of-Thought performed better than Tree-of-Thought when using both positive and negative samples
claim c5 [qualitative]: utilization of a partially annotated dataset has a marginal effect on model performance
claim c6 [qualitative]: This is the first investigation to combine a retrieval-based LLM and Chain-of-Thought methodology to enhance the performance in biomedical few-shot NER

Large language models as tax attorneys: a case study in legal capabilities emergence (2024)
seam: text; tags: few-shot-prompting, retrieval-augmented-generation, legal-reasoning, evaluation
The paper evaluates off-the-shelf LLM systems (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and prior OpenAI model releases) on tax law reasoning tasks. The input is a tax law question; the system applies few-shot prompting (example question-answer pairs) and optionally retrieves relevant legal authority (tax code sections or regulations) to inject as additional context via text prompt augmentation. The output is the model's text reasoning and answer. No training or fine-tuning of the LLM is performed; all enhancement is via prompt engineering and retrieval-augmented context.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LLMs demonstrate emerging legal understanding capabilities, with improved performance in each subsequent OpenAI model release
claim c2 [qualitative]: Retrieving and using relevant legal authority has an impact on performance when provided as additional legal context to LLMs
claim c3 [qualitative]: Few-shot prompting, presenting examples of question-answer pairs, significantly enhances the performance of GPT-4
claim c4 [qualitative]: LLMs, particularly when combined with prompting enhancements and the correct legal texts, can perform at high levels of accuracy but not yet at expert tax lawyer levels

Associative memory of structured knowledge (2022)
seam: own-activations; tags: vsa, associative-memory, fixed-point-attractors, structured-knowledge, partial-cue-retrieval
A recurrent neural network (RNN) model that encodes structured knowledge—sets of binary relations between events and attributes—using vector symbolic architecture (VSA) schemes, then stores the binarized VSA patterns as fixed points/attractors via associative memory plasticity rules. Partial cues are used to retrieve complete knowledge structures and their individual components (events, attributes) from the learned fixed points.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The model allows for efficient storage of knowledge structures, such that memorized structures as well as their individual building blocks (events and attributes) can be subsequently retrieved from partial retrieving cues.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Long-term memory of structured knowledge relies on a new principle of computation beyond the memory basins.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The model can be extended to store sequences of memories as single attractors.

Structure-informed Language Models Are Protein Designers (2023)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: protein-design, structure-conditioning, adapter-surgery, iterative-refinement, fold-based-generation
LM-Design reprograms sequence-based protein language models (pLMs) pre-trained on natural protein sequences to perform structure-conditioned protein design. A lightweight structural adapter is implanted into the pLM via structural surgery, injecting structural awareness without retraining the base model. During inference, iterative refinement optimizes generated sequences for a given protein fold. The pLM's sequential evolutionary knowledge is combined with structural conditioning to guide sequence generation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LM-Design improves state-of-the-art results by 4% to 12% accuracy gains in sequence recovery on single-chain benchmarks
claim c2 [quantitative]: Achieves 55.65% sequence recovery accuracy on CATH 4.2 single-chain benchmark
claim c3 [quantitative]: Achieves 56.63% sequence recovery accuracy on CATH 4.3 single-chain benchmark
claim c4 [quantitative]: Achieves greater than 60% sequence recovery accuracy when designing protein complexes
claim c5 [qualitative]: LM-Design can leverage both structural and sequential knowledge to accurately handle structurally non-deterministic regions
claim c6 [qualitative]: LM-Design benefits from scaling data and model size
claim c7 [qualitative]: LM-Design generalizes to other proteins such as antibodies and de novo proteins

From Mechanistic Interpretability to Mechanistic Biology: Training, Evaluating, and Interpreting Sparse Autoencoders on Protein Language Models (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, mechanistic-interpretability, residual-stream, linear-probing, protein-biology
Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are trained on the residual stream activations of ESM-2, a protein language model. The SAE decomposes pLM hidden states into interpretable sparse features. Features are characterized through linear probing onto protein properties (thermostability, subcellular localization) and visualized to identify sequence determinants and hypothesize unknown biological mechanisms.
claim c1 [qualitative]: pLMs use a combination of generic features and family-specific features to represent a protein
claim c2 [qualitative]: Known sequence determinants of properties such as thermostability and subcellular localization can be identified by linear probing of SAE features
claim c3 [qualitative]: For predictive features without known functional associations, the study hypothesizes their role in unknown mechanisms and provides visualization tools to aid their interpretation

Sparse Autoencoders Reveal Interpretable Features in Single-Cell Foundation Models (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, activation-steering, interpretability, feature-intervention, single-cell-biology
The paper trains sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on the hidden representations of three single-cell foundation models (scGPT, scFoundation, Geneformer). The SAE learns a dictionary of interpretable features from the frozen model's activations. Information flows from the scFM's hidden states into the SAE encoder, which produces sparse feature activations that can be decoded back. The learned features are then intervened upon (suppressed or activated) to steer model behavior, with interventions returning as modifications to the hidden state that propagate forward through the frozen scFM.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAE-learned features reveal diverse and complex biological and technical signals, which emerge even in pre-trained models
claim c2 [qualitative]: The encoding of information differs between scFMs with distinct training protocols and architectures
claim c3 [qualitative]: SAE-derived features are functionally related to model behavior and can be intervened upon
claim c4 [qualitative]: Suppressing batch-associated features reduces unwanted technical variation and improves data integration while preserving the core biological signal
claim c5 [qualitative]: Activating drug-encoding features steers control cells toward drug-perturbed states in a concentration-dependent manner

Chat with the Environment: Interactive Multimodal Perception Using Large Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: epistemic-action, multimodal-grounding, interactive-perception, robot-planning, reasoning-loop
An interactive perception system where a large language model serves as the backbone to plan and reason over multimodal robot sensing (vision, sound, haptics, proprioception). The LLM instructs epistemic actions to sample sensory information in a partially observable environment, reasons over the resulting multimodal observations, and plans task execution. Multimodal modules ground the LLM's processing by providing sensory context that extends its ability to decide on information-gathering actions before task execution.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LLMs can provide high-level planning and reasoning skills and control interactive robot behavior in a multimodal environment, while multimodal modules with the context of the environmental state help ground the LLMs and extend their processing ability.
claim c2 [qualitative]: An interactive perception framework enables a robot to decide on a range of epistemic actions in order to sample sensory information among multiple modalities before being able to execute the task correctly in a partially observable state.

Can ChatGPT Enable ITS? The Case of Mixed Traffic Control via Reinforcement Learning (2023)
seam: text; tags: llm-assisted-design, reinforcement-learning, motive-elicitation, traffic-control, user-study
The system augments a human user's ability to design RL agents for traffic control by using ChatGPT as a knowledge assistant. A user (novice or expert) interacts with ChatGPT via natural language to formulate state spaces and reward functions for a Markov Decision Process (MDP) in mixed traffic control environments. ChatGPT's responses are then used to construct and train an RL agent in one of three traffic simulation environments (ring road, bottleneck, intersection). The flow is: user query → ChatGPT reasoning/suggestion → user implements MDP components → RL training → policy evaluation.
claim c1 [quantitative]: ChatGPT increases number of successful policies by 150% for intersection compared to solely beginner capabilities
claim c2 [quantitative]: ChatGPT increases number of successful policies by 136% for bottleneck compared to solely beginner capabilities
claim c3 [qualitative]: Some ChatGPT-assisted policies outperform expert-designed policies in intersection and bottleneck scenarios
claim c4 [negative]: ChatGPT does not provide consistent improvements across all scenarios
claim c5 [qualitative]: Study evaluated whether novices can leverage ChatGPT to solve complex mixed traffic control problems through developing state space and reward function formulations

From Decision to Action in Surgical Autonomy: Multi-Modal Large Language Models for Robot-Assisted Blood Suction (2025)
seam: text; tags: hierarchical-planning, vision-language, surgical-autonomy, task-reasoning, reinforcement-learning
A hierarchical two-level system combining a multi-modal LLM (vision + text) for high-level surgical task planning and reasoning with a lower-level deep reinforcement learning model for motion planning and execution. The LLM receives visual input from the surgical scene, reasons about dynamic conditions (blood clots, active bleeding), and outputs task decisions that guide the RL agent's real-time control of a surgical robot for blood suction.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Using a multi-modal LLM as a higher-level reasoning unit can account for surgical complexities to achieve a level of reasoning previously unattainable in robot-assisted surgeries
claim c2 [qualitative]: The multi-modal LLM integration enables reasoning and adaptation to changes in the environment, including blood clots and active bleeding, to influence decision-making in autonomous blood suction
claim c3 [qualitative]: Multi-modal LLMs enhance contextual understanding and decision-making in robotic-assisted surgeries

A Survey on Symbolic Knowledge Distillation of Large Language Models (2024)
seam: none; tags: survey, knowledge-distillation, symbolic-extraction, interpretability
This is a survey paper on symbolic knowledge distillation in large language models. It does not present a novel system or mechanism, but rather reviews and categorizes existing research on the process of extracting implicit knowledge from LLMs (such as GPT-3 and BERT) and converting it into explicit, symbolic form to improve interpretability, efficiency, and applicability of smaller AI models.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Symbolic knowledge distillation is crucial for enhancing the interpretability, efficiency, and applicability of LLMs
claim c2 [qualitative]: The core challenge of symbolic knowledge distillation is maintaining the depth of knowledge in a comprehensible format
claim c3 [qualitative]: Symbolic knowledge distillation can be used to improve the transparency and functionality of smaller, more efficient artificial intelligence models

Grammar-Based Game Description Generation Using Large Language Models (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: constrained-decoding, grammar-guided-generation, iterative-refinement, parser-feedback
A two-stage framework that leverages Large Language Models to generate Game Description Language (GDL) from natural language. The first stage gradually constructs a minimal grammar based on GDL specifications. The second stage iteratively refines game descriptions through grammar-guided generation, using a specialized parser to identify valid subsequences and candidate symbols from LLM responses, ensuring grammatical correctness through iterative improvement cycles.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Our iterative improvement approach significantly outperforms baseline methods that directly use LLM outputs
claim c2 [qualitative]: Generating GDL descriptions from natural language remains a challenging task

Survey on Large Language Model-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning: Concept, Taxonomy, and Methods (2024)
seam: text; tags: survey, llm-rl-integration, information-processor, reward-design, decision-making, task-planning, taxonomy
A survey categorizing how large language models augment reinforcement learning agents through four functional roles: information processor (interpreting observations, language feedback, task descriptions), reward designer (specifying or learning reward functions), decision-maker (policy generation, planning, action selection), and generator (environment/task/data synthesis). Information flows from the RL agent-environment loop through the LLM to enhance sample efficiency, multitask learning, and high-level task planning.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Large language models with extensive pretrained knowledge and high-level general capabilities emerge as a promising avenue to augment reinforcement learning in aspects such as multitask learning, sample efficiency, and high-level task planning.
claim c2 [qualitative]: LLMs function in four roles in RL: information processor, reward designer, decision-maker, and generator.
claim c3 [qualitative]: As an information processor, LLMs interpret observations, language feedback, and task descriptions to enhance state representation and understanding in RL.
claim c4 [qualitative]: As a reward designer, LLMs specify or learn reward functions to guide RL agent behavior.
claim c5 [qualitative]: As a decision-maker, LLMs generate policies, perform planning, and select actions for RL agents.
claim c6 [qualitative]: As a generator, LLMs synthesize environments, tasks, and training data to support RL learning.
claim c7 [qualitative]: LLM-enhanced RL has potential applications in robotics, autonomous driving, and energy systems.

L2M-AID: Autonomous Cyber-Physical Defense by Fusing Semantic Reasoning of Large Language Models with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: llm-semantic-bridge, multi-agent-rl, cyber-physical-defense, state-abstraction, cooperative-strategies
L2M-AID fuses LLM semantic reasoning with multi-agent reinforcement learning (MAPPO) for industrial cyber-physical defense. An LLM acts as a semantic bridge, translating unstructured IIoT telemetry into contextual state representations that capture adversary intent. This semantically-enriched state feeds collaborative agents trained by MAPPO, which learn cooperative defense strategies optimized by a reward function balancing threat neutralization against physical process stability disruption penalties.
claim c1 [quantitative]: L2M-AID achieves a 97.2% detection rate while reducing false positives by over 80%
claim c2 [quantitative]: L2M-AID improves response times by a factor of four
claim c3 [qualitative]: L2M-AID significantly outperforms traditional IDS, deep learning anomaly detectors, and single-agent RL baselines across key metrics
claim c4 [qualitative]: L2M-AID demonstrates superior performance in maintaining physical process stability
claim c5 [qualitative]: LLM as semantic bridge translates vast, unstructured telemetry into rich, contextual state representation, enabling agents to reason about adversary intent rather than merely matching patterns

egg: Fast and extensible equality saturation (2021)
seam: none; tags: e-graph, equality-saturation, rewrite-rules, congruence-closure, e-class-analysis, rebuilding
egg is a library implementing e-graphs (equality graphs) for equality saturation. An e-graph compactly represents many equivalent expressions as equivalence classes (e-classes) with congruence relations between them. The system reads in a program/expression, applies rewrite rules that add equivalent expressions to the e-graph, and saturates the graph until no new rewrites apply. Two key mechanisms: (1) rebuilding, an amortized invariant restoration technique that efficiently maintains congruence after rule application, and (2) e-class analyses, a domain-specific analysis framework that attaches analyses to e-classes to filter and guide rewrites. Information flows via rule matching over e-graph nodes, rule application adding new e-nodes, and periodic rebuilding to restore parent-child congruence invariants.
claim c1 [qualitative]: A new amortized invariant restoration technique called rebuilding takes advantage of equality saturation's distinct workload, providing asymptotic speedups over current techniques in practice.
claim c2 [qualitative]: A general mechanism called e-class analyses integrates domain-specific analyses into the e-graph, reducing the need for ad hoc manipulation.
claim c3 [qualitative]: egg's case studies on three previously published applications of equality saturation highlight how egg's performance and flexibility enable state-of-the-art results across diverse domains.

End-to-End Multimodal Fact-Checking and Explanation Generation: A Challenging Dataset and Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: multimodal-retrieval, claim-verification, explanation-generation, pipeline
End-to-end multimodal fact-checking system that takes a claim and web sources (articles, images, videos, tweets) as input. The system operates through three pipelined subtasks: (1) multimodal evidence retrieval to find relevant textual and visual evidence from the collection, (2) claim verification to predict a truthfulness label (support, refute, or not enough information), and (3) explanation generation to produce a natural-language statement summarizing the reasoning and ruling. State-of-the-art neural architectures are applied to each subtask in sequence.
claim c1 [qualitative]: We construct MOCHEG, a large-scale dataset consisting of 15,601 claims where each claim is annotated with a truthfulness label and a ruling statement, and 33,880 textual paragraphs and 12,112 images in total as evidence.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Performance of the state-of-the-art end-to-end multimodal fact-checking does not provide satisfactory outcomes.
claim c3 [qualitative]: We are the first to build the benchmark dataset and solutions for end-to-end multimodal fact-checking and explanation generation.

LayoutLLM-T2I: Eliciting Layout Guidance from LLM for Text-to-Image Generation (2023)
seam: text; tags: layout-planning, in-context-learning, diffusion-conditioning, two-stage-generation, spatial-reasoning
LayoutLLM-T2I is a two-stage text-to-image generation system. Stage 1 uses an LLM with in-context learning to generate a coarse-grained spatial layout (bounding boxes and object positions) from a text prompt, without requiring manual guidance. Stage 2 conditions a diffusion model (building on Stable Diffusion) on both the original text prompt and the automatically generated layout to produce high-fidelity images via an object-interaction diffusion method. Information flows from text → LLM → layout representation → diffusion model → image.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Current text-to-image models face misalignment issues including problematic spatial relation understanding and numeration failure in complex natural scenes
claim c2 [qualitative]: Proposes a coarse-to-fine paradigm to achieve layout planning and image generation without requiring manual guidance information from users
claim c3 [qualitative]: Generates coarse-grained layout conditioned on textual prompt via in-context learning based on Large Language Models
claim c4 [qualitative]: Proposes a fine-grained object-interaction diffusion method to synthesize high-faithfulness images conditioned on the prompt and automatically generated layout
claim c5 [qualitative]: Method outperforms state-of-the-art models in terms of layout and image generation

TinyNS: Platform-aware Neurosymbolic Auto Tiny Machine Learning (2023)
seam: external-engine; tags: neurosymbolic, architecture-search, bayesian-optimization, hardware-aware, extreme-edge, constraint-satisfaction
TinyNS is a platform-aware neural-symbolic architecture search framework that jointly optimizes symbolic and neural operators for extreme-edge deployment. The system uses a gradient-free Bayesian optimizer to search over a discontinuous, conditional space of model configurations (5 neurosymbolic types), generating microcontroller code that interleaves symbolic rules/physics constraints with neural components. During optimization, TinyNS communicates with target hardware to ensure resource feasibility (memory, compute, latency), guaranteeing deployability before code generation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: TinyNS provides recipes and parsers to automatically write microcontroller code for five types of neurosymbolic models, combining the context awareness and integrity of symbolic techniques with the robustness and performance of machine learning models.
claim c2 [qualitative]: TinyNS uses a fast, gradient-free, black-box Bayesian optimizer over discontinuous, conditional, numeric, and categorical search spaces to find the best synergy of symbolic code and neural networks within the hardware resource budget.
