claim c3 [qualitative]: To guarantee deployability, TinyNS talks to the target hardware during the optimization process.
claim c4 [qualitative]: In all use cases, TinyNS outperforms purely neural or purely symbolic approaches while guaranteeing execution on real hardware.

Knowledge-Aware Code Generation with Large Language Models (2024)
seam: text; tags: knowledge-injection, prompt-augmentation, code-generation, algorithmic-reasoning
KareCoder integrates a Knowledge Library of algorithmic and data structure concepts with LLM code generation through prompt engineering. The system constructs prompts that combine task description with relevant knowledge from the library, injecting this augmented context into the LLM's reasoning process to improve code generation for programming problems, especially those the LLM has not encountered during pre-training.
claim c1 [quantitative]: KareCoder achieved a relative improvement of 23.3% on the Pass@1 metric on the CodeF post2021-9 dataset compared to code directly generated by ChatGPT
claim c2 [qualitative]: KareCoder enhances LLMs' understanding and problem-solving capabilities by integrating prompt and knowledge from the library into the LLMs' code generation reasoning process, especially on Pass@1 metrics
claim c3 [qualitative]: KareCoder performs well compared to other methods when dealing with problems that LLMs have previously encountered
claim c4 [qualitative]: LLMs struggle to effectively apply algorithmic and data structure knowledge when faced with specific new problems, despite having learned such knowledge
claim c5 [negative]: ChatGPT exhibits proficient performance on problems it has encountered during its pre-training phase, but this performance deteriorates when faced with novel problems
claim c6 [qualitative]: KareCoder demonstrated outstanding performance in handling novel problems previously unencountered by LLMs

Modality Plug-and-Play: Runtime Modality Adaptation in LLM-Driven Autonomous Mobile Systems (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: modality-adaptation, kv-injection, runtime-routing, flexible-layer-insertion, autonomous-mobile-systems
MPnP connects data encoders (for diverse input modalities) to flexible positions across the last blocks of a frozen LLM, rather than only to the input layer. The connection points and their weights are made fully trainable at runtime, allowing the model to adaptively select which modalities to route into which LLM layers based on environmental context and task requirements. Information flows from per-modality encoders into learned adapter projections that inject activations into mid-to-late transformer blocks of the host LLM.
claim c1 [qualitative]: 3.7× FLOPs reduction compared to best baselines
claim c2 [qualitative]: 30% memory usage reduction compared to best baselines
claim c3 [qualitative]: requires only few hundreds of training samples at runtime
claim c4 [qualitative]: completes modality adaptation within few minutes on weak devices
claim c5 [qualitative]: high compute and data efficiency compared to fixed-connection baselines that train encoders only at the LLM input layer

Following Clues, Approaching the Truth: Explainable Micro-Video Rumor Detection via Chain-of-Thought Reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: chain-of-thought, knowledge-retrieval, multimodal, distillation, explainability
ExMRD is an explainable micro-video rumor detection framework that uses a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) guided by a novel R3CoT (Refining, Retrieving, Reasoning on MVRD) inference mechanism to reorganize video content, retrieve domain knowledge about rumors, and generate explanations. A smaller trained language model (SLReviewer) distills the MLLM outputs to produce efficient and reliable predictions with rationales, avoiding direct fine-tuning of the expensive MLLM.
claim c1 [qualitative]: ExMRD significantly outperforms competitive baselines while providing high-quality rationales
claim c2 [qualitative]: R3CoT mechanism enables Multimodal Large Language Models to reorganize the original video content, retrieve domain knowledge related to rumors, and generate explainable conclusions regarding whether the micro-video contains rumor information
claim c3 [qualitative]: SLReviewer distills the outputs of R3CoT guided MLLMs to ensure efficient and reliable predictions
claim c4 [qualitative]: Existing Micro-Video Rumor Detection methods are generally black-box, which lacks transparency and makes it difficult to understand the reasoning behind classification decisions

Retrieval And Structuring Augmented Generation with Large Language Models (2025)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmentation, structured-knowledge, taxonomy-construction, information-extraction, prompt-integration, reasoning-frameworks, knowledge-embedding
A survey of Retrieval And Structuring (RAS) Augmented Generation methods that integrate dynamic information retrieval (sparse, dense, and hybrid approaches) with structured knowledge representations (taxonomy construction, hierarchical classification, information extraction) to augment LLMs. Information flows from external retrieval engines into structured intermediate representations, which are then integrated with LLMs via prompt-based methods, reasoning frameworks, and knowledge embedding techniques to mitigate hallucination, outdated knowledge, and domain expertise limitations.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LLMs face critical challenges when deployed in real-world applications, including hallucination generation, outdated knowledge, and limited domain expertise
claim c2 [qualitative]: Retrieval And Structuring Augmented Generation addresses limitations by integrating dynamic information retrieval with structured knowledge representations
claim c3 [qualitative]: Retrieval mechanisms include sparse, dense, and hybrid approaches for accessing external knowledge
claim c4 [qualitative]: Text structuring techniques such as taxonomy construction, hierarchical classification, and information extraction transform unstructured text into organized representations
claim c5 [qualitative]: Structured representations integrate with LLMs through prompt-based methods, reasoning frameworks, and knowledge embedding techniques
claim c6 [qualitative]: Technical challenges include retrieval efficiency, structure quality, and knowledge integration

Advancing Tool-Augmented Large Language Models via Meta-Verification and Reflection Learning (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: tool-planning, tool-reflection, error-correction-loop, instruction-dataset-curation, verifier-pipeline
Tool-MVR is an open-source LLM (Qwen-7B) finetuned on two high-quality instruction datasets to improve tool-augmented reasoning. The system operates in two stages: (1) Multi-Agent Meta-Verification (MAMV) validates APIs, queries, and reasoning trajectories offline to construct ToolBench-V, eliminating hallucinated API calls from training data; (2) Exploration-based Reflection Learning (EXPLORE) uses tool feedback in a dynamic 'Error → Reflection → Correction' loop to construct ToolBench-R, enabling the model to recover from tool invocation failures. Information flow: raw tool-use instructions → MAMV validation pipeline → cleaned ToolBench-V → finetuning + parallel EXPLORE feedback collection → ToolBench-R → combined finetuning → inference with improved tool planning and reflection.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Tool-MVR achieves state-of-the-art performance on StableToolBench, surpassing both ToolLLM (by 23.9%) and GPT-4 (by 15.3%)
claim c2 [quantitative]: Tool-MVR reduces API calls by 31.4%
claim c3 [quantitative]: On RefineToolBench, Tool-MVR achieves a 58.9% error correction rate
claim c4 [negative]: Current models face weak tool reflection abilities: over 90% of errors cannot be corrected resulting from static imitation learning
claim c5 [qualitative]: Multi-Agent Meta-Verification (MAMV) rigorously validates APIs, queries, and reasoning trajectories to construct ToolBench-V, a new high-quality instruction dataset that addresses the limitation of unreliable tool planning and invocation
claim c6 [qualitative]: Exploration-based Reflection Learning (EXPLORE) enhances tool reflection capabilities by leveraging tool feedback through a dynamic 'Error → Reflection → Correction' learning paradigm
claim c7 [qualitative]: Tool-MVR demonstrates strong generalization capabilities across unseen tools and scenarios

Monte Carlo Tree Search for Graph Reasoning in Large Language Model Agents (2025)
seam: external-engine; tags: graph-retrieval, mcts, knowledge-graph, rag, reasoning-loop
Graph-MCTS enhances LLM reasoning by using Monte Carlo Tree Search to guide structured exploration of graph-based knowledge. The system treats graph traversal as a search problem, where MCTS explores connections between entities (papers, authors, citations) and incorporates discovered knowledge into the LLM's reasoning loop. Information flows from the external graph through MCTS-directed retrieval, with results returned as text context to the LLM for token generation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Graph-MCTS consistently outperforms existing augmentation methods across multiple LLM architectures
claim c2 [qualitative]: Most existing RAG approaches focus solely on textual data and neglect connections between pieces of knowledge, which are important in domains such as scientific publishing where entities like papers, authors, and citations form rich graphs
claim c3 [qualitative]: Meaning in graph-structured domains emerges not only from individual texts but also from their relationships

SyLeR: A Framework for Explicit Syllogistic Legal Reasoning in Large Language Models (2025)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmented-generation, syllogistic-reasoning, legal-qa, sft-rl-pipeline, reward-shaping, explainability
SyLeR empowers LLMs to perform explicit syllogistic legal reasoning through a three-stage pipeline: (1) tree-structured hierarchical retrieval mechanism that combines relevant legal statutes and precedent cases into comprehensive major premises, (2) supervised fine-tuning warm-up to establish foundational understanding of syllogistic reasoning, and (3) reinforcement learning with structure-aware reward mechanism to refine generation of diverse logically sound reasoning paths. The system bridges external legal documents (text) into the model via retrieval-augmented context, then steers the model's generation toward explicit syllogistic form through reward-shaped RL.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Existing large language models can generate responses to legal questions but fail to perform explicit syllogistic reasoning, often producing implicit and unstructured answers that lack explainability and trustworthiness
claim c2 [qualitative]: SyLeR integrates a tree-structured hierarchical retrieval mechanism to effectively combine relevant legal statutes and precedent cases, forming comprehensive major premises
claim c3 [qualitative]: Supervised fine-tuning warm-up establishes a foundational understanding of syllogistic reasoning
claim c4 [qualitative]: Reinforcement learning with a structure-aware reward mechanism refines the model's ability to generate diverse logically sound and well-structured reasoning paths
claim c5 [qualitative]: SyLeR significantly improves response accuracy across in-domain and cross-domain user groups (legal laypersons and practitioners)
claim c6 [qualitative]: Results show SyLeR consistently delivers explicit, explainable, and trustworthy legal reasoning
claim c7 [qualitative]: Experiments span multiple dimensions: in-domain and cross-domain user groups (legal laypersons and practitioners), multiple languages (Chinese and French), and different LLM backbones (legal-specific and open-domain LLMs)

RAG-IoE: IoT context-aware information retrieval with Large Language Models in Industry 5.0 (2025)
seam: text; tags: rag, knowledge-graph, vector-database, hybrid-retrieval, iot, context-aware
RAG-IoE integrates a context-aware data model for Internet of Everything (IoE) networks with a hybrid retrieval-augmented generation pipeline. The system organizes IoE data within a semantic framework, combining structured search on a Knowledge Graph with vector database retrieval of embeddings from unstructured data. Retrieved results are refined through LLM-driven reasoning, producing context-based information guidance for industrial workers. The seam is text: external structured and unstructured data sources are retrieved and formatted into text prompts for the LLM.
claim c1 [qualitative]: RAG-IoE combines structured search on a Knowledge Graph with unstructured data retrieval using embeddings stored in a vector database, followed by LLM-driven reasoning to refine results
claim c2 [qualitative]: The architecture enhances decision-making, reduces cognitive overload, and ensures precise guidance for industrial operators
claim c3 [qualitative]: The approach organizes IoE data within a semantic framework, integrating hybrid retrieval methods to enable adaptive, scalable, and context-based information retrieval from both structured and unstructured data sources
claim c4 [qualitative]: RAG-IoE addresses the challenge that traditional information retrieval techniques struggle with the heterogeneous, dynamic data generated in industrial settings

A Survey on Retrieval-Augmented Text Generation for Large Language Models (2026)
seam: text; tags: rag, retrieval, external-knowledge, survey, generation
This is a survey paper organizing the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) paradigm into four sequential stages: pre-retrieval (query preparation), retrieval (information extraction from external sources), post-retrieval (result processing), and generation (LLM output production). RAG integrates information retrieval techniques with deep learning to inject up-to-date external information into LLMs at generation time, addressing the static knowledge and hallucination problems of base LLMs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: RAG merges information retrieval (IR) techniques with deep learning advancements to address the static limitations of large language models (LLMs) by enabling the dynamic integration of up-to-date external information
claim c2 [qualitative]: RAG provides a cost-effective solution to the generation of plausible but possibly incorrect responses by LLMs, thereby enhancing the accuracy and reliability of their outputs through the use of real-world data
claim c3 [qualitative]: The RAG paradigm is organized into four categories: pre-retrieval, retrieval, post-retrieval, and generation, offering a detailed perspective from the retrieval viewpoint

Variational Deep Logic Network for Joint Inference of Entities and Relations (2021)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: joint-entity-relation-extraction, variational-em, logic-constraints, self-attention, knowledge-integration
A variational deep logic network combining a self-attention-based deep neural network for implicit feature learning with a relational logic network for explicit reasoning about entity-relation correlations. The two components are trained interactively via variational EM, allowing information to flow from neural representations into logic constraints and vice versa, enabling joint entity and relation extraction with structured reasoning.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The model incorporates both representation learning and relational reasoning via the variational EM algorithm to jointly perform entity extraction and relation prediction while explicitly encoding correlations between entity types and relations
claim c2 [qualitative]: A deep neural network learns high-level features with implicit interactions via the self-attention mechanism, while a relational logic network explicitly exploits target interactions between entities and relations
claim c3 [qualitative]: The two components are trained interactively to bring the best of both worlds, addressing limitations of pure neural approaches that fail to encode intensive correlations and rule-based approaches that suffer from error propagation and inflexibility
claim c4 [qualitative]: The method is evaluated on fine-grained sentiment terms extraction, end-to-end relation prediction, and end-to-end event extraction tasks

Algebraic Cekirge Method for Deterministic and Energy-efficient Transformer Language Models (2025)
seam: none; tags: deterministic-algebraic-learning, closed-form-attention, sigma-regularization, matrix-inversion, energy-efficient-inference
A deterministic algebraic framework for transformer language models that constructs σ-regularized, nonsingular matrices for Q, K, V, and output weights, enabling attention and decoding to be computed in closed form without iterative optimization. Weight matrices are determined algebraically from paired input–output examples via direct matrix inversion, eliminating stochastic gradient descent and replacing iterative back propagation with finite algebraic equilibrium.
claim c1 [quantitative]: The deterministic algebraic solution reproduces the predictive behavior of gradient descent while reducing computation time by more than sixty-fold
claim c2 [qualitative]: σ-regularization guarantees matrix invertibility and numerical stability
claim c3 [qualitative]: Attention and decoding can be computed in closed form without iterative stochastic gradient descent
claim c4 [qualitative]: The framework unifies Four-Cluster Deterministic Map, Frozen-Library Forward Training, σ-Matrix Fast Learning, and Inverse Deterministic Energy-Saving Training (IDEST) into a closed, energy-saving learning process
claim c5 [qualitative]: Deterministic algebraic decoding achieves comparable accuracy to gradient descent while operating with markedly lower computational energy
claim c6 [qualitative]: The model's internal mappings preserve identity under small deterministic perturbations (ε) but lose interpretability under stochastic noise

From Codebooks to Promptbooks: Extracting Information from Text with Generative Large Language Models (2025)
seam: text; tags: information-extraction, prompt-engineering, in-context-learning, structured-extraction
The system uses open-source large language models with targeted prompts to extract structured biographical information (age, occupation, origin) from unstructured newspaper obituary text. Information flows from raw text through prompt-driven prompting strategies into structured extracted fields. The paper treats prompts as configurable "promptbooks" (an analogue to traditional codebooks in qualitative research) and evaluates how different model sizes and prompting techniques affect extraction accuracy and error patterns.
claim c1 [qualitative]: GenAI allows researchers to define their own IE tasks and execute them via targeted prompts, changing IE from requiring specialized models to using flexible, accessible prompt-driven approaches.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Sociologists can develop and evaluate prompt architectures for information extraction tasks by turning codebooks into 'promptbooks.'
claim c3 [qualitative]: Different model sizes and prompting techniques affect extraction performance.
claim c4 [negative]: Non-random error patterns in extraction can bias downstream analyses, presenting a risk when using GenAI for information extraction.

Imperative learning: A self-supervised neuro-symbolic learning framework for robot autonomy (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: neuro-symbolic, bilevel-optimization, self-supervised, reciprocal-learning, symbolic-reasoning, memory-retrieval
Imperative learning (IL) is a neuro-symbolic framework for robot autonomy comprising three reciprocally-learning modules: (1) a neural module that learns continuous representations, (2) a reasoning engine that applies symbolic logical, physical, and geometric reasoning to generate task constraints and objectives, and (3) a memory system that stores and retrieves learned symbolic rules and trajectories. Information flows bidirectionally: the neural module produces activations that the reasoning engine interprets symbolically; the reasoning engine generates symbolic outputs (rules, constraints, rewards) that guide neural learning; the memory system provides both as context. The framework formulates this coupling as bilevel optimization (BLO) enabling reciprocal updates.
claim c1 [qualitative]: IL significantly enhances robot autonomy capabilities across path planning, rule induction, optimal control, visual odometry, and multi-robot routing
claim c2 [qualitative]: IL overcomes the label-intensive obstacles associated with data-driven approaches by leveraging self-supervised learning and symbolic reasoning
claim c3 [qualitative]: The bilevel optimization formulation of IL enables reciprocal learning over the three modules (neural, reasoning, memory)
claim c4 [qualitative]: IL takes advantage of symbolic reasoning concerning logical reasoning, physical principles, and geometric analysis to improve generalization to ever-changing environments

SDIGLM: leveraging large language models and multimodal chain of thought for structural damage identification (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: multimodal-llm, semantic-segmentation, chain-of-thought, visual-reasoning, damage-identification
SDIGLM is a multimodal large language model built on VisualGLM-6B that integrates a U-Net-based semantic segmentation module to generate defect segmentation maps as visual chain of thought (CoT). The system processes images and text jointly, combining segmentation outputs with language-based CoT (via prompt engineering) and multiround dialogue fine-tuning to perform structural damage identification and generate natural language descriptions of damage characteristics.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SDIGLM achieves an accuracy of 95.24% across various infrastructure types
claim c2 [qualitative]: SDIGLM surpasses general-purpose MLLMs in structural damage identification
claim c3 [qualitative]: The model effectively describes damage characteristics such as hole size, crack direction, and corrosion severity
claim c4 [qualitative]: Integration of U-Net-based semantic segmentation module generates defect segmentation maps as visual chain of thought
claim c5 [qualitative]: Multiround dialogue fine-tuning dataset enhances logical reasoning capabilities for structural damage analysis

LLM4TDG: test-driven generation of large language models based on enhanced constraint reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: constraint-reasoning, test-generation, prompt-enhancement, backtracking, code-generation
LLM4TDG is a test-driven generation framework that enhances LLMs' constraint reasoning by formally defining a constraint dependency graph and converting it into context constraints. The system feeds these constraint-enhanced prompts to LLMs (general-domain and code-generation-specific), which generate test drivers. A constraint reasoning and backtracking mechanism post-processes or guides generation to ensure generated tests satisfy the defined constraints.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Our approach significantly enhances LLMs' ability to comprehend constraints in testing objectives, achieving a 47.62% increase in constraint understanding across 147 testing tasks.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Employing LLM4TDG significantly improves the average pass@k metric of all LLMs by 10.41%.
claim c3 [quantitative]: The pass@k metric for CodeQwen-chat has improved by up to 18.66%.
claim c4 [quantitative]: The metric surpasses the state-of-the-art GPT-4, with a performance of 92.16% on HUMANEVAL and 87.14% on HUMANEVAL+.
claim c5 [quantitative]: The metric surpasses the state-of-the-art GPT-4, with a performance of 92.16% on HUMANEVAL and 87.14% on HUMANEVAL+.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Experiments were conducted on a dataset of Python third-party libraries containing malicious behavior in the context of security testing tasks, validating the effectiveness of our method in real-world applications and its generalization capabilities.

A zero-shot self-improving NER method for cyber threat intelligence via knowledge injection (2025)
seam: text; tags: zero-shot-ner, knowledge-injection, in-context-learning, hallucination-mitigation, consistency-filtering, self-retrieval
A zero-shot NER pipeline for cyber threat intelligence that injects domain knowledge into an LLM via a four-module framework: (1) domain knowledge extractor enriches threat intelligence vocabulary and definitions; (2) reliable data annotator generates entity annotations in-context; (3) high-consistency annotation filter removes low-confidence predictions to mitigate hallucinations; (4) self-retrieval reasoner performs multi-stage reasoning over filtered annotations. The system operates without task-specific training, improving entity recognition through iterative in-context learning cycles that progressively refine consistency and accuracy.
claim c1 [quantitative]: F1 score of 67.7% on LLM-TIKG dataset
claim c2 [quantitative]: F1 score of 61.41% on APT-NER dataset
claim c3 [quantitative]: F1 score of 74.56% on LADDER dataset
claim c4 [quantitative]: F1 score of 65.83% on CDTier dataset
claim c5 [qualitative]: Improvement of 7.66% over the average F1 score of other baseline methods
claim c6 [qualitative]: The domain knowledge extractor enhances LLM comprehension of specialized threat intelligence
claim c7 [qualitative]: The high-consistency annotation filter mitigates hallucinations by generating, filtering, and reasoning upon high-consistency data
claim c8 [qualitative]: The method operates under strict zero-shot conditions without task-specific training data

Exploring Advanced Large Language Models with LLMsuite (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: rag, pal, react, langchain, lora, rlhf, rest, multi-step-reasoning
Tutorial survey covering LLM development pipelines: retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and program-aided language models (PAL) augment base LLMs with external knowledge and symbolic reasoning; ReAct and LangChain frameworks coordinate multi-step reasoning loops. Fine-tuning methods (instruction tuning, LoRA, RLHF, ReST) adapt frozen or partially frozen LLM weights. No single unified system described; the paper catalogs techniques and their integration patterns.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Large Language Models such as ChatGPT and Gemini have inherent limitations like temporal knowledge cutoffs, mathematical inaccuracies, and the generation of incorrect information
claim c2 [qualitative]: Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) proposes a solution to LLM limitations
claim c3 [qualitative]: Program-Aided Language Models (PAL) proposes a solution to LLM limitations
claim c4 [qualitative]: ReAct and LangChain frameworks enhance LLM performance and reliability, especially in multi-step reasoning and complex task execution
claim c5 [qualitative]: Instruction fine-tuning improves LLM performance
claim c6 [qualitative]: Parameter-efficient methods like LoRA enable fine-tuning of LLMs
claim c7 [qualitative]: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) improves LLM alignment and performance
claim c8 [qualitative]: Reinforced Self-Training (ReST) is a fine-tuning strategy for LLMs

Real-Time Anomaly Detection and Reactive Planning with Large Language Models (2024)
seam: text; tags: anomaly-detection, embedding-classifier, fallback-selection, model-predictive-control, runtime-monitor, safety-verification
A two-stage reasoning framework for robotic anomaly detection and reactive planning. Stage 1: a fast binary anomaly classifier operating in LLM embedding space, which may trigger Stage 2: a slower generative LLM fallback selection stage. Both stages feed into a model predictive control (MPC) strategy that maintains feasibility of multiple fallback plans to account for the slow reasoner's latency, ensuring safety during anomaly response.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Fast anomaly classifier outperforms autoregressive reasoning with state-of-the-art GPT models, even when instantiated with relatively small language models
claim c2 [qualitative]: Two-stage framework with MPC strategy maintains joint feasibility of continuing along various fallback plans to account for slow reasoner's latency as soon as an anomaly is detected, thus ensuring safety
claim c3 [qualitative]: Runtime monitor improves the trustworthiness of dynamic robotic systems, such as quadrotors or autonomous vehicles, under resource and time constraints
claim c4 [qualitative]: Fast binary anomaly classifier mitigates computational expense of LLMs such that they may be applied online to robotic systems

Neural-Symbolic Integration: A Compositional Perspective (2021)
seam: external-engine; tags: neural-symbolic-integration, compositional-architecture, deduction-abduction, modular-reasoning, bidirectional-inference
A compositional framework treating neural and symbolic systems as interchangeable black-box modules integrated into a single architecture. Each module exposes methods for forward reasoning (deduction), inverse reasoning (abduction for symbolic; induction for neural), enabling bidirectional information flow between subsystems without assumptions on their internal structure. The framework enables efficient training of the neural component by leveraging symbolic constraints and reasoning capabilities.
claim c1 [qualitative]: A symbolic module with any choice for syntax and semantics, as long as deduction and abduction methods are exposed, can be cleanly integrated with a neural module and facilitate the latter's efficient training
claim c2 [qualitative]: Empirical performance exceeds that of previous work
claim c3 [qualitative]: The framework treats neural and symbolic systems as black boxes to be integrated as modules into a single architecture, without making assumptions on their internal structure and semantics

Weakly Supervised Neuro-Symbolic Module Networks for Numerical Reasoning over Text (2022)
seam: external-engine; tags: neuro-symbolic, module-networks, reinforcement-learning, discrete-reasoning, numerical-qa, weak-supervision
Weakly Supervised Neuro-Symbolic Module Network (WNSMN) for numerical reasoning over text. The system executes a noisy heuristic program derived from dependency parsing of the query as discrete actions over both neural and symbolic reasoning modules. Information flows from question text through dependency parsing into a sequence of discrete module operations (arithmetic, comparison, filtering over passage spans and numerical values), trained end-to-end via reinforcement learning with answer-matching reward signal, producing a final numerical answer.
claim c1 [quantitative]: WNSMN outperforms NMN by 32% in exact match accuracy on the subset of DROP having numerical answers under comparable weakly supervised settings
claim c2 [quantitative]: WNSMN outperforms the reasoning-free generative language model GenBERT by 8% in exact match accuracy under comparable weakly supervised settings
claim c3 [qualitative]: WNSMN learns to execute a noisy heuristic program obtained from the dependency parse of the query, as discrete actions over both neural and symbolic reasoning modules
claim c4 [qualitative]: WNSMN is trained with answers as the sole supervision for numerical reasoning based MRC
claim c5 [qualitative]: Contemporary Neural Module Networks models fail to generalize to more open-ended settings without strong supervision in form of specialized program annotation from the QA pairs through various heuristic parsing and exhaustive computation of all possible discrete operations on discrete arguments

DeepStochLog: Neural Stochastic Logic Programming (2022)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: neural-symbolic, logic-programming, stochastic-inference, grammar-rules, end-to-end-training
DeepStochLog extends stochastic definite clause grammars (a kind of stochastic logic program) with neural predicates (grammar rules). The system performs end-to-end training by integrating learned neural components into a symbolic inference engine. Information flows from input through neural rule evaluations embedded in the logic program's clause derivations, enabling joint neural-symbolic inference and learning.
claim c1 [qualitative]: inference and learning in neural stochastic logic programming scale much better than for neural probabilistic logic programs
claim c2 [qualitative]: DeepStochLog achieves state-of-the-art results on challenging neural-symbolic learning tasks

On Grounded Planning for Embodied Tasks with Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: planning, embodied-tasks, table-conditioning, iterative-decoding, grounding
G-PlanET is a planning system where a language model ingests a high-level goal and a structured data table describing objects in a specific environment, then outputs a step-by-step actionable plan for a robotic agent. The system uses table-based environment encoding and iterative decoding to enable grounded plan generation without direct visual perception or physical feedback.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The use of tables for encoding the environment and an iterative decoding strategy can significantly enhance the LMs' ability in grounded planning
claim c2 [qualitative]: Language models possess commonsense knowledge of the physical world but lack the ability to perceive the environment through vision and feedback from the physical environment
claim c3 [qualitative]: This is the first investigation into whether language models have the capacity to generate grounded, executable plans for embodied tasks

Hypothesis, Verification, and Induction: Grounding Large Language Models with Self-Driven Skill Learning (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: hypothesis-verification-induction, skill-learning, environment-grounding, self-driven-learning, interactive-verification
HYVIN is a framework that grounds LLMs to environments through iterative hypothesis-verification-induction cycles. The LLM proposes hypotheses of sub-goals; these are verified by interaction with the environment (BabyAI simulator); successful verifications yield generalized skills that are learned and reused for more complex downstream tasks. Information flows from LLM reasoning → environment interaction → skill extraction → skill injection back into LLM planning.
claim c1 [qualitative]: HYVIN achieves comparable performance in the most challenging tasks compared with imitation learning methods that cost millions of demonstrations
claim c2 [qualitative]: HYVIN autonomously grounds the LLM onto the environment without requiring fine-tuning or pre-defined behavior APIs for every single task
claim c3 [qualitative]: Learned skills can be utilized to accomplish more complex tasks that fail to pass the verification phase

Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners (2024)
seam: text; tags: agent-loop, symbolic-reasoning, text-game, action-selection
An LLM agent initialized with a role receives observations and valid actions from text-based game environments, along with a symbolic module. The agent processes these inputs to select and execute actions within the game, forming a perception-decision-action loop where the LLM reasons over symbolic task constraints (math, map reading, sorting, common sense) and returns structured action choices to the environment.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LLM agent achieves an average performance of 88% across all tasks
claim c2 [qualitative]: Our method significantly enhances the capability of LLMs as automated agents for symbolic reasoning
claim c3 [qualitative]: LLM agent is effective in text-based games involving symbolic tasks

MindMap: Constructing Evidence Chains for Multi-Step Reasoning in Large Language Models (2024)
seam: text; tags: evidence-chain-organization, multi-step-reasoning, fact-retrieval, prompt-integration
MindMap organizes facts into evidence chains (sets of facts sharing the same subject) and integrates this structure into existing LLM reasoning frameworks. The model selects relevant evidence chains rather than independent facts during reasoning, with the chains fed as text context into CoT or Selection-Inference prompting pipelines.
claim c1 [qualitative]: MindMap can be integrated with existing reasoning frameworks, such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Selection-Inference (SI), by letting the model select relevant evidence chains instead of independent facts.
claim c2 [qualitative]: MindMap can significantly improve CoT and SI, especially in multi-step reasoning tasks.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Evidence chains organize related facts together to avoid missing important information in multi-step reasoning.

Neural Reasoning for Sure Through Constructing Explainable Models (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: sphere-embedding, set-theoretic-reasoning, syllogistic-logic, hyperbolic-geometry, semantic-model-construction, verifier-loop
Hyperbolic Sphere Neural Network (HSphNN) extends vector embeddings into sphere embeddings to explicitly encode set-theoretic relations through sphere boundaries in hyperbolic vector space. Part-whole relations are represented as sphere configurations, enabling symbolic reasoning over Aristotelian syllogisms. The network constructs a target sphere configuration through neighbourhood transitions, achieving reasoning outputs with explicit set-theoretic semantics alongside traditional neural computation. HSphNN can be integrated with external LLMs (ChatGPT variants) via prompting to verify and improve their reasoning.
claim c1 [theoretical]: HSphNN constructs a sphere configuration as its semantic model for any consistent set-theoretic relation within a constant number M of epochs, with M=1 for HSphNN
claim c2 [qualitative]: HSphNN is the first reasoning-for-sure neural network for all types of Aristotelian syllogistic reasoning
claim c3 [quantitative]: HSphNN successfully checks both decisions and explanations of ChatGPT (gpt-3.5-turbo and gpt-4o) without errors
claim c4 [quantitative]: HSphNN improves the performance of gpt-3.5-turbo from 46.875% to 58.98% through prompts
claim c5 [quantitative]: HSphNN improves the performance of gpt-4o from 82.42% to 84.76% through prompts
claim c6 [qualitative]: HSphNN achieves the symbolic level rigour of syllogistic reasoning
claim c7 [qualitative]: Set-theoretic relations are introduced explicitly and seamlessly into neural networks by extending vector embedding into sphere embedding, so that part-whole relations can explicitly encode set-theoretic relations through sphere boundaries in the vector space

MTL-LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation for Multi-Task Learning (2025)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: peft, low-rank-adaptation, multi-task-learning, task-specific-modules, parameter-efficient
MTL-LoRA augments standard LoRA by adding task-adaptive parameters that operate in the low-dimensional intrinsic space. During fine-tuning, the base pretrained model's weights are frozen; task-specific and shared LoRA modules project input features through low-rank bottlenecks (A, B matrices), and task-adaptive parameters learned in this projected space differentiate task-specific information while capturing shared knowledge across tasks. The mechanism allows a single pretrained backbone to jointly adapt to multiple target domains with limited trainable parameters.
claim c1 [qualitative]: MTL-LoRA augments LoRA by incorporating additional task-adaptive parameters that differentiate task-specific information and capture shared knowledge across various tasks within low-dimensional spaces
claim c2 [qualitative]: LoRA tends to obscure the distinction between tasks by projecting sparse high-dimensional features from different tasks into the same dense low-dimensional intrinsic space, leading to task interference and suboptimal performance
claim c3 [qualitative]: MTL-LoRA outperforms LoRA and its various variants with comparable or even fewer learnable parameters in MTL setting

Attributive Reasoning for Hallucination Diagnosis of Large Language Models (2025)
seam: own-activations; tags: hallucination-mitigation, post-hoc-decoding, internal-signal-analysis, attribution-framework, differential-penalty
The paper introduces an attribution framework to trace hallucination origins using internal signals of LLMs, combined with a Differential Penalty Decoding (DPD) strategy that adjusts post-probabilities during decoding to reduce hallucinations. The system analyzes internal LLM states to diagnose hallucination categories and applies penalty adjustments to output tokens based on reliability signals.
claim c1 [qualitative]: We introduce an attribution framework to trace the origins of hallucinations based on the internal signals of LLMs
claim c2 [qualitative]: We develop a new benchmark named RelQA-Cate, which includes eight categories of hallucinations for the answers generated by LLMs
claim c3 [qualitative]: We present a novel Differential Penalty Decoding (DPD) strategy for reducing hallucinations through adjusting post-probabilities of each answer
claim c4 [quantitative]: The performance on answer reliability has significant improvement, achieving 28.25% at most

Audio Entailment: Assessing Deductive Reasoning for Audio Understanding (2025)
seam: text; tags: audio-language-model, entailment-reasoning, caption-before-reason, logical-reasoning, benchmark
The paper evaluates Audio-Language Models (ALMs) on a novel audio entailment task. ALMs are foundation models trained on audio-text pairs. The paper benchmarks existing ALMs in zero-shot and linear-probe settings, and proposes a two-stage pipeline ('caption-before-reason') where an ALM first generates a caption from audio, then uses that caption to reason about a hypothesis and classify the entailment relationship (entailment, neutral, or contradiction). Information flows from audio through the ALM's audio encoder and text decoder/reasoning components.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Audio-Language Models show deficiencies in logical reasoning on the audio entailment task
claim c2 [quantitative]: caption-before-reason improves Zero-Shot performance by an absolute 6%
claim c3 [quantitative]: caption-before-reason improves linear-probe performance by an absolute 3%
claim c4 [qualitative]: Audio entailment task assesses whether a text hypothesis of audio content can be deduced from an audio recording, with three possible conclusions: entailment, neutral, or contradiction

Debate on Graph: A Flexible and Reliable Reasoning Framework for Large Language Models (2025)
seam: text; tags: retrieval, knowledge-graph, iterative-refinement, multi-agent-debate, subgraph-focusing, kgqa
Debate on Graph (DoG) is an iterative interactive framework that integrates LLMs with knowledge graphs for KGQA. Information flows as follows: the system employs a multi-role debate team (debater roles) that interactively reason over a knowledge graph, using a subgraph-focusing mechanism to narrow the search space after each reasoning step. At each iteration, the LLM attempts to generate an answer based on the current subgraph, then refines its reasoning by debating candidate relations and paths, gradually simplifying complex questions while filtering false-positive relations. The framework alternates between graph traversal, answer generation, and debate-driven path refinement until convergence.
claim c1 [quantitative]: DoG outperforms the state-of-the-art method ToG by 23.7% in accuracy on WebQuestions
claim c2 [quantitative]: DoG outperforms the state-of-the-art method ToG by 9.1% in accuracy on GrailQA
claim c3 [qualitative]: DoG employs a subgraph-focusing mechanism, allowing LLMs to perform answer trying after each reasoning step, thereby mitigating the impact of lengthy reasoning paths
claim c4 [qualitative]: DoG utilizes a multi-role debate team to gradually simplify complex questions, reducing the influence of false-positive relations
claim c5 [qualitative]: Experimental results on five public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our architecture
claim c6 [qualitative]: Integration experiments with various LLMs on the mentioned datasets highlight the flexibility of DoG

What Are Step-Level Reward Models Rewarding? Counterintuitive Findings from MCTS-Boosted Mathematical Reasoning (2025)
seam: text; tags: step-level-reward, mcts-annotation, process-supervision, mathematical-reasoning, preference-learning
The paper investigates step-level reward models (SRMs) trained via MCTS-based preference annotation for mathematical reasoning. The system uses MCTS to generate step-level preference pairs, trains a reward model on these pairs to score intermediate reasoning steps, and uses the trained model to guide or supervise the reasoning process. The seam cell is text-based: preference labels and step descriptions cross the model boundary as training targets, and the trained reward model scores steps represented as text sequences.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Removal of natural language descriptions of thought processes has minimal impact on the efficacy of SRMs
claim c2 [qualitative]: SRMs are adept at assessing the complex logical coherence present in mathematical language while having difficulty in natural language
claim c3 [qualitative]: Step-level reward models can significantly enhance mathematical reasoning performance through process supervision or step-level preference alignment based on reinforcement learning
claim c4 [qualitative]: AlphaZero-like methods, where Monte Carlo Tree Search is employed for automatic step-level preference annotation, have proven particularly effective

DeepSym: Deep Symbol Generation and Rule Learning for Planning from Unsupervised Robot Interaction (2022)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: symbol-grounding, sensorimotor-learning, decision-tree-distillation, probabilistic-planning, ppddl-rule-extraction
DeepSym trains a predictive deep encoder-decoder network on robot interaction data: the encoder compresses images and actions into a binary bottleneck layer representing action-grounded object categories; the decoder reconstructs scene effects in pixel coordinates. After training, a decision tree distills the network's decoder function, and decision paths are converted into probabilistic rules in PPDDL format for symbolic planning. The system bridges sensorimotor experience (raw images, actions, effects) to discrete symbolic representations and executable plans.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The binary latent vector represents action-driven object categories based on the interaction experience of the robot
claim c2 [qualitative]: The system discovers discrete representations of object properties such as 'rollable' and 'insertable'
claim c3 [qualitative]: Learned symbols allow for the generation of effective plans for achieving goals, such as building towers of the desired height
claim c4 [qualitative]: The system is applicable to the MNIST 8-puzzle domain in which learned symbols allow for the generation of plans that move the empty tile into any given position
claim c5 [qualitative]: Probabilistic rules are extracted from decision tree paths and represented in Probabilistic Planning Domain Definition Language (PPDDL), allowing off-the-shelf planners to operate on the knowledge extracted from sensorimotor experience

Scalable Neural-Probabilistic Answer Set Programming (2023)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: neuro-symbolic, answer-set-programming, probabilistic-predicates, neural-wrapper, program-pruning, joint-probability
SLASH integrates neural networks with answer set programming (ASP) via Neural-Probabilistic Predicates (NPPs). NPPs wrap trained deep models (or combinations thereof) as probabilistic predicates in a logic program. The system grounds the ASP program, prunes stochastically insignificant atoms to reduce the ground program size, and performs tractable probabilistic inference over the remaining ground rules. Information flows from neural model outputs (which estimate probabilities for ground atoms) into logical inference, unified through ASP semantics.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SLASH allows for integration of tractable probabilistic inference within a Deep Probabilistic Programming Language
claim c2 [qualitative]: NPPs are a novel design principle allowing for combining all deep model types and combinations thereof to be represented as a single probabilistic predicate
claim c3 [qualitative]: A novel +/− notation is introduced for answering various types of probabilistic queries by adjusting the atom notations of a predicate
claim c4 [qualitative]: Pruning of stochastically insignificant parts of the ground program speeds up reasoning without sacrificing predictive performance
claim c5 [qualitative]: SLASH offers the power of true joint probability estimation, improving upon prior Deep Probabilistic Programming Language approaches that allow only for limited conditional probabilistic queries

Cross-Modal Retrieval Augmentation for Multi-Modal Classification (2021)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmentation, cross-modal-alignment, vqa, multi-modal-transformer, knowledge-retrieval
The system trains a cross-modal alignment model to embed images and captions in a shared space, then uses this model as a retrieval component to augment a multi-modal transformer for visual question answering. At inference, the system retrieves relevant image-caption pairs from an external knowledge source and incorporates them into the VQA model's context, with support for hot-swapping retrieval indices.
claim c1 [qualitative]: the trained alignment model achieves substantial improvements in performance on image-caption retrieval w.r.t. similar methods
claim c2 [qualitative]: retrieval-augmented multi-modal transformers using the trained alignment model improve results on VQA over strong baselines
claim c3 [qualitative]: the approach supports novel applications for inference time such as hot-swapping indices

Adaptable and Interpretable Neural MemoryOver Symbolic Knowledge (2021)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: knowledge-base-injection, entity-relation-embeddings, symbolic-neural-bridge, interpretable-retrieval, knowledge-grounded-generation
A neural-symbolic system that stores and retrieves knowledge from a symbolic knowledge base (KG) by learning a mapping between entity/relation embeddings and neural activations. The system uses a trainable bridge (learned KV projections or soft prompts) to inject retrieved symbolic knowledge into a language model's hidden states, enabling the model to condition generation on external symbolic facts without modifying the base model weights.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The model learns an interpretable and adaptable neural memory over symbolic knowledge, enabling retrieval and integration of facts from a knowledge base into neural generation
claim c2 [qualitative]: The approach allows adaptation to new knowledge without retraining the base language model, by learning new entity and relation embeddings and updating the retrieval mechanism

Enriching Transformers with Structured Tensor-Product Representations for Abstractive Summarization (2021)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: tensor-product-representation, role-filler-binding, compositional-structure, structured-injection, abstractive-summarization
The system augments a Transformer encoder-decoder (BART) with structured tensor-product representations (TPR). During encoding, the model learns to map input tokens into a compositional role-filler binding space where roles encode syntactic/semantic positions and fillers encode lexical content. This bound representation is injected into the decoder via a trained projection layer, enriching the Transformer's latent reasoning with explicit compositional structure before autoregressive decoding.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The model achieves improvements over BART baseline on CNN/DailyMail abstractive summarization
claim c2 [qualitative]: Tensor-product representations enable the model to learn explicit role-filler bindings that capture syntactic and semantic structure
claim c3 [qualitative]: The structured representations improve interpretability by making compositional reasoning visible through role and filler assignments
claim c4 [qualitative]: The injected TPR structure helps the decoder generate more abstractive (rather than extractive) summaries

Automatic Generation of Socratic Subquestions for Teaching Math Word Problems (2022)
seam: text; tags: question-generation, socratic-method, reinforcement-learning, input-conditioning, math-word-problems
The system uses large language models to generate sequential Socratic subquestions that guide math word problem solving. Questions are generated via input conditioning and reinforcement learning schemes, then used either to prompt the LM solver directly (text-in-context) or to guide human solvers. The LM reads the problem statement and prior questions/answers, generates the next question constrained by learned properties, and the solver (human or model) processes it to advance toward the final answer.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LMs constrained with desirable question properties generate superior questions and improve the overall performance of a math word problem solver
claim c2 [qualitative]: Socratic questioning strategy can enhance both human performance and assist math word problem solvers
claim c3 [qualitative]: The difficulty level of problems plays an important role in determining whether questioning improves or hinders human performance
claim c4 [qualitative]: Large language models show ability in generating sequential questions for guiding math word problem-solving
claim c5 [qualitative]: Both automatic and human quality evaluations confirm that constrained question generation outperforms unconstrained approaches

ATTEMPT: Parameter-Efficient Multi-task Tuning via Attentional Mixtures of Soft Prompts (2022)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: soft-prompt-mixing, attention-interpolation, parameter-efficient-tuning, multi-task-transfer, prefix-tuning
ATTEMPT learns an attention module that dynamically interpolates fixed source task soft prompts and a task-specific target prompt. During inference and training on a target task, the attention mechanism computes per-instance weights over source prompts and the target prompt; these weighted prompts are concatenated and prepended to the input tokens. Only the target prompt and attention weights are trainable; the base LM and source prompts remain frozen. Source prompts are pre-computed soft prompt vectors derived from large-scale source tasks.
claim c1 [qualitative]: ATTEMPT updates 2,300 times fewer parameters than full fine-tuning
claim c2 [qualitative]: ATTEMPT significantly outperforms prompt tuning and outperforms or matches fully fine-tuned or other parameter-efficient tuning approaches that use 10 times more parameters
claim c3 [qualitative]: ATTEMPT overcomes instability of prompt tuning and achieves high task performance using learned knowledge from high-resource tasks
claim c4 [qualitative]: ATTEMPT outperforms previous work in few-shot learning settings
claim c5 [qualitative]: ATTEMPT is modular using pre-trained soft prompts, and can flexibly add or remove source prompts for effective knowledge transfer

TIARA: Multi-grained Retrieval for Robust Question Answering over Large Knowledge Base (2022)
seam: text; tags: retrieval, constrained-decoding, kbqa, multi-grained
TIARA is a knowledge-base question-answering (KBQA) system built on pre-trained language models. It performs multi-grained retrieval to surface relevant KB context at three levels—entities, exemplary logical forms, and schema items—which are injected into the PLM's context. The PLM then generates executable logical forms, with constrained decoding applied to enforce syntactic and semantic correctness and reduce generation errors.
claim c1 [quantitative]: TIARA outperforms previous SOTA on GrailQA by at least 4.1 F1 points
claim c2 [quantitative]: TIARA outperforms previous SOTA on WebQuestionsSP by at least 1.1 F1 points
claim c3 [quantitative]: On GrailQA, TIARA outperforms previous models in all categories, with an improvement of 4.7 F1 points in zero-shot generalization
claim c4 [qualitative]: Multi-grained retrieval helps the PLM focus on the most relevant KB context: entities, exemplary logical forms, and schema items
claim c5 [qualitative]: Constrained decoding is used to control the output space and reduce generation errors

Towards Large-Scale Interpretable Knowledge Graph Reasoning for Dialogue Systems (2022)
seam: external-engine; tags: knowledge-graph-reasoning, differentiable-traversal, dialogue-generation, interpretable-paths
A transformer model that directly traverses a large-scale knowledge graph to generate dialogue responses. The model performs differentiable graph walks, where at each step it selects which edge to follow in the KG, and uses the traversed reasoning path to generate the response. The KG serves as an external structured knowledge source that the model learns to navigate end-to-end.
claim c1 [qualitative]: This is the first work to have transformer models generate responses by reasoning over differentiable knowledge graphs.
claim c2 [qualitative]: The method can effectively and efficiently incorporate a knowledge graph into a dialogue system with fully-interpretable reasoning paths.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The proposed method allows a single transformer model to directly walk on a large-scale knowledge graph to generate responses.
claim c4 [qualitative]: The method investigates reasoning abilities on both task-oriented and domain-specific chitchat dialogues.

Augmenting Pre-trained Language Models with QA-Memory for Open-Domain Question Answering (2023)
seam: text; tags: retrieval-augmented-generation, qa-memory, question-answer-pairs, multi-hop-reasoning, pre-training-task
A text-to-text pre-trained language model augmented with a large memory of question-answer pairs. The system retrieves relevant QA pairs from memory, and a pre-trained retrieval component learns to select which pairs are relevant to an input question. Retrieved pairs are concatenated as context and passed to the text-to-text model to generate the final answer. The system supports multi-hop reasoning by iteratively retrieving and reasoning over multiple QA pairs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: A new pre-training task for the latent step of question retrieval substantially simplifies training and greatly improves performance on smaller QA benchmarks
claim c2 [qualitative]: Question-answer pairs provide practical advantages including being more memory and compute-efficient compared to existing open-book ODQA methods that retrieve from large text corpora or knowledge bases
claim c3 [qualitative]: The QA system can answer multi-hop questions that do not explicitly appear in the collection of stored question-answer pairs

Theory of Mind for Multi-Agent Collaboration via Large Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: multi-agent-collaboration, theory-of-mind, text-game, belief-state-representation, emergent-behavior
LLM-based agents operate in a multi-agent cooperative text game with Theory of Mind inference requirements. Agents receive text observations of game state and must coordinate with other agents to complete tasks. The system compares three agent types: LLM-based (which read task state as text and generate actions), MARL baselines (reinforcement learning), and planning-based baselines. A key intervention adds explicit belief state representations (text descriptions of agent beliefs about other agents' knowledge) that are injected into the LLM context to improve planning and ToM reasoning.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LLM-based agents demonstrate evidence of emergent collaborative behaviors and high-order Theory of Mind capabilities
claim c2 [qualitative]: LLM-based agents show systematic failures in managing long-horizon contexts and hallucination about task state
claim c3 [qualitative]: Explicit belief state representations enhance task performance and accuracy of ToM inferences for LLM-based agents
claim c4 [qualitative]: LLM-based agents show limitations in planning optimization due to long-horizon context management failures

Knowledge Rumination for Pre-trained Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: prompt-engineering, knowledge-consolidation, latent-knowledge-activation, in-context
Knowledge Rumination augments pre-trained language models by prepending a prompt ("As far as I know") to activate and consolidate latent knowledge already encoded in the model's parameters. The system operates in-context without external retrieval: the prompt triggers the model to surface and reuse internal knowledge representations, which are then incorporated into the final task prediction.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Pre-trained language models may have already encoded rich knowledge in their pre-trained parameters but fail to fully utilize them when applying to knowledge-intensive tasks
claim c2 [qualitative]: By simply adding a prompt like 'As far as I know' to PLMs, the model can review related latent knowledge and inject them back into the model for knowledge consolidation
claim c3 [qualitative]: Knowledge Rumination is effective across various language models, including RoBERTa, DeBERTa, and GPT-3
claim c4 [qualitative]: The approach demonstrates effectiveness on six commonsense reasoning tasks and GLUE benchmarks

Small Language Models Fine-tuned to Coordinate Larger Language Models improve Complex Reasoning (2023)
seam: text; tags: modular-reasoning, problem-decomposition, policy-gradient-finetuning, solver-agnostic, cot-guidance
DaSLaM is a modular reasoning system comprising two components: a small (13B) decomposition generator fine-tuned via policy gradient optimization to decompose complex problems into simpler subproblems, and a solver LM (treated as a black box) that answers the decomposed subproblems. The decomposer learns to interact with and guide the solver through a sequence of subproblems, with information flowing from problem → decomposer → subproblems → solver → answers.
claim c1 [qualitative]: text-davinci-003 (175B) with DaSLaM decomposition produces competitive or better performance compared to GPT-4
claim c2 [qualitative]: DaSLaM decomposition provides significant performance improvement across solver LMs of diverse sizes
claim c3 [qualitative]: Problem decomposition and solution generation are distinct capabilities better addressed in separate modules than by one monolithic LLM
claim c4 [qualitative]: DaSLaM's modular fine-tuning technique is superior to decomposer LLMs based on prompting alone
claim c5 [qualitative]: A 13B decomposition generator trained via policy gradient can render the decomposition method solver-agnostic
claim c6 [theoretical]: Small, fine-tuned decomposition generators can guide larger solver LMs through multi-step reasoning by learning to decompose problems into subproblems requiring fewer reasoning steps

Query Rewriting in Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: query-rewriting, retrieval-augmented-generation, reinforcement-learning, retrieve-then-read
A three-stage retrieval-augmented LLM pipeline: (1) a trainable small language model rewrites the input query to better match needed knowledge, (2) a web search engine retrieves contexts using the rewritten query, (3) a frozen black-box LLM reader generates answers from the retrieved contexts. The rewriter is trained via reinforcement learning using feedback signals from the frozen reader.
claim c1 [qualitative]: A rewrite-retrieve-read framework is proposed that pays attention to adaptation of the search query itself, addressing the gap between input text and needed knowledge in retrieval
claim c2 [qualitative]: A trainable scheme is proposed where a small language model acts as a rewriter trained using feedback from the LLM reader by reinforcement learning to better align the query to the frozen reader
claim c3 [qualitative]: Experiments show consistent performance improvement on downstream tasks including open-domain QA and multiple-choice QA
claim c4 [qualitative]: The framework is proven effective and scalable, bringing a new framework for retrieval-augmented LLM

MAF: Multi-Aspect Feedback for Improving Reasoning in Large Language Models (2023)
seam: text; tags: feedback-loop, self-improvement, multi-module, iterative-refinement, external-tools
Multi-Aspect Feedback (MAF) is an iterative refinement framework that routes LM-generated reasoning chains through multiple specialized feedback modules—each a frozen LM or external tool designed to detect and correct a specific error category (hallucination, intermediate reasoning errors, mathematical errors). Corrected reasoning is re-fed to the main LM for refinement across multiple iterations. Information flow: LM output → specialized feedback engines (text-based corrections) → revised input → LM iteration.
claim c1 [quantitative]: improvement of up to 20% in Mathematical Reasoning
claim c2 [quantitative]: improvement of up to 18% in Logical Entailment
claim c3 [qualitative]: existing approaches relying on a single generic feedback source fail to address the diverse error types found in LM-generated reasoning chains
claim c4 [qualitative]: LMs still face challenges such as hallucination, generating incorrect intermediate reasoning steps, and making mathematical errors
claim c5 [qualitative]: Multi-Aspect Feedback integrates multiple feedback modules, including frozen LMs and external tools, each focusing on a specific error category

Reasoning with Language Model is Planning with World Model (2023)
seam: text; tags: monte-carlo-tree-search, planning, world-model, reasoning-tree, exploration-exploitation
RAP (Reasoning via Planning) repurposes an LLM to serve dual roles: a reasoning agent that incrementally builds a reasoning tree, and a world model that predicts future states and rewards. A Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) algorithm orchestrates exploration of the reasoning space, using the LLM's state-prediction and reward-scoring capabilities to balance exploration versus exploitation and guide the search toward high-reward reasoning paths. The LLM generates reasoning steps, the world model scores trajectories and predicts outcomes, and MCTS selects which reasoning branches to expand next.
claim c1 [quantitative]: RAP on LLaMA-33B surpasses CoT on GPT-4 with 33% relative improvement in plan generation
claim c2 [qualitative]: RAP demonstrates superiority over strong baselines including CoT and least-to-most prompting with self-consistency on a variety of challenging reasoning problems such as plan generation, math reasoning, and logical inference
claim c3 [theoretical]: LLMs lack an internal world model for predicting world states and simulating long-term action outcomes, which prevents them from performing deliberate planning akin to human brains involving exploring alternative reasoning paths, anticipating future states and rewards, and iteratively refining reasoning steps
claim c4 [qualitative]: RAP incorporates a principled planning algorithm based on Monte Carlo Tree Search for strategic exploration in the vast reasoning space, balancing exploration versus exploitation to achieve high-reward reasoning paths efficiently

Generating Deep Questions with Commonsense Reasoning Ability from the Text by Disentangled Adversarial Inference (2023)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: question-generation, disentangled-factors, commonsense-reasoning, adversarial-inference, context-retrieval, complexity-scoring
A question generation system that retrieves commonsense context relevant to input text, then disentangles question generation into two latent factors: reasoning content and verbalized expression. The model uses a discriminator to score answering complexity and applies adversarial inference to learn the disentangled factors from data. At generation time, the expressive factor is sampled from learned distributions to produce diverse questions whose answers require reasoning over disjoint contexts and external commonsense knowledge.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Existing methods may produce shallow questions that can be answered by simple word matching
claim c2 [qualitative]: The proposed model aims to yield deep-level and to-the-point questions from text by simultaneously considering asking contents, expressive ways, and answering complexity
claim c3 [qualitative]: The model retrieves text-related commonsense context and disentangles key factors that control questions in terms of reasoning content and verbalized way, with independence priors and constraints imposed to facilitate disentanglement
claim c4 [qualitative]: A discriminator is developed to promote deep results by considering answering complexity
claim c5 [qualitative]: Through adversarial inference, latent factors are learned from data and diverse questions are yielded by sampling the expressive factor from data distributions
claim c6 [qualitative]: Evaluations on two typical datasets show the effectiveness of the approach

Inducing Character-level Structure in Subword-based Language Models with Type-level Interchange Intervention Training (2023)
seam: own-activations; tags: causal-intervention, character-level-reasoning, activation-steering, interpretability
The system augments a subword-based language model by learning causal structures for character-level representations through interchange intervention training. A causal model treats each character as a typed variable; the framework learns interventions that map characters to robust internal representations, enabling the subword model to perform character-level reasoning (spelling, arithmetic, word games) while maintaining access to subword-level meaning and context. Character representations are learned via training interventions on the frozen subword model's activations.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Our method outperforms character-level models on more complex tasks that blend form, meaning, and context, such as spelling correction in context and word search games.
claim c2 [qualitative]: While character-level models still perform best on purely form-based tasks like string reversal, our method outperforms character-level models on more complex tasks.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Compared with standard subword-based models, our approach significantly improves robustness on unseen token sequences.
claim c4 [qualitative]: The approach leads to human-interpretable internal representations of characters.
claim c5 [qualitative]: The method treats each character as a typed variable in a causal model and learns such causal structures by adapting the interchange intervention training method of Geiger et al. (2021).

Monte Carlo Thought Search: Large Language Model Querying for Complex Scientific Reasoning in Catalyst Design (2023)
seam: text; tags: mcts-search, chain-of-thought, reasoning-augmentation, goal-driven-search
The system augments LLM reasoning for catalyst discovery using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) over the LLM's generation space. The LLM generates candidate reasoning steps (nodes in the search tree); MCTS explores and evaluates these steps to navigate the combinatorial space of chemical properties and trade-offs. Information flows as: query → LLM generates reasoning steps → MCTS selects/expands/evaluates nodes → aggregates best reasoning path → outputs final answer. The mechanism treats the LLM as a generative engine for step proposals, not as a frozen oracle.
claim c1 [quantitative]: our approach can improve over the best baseline by 25.8%
claim c2 [qualitative]: our approach can augment scientist's reasoning and discovery process with novel insights
claim c3 [qualitative]: large language models have demonstrated novel capabilities for chemistry through complex instruction following capabilities and high quality reasoning
claim c4 [qualitative]: a goal-driven combinatorial search using LLMs has not been explored in detail

ReFT: Reasoning with Reinforced Fine-Tuning (2024)
seam: text; tags: sft-warmup, ppo-rl, chain-of-thought, reasoning, math-problem-solving
ReFT is a two-stage fine-tuning approach for reasoning in LLMs. Stage 1: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Chain-of-Thought annotations warms up the model. Stage 2: on-line reinforcement learning via PPO samples multiple reasoning paths for each question and derives rewards from ground-truth answers, enabling the model to learn from diverse solution trajectories rather than single annotated paths.
claim c1 [qualitative]: ReFT significantly outperforms SFT
claim c2 [qualitative]: ReFT obtains improvement by learning from the same training questions as SFT, without relying on extra or augmented training questions
claim c3 [qualitative]: Performance can be potentially further boosted by combining inference-time strategies such as majority voting and re-ranking

Math-Shepherd: Verify and Reinforce LLMs Step-by-step without Human Annotations (2024)
seam: text; tags: step-verification, process-reward, self-improvement, math-reasoning, feedback-loop, no-human-annotation
Math-Shepherd is a self-improvement framework for LLMs that verifies intermediate reasoning steps in mathematical problem-solving without human annotations. The system uses a trained verifier model to assess step-by-step solutions, provides feedback signals to the base LLM, and reinforces correct reasoning trajectories through a feedback loop. The base model generates solutions, the verifier scores intermediate steps, and this scoring signal is used to reinforce or correct the generation process.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Math-Shepherd can verify and reinforce LLMs step-by-step without human annotations
claim c2 [qualitative]: The framework learns to assess intermediate reasoning steps in mathematical problem-solving through self-verification
claim c3 [qualitative]: The system provides feedback signals to reinforce correct reasoning trajectories

Large Language Models Can Learn Temporal Reasoning (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: temporal-reasoning, graph-intermediate, text-to-graph-translation, chain-of-thought, synthetic-dataset
TG-LLM is a framework that teaches LLMs temporal reasoning by learning to translate natural-language text into temporal graph (TG) representations, a latent symbolic intermediate form. The LLM is fine-tuned on a synthetic dataset (TGQA) to perform text-to-TG translation, then learns to reason over these graphs via Chain-of-Thought bootstrapping. Information flows from text input → LLM translation to TG representation → LLM reasoning over TG structure → final temporal reasoning output.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The capability of TG translation learned on the TGQA dataset can be transferred to other TR tasks and benchmarks
claim c2 [qualitative]: Chain-of-Thought bootstrapping and graph data augmentation strategies, which maintain a balance between usefulness and diversity, bring more reliable CoTs and final results than vanilla CoT distillation
claim c3 [qualitative]: Temporal reasoning presents a significant challenge for LLMs due to its reliance on diverse temporal concepts and intricate temporal logic
claim c4 [qualitative]: A temporal graph (TG) latent representation enhances the learning of temporal reasoning compared to reasoning over the original context

mCoT: Multilingual Instruction Tuning for Reasoning Consistency in Language Models (2024)
seam: text; tags: chain-of-thought, instruction-tuning, multilingual, math-reasoning, consistency
mCoT is a 7B parameter language model trained via multilingual chain-of-thought instruction tuning. The system takes text input in one of eleven languages, applies supervised fine-tuning on a multilingual math reasoning dataset (mCoT-MATH) with CoT-annotated examples, and outputs reasoning traces followed by answers. Information flows from multilingual text input through the transformer backbone (trained on instruction tuning) to produce step-by-step reasoning and final answers across languages.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Existing LLMs show substantial variation across languages, and especially low performance for lesser resourced languages
claim c2 [qualitative]: The 7B parameter model mCoT achieves impressive consistency across languages
claim c3 [qualitative]: mCoT achieves superior or comparable performance to close-source and open-source models even of much larger sizes
claim c4 [qualitative]: Multilingual CoT instruction tuning boosts reasoning capability across languages, thereby improving model consistency

Gemma Scope: Open Sparse Autoencoders Everywhere All At Once on Gemma 2 (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, sparse-features, interpretability, residual-stream, multi-layer, activation-analysis
The paper presents Gemma Scope, a collection of sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on Gemma 2 language models at multiple layers and scales. SAEs are applied across the residual stream to decompose model activations into sparse, interpretable features. Information flows from frozen Gemma 2 activations through trained SAE encoders to produce sparse latent codes, which are decoded back to approximate the original activation space for analysis and interpretation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Open sparse autoencoders are trained on Gemma 2 at multiple layers and scales to decompose model activations into sparse, interpretable features

Accelerating Sparse Autoencoder Training via Layer-Wise Transfer Learning in Large Language Models (2024)
seam: own-activations; tags: transfer-learning, layer-wise-transfer, sae, interpretability, warm-start-initialization
The system trains Sparse AutoEncoders (SAEs) on LLM layer activations using transfer learning. An SAE trained on one layer is fine-tuned on adjacent layers, leveraging shared representations across the LLM's depth to accelerate convergence. Information flow: raw layer activations → pre-trained SAE (source layer) → fine-tuned SAE (target layer); the pre-trained weights are warm-start initialization for downstream layers.
claim c1 [qualitative]: fine-tuning SAEs using pre-trained models from nearby layers not only maintains but often improves the quality of learned representations
claim c2 [qualitative]: transfer learning significantly accelerates convergence of SAE training
claim c3 [qualitative]: strategic reuse of pre-trained SAEs is a promising approach particularly in settings where computational resources are constrained
claim c4 [qualitative]: shared representations are found across adjacent layers of LLMs

MedAdapter: Efficient Test-Time Adaptation of Large Language Models Towards Medical Reasoning (2024)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: test-time-adaptation, solution-ranking, adapter, biomedical-domain, privacy-preserving
MedAdapter is a post-hoc test-time adapter that ranks candidate solutions generated by an LLM without modifying the LLM itself. A small BERT-sized adapter network is trained to score or rank multiple solution candidates produced by the frozen LLM, selecting the best one at inference time. The adapter receives representations of candidate solutions and outputs ranking scores that guide solution selection.
claim c1 [quantitative]: MedAdapter achieves average performance improvements of 18.24% on white-box LLMs
claim c2 [quantitative]: MedAdapter achieves average performance improvements of 10.96% on black-box LLMs
claim c3 [qualitative]: MedAdapter effectively adapts both white-box and black-box LLMs in biomedical reasoning without requiring extensive computational resources or sharing data with third parties
claim c4 [qualitative]: MedAdapter yields enhanced performance when combined with train-time adaptation, highlighting a flexible and complementary solution to existing adaptation methods
claim c5 [qualitative]: MedAdapter adapts the original model by fine-tuning only a small BERT-sized adapter to rank candidate solutions generated by LLMs instead of fine-tuning the entire LLM

EHRAgent: Code Empowers Large Language Models for Few-shot Complex Tabular Reasoning on Electronic Health Records (2024)
seam: external-engine; tags: code-generation, tool-use, iterative-refinement, error-feedback, multi-table-reasoning
