claim c6 [qualitative]: Learned graphs are sparse (5-6% edge density), domain-specific, and stable across seeds
claim c7 [qualitative]: Causal Concept Graphs capture learned causal dependencies between concepts in language model latent space for multi-step reasoning
claim c8 [qualitative]: Graph-guided interventions induce larger downstream effects than random interventions

Holographic Invariant Storage: Design-Time Safety Contracts via Vector Symbolic Architectures (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vsa, safety-contract, bipolar-encoding, context-drift, noise-robustness, holographic-storage
Holographic Invariant Storage assembles properties of bipolar Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSA) into a design-time safety contract for LLM context-drift mitigation. The system uses closed-form mathematical guarantees about signal recovery, noise robustness, and multi-signal capacity to enable pre-deployment budgeting of recovery fidelity and codebook capacity. A VSA encoding binds safety properties into distributed vector representations that can be injected into LLM context and recovered with quantified fidelity bounds, crossing the model boundary via text or activation-space injection of safety constraints.
claim c1 [theoretical]: single-signal recovery fidelity converges to 1/√2 ≈ 0.707 regardless of noise depth or content
claim c2 [theoretical]: continuous-noise robustness bound 2Φ(1/σ) - 1
claim c3 [theoretical]: multi-signal capacity degradation approximately √(1/(K+1))
claim c4 [quantitative]: closed-form guarantees validated by Monte Carlo simulation
claim c5 [qualitative]: safety re-injection improves adherence at the 2B scale
claim c6 [qualitative]: pilot behavioral experiment across four LLMs (2B–7B, 720 trials) confirms safety re-injection efficacy with full results in appendix
claim c7 [qualitative]: HIS enables systems engineers to budget recovery fidelity and codebook capacity at design time
claim c8 [qualitative]: design-time safety contracts provide property no timer or embedding-distance metric provides

Learning Retrieval Models with Sparse Autoencoders (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, sparse-retrieval, learned-sparse-retrieval, interpretability, multilingual
SPLARE trains Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to decompose LLM representations into interpretable latent features for Learned Sparse Retrieval. Queries and documents are encoded into high-dimensional sparse representations by projecting LLM activations through a trained SAE, producing semantically structured features for efficient retrieval. The method operates by training an SAE decoder to map from dense LLM hidden states to sparse latent codes, which replace vocabulary-space projections used in prior LSR work.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAE-based representations offer the potential to produce more semantically structured, expressive, and language-agnostic features compared to vocabulary-based LSR approaches
claim c2 [qualitative]: SPLARE consistently outperforms vocabulary-based LSR in multilingual and out-of-domain settings
claim c3 [qualitative]: SPLARE-7B achieves top results on MMTEB's multilingual and English retrieval tasks
claim c4 [qualitative]: A 2B-parameter variant was developed with a significantly lighter footprint

How Pruning Reshapes Features: Sparse Autoencoder Analysis of Weight-Pruned Language Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, weight-pruning, feature-analysis, interpretability, activation-geometry
The paper uses Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as interpretability probes to analyze how weight pruning reshapes the feature geometry of language models. SAEs are applied to three model families (Gemma 3 1B, Gemma 2 2B, Llama 3.2 1B) after subjecting them to unstructured pruning via magnitude-based or Wanda methods across sparsity levels from 0–60%. The SAE encodes model activations into sparse feature representations; the paper measures survival, transferability, and causal relevance of these features pre- and post-pruning.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Rare SAE features--those with low firing rates--survive pruning far better than frequent ones, with within-condition Spearman correlations of rho = -1.0 in 11 of 17 experimental conditions.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Pruning acts as implicit feature selection, preferentially destroying high-frequency generic features while preserving specialized rare ones.
claim c3 [quantitative]: Wanda pruning preserves feature structure up to 3.7x better than magnitude pruning.
claim c4 [quantitative]: Pre-trained SAEs remain viable on Wanda-pruned models up to 50% sparsity.
claim c5 [negative]: Geometric feature survival does not predict causal importance--a dissociation with implications for interpretability under compression.
claim c6 [qualitative]: The paper presents the first systematic study of how unstructured pruning reshapes the feature geometry of language models using Sparse Autoencoders as interpretability probes.

Neurosymbolic Counterpoint Generation (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: symbolic-music-generation, counterpoint, constrained-decoding, transformer, tokenization, rule-enforcement
An autoregressive Transformer model generates Renaissance counterpoint in MIDI symbolic format by flattening multi-voice polyphonic music into a sequence. The model uses tokenization of symbolic note events and incorporates a symbolic component that enforces contrapuntal rules as constraints during generation. Information flows from encoded token sequences through transformer layers to predicted next tokens, with symbolic rule enforcement applied to filter or guide valid outputs.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The proposed model incorporates a symbolic component that enforces fundamental contrapuntal rules.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Flattened sequence models, when combined with appropriate tokenization and symbolic constraints, are capable of generating rich, multi-layered contrapuntal music.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The research explores the capacity of deep learning models to manage independent voices and strict stylistic constraints.
claim c4 [qualitative]: The thesis compares multiple data representation strategies with distinct tokenization methods.

LangFIR: Discovering Sparse Language-Specific Features from Monolingual Data for Language Steering (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, activation-steering, language-control, feature-filtering, sparse-features
LangFIR discovers sparse language-specific features from monolingual data by decomposing residual activations using sparse autoencoders (SAEs), then filters out language-agnostic features using random-token sequences to isolate language-identity directions. These sparse feature directions are aggregated into steering vectors that are added to model activations at inference time to control multilingual output language.
claim c1 [qualitative]: LangFIR discovers language-specific SAE features using only a small amount of monolingual data and random-token sequences, without requiring multilingual or parallel data.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Many SAE features consistently activated by target-language inputs do not encode language identity; random-token sequences surface these language-agnostic features, allowing filtering to isolate language-specific features.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Language-specific features discovered by LangFIR are extremely sparse, highly selective for their target language, and causally important: directional ablation increases cross-entropy loss only for the corresponding language.
claim c4 [qualitative]: LangFIR achieves the best average accuracy BLEU across three models (Gemma 3 1B, Gemma 3 4B, and Llama 3.1 8B), three datasets, and twelve target languages, outperforming the strongest monolingual baseline.
claim c5 [qualitative]: LangFIR surpasses methods that rely on parallel data for multilingual generation control.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Language identity in multilingual LLMs is localized in a sparse set of feature directions discoverable with monolingual data.

Learning to Edit Knowledge via Instruction-based Chain-of-Thought Prompting (2026)
seam: text; tags: knowledge-editing, chain-of-thought, retrieval-augmented-generation, supervised-fine-tuning, policy-optimization
A knowledge editing system that trains LLMs to reason over edited knowledge using chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. The pipeline: (1) language model agents generate CoTs for both structured (fact triples) and unstructured (news, articles) edited data to create instruction data; (2) the target model is trained via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to reason over edited knowledge; (3) at inference, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) dynamically retrieves relevant edited facts and provides them as context for the model to perform real-time knowledge editing.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Our method achieves strong generalization across six diverse knowledge editing scenarios with just a single round of training on three open-source language models
claim c2 [qualitative]: Current knowledge editing approaches face poor generalization: most approaches rigidly inject new knowledge without ensuring that the model can use it effectively to solve practical problems
claim c3 [qualitative]: Current knowledge editing methods focus primarily on structured fact triples, overlooking the diverse unstructured forms of factual information (e.g., news, articles) prevalent in real-world contexts
claim c4 [qualitative]: CoT2Edit leverages language model agents for both structured and unstructured edited data to generate CoTs, building high-quality instruction data

Bridging Time and Space: Decoupled Spatio-Temporal Alignment for Video Grounding (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: video-grounding, spatio-temporal-decoupling, semantic-bridging, query-guided-localization, mllm-adapter, multi-task-learning
Bridge-STG is an end-to-end framework for spatio-temporal video grounding that decouples temporal and spatial localization while maintaining semantic coherence. A Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) performs temporal reasoning to produce temporal reasoning context. This is distilled via a Spatio-Temporal Semantic Bridging (STSB) mechanism with Explicit Temporal Alignment (ETA) into enriched bridging queries. These queries serve as a semantic interface to a purpose-built spatial decoder. The spatial decoder uses Query-Guided Spatial Localization (QGSL) with multi-layer interactive queries and positive/negative frame sampling to perform spatial localization while reducing visual token redundancy.
claim c1 [quantitative]: Bridge-STG improves average m_vIoU from 26.4 to 34.3 on VidSTG
claim c2 [qualitative]: Bridge-STG achieves state-of-the-art performance among MLLM-based methods across multiple benchmarks
claim c3 [qualitative]: Bridge-STG demonstrates strong cross-task transfer across various fine-grained video understanding tasks under a unified multi-task training regime
claim c4 [qualitative]: Spatio-Temporal Semantic Bridging (STSB) mechanism with Explicit Temporal Alignment (ETA) distills the MLLM's temporal reasoning context into enriched bridging queries as a robust semantic interface
claim c5 [qualitative]: Query-Guided Spatial Localization (QGSL) module leverages bridging queries to drive a purpose-built spatial decoder with multi-layer interactive queries and positive/negative frame sampling to eliminate dual-domain visual token redundancy

MeloTune: On-Device Arousal Learning and Peer-to-Peer Mood Coupling for Proactive Music Curation (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: cfc-network, affect-prediction, peer-to-peer-mood, on-device-inference, arousal-learning, mmp-protocol, svaf-fusion
MeloTune is an iPhone-deployed music curation agent running two closed-form continuous-time (CfC) neural networks: a private listener-level CfC that predicts short-horizon affective trajectories on Russell's circumplex to drive proactive curation, and a shared mesh-runtime CfC at MMP Layer 6 that integrates Cognitive Memory Blocks (CMBs) from co-listening peers. CfC hidden states remain on-device; only structured CMBs are transmitted peer-to-peer. A learned Personal Arousal Function (PAF) replaces the standard linear audio-intensity-to-arousal mapping with per-listener learned adjustments trained from behavioral signals (skip, completion, favorite, volume) and drift between declared and inferred mood. The system runs on-device via CoreML inference.
claim c1 [quantitative]: The model achieves trajectory MAE 0.414 on held-out validation
claim c2 [quantitative]: Pattern accuracy 96.6% on held-out validation
claim c3 [quantitative]: Intent accuracy 69.4% on held-out validation
claim c4 [qualitative]: The model comprises 94,552 parameters
claim c5 [qualitative]: PAF evidence from a live deployment session (46 observations across 11 genres) demonstrates that the learning loop operates end-to-end, with pop reaching full confidence after 22 observations
claim c6 [qualitative]: All inference runs on-device via CoreML
claim c7 [qualitative]: This is the first production deployment of MMP/SVAF on consumer mobile hardware
claim c8 [qualitative]: The same track receives different arousal predictions for different listeners

Are LLM Uncertainty and Correctness Encoded by the Same Features? A Functional Dissociation via Sparse Autoencoders (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, feature-suppression, activation-intervention, uncertainty-calibration, ablation, interpretability
The system uses sparse autoencoders (SAEs) applied to internal activations of Llama-3.1-8B and Gemma-2-9B to identify and classify feature populations by their functional role along two independent dimensions: correctness and confidence. A 2×2 partitioning framework categorizes model predictions, SAEs extract features for each quadrant independently, and targeted suppression of identified features measures their causal contribution to accuracy and entropy. Features are categorized as pure uncertainty, pure incorrectness, or confounded; suppression experiments and selective abstention via learned classifiers form the central mechanism.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Pure uncertainty features are functionally essential: suppressing them severely degrades accuracy
claim c2 [qualitative]: Pure incorrectness features are functionally inert: despite showing statistically significant activation differences between correct and incorrect predictions, the majority produce near-zero change in accuracy when suppressed
claim c3 [quantitative]: Targeted suppression of confounded features yields a 1.1% accuracy improvement and a 75% entropy reduction, with effects transferring across the ARC-Challenge and RACE benchmarks
claim c4 [qualitative]: The activations of just 3 confounded features from a single mid-network layer predict model correctness (AUROC ~0.79)
claim c5 [qualitative]: Selective abstention using the 3-feature correctness predictor raises accuracy from 62% to 81% at 53% coverage
claim c6 [theoretical]: Uncertainty and correctness are distinct internal phenomena, encoded by separate feature populations rather than the same mechanisms

Knowledge Capsules: Structured Nonparametric Memory Units for LLMs (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: kv-injection, retrieval, nonparametric-memory, knowledge-capsules, attention-mechanism, multi-hop-reasoning
Knowledge Capsules are structured nonparametric memory units that represent normalized relational knowledge, constructed from document corpora using a frozen base model. An External Key-Value Injection (KVI) framework compiles capsules into attention-compatible key-value representations that are injected directly into the model's attention computation, allowing external knowledge to participate at the memory level rather than as context tokens. The frozen base model processes queries, and the injected KV pairs from capsules augment the attention mechanism without parameter updates.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Knowledge Capsules consistently outperform RAG and GraphRAG across multiple QA benchmarks, with improved stability and accuracy in long context and multi-hop reasoning scenarios
claim c2 [qualitative]: Knowledge Capsules require no parameter updates to the base model
claim c3 [qualitative]: RAG operates purely through context expansion, where external knowledge competes as tokens within the attention mechanism, making its influence indirect and often unstable in long context and multi-hop reasoning scenarios
claim c4 [qualitative]: Knowledge Capsules can be constructed directly from document corpora using a frozen base model
claim c5 [qualitative]: The External Key-Value Injection framework enables external knowledge to directly participate in the model's attention computation by compiling capsules into attention-compatible key-value representations

Why Are Some Emotions Harder for LLMs? Uncovering the Causal Mechanisms of Emotion Inference via Sparse Autoencoders (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, causal-intervention, emotion-inference, feature-steering, mechanistic-interpretability
The paper uses sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to decompose LLM hidden states into interpretable features and identify causal sparse emotion features that drive emotion inference. Information flows from LLM activations through SAE decomposition to sparse feature analysis, then through causal intervention experiments (targeted steering of weak features and global optimization of steering vectors) to test whether identified features causally control emotion recognition.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Some emotions, such as surprise and fear, rely on highly concentrated feature sets, whereas disgust exhibits a more distributed sparse causal organization
claim c2 [qualitative]: Disgust causal features are generally weaker, frequently co-activate with features for other emotions, and are often overshadowed by causal features for anger
claim c3 [qualitative]: These representational differences provide a mechanistic explanation for why LLMs struggle more with certain emotions
claim c4 [qualitative]: Targeted steering of weaker causal features can mitigate emotion-specific failures
claim c5 [qualitative]: Global optimization of a steering vector over the identified causal features improves overall emotion recognition performance

Persistent Visual Memory: Sustaining Perception for Deep Generation in LVLMs (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: visual-memory, retrieval-pathway, attention-mitigation, kv-injection, parallel-ffn-branch
Persistent Visual Memory (PVM) is a lightweight learnable module integrated as a parallel branch alongside the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) in Large Vision-Language Models. It establishes a distance-agnostic retrieval pathway that directly provides visual embeddings during autoregressive generation, mitigating visual signal dilution caused by textual history expansion and attention partition decay.
claim c1 [qualitative]: PVM brings notable improvements with negligible parameter overhead, delivering consistent average accuracy gains across both 4B and 8B scales, particularly in complex reasoning tasks that demand persistent visual perception.
claim c2 [qualitative]: PVM shows improved robustness in longer generations and accelerates internal prediction convergence.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Visual Signal Dilution phenomenon: the accumulation of textual history expands the attention partition function, causing visual attention to decay inversely with generated sequence length.

Sparse Autoencoder Decomposition of Clinical Sequence Model Representations: Feature Complexity, Task Specialisation, and Mortality Prediction (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, activation-analysis, clinical-nlp, interpretability, probing, intervention
The system applies sparse autoencoders (TopK SAEs) to FlatASCEND, a 14.5M-parameter autoregressive clinical sequence model trained on EHR data. SAEs decompose residual stream activations at all 10 transformer depth layers into interpretable sparse features. Information flows from EHR tokens through the frozen clinical model, whose activations are read by trained SAE encoders that project into a learned sparse dictionary space, then reconstructed; downstream analysis applies linear probes and intervention methods (delta-mode perturbation) to probe feature-level representations for clinical tasks.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAE decomposition reveals progressive abstraction across transformer depth: layer-0 features are near-perfect token detectors while layer-6 features span approximately 30 token types across multiple clinical categories
claim c2 [quantitative]: Layer-0 features are near-perfect token detectors with 45.7% singleton features
claim c3 [quantitative]: Layer-6 features have 0.5% singleton features
claim c4 [qualitative]: SAE features outperform dense representations for discrete event prediction (mortality) under full-sequence simple linear probes, but dense representations outperform for continuous magnitude prediction (length of stay)
claim c5 [quantitative]: In clinically relevant leakage-safe windows, dense representations match or exceed SAE features across all tested settings: eICU-CRD 48-hour mortality AUC SAE 0.871 versus dense 0.880
claim c6 [quantitative]: MIMIC-IV mortality AUC: SAE 0.836 versus dense 0.914
claim c7 [quantitative]: INSPECT 1-year/3-year mortality AUC: SAE 0.697 versus dense 0.800
claim c8 [negative]: Feature reproducibility across random seeds is 21%, and individual features should be interpreted as illustrative rather than stable. Delta-mode intervention effects are larger than random controls in 3 of 4 conditions but not formally significant

Feature Starvation as Geometric Instability in Sparse Autoencoders (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-autoencoder, feature-starvation, elastic-net, convexity, lipschitz-stability, polysemantic-disentanglement
Adaptive elastic net SAEs (AEN-SAEs) are sparse autoencoders that combine an ℓ2 structural regularization term enforcing strong convexity and Lipschitz stability with adaptive ℓ1 reweighting to suppress spurious features and eliminate shrinkage bias. The encoder maps dense LLM activations to a sparse, monosemantic feature dictionary; the decoder reconstructs activations from selected features. Information flows from frozen LLM activations through a learned encoder (sparse) to a learned decoder, with the ℓ2+adaptive-ℓ1 loss controlling the geometry of the sparse coding map to prevent feature starvation.
claim c1 [theoretical]: Feature starvation is a fundamental optimization-geometric pathology of overcomplete dictionaries: the ℓ1-induced sparse coding map is unstable and fundamentally misaligned with shallow, amortized encoders
claim c2 [theoretical]: AEN-SAEs yield a Lipschitz-continuous sparse coding map and recover the global feature support under mild assumptions
claim c3 [qualitative]: AEN-SAEs mitigate feature starvation without auxiliary heuristics while maintaining competitive reconstruction abilities
claim c4 [qualitative]: Adaptive ℓ1 reweighting eliminates shrinkage bias and suppresses spurious features
claim c5 [qualitative]: The ℓ2 structural term enforces strong convexity and Lipschitz stability, jointly controlling the curvature and interaction structure of the induced polyhedral geometry
claim c6 [qualitative]: AEN-SAEs are evaluated across synthetic settings and LLMs (Pythia 70M, Llama 3.1 8B)
claim c7 [negative]: Standard ℓ1-regularized SAEs suffer from feature starvation (dead neurons) and shrinkage bias, often requiring computationally expensive heuristic resampling and nondifferentiable hard-masking methods

From Token Lists to Graph Motifs: Weisfeiler-Lehman Analysis of Sparse Autoencoder Features (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, graph-kernel, weisfeiler-lehman, feature-clustering, mechanistic-interpretability, co-occurrence-structure
The paper constructs a graph-structured representation of sparse autoencoder (SAE) features by modeling each feature as a token co-occurrence graph, where nodes are frequent tokens near strong activations and edges connect co-occurring token pairs within local context windows. A custom Weisfeiler-Lehman style frequency-binned graph kernel computes similarity over this structural space. Features are then clustered using this graph-based similarity, and results are compared against decoder cosine similarity and token-histogram baselines.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Graph-based clustering recovers heuristic motif families (punctuation-heavy patterns, language and script clusters, and code-like templates) that are not recovered by clustering on decoder cosine similarity
claim c2 [qualitative]: A token-histogram baseline achieves higher overall purity than graph-based clustering
claim c3 [qualitative]: The contribution of the graph view is complementary rather than dominant: it surfaces structural relationships that token-frequency and decoder-weight views alone do not capture
claim c4 [qualitative]: Cluster assignments are stable across graph-construction hyperparameters and random seeds

SMIXAE: Towards Unsupervised Manifold Discovery in Language Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, manifold-discovery, mixture-model, unsupervised-interpretation
SMIXAE extends sparse autoencoders (SAEs) by introducing a mixture-of-autoencoders architecture to directly model multidimensional features in transformer activations. Instead of tiling multidimensional features across independent SAE directions (which must be grouped post-hoc), SMIXAE trains multiple SAE components whose outputs are combined, enabling unsupervised discovery of manifold structures in language model hidden states. Information flows from frozen model activations into the trained mixture components, which jointly reconstruct activation patterns.
claim c1 [qualitative]: SAEs have an inability to directly model multidimensional features, instead tiling such features by a set of independent directions that must be grouped together after the SAE training phase, impeding discoverability and interpretation of learned feature representations
claim c2 [qualitative]: SMIXAE models have success in directly learning previously identified manifold structures within the open source Gemma 2 2B and 9B models
claim c3 [qualitative]: SMIXAE models find novel structures within the open source Gemma 2 2B and 9B models

AnomalyClaw: A Universal Visual Anomaly Detection Agent via Tool-Grounded Refutation (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: refutation-loop, vlm-agent, tool-grounding, anomaly-detection, multi-round-reasoning, cross-domain, self-evolution
AnomalyClaw is a training-free visual anomaly detection agent that augments vision-language models (VLMs) with a multi-round refutation process. In each round, the agent proposes candidate anomalies and refutes each against normal-sample references using a 13-tool library for visual verification, reference parsing, and frozen expert probing. The system operates entirely at inference time without model training, using the VLM's reasoning to iteratively refine anomaly judgments through evidence gathering and contradiction detection.
claim c1 [quantitative]: AnomalyClaw achieves consistent macro-AUROC improvements over single-step direct inference with +6.23 pp on GPT-4.5
claim c2 [quantitative]: AnomalyClaw achieves consistent macro-AUROC improvements over single-step direct inference with +7.93 pp on Seed2.0-lite
claim c3 [quantitative]: AnomalyClaw achieves consistent macro-AUROC improvements over single-step direct inference with +3.52 pp on Qwen3.5-VL-27B
claim c4 [quantitative]: Verbalized self-evolution extension delivers a +2.09 pp mean gain on Qwen3.5-VL-27B
claim c5 [quantitative]: Self-evolution gain is comparable to a K = 10 oracle-label supervised baseline (+1.99 pp)
claim c6 [qualitative]: Single-inference VLM judgments are unreliable, since they rely more on prior knowledge than on normal-sample references or fine-grained feature evidence
claim c7 [qualitative]: Agentic refutation improves anomaly understanding and reasoning of VLMs, rather than merely aggregating tool outputs
claim c8 [qualitative]: Self-evolution builds an online rulebook from internal-branch disagreement without oracle labels

Descriptive Collision in Sparse Autoencoder Auto-Interpretability: When One Explanation Describes Many Features (2026)
seam: text; tags: sparse-autoencoders, auto-interpretability, feature-analysis, explanation-scoring, polysemanticity
The paper analyzes sparse autoencoders (SAEs) that decompose language model activations into interpretable features, and studies automated natural-language explanation assignment pipelines. The work does not propose a new system but rather diagnoses a failure mode in existing auto-interpretability workflows: when multiple distinct features receive identical or near-identical textual explanations, the explanation fails to discriminate feature identity. The paper formalizes this 'descriptive collision' problem and proposes corrective scoring metrics (collision-adjusted detection and discrimination scoring) that penalize non-discriminative explanations.
claim c1 [quantitative]: The mean annotation string is reused across 3.07 features
claim c2 [quantitative]: 82.1% of features share their annotation with at least one other feature
claim c3 [quantitative]: The single most common annotation string ('plural nouns') labels 101 distinct features spanning 18 layers and four model components
claim c4 [quantitative]: The average annotation resolves only 70% of feature identity
claim c5 [negative]: Current detection-style auto-interpretability scoring is invariant to collision
claim c6 [qualitative]: Ignoring descriptive collision inflates reported feature interpretability by a quantity equal to roughly one-third of the bits required to identify a feature
claim c7 [qualitative]: The collision problem is independent of, and additive with, previously identified failure modes of auto-interpretability
claim c8 [qualitative]: Descriptive collision is a complementary, structurally distinct problem to polysemanticity

MeMo: Memory as a Model (2026)
seam: text; tags: knowledge-encoding, retrieval-augmentation, modular-memory, plug-and-play
MeMo encodes new domain-specific knowledge into a dedicated memory model while keeping the host LLM frozen. The memory model learns to represent cross-document relationships and is queried at inference time to retrieve relevant information, which is then passed to the frozen LLM as context. The system operates without access to LLM weights or logits, enabling integration with both open and proprietary models.
claim c1 [qualitative]: MeMo captures complex cross-document relationships
claim c2 [qualitative]: MeMo is robust to retrieval noise
claim c3 [qualitative]: MeMo avoids catastrophic forgetting in the LLM
claim c4 [qualitative]: MeMo does not require access to the LLM's weights or output logits, enabling plug-and-play integration with both open and proprietary closed-source LLMs
claim c5 [qualitative]: MeMo's retrieval cost is independent of corpus size at inference time
claim c6 [qualitative]: MeMo achieves strong performance compared to existing methods across diverse settings

A neurosymbolic Approach with Epistemic Deep Learning for Hierarchical Image Classification (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: focal-sets, fuzzy-logic, epistemic-uncertainty, hierarchical-reasoning, belief-theory, logical-consistency
The system augments a Swin Transformer backbone with a neurosymbolic layer that combines focal set reasoning and differentiable fuzzy logic. The transformer encodes images into an embedding space where data-driven focal sets are induced to capture epistemic uncertainty over multiple plausible fine-grained classes. A belief-theoretic layer applies fuzzy membership functions and t-norm conjunctions to enforce logical consistency between fine- and coarse-grained hierarchical predictions. A learnable composite loss balances calibration, mass regularization, and logical consistency, allowing the model to adaptively trade off symbolic structure against data-driven evidence.
claim c1 [qualitative]: the framework maintains accuracy on par with transformer baselines while providing more calibrated and interpretable predictions
claim c2 [qualitative]: focal sets induce epistemic uncertainty over multiple plausible fine-grained classes within the learnt embedding space
claim c3 [qualitative]: fuzzy membership functions and t-norm conjunctions encourage consistency between fine- and coarse-grained predictions
claim c4 [qualitative]: the framework reduces overconfidence in predictions
claim c5 [qualitative]: the framework enforces high logical consistency across hierarchical outputs
claim c6 [negative]: deep neural networks often produce overconfident predictions as which fail to express epistemic uncertainty, and frequently violate logical or structural constraints present in the data

TEMPO: Temporal Enforcement via Mode-Separated Policy Optimization for Trustworthy LLM Backtesting (2026)
seam: text; tags: temporal-constraint, rlhf-rl-policy, multi-mode-reward, knowledge-retention, backtesting
TEMPO trains LLMs to enforce temporal discipline during backtesting by learning to condition evidence selection on instance-specific cutoff dates. A two-mode reward function (leakage mode + performance mode) is applied within a GRPO-based training pipeline. The leakage mode drives post-cutoff claims toward zero as a prerequisite; once temporal compliance is achieved, the performance mode optimizes task accuracy. The model learns to discover temporally valid reasoning strategies without erasing knowledge.
claim c1 [qualitative]: TEMPO reduces leakage from 2~13% to 0.6~3.7% across all conditions
claim c2 [qualitative]: task performance improving 6~13% where strong pre-cutoff signals exist
claim c3 [qualitative]: task performance maintained where the prediction task is inherently difficult from valid information alone
claim c4 [theoretical]: training monotonically decreases leakage, converges to the leak-free optimum, and improves task performance once compliance is achieved
claim c5 [negative]: Prompt-based constraints fail when suppressed content is causally related to the prediction
claim c6 [negative]: knowledge unlearning cannot address temporal compliance because it is instance-specific: the same fact may be legitimate evidence for one cutoff date and a violation for another

Distributional Alignment as a Criterion for Designing Task Vectors in In-Context Learning (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: task-vectors, in-context-learning, distributional-alignment, linear-regression, hidden-state-compression
The paper proposes Linear Task Vector (LTV), a method for compressing in-context learning demonstrations into compact hidden-state representations. LTV uses a closed-form linear mapping that estimates demonstration effects through regression to minimize the discrepancy between task vector-based and ICL-based inference. The mechanism operates by extracting task vectors from LLM hidden states and applying a learned linear transformation to align the predictive distribution (next-token probabilities) with full ICL.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LTV consistently outperforms existing task vector baselines, improving average accuracy by 9.2%
claim c2 [qualitative]: LTV reduces inference latency
claim c3 [qualitative]: d_NTP serves as a performance proxy, exhibiting a strong negative correlation with downstream accuracy
claim c4 [qualitative]: task vectors from a larger model can enhance a smaller model's performance by 6.4%
claim c5 [qualitative]: LTV outperforms the baselines on regression tasks
claim c6 [qualitative]: Inference using task vectors should align their predictive distribution with that of ICL

Sutra: Tensor-Op RNNs as a Compilation Target for Vector Symbolic Architectures (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: vsa-compilation, tensor-lowering, kleene-logic, rotation-binding, differentiable-symbolic, program-synthesis
Sutra is a typed, purely functional programming language that compiles to a fused PyTorch tensor-operation graph over frozen embedding substrates. The compiler beta-reduces primitives, control flow, and string I/O to tensor operations. Rotation binding, unbinding, bundling, polynomial Kleene three-valued logic, and tail-recursive loops lower to tensor operations via Lagrange-interpolated polynomials. Information flows from symbolic source code through compilation to a single differentiable tensor graph that can run on multiple frozen embedding modalities (text encoders or protein language models) and backpropagate gradients through the compiled operations.
claim c1 [quantitative]: decodes bundles at 100% accuracy through width k=8 on every substrate, where the textbook Hadamard product has already collapsed
claim c2 [quantitative]: Hadamard product collapse on all-minilm
claim c3 [quantitative]: a fuzzy-rule classifier written in .su trains from random init to 100.0 +/- 0.0% by backpropagating through the emitted graph
claim c4 [quantitative]: chance baseline for five-class classification
claim c5 [quantitative]: a weighted variant additionally trains a scalar cosine gain and writes it back into the .su source as a numeric literal; recompiling reproduces the trained behaviour to ~2e-7 per logit
claim c6 [qualitative]: The same program runs on four frozen embeddings spanning two modalities and decodes bundles at 100% accuracy through width k=8 on every substrate
claim c7 [qualitative]: PyTorch autograd flows through the actually compiled graph: the symbolic source unmodified trains from random init to 100% by backpropagating through the emitted graph
claim c8 [qualitative]: The same artifact is therefore both a logic program and a trainable neural network

Dynamic Mixture of Latent Memories for Self-Evolving Agents (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: mixture-of-experts, latent-memory-injection, continual-learning, catastrophic-forgetting-mitigation, routing-by-matching, parameter-efficient
MoLEM is a mixture-of-experts (MoE) framework that treats multiple experts as independent carriers to generate latent memory. A router selects and weights experts through key-query matching, and the aggregated latent memory is injected into the reasoning process. The base model remains frozen; all experiential knowledge is internalized into additional expert and routing modules. For continual learning, each training stage includes a lightweight autoencoder that selects the appropriate routing group at inference, with fallback to the pretrained model for out-of-distribution inputs.
claim c1 [quantitative]: After the full continual-learning sequence, our method improves the average accuracy by 10.40% over the Vanilla pretrained baseline
claim c2 [qualitative]: The base model for reasoning remains entirely frozen, with all experiential knowledge internalized into the additional modules, avoiding catastrophic forgetting
claim c3 [qualitative]: A router selects and weights experts through key-query matching, and the aggregated latent memory is injected into the reasoning process
claim c4 [qualitative]: For each training stage, a lightweight autoencoder selects the appropriate routing group at inference, and inputs that match no stage fall back to the pretrained model
claim c5 [negative]: None of the competing methods consistently exceed the baseline across different training orders

Steered Generation via Gradient-Based Optimization on Sparse Query Features (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: activation-steering, sae, query-intervention, gradient-optimization, prototype-learning
The system applies Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to attention query activations in LLMs to decompose them into interpretable sparse features. During inference, gradient-based optimization adjusts the sparse representation to align with learned class prototypes of target behaviors, steering generation without modifying the base model weights. Information flows as: forward pass → query activation extraction → SAE encoding to sparse features → gradient optimization against prototype targets → sparse feature manipulation → generation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Manipulating attention query activations offers sharper steerability than general state interventions
claim c2 [qualitative]: Sparse query representations provide the necessary disentanglement for unified, interpretable control over both logical planning and stylistic nuance
claim c3 [qualitative]: Optimizing sparse query features enables effective navigation of rigid planning requirements (safe vs. short paths) in Textualized Gridworld
claim c4 [qualitative]: The framework steers the cognitive complexity of feedback (Bloom's Taxonomy) in a high-dimensional educational domain
claim c5 [qualitative]: Interventions on dense states can entangle distinct semantic features

HyperGuide: Hyperbolic Guidance for Efficient Multi-Step Reasoning in Large Language Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: hyperbolic-geometry, activation-projection, adapter-steering, multi-step-reasoning, self-play-finetuning
HyperGuide injects a hyperbolic geometric signal into LLM decoding to guide multi-step reasoning. A lightweight head projects the model's hidden states into hyperbolic space, where distance-to-origin encodes solution proximity and angular separation distinguishes reasoning branches. A low-rank adapter is fine-tuned via interactive learning on the model's own reasoning attempts to act on this injected geometric signal during step-by-step generation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The hyperbolic space matches the asymmetry of combinatorial reasoning trees: distance-to-origin naturally encodes solution proximity while angular separation distinguishes branches requiring different next operations
claim c2 [qualitative]: The geometric signal yields consistent gains across multiple benchmarks, with larger improvements on deeper reasoning chains
claim c3 [qualitative]: In combinatorial reasoning trees, solution-bearing states are few while dead ends are exponentially numerous; hyperbolic space has compact volume near the origin and exponentially expanding capacity toward the boundary

Neural Bayesian Sequential Routing (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: bayesian-routing, evidence-accumulation, dag-inference, uncertainty-quantification, conjugate-update, early-exiting
NBSR models neural inference as active evidence accumulation over a hierarchical DAG within a Dirichlet–Categorical conjugate framework. Neural experts query a persistent global knowledge oracle to extract positive evidence vectors (pseudo-counts), which update a Dirichlet belief state via exact conjugate addition. A Gumbel-Softmax Straight-Through estimator enables hard, path-dependent routing while preserving surrogate gradients for end-to-end training. Dirichlet precision and entropy provide uncertainty quantification, early exiting, OOD abstention, and cost-aware evidence acquisition mechanisms.
claim c1 [theoretical]: Under strictly positive evidence extraction, total Dirichlet precision increases monotonically along any valid trajectory
claim c2 [theoretical]: Marginal predictive variance is bounded, formalizing sequential hypothesis sharpening
claim c3 [theoretical]: Under idealized capacity and optimization assumptions, the terminal Dirichlet expectation recovers the Bayes-optimal conditional distribution
claim c4 [qualitative]: NBSR achieves competitive predictive performance while providing transparent routing traces, path-dependent evidence attribution, uncertainty-aware decision control, and resource-rational inference
claim c5 [qualitative]: NBSR provides uncertainty quantification, entropy-based early exiting, OOD abstention, and cost-aware evidence acquisition mechanisms

Sign-Aware Gated Sparse Autoencoders: Modeling Anticorrelated Features with Bi-Jump-ReLU Activations (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-autoencoder, interpretability, anticorrelated-features, gated-sparsity, bipolar-latents, activation-steering
Sign-Aware Gated Sparse Autoencoder (SA-GSAE) extends SAEs by replacing single-sided sparsity with two-sided gating over signed magnitudes. A polarity-sensitive gate selects support on either positive or negative sign; a signed-magnitude reconstruction path bypasses L1 shrinkage; an auxiliary reconstruction loss prevents gate collapse. Bipolar latents encode both signs along a shared direction via a Bi-Jump-ReLU activation, allowing anticorrelated features to share a single latent when beneficial. The model is trained end-to-end on LLM hidden activations with joint optimization of gating, magnitude, and auxiliary paths.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Half-width SA-GSAE at width H strictly Pareto-dominates full-width Gated SAE at 2H over the entire swept L0 overlap on 3 of 6 cells (both MLP-output hookpoints and resid-mid/Pythia-1B)
claim c2 [quantitative]: On remaining 3 cells SA-GSAE matches R² within 0.025 (max gap -0.008) while cutting dead fraction by 0.35–0.62 absolute
claim c3 [qualitative]: Sweep-geomean dead-fraction reductions are ~100x–500x on MLP-output cells and Pythia-1B resid, ~2x–4x on attention cells and SmolLM3-3B resid
claim c4 [qualitative]: Ablation: removing auxiliary loss causes reconstruction to collapse to R² 0.27 with 98% dead latents
claim c5 [qualitative]: Ablation: tying r_i^+ = r_i^- (symmetric variant) is indistinguishable from separate paths (|ΔR²| = 0.0015)
claim c6 [qualitative]: Two-sided gate and auxiliary loss are load-bearing components
claim c7 [qualitative]: On MLP-output hookpoints, most latents carry both polarities; on attention hookpoints, bipolar structure concentrates in a small set of top latents
claim c8 [negative]: Full-width SA-GSAE exhibits a reproducible reconstruction collapse at SmolLM3-3B resid that the half-width variant entirely avoids

Citation-Closure Retrieval and Per-Rule Attribution for Real-World Regulatory Compliance Question Answering (2026)
seam: external-engine; tags: citation-traversal, knowledge-graph-retrieval, per-rule-attribution, evidence-closure, regulatory-qa
RefWalk is a unified retrieval framework for regulatory compliance QA that traverses cross-document citations within an Operational Knowledge Graph, anchored by shared topic queries. It retrieves multi-view candidate evidence sets, fuses them via max-based aggregation, and enforces per-rule attribution to explicitly map claims back to regulatory sources. The system operates on a structured procedural knowledge graph derived from national R&D regulations, contrasting with flat RAG baselines that fail to handle tiered authority structures and citation closure.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Existing RAG systems struggle with regulatory compliance QA due to flattened citation edges, fragmented retrieval expansions, and fragile post-hoc attribution
claim c2 [qualitative]: RefWalk establishes a strong baseline with substantial improvements in retrieval recall and citation accuracy on RegOps-Bench
claim c3 [qualitative]: Existing systems exhibit saturation on flat-structure rules in U.S. health compliance (HIPAA) evaluation
claim c4 [qualitative]: RegOps-Bench features an Operational Knowledge Graph derived from complex national R&D regulations, requiring structured procedural lookups and evidence-set closure rather than entity resolution or case-law reasoning
claim c5 [qualitative]: RefWalk traverses cross-document citations, fuses multi-view candidates via max-based aggregation, and enforces per-rule attribution to explicitly map claims to sources

Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-autoencoders, dictionary-learning, activation-steering, interpretability, monosemanticity
The system trains sparse autoencoders on the middle layer residual stream of Claude 3 Sonnet to extract interpretable features. The autoencoder learns a dictionary of up to 34 million features that decompose the model's internal activations into sparse, interpretable components. Information flows from the frozen language model's residual stream through the trained sparse autoencoder to produce feature activations; these features are then used for steering experiments and causal analysis by injecting manipulated activations back into the model.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Sparse autoencoders can extract interpretable features from Claude 3 Sonnet, a production-scale language model, addressing the open question of whether dictionary learning methods scale beyond small transformers.
claim c2 [quantitative]: Trained sparse autoencoders with up to 34 million features on the model's middle layer residual stream.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The resulting features are multilingual and multimodal (generalizing to images despite text-only training).
claim c4 [qualitative]: Features can be used to steer model behavior in ways consistent with their interpretations, including features corresponding to famous entities and locations, abstract concepts like sarcasm or errors in code.
claim c5 [qualitative]: Features relevant to ways in which language models might cause harm--including features representing deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, and bias--causally influence model outputs when manipulated.
claim c6 [negative]: Our suite of features is incomplete, and we lack rigorous methods for evaluating whether our features faithfully capture model computations.

Do Proactive Agents Really Need an LLM to Decide When to Wake and What to Anchor? (2026)
seam: text; tags: graph-encoding, trigger-decision, selective-llm-invocation, event-stream-routing
A proactive agent system that monitors user activity as a structured event stream (actor, verb, object, timestamp tuples) maintained by the operating system in graph form. A small temporal-graph-learning (TGL) model encodes this event stream, producing per-event trigger probabilities and per-entity routing scores in a single forward pass. Only when the trigger fires does the system invoke a downstream LLM to convert the structured handoff into a fluent user-facing sentence. The approach avoids the round-trip of rendering structured events as text for LLM decision-making and back again.
claim c1 [qualitative]: TGL improves F1 on each of 14 backbones
claim c2 [qualitative]: TGL F1 improvement reaches up to +46.0 on some backbones
claim c3 [qualitative]: TGL runs at 11.13 ms per event on a GPU server
claim c4 [qualitative]: TGL runs at 13.99 ms per event on a consumer laptop
claim c5 [qualitative]: TGL is approximately 4–7x faster than every single-forward LLM-as-trigger configuration on GPU server
claim c6 [qualitative]: TGL is approximately 12–83x faster than every single-forward LLM-as-trigger configuration on consumer laptop
claim c7 [qualitative]: TGL checkpoint gives strongest trigger AUCs and most stable deployed threshold across trigger-architecture comparisons
claim c8 [qualitative]: TGL has approximately 220 MiB BF16 resident footprint deployable on-device

Feature Geometry of LoRA Adapters: A Sparse Autoencoder Analysis of Representational Divergence in Fine-Tuned Language Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, lora-analysis, residual-stream-decomposition, representational-geometry, interpretability
The paper investigates LoRA-induced representational changes in Gemma-2-9B by training adapter-specific Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) on delta activations (residual stream contributions from LoRA adapters alone) across multiple transformer layers. Information flows through the model's residual stream; LoRA updates are isolated via delta activation subtraction, then decomposed by SAEs trained on this adapter-specific signal. Comparison with pretrained SAE dictionaries uses cosine similarity of decoder directions, principal-angle analysis, and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to measure geometric alignment between learned and pretrained feature spaces.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Across layers and ranks, we consistently observe comparatively weak geometric alignment between LoRA-induced feature dictionaries and pretrained SAE features.
claim c2 [qualitative]: Adapter-specific SAEs reconstruct delta activations more effectively than pretrained SAEs, suggesting that LoRA updates occupy partially distinct representational structure within the residual stream.
claim c3 [qualitative]: Feature density increases with rank and depth, while geometric divergence remains relatively stable across ranks.
claim c4 [qualitative]: LoRA fine-tuning can induce feature structures that are not fully captured by pretrained interpretability dictionaries.

Steering LLMs? Actually, Sparse Autoencoders can outperform simple baselines (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, activation-steering, interpretability, feature-selection, model-steering
The paper applies Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to steer LLM outputs by decomposing hidden activations into interpretable features. A supervised pipeline selects and labels SAE features, which are then used to intervene on model activations during generation. The seam is the model's own hidden states: SAE features are extracted from internal activations, labeled via supervised methods, and written back through activation steering to modify output behavior.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Sparse Autoencoders can perform close to on par with the reference LoRA performance on the AxBench benchmark, when features are selected and labelled with our supervised pipeline.
claim c2 [qualitative]: SAEs did not live up to their original hype due to poor steering performance relative to a set of simple baselines in Wu et al. (2025), but this work serves as a partial rebuttal showing SAEs perform closer to reference methods when properly configured.
claim c3 [qualitative]: The supervised pipeline selects features that are surprisingly causal of their identified labels when using only its interpretability-based components.
claim c4 [negative]: High sparsity (low l0) may not be crucial for successful steering based on interpretability, which is in contrast to the earlier findings in Wang et al. (2025).

On the Relationship Between Activation Outliers and Feature Death in Sparse Autoencoders (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-autoencoder, activation-analysis, feature-death, mean-centering, interpretability
The paper studies sparse autoencoders (SAEs) that decompose neural network activations into interpretable features. SAEs map high-dimensional activations through a dictionary matrix and ReLU nonlinearity to produce sparse feature vectors. The core mechanism examined is how dimension-level activation outliers—dimensions with large mean magnitude relative to per-token variation—shift pre-activations at initialization based on each feature's alignment with the activation mean, causing features anti-aligned with the mean to receive permanently negative pre-activations and never activate (feature death). The paper formalizes outlier severity as γ = ‖μ‖/‖σ‖ and proposes mean-centering as a solution.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Death rates vary dramatically between models: near-zero on GPT-2, over 70% on AlphaFold3 with identical configurations
claim c2 [quantitative]: Outlier severity γ = ‖μ‖/‖σ‖ predicts initial death rates (Spearman ρ = 0.89 for dead-by-TopK, 0.82 for dead-by-ReLU) across 454 model-layer combinations
claim c3 [quantitative]: Spearman ρ = 0.82 for dead-by-ReLU across 454 model-layer combinations
claim c4 [qualitative]: Features anti-aligned with the activation mean receive permanently negative pre-activations and never fire
claim c5 [qualitative]: Dead features can revive during training, but recovery requires the SAE bias to learn the activation mean, a process that is prohibitively slow at high γ
claim c6 [qualitative]: Mean-centering (subtracting the activation mean) sidesteps outlier-induced death and eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models
claim c7 [theoretical]: Dimension-level activation outliers cause feature death by shifting pre-activations at initialization based on each feature's alignment with the activation mean

How Quantization Changes Interpretable Features: A Sparse Autoencoder Analysis of Language Models (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, quantization, feature-survival, interpretability-measurement
The paper measures how interpretable features (extracted via sparse autoencoders from full-precision language models) degrade under quantization. A frozen SAE trained on full-precision activations serves as a fixed basis; the authors encode activations from both full-precision and quantized (INT8–INT4) versions of the same model on identical tokens, then measure per-feature survival via Pearson correlation. The mechanism is observational: no new model or training is introduced, only measurement of feature persistence across precision levels.
claim c1 [quantitative]: 62.4 percent of active features surviving at INT6 on Pythia-70M
claim c2 [quantitative]: 51.3 percent surviving at INT6 on Gemma-2-2B
claim c3 [qualitative]: feature survival is graded: features degrade systematically rather than failing all at once, with most non-survivors blurred rather than destroyed
claim c4 [quantitative]: survival is predictable from full-precision statistics alone, with cross-validated AUCs of 0.92 to 0.97
claim c5 [qualitative]: peak activation as the strongest marginal predictor
claim c6 [negative]: on Gemma-2-2B, INT7 improves perplexity while degrading 18.7 percent of features
claim c7 [quantitative]: quantization and matched-perplexity magnitude pruning damage strongly overlapping feature sets, with Jaccard overlap of 0.79 to 0.86
claim c8 [quantitative]: damage-score Spearman correlation of 0.98 between quantization and matched-perplexity magnitude pruning

Sparse Autoencoders for Interpretable Emotion Control in Text-to-Speech (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, activation-steering, emotion-control, interpretability, latent-feature-intervention
The system analyzes and controls emotion in LLM-based TTS models by training sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on semantic hidden states to identify sparse latent features associated with emotion. A feature-level intervention framework then steers subsets of these sparse features to induce or suppress emotions without modifying backbone model parameters. The mechanism reads the LLM's own hidden activations, decomposes them via SAE, and writes modified activations back to control acoustic attributes like pitch.
claim c1 [qualitative]: emotional variation is distributed across multiple sparse latent features
claim c2 [qualitative]: intervening on a small subset of sparse latent features enables interpretable emotion control
claim c3 [qualitative]: distinct latent features are associated with specific acoustic attributes (e.g., pitch)
claim c4 [qualitative]: emotional expression arises from coordinated latent contributions rather than a single global shift
claim c5 [qualitative]: steering sparse latent features achieves comparable or superior emotion induction and suppression performance relative to global steering and existing TTS baselines
claim c6 [qualitative]: feature-level intervention framework enables bidirectional emotion induction and suppression without modifying backbone parameters

Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders for Effective Mechanistic Interpretability (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, mechanistic-interpretability, feature-splitting, subspace-learning, block-sparsity, decoder-basis
Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA) replace single-vector decoder directions in traditional SAEs with learned decoder subspaces. The system uses block sparsity via Top-s group gating and adapts each group's effective rank with nuclear-norm regularization. Information flows from model activations into a sparse latent code (block-sparse, gated by Top-s groups) whose groups are decoded via learned subspace projections rather than fixed directions, with regularization to control rank.
claim c1 [theoretical]: Reconstructing a feature of intrinsic dimension d_i ≥ 2 to error ε with single-direction decoders forces a number of atoms that is exponential in d_i
claim c2 [theoretical]: There exists a continuous path from the true d_i-dimensional basis to a strictly lower risk of the ℓ1-regularized SAE objective, whose descent directions drive any trained dictionary into the exponential regime
claim c3 [theoretical]: Once the block size satisfies r ≥ d_i, a single group not only can represent the entire feature slice but is the global minimizer of the SASA objective
claim c4 [theoretical]: SASA consolidation yields sample complexity polynomial in d_i rather than exponential
claim c5 [qualitative]: SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability
claim c6 [qualitative]: SASA matches or exceeds standard SAEs while training on roughly half the token budget

LatentSkill: From In-Context Textual Skills to In-Weight Latent Skills for LLM Agents (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: lora-adapter, hypernetwork, weight-space-skills, skill-composition, parameter-arithmetic
LatentSkill converts textual skill descriptions into plug-and-play LoRA adapters via a pretrained hypernetwork. A hypernetwork reads task-specific text skills and generates LoRA weights (low-rank matrices) that are injected into a frozen LLM's weight space. Skills are stored in adapter parameters rather than context, eliminating per-step skill tokens while enabling modular composition via parameter-space arithmetic and LoRA scaling coefficients.
claim c1 [quantitative]: LatentSkill improves ALFWorld success by 21.4 points on the seen split with 64.1% fewer prefill tokens compared to in-context skill baseline
claim c2 [quantitative]: LatentSkill improves ALFWorld success by 13.4 points on the unseen split with 64.1% fewer prefill tokens compared to in-context skill baseline
claim c3 [quantitative]: LatentSkill improves Search-QA exact match by 3.0 points with 72.2% lower skill-token overhead compared to in-context skill baseline
claim c4 [qualitative]: Generated skill LoRAs form a structured semantic geometry that can be precisely controlled via the LoRA scaling coefficient
claim c5 [qualitative]: Skills can be composed through parameter-space arithmetic when skill components are aligned
claim c6 [qualitative]: Weight-space skills remove per-step skill tokens while preserving modular loading, scaling, and composition

Auto-Configuring Scientific Simulators with Lightweight Coding-Agent Adapters (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: coding-agent-adapter, retrieval, procedural-memory, validation-gating, self-evolution
SIGA is a lightweight adapter that wraps an off-the-shelf coding agent (TreeSim) to configure scientific simulators by supplying an executable contract through retrieval of example decks, procedural memory of past trajectories, agent-callable validation functions, and validation-gated termination. The coding agent remains frozen; only the adapter (retrieval index, memory store, validation rules) is external and mutable. Information flows: the agent queries the adapter for relevant examples and validation feedback, runs simulator commands, and terminates only when validation gates pass.
claim c1 [quantitative]: on harder held-out tasks SIGA improves TreeSim from 0.720 to 0.789
claim c2 [quantitative]: SIGA reduces across-run standard deviation by about 16x by preventing empty or invalid decks
claim c3 [qualitative]: in a human calibration, SIGA reaches in about five minutes the deck quality a domain expert reached in about three hours
claim c4 [qualitative]: SIGA's main gain is reliability on harder held-out tasks
claim c5 [qualitative]: transfers to OpenFOAM and LAMMPS show the recipe is portable but interface-dependent: completion gates help when structural completeness is the bottleneck, while memory and retrieval help when value correctness is
claim c6 [qualitative]: the adapter supplies the simulator's executable contract through retrieval, procedural memory, agent-callable validation, and validation-gated termination while leaving the model and loop frozen
claim c7 [qualitative]: prior trajectories can rewrite the adapter contents without modifying the underlying agent

One Stone, Three Birds: Self-adaptive Optimal Transport for Multi-VLM Selection, Adaptation, and Ensembling (2026)
seam: text; tags: multi-model-selection, optimal-transport, self-adaptive, unsupervised-adaptation, ensemble-integration, training-free
OSTB is a training-free framework that operates on frozen candidate VLMs by estimating a consensus sample-to-class transport plan via optimal transport over their combined outputs. The framework reads predictions (class probabilities and/or embeddings) from multiple VLMs, solves a self-adaptive optimal transport problem to infer a latent sample-class structure without modifying VLM parameters, and reuses this learned transport plan for three downstream tasks: (1) ranking VLMs by semantic and visual reliability, (2) fitting transport-conditioned visual classifiers for target adaptation, and (3) integrating predictions via reliability-weighted ensemble.
claim c1 [qualitative]: OSTB improves model ranking, adaptation stability, and ensemble robustness under heterogeneous candidate pools
claim c2 [qualitative]: The three deployment decisions (model selection, target adaptation, prediction integration) depend on the same latent object: a trustworthy sample-class structure in the target set
claim c3 [qualitative]: A consensus sample-to-class transport plan can be estimated without updating VLM parameters by leveraging complementary evidence from multiple VLMs
claim c4 [qualitative]: The learned transport structure is reused for model selection by ranking by combined semantic and visual reliability induced by the consensus plan
claim c5 [qualitative]: Target adaptation is obtained by fitting transport-conditioned visual classifiers
claim c6 [qualitative]: Ensembling is implemented through reliability-aware probabilistic integration using the learned transport plan

TrustMargin: Training-Free Arbitration between Parametric Memory and Retrieved Evidence in Large Language Models (2026)
seam: text; tags: answer-arbitration, source-selection, training-free, likelihood-scoring, rag-parametric-fusion
TRUSTMARGIN is a training-free arbitration layer that selects between two frozen-model answers: one from direct generation (parametric memory) and one from RAG-augmented generation (retrieved evidence). It scores both candidates using the model's own token likelihoods, combining a parametric-prior margin (testing whether the model's memory accepts the retrieved answer) and an evidence-binding margin (measuring question-specific support from passages while discounting passage-only salience). The system operates post-generation on existing candidate answers without fine-tuning, external judges, or additional decoding.
claim c1 [qualitative]: TRUSTMARGIN consistently improves over Direct generation and BM25-RAG across 2WIKIMQA and CWQA with three LLaMA scales
claim c2 [qualitative]: TRUSTMARGIN recovers part of the Direct/RAG oracle gap
claim c3 [qualitative]: TRUSTMARGIN generalizes to multiple training-free RAG pipelines
claim c4 [qualitative]: TRUSTMARGIN is training-free, plug-and-play, does not require fine-tuning, external judges, or additional generation
claim c5 [theoretical]: The parametric-prior margin tests whether the model's parametric memory accepts the retrieved answer
claim c6 [theoretical]: The evidence-binding margin discounts passage-only salience and measures question-specific support from retrieved passages

SVoT: State-aware Visualization-of-Thought for Spatial Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: reinforcement-learning, intermediate-state-verification, spatial-reasoning, multimodal-chain-of-thought, reward-design
SVoT is a reinforcement learning framework that augments multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with interleaved text and visual reasoning over spatial state sequences. The model generates intermediate states (text descriptions and visualizations) alongside transition reasoning chains, enabling verification of action preconditions and effects. A trained reward function evaluates the correctness of generated states and transitions. The MLLM backbone is fine-tuned via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to maximize rewards that capture both state accuracy and transition validity.
claim c1 [quantitative]: SVoT with transition-aware supervision achieves state-of-the-art performance across the introduced domains, yielding up to a 65% absolute accuracy gain on out-of-distribution test sets.
claim c2 [qualitative]: SVoT integrates transition reasoning chains into the generation processes, enabling the model to verify action preconditions and effects through interleaved textual and visual reasoning.
claim c3 [qualitative]: SVoT generates interleaved, verifiable intermediate states and visualizations to enable reliable multi-hop inference over both intermediate states and state transitions.
claim c4 [negative]: Current studies often leave intermediate states unverified and treat state transitions as implicit processes, which limits reliability in multi-hop spatial reasoning.
claim c5 [negative]: Existing benchmarks reduce state transitions to single-variable updates, substantially simplifying the problems.
claim c6 [qualitative]: Five domains are established by extending classical environments and introducing two novel domains, Pacman and Gather, that require multi-object interactions and numerical reasoning.

Seeing Before Colliding: Anticipatory Safe RL with Frozen Vision-Language Models (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: constrained-rl, lagrangian-multiplier, anticipatory-safety, vlm-cost-signal, confidence-gating, vision-language
VLM-Safe-RL integrates a frozen vision-language model (CLIP) into constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) optimization via PPO-Lagrangian. A frozen VLM encodes visual observations and outputs per-step anticipatory cost scores (via margin-based logistic confidence gating), which are injected into the Lagrange multiplier update alongside traditional post-hoc cost signals. Two independent pathways (Decoupled Dual-Path CLIP) separate reward and cost objectives; Confidence Gating applies Bayes-optimal weighting to the VLM cost term. The composed algorithm (VLMPPOLag) runs on top of a frozen vision-language backbone without retraining it.
claim c1 [qualitative]: VLMPPOLag+Conf is the only configuration in the default budget comparison that simultaneously retains substantive return (J_r ≈ 40) and holds cost within budget on a majority of seeds
claim c2 [qualitative]: catastrophe rate reduced from 41% to 26% on held-out MetaDrive Medium (95% bootstrap CI [-26, -5] pp)
claim c3 [qualitative]: The mechanism generalizes to held-out MetaDrive Medium and shows directionally consistent transfer to Bullet Safety-Gym
claim c4 [negative]: The approach does not transfer to MetaDrive Easy/Hard and fails with Qwen2-VL backbone
claim c5 [negative]: The MetaDrive Hard failure is traced to a Lagrangian-regulation pathology rather than the VLM signal itself
claim c6 [qualitative]: Decoupled Dual-Path CLIP maintains independent reward and cost paths that respect the CMDP's factorization
claim c7 [qualitative]: Confidence Gating derives a Bayes-optimal weight from a logistic noise model on the CLIP margin
claim c8 [qualitative]: This is the first work to use frozen VLM signals as an anticipatory cost term inside the CMDP Lagrangian update

Decoupled Mixture-of-Experts for Parametric Knowledge Injection (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: mixture-of-experts, knowledge-injection, modular-experts, router, uncertainty-aware, kv-cache-preserving
DMoE is a modular knowledge injection system that decouples expert modules and a router from a base LLM. External knowledge corpora are converted into independently updatable expert modules. A lightweight uncertainty-aware router activates relevant experts only when the base model lacks sufficient knowledge during generation. Experts are attached only to the final-layer feed-forward network to preserve KV-cache reuse during auto-regressive inference, enabling parameter-level knowledge augmentation while avoiding full model retraining.
claim c1 [qualitative]: DMoE decouples both experts and the router from the base model, converting external knowledge corpora into independently updatable expert modules
claim c2 [qualitative]: DMoE uses a lightweight uncertainty-aware router to activate relevant experts only when the base model lacks sufficient knowledge during generation
claim c3 [qualitative]: By attaching experts only to the final-layer feed-forward network, DMoE preserves KV-cache reuse while enabling parameter-level knowledge augmentation
claim c4 [qualitative]: DMoE avoids catastrophic forgetting, knowledge conflict, and costly updates compared to post-training based methods
claim c5 [qualitative]: DMoE consistently improves answer quality over retrieval and adapter-based baselines on knowledge-intensive benchmarks

Cascaded Sparse Autoencoders Learn Multi-Level Visual Concepts in Multimodal LLMs (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, hierarchical-decomposition, interpretability, concept-steering, visual-representation
Cascaded Sparse Autoencoders (CSAEs) decompose multimodal LLM visual representations into hierarchical, interpretable features. A first-level SAE is trained on model activations to extract low-level sparse features; a second-level SAE then trains directly on the decoder weights of the first SAE, treating learned feature directions as inputs to learn higher-level concept abstractions. Information flows from frozen MLLM activations → first-level SAE sparse codes → first-level decoder weights → second-level SAE sparse codes, enabling multi-level concept organization without nesting coupling or stacking bottlenecks.
claim c1 [qualitative]: CSAEs improve interpretability in terms of hierarchical concept coherence over state-of-the-art SAE baselines
claim c2 [qualitative]: The learned concept groups support effective group-level interventions in MLLM outputs
claim c3 [qualitative]: CSAEs avoid drawbacks from the shared-prefix coupling of nesting, Matryoshka-style hierarchies and the bottlenecks of naively stacked SAEs
claim c4 [qualitative]: CSAEs enable learning of 'concepts of concepts' by training a second-level SAE directly on the decoder weights of the first-level SAE

From Sparse Features to Trustworthy Proxies: Certifying SAE-Based Interpretability (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sae, interpretability, generalization-bound, faithfulness-certification, proxy-model, activation-reconstruction
The paper proposes a post-hoc certification framework for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) applied to frozen language models. A sparse proxy is constructed by replacing a native hidden activation with its SAE reconstruction. The framework derives a generalization-based upper bound on the base model's expected risk using four measurable quantities: proxy risk, SAE reconstruction gap, concept-pool mismatch, and sparse complexity. This bound serves as an operational criterion for determining when SAE-based explanations faithfully represent the underlying model's behavior.
claim c1 [qualitative]: The bound becomes non-vacuous on GPT-2 Small, Gemma-2B, and Llama-3-8B at practical sample sizes
claim c2 [qualitative]: A non-vacuous bound indicates that the extracted sparse features retain meaningful predictive information
claim c3 [qualitative]: Small reconstruction and mismatch errors indicate that the proxy remains behaviorally close to the original model
claim c4 [qualitative]: A detailed layerwise analysis of Llama-3-8B reveals a strong depth dependence, with later layers becoming much easier to certify, associated with both stronger local fidelity and weaker downstream error amplification
claim c5 [qualitative]: Feature-shuffling ablations show that the decomposition distinguishes genuine semantic alignment from mere statistical sparsity, providing a useful diagnostic for when SAE-based explanations become less reliable
claim c6 [theoretical]: The framework derives an upper bound on the base model's expected risk using four measurable quantities: proxy risk, SAE reconstruction gap, concept-pool mismatch, and sparse complexity

Rational Sparse Autoencoder (2026)
seam: own-activations; tags: sparse-autoencoder, rational-activation, mechanistic-interpretability, remez-exchange, activation-approximation
Rational Sparse Autoencoder (RSAE) replaces fixed encoder nonlinearities (ReLU, JumpReLU, TopK) in standard SAEs with a trainable rational function. A two-stage pipeline initializes rational coefficients via relaxed Remez exchange on synthetic data, calibrates scale parameters, then fine-tunes under sparsity-regularized reconstruction loss. The rational activation uniformly approximates existing SAE activation primitives while adapting to pre-activation geometry.
claim c1 [qualitative]: Rational activations are flexible enough to uniformly approximate the activation primitives used by existing SAE families on compact domains
claim c2 [qualitative]: RSAE strictly improves on baseline SAEs after the fine-tuning step, both on reconstruction-side metrics and on downstream-behaviour metrics
claim c3 [qualitative]: RSAE gains are consistent across host language models, across baseline activation families, and across the full range of baseline sparsity tested
claim c4 [qualitative]: Feature-level interpretability is preserved under sparse probing
claim c5 [qualitative]: The upgrade adds only a handful of scalar parameters per autoencoder and runs in minutes on a single consumer GPU

NeSyCat Torch: A Differentiable Tensor Implementation of Categorical Semantics for Neurosymbolic Learning (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: categorical-semantics, monad-parametric, neurosymbolic-logic, lazy-marginalisation, differentiable-tensor, first-order-reasoning
NeSyCat Torch interprets neurosymbolic semantics via differentiable tensor implementations of categorical logic. The system encodes first-order predicates and functions as neural networks, implementing truth-value aggregation through monadic semantics. Information flows from symbolic axioms written in monad-based do-notation through a parametric monad (distribution, log-tensor, or batch) that performs lazy pruning and marginalisation via monadic bind. Outputs are tensor-based implementations (HaskTorch, JAX, PyTorch backends) that support both symbolic reasoning and end-to-end differentiation.
claim c1 [qualitative]: NeSyCat subsumes classical, fuzzy, probabilistic and neural systems under a single inductive definition of truth, parametric in a strong monad and an aggregation structure on truth-values
claim c2 [qualitative]: The lazy log-tensor monad over the log-semiring enables numerically stable, differentiable training with lazy pruning of unneeded branches
claim c3 [qualitative]: On MNIST addition, HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch implementations outperform LTN and DeepProbLog in speed and accuracy
claim c4 [qualitative]: NeSyCat Torch achieves nearly the accuracy of DeepStochLog on MNIST addition
claim c5 [qualitative]: Unlike DeepStochLog, the framework applies uniformly to many first-order neurosymbolic approaches
claim c6 [qualitative]: The construction is parametric in the monad; instantiating it with the Giry monad extends the approach to continuous probability

FlowEdit: Associative Memory for Lifelong Pronunciation Adaptation in Flow-Matching TTS (2026)
seam: trained-bridge; tags: lifelong-adaptation, episodic-memory, hopfield-network, text-embedding-perturbation, content-addressable-retrieval, pronunciation-correction
